### yewwei.tan — 2014-01-22T12:10:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-106605>

([The Motivation for the journal title](http://scrivle.com/posts/take-it-slow))

Starting a log over here to post random thoughts along the road to optimal. Updates will probably be pretty sporadic.

Current State:

\- 22.5yo Male
\- 189cm/6'2'' height, 84+kg/186lbs weight at 13-15% body fat
\- sitting pretty low on the inflammation side

Current Goals:

**(1) get back to a lowish body fat percentage and actually stay there**

I dieted successfully in 2012, and I would say I want to get back to the leanness that I got in [this picture](http://tanyewwei.com/pics/aac9b47d-483d-47d4-83d3-e81053d71177.JPG) (warning! that's a picture of me in my underwear when I still had dreadlocks ). I'd think I was just around 10% body fat, and around 79kg/174lbs. I got to that bodyfat percentage and was still feeling great -- deadlifting 180kg, squatting 140+kg, and energy level generally pretty high.

Then I made the mistake of trying to get even leaner than that, and after another 3 months of trying, I ended up 3kg lighter than in the picture and feeling shitty. Judging by pictures, I seem to have lost more lean mass than fat.

This time, I'm getting there mainly through CT and some caloric restriction, while eating a ketogenic Epi-Paleo diet (verified using ketone strips).

**(2) Keep mental performance very high**

I'm definitely what you can call a knowledge worker, and generally spend 12-14hrs a day doing mental activity of some form.

I already do this on a daily basis by eating a ketogenic diet, getting enough sleep, etc ... I just want to make sure that I maintain this through the entire year.

**(3) Avoid recurrence of any AI symptoms**

I took a major hit after eating some mystery meat in 2011. Constipated for days, and then started feeling pretty bad for the next few months. That kicked off the whole journey to start fixing my health before it got hold of me.

Symptoms always involved skin redness in the hands and feet, along with constipation. Jack alluded to the next blog hitting on this very topic, so I'm going to study that and give my thoughts.

In any case, I haven't had any flares for about 9 months now, and my labs seem to show that inflammation is pretty low. Let's keep it that way.

**(4) Fix some nagging issues**

\- finger nails getting a little brittle
\- onset of night-time leg cramps in the last 2 months
\- some water retention in my ankles during hot days (doesn't happen if temp is below 30C)

I'm suspecting some form of Iron deficiency here. Some lab tests in October 2013 showed transferrin saturation at 30%, Iron at 19 umol/L, and serum ferritin at 252 ug/L. I took the high serum ferritin to mean that iron was high, and took some measures to drop iron (lactoferrin + 2 blood donation runs). That's when the symptoms started.

In hindsight, when I got that high serum ferritin, my hsCRP was at 0.3mg/L, and total cholesterol was at 220mg/dL, HDL was 104mg/dL, and Trigs was 35mg/dL. I should have taken that a transient marker of localized (or possibly remnant) inflammation, and the appropriate response should have been to wait and see where I was after a couple of months, instead of jump straight into an iron reduction protocol.

For now, I'm just going to throw in a bunch of high iron seafood and red meat to my diet on a regular basis, and then assess in 1-2 months.

**(5) Fix any unexpected problems that do arise**

You never know what can happen!

\---

I'll fill in various details as I make more posts.

\---

I've been experimenting with Dave Asprey's Upgraded Brain Octane Oil, which is pure C8 MCTs.

Yesterday I had one tablespoon in the morning. I definitely felt a boost compared to regular MCT, even above how I feel during my regular ketotic state (I hover between 1.0mM and 2.5mM on a daily basis).

Today, I had two tablespoons, and at about the hour mark, I was starting to get some tunnel vision and slight light-headedness. I took some basic health metrics:

\- Ketones: 4.1mM
\- Blood Glucose: 4.2mmol/L (75mg/dL)
\- Heart Rate Variability: very erratic, bouncing between very good and very bad
\- heart rate: 62 to 75 per min (my usual heart rate is 50 to 60)

Obviously there's such a thing as too much MCT for the brain to handle

Anyway, obviously Dave Asprey's stuff works. Question is whether it's better per se for my needs, and honestly, I already stay in ketosis without it, so it probably is not needed.

I should ask my Dad to give this a shot (he's eating the Perfect Health Diet), and then test his ketones.

I also don't seem to get any of these effects from regular MCT oil (which is probably a 40% C8, 60% C10 blend), and can get up to 6 tablespoons a day with no issues. Must have something to do with the rate of ketone formation.

Where I could potentially see it useful is to stay somewhat ketotic in the face of a higher than normal carbohydrate or protein intake. So you'd take it to "curb the damage" of eating a banana out of season. (still don't like bananas)

Technically my first name is "Yew-wei", but "Yew" is fine as well!

I'm staying away from coffee for now, so it's water for me. I don't use boiling water, but that's just because I want to drink it instantly and not scald my tongue (I don't think that temps below boiling point affect any of the beneficial compounds in chocolate).

Nuts are either from the Royal Nut Company down in Brunswick VIC -- [http://www.royalnutcompany.com.au](http://www.royalnutcompany.com.au) , or from Ross's Nuts down in Preston -- [http://rosssqualitynuts.com.au](http://rosssqualitynuts.com.au) . Both are factory outlets, so the throughput is pretty high and hence the foodstuffs are pretty fresh.

Woolies butter is great, though I'm still being cautious with dairy - no more than 1-2 tablespoons for me right now.

I haven't tried the duck from ALDI. It's one of my favourite meats, but I think I've still got a slight phobia to duck, since that was what I was eating during my last AI flare. That was Cantonese style roast duck though, so it's probably from the soy glaze rather than the duck itself. But you know, us humans are irrational ...

\---

Regarding the Upgraded Brain Octane, I think it's a case of how quickly the ketones are produced in the bloodstream. Today I had 2 tablespoons, but one at 7am, and another at 10+am. Each time I just an energy boost and that was it. No other adverse effects. So it's 2 tablespoons at once that my body can't handle.

I'm sure everyone will have a different effect, so that's something to experiment with.

I did get it online, from [http://optimoz.com](http://optimoz.com) . Not a cheap buy at all, but I would say it's worth it if you really get a good effect out of it.

I guess this answers caroline's question I forgot to address above.

\---

Regarding salt in hot choc, I think that hot chocolate by itself isn't exactly a good way to rehydrate in the first place, and I doubt the addition of some sea salt would make it any worse.

So in my mind, I should just grab a cup of hot chocolate (which is maybe 400mL / 14oz), then get another 500mL of pure water once I'm done with the hot choc. That said, it's pretty easy for me to down a liter of fluid in the morning, which may not be the case for many people.

\---

### yewwei.tan — 2014-01-25T11:32:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-107001>

Ever since the last month or so, I've been filtering tap water through a basic counter-top reverse osmosis system.

I got this unit from PSI filters -- [http://www.psifilters.com.au/revers...reverse-osmosis-systems/4-STAGE-COUNTERTOP-RO](http://www.psifilters.com.au/reverse-osmosis/countertop-reverse-osmosis-systems/4-STAGE-COUNTERTOP-RO) . I also got 2 sets of replacement cartridges -- [http://www.psifilters.com.au/revers...replacement-countertop-ro-cartridge-twin-deal](http://www.psifilters.com.au/reverse-osmosis/countertop-reverse-osmosis-systems/replacement-countertop-ro-cartridge-twin-deal)

Each filter set supposedly gets you 2000L of water, so that's $330 for 6000L, or 5.5cents per litre, which is decently cheap for RO water. Free delivery all the way from Tassie too!

It filters surprisingly quickly too -- about 30mins to almost fill my 12L glass jug.

Their site claims that they got a 92-96% rejection for the halogens (chloride, bromide, fluoride), as well as 96-99% on medications. In any case, from what I've seen, most RO systems will basically take out 90+% of any water impurities, which is much better than not having them.

But other than that, I'm taking any other measures. Maybe once I'm a rich man, I'll have a way to catch rainwater, then put it through RO, then pipe it into a transparent tank above the roof of my house (to collect photons from the sun), making sure the tank has a magnetic base, and then when I want to drink it, it gets funneled down through a pipe which is shaped such that water passing through it is vortexed on the way down, so that I can clink together a couple of (RO water) ice cubes and enjoy the best water that I can get.

(All dollar amounts in Aussie dollars of course!)

### yewwei.tan — 2014-01-27T21:05:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-107056>

Looks like about 36 hours worth of data was lost during the downtime.

As a software developer dealing with large information systems myself, I can't say that the way the site is managed is very good at all, but oh well, that's always the danger of putting stuff on the internet

\---

Anyway, to caroline's point above, I gave a response about how we should distinguish EZ water that we drink from EZ water in our cells.

Having lots of EZ water in our cells is obviously the end goal here, and that is achieved largely by having a great redox potential, and avoiding any chronic non-native inflammatory insults (EMF, poor sleep, bright light at the wrong times, etc ...).

Drinking EZ water is obviously great! But I think that drinking good water isn't going to make up for the stress of living on the 17th of an apartment block while being smothered in WiFi Signals.

The next step for me will be to read Pollack's book on water to truly understand the way things actually work. It's being shipped to me at the time of this writing, and I'll be making notes once I get it.

\---

Additionally, Jack just made these comments on the Optimal Reset Facebook group:

> Mineral's do not equal salts.....and when I say salts we are talking chemical definition not just table salt in water. We are also talking salt in the water not your food. In chemistry, salts are ionic compounds that can result from the neutralization reaction of an acid and a base. They are composed of related numbers of cations (positively charged ions) and anions (negative ions) so that the product is electrically neutral (without a net charge). These component ions can be inorganic such as chloride (Cl−), as well as organic such as acetate (C2H3O2−) and monatomic ions such as fluoride (F−), as well as polyatomic ions such as sulfate (SO42−).
>
> Water that undergoes charge separation generates its own protons.......and those protons flow based upon the electromagnetic force they are under. You may not understand this yet but you will. The think that create protons is infra red but what drives their flow in all fluids is sunlight.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-01-27T21:06:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-107057>

**Current CT Protocol**

It being summertime here, I've actually stopped full-immersion style CT. Water temp is just too high (20+C), and I don't have the facilities to make enough ice at the moment.

Hence I'd rather not be sitting in a tub of fluoridated tub of warm water.

Instead, it's been spot CT around the torso using the CoolFatBurner and CoolGutBuster for anywhere from 1 to 2 hours each day. Definitely leaves the entire torso nice and pink afterward, and hopefully that's a decent substitude until autumn time when I can actually get <15C water.

### Josh — 2014-01-27T22:54:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-107065>

A friend who has built water plants and specializes in systems designed my system with the 5 stages of filtration with carbon and fabric down to 0.5 microns and the UV light before the R/O membrane, a ste of 6 round magnets with a hole in the center around the supply tube coming from the tank with the polarity south to north in the direction of the flow to handle homeopathic imprints, another 0.5micron carbon after the tank and then through a basic vortex unit. THe water is nice. My friend says that the main issue is bacteria and particles damaging the membrane. By putting the UV and more robust filtration before the membrane, the mebrane is preserved for much longer.

### Josh — 2014-01-28T02:45:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-107090>

I doubt you could configure a counter top this way. I have mine under the kitchen sink. I think the parts cost me about 400-500 USD total.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-01-28T04:18:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-107100>

No way a counter top system is going to beat a good sink installed unit, especially one with UV treatment and vortexing! That setup sounds (and tastes) awesome by the way.

For those interested, the membrane that the unit I linked to is using is the DOW Filtec BW60-1812-75: [http://www.wateranywhere.com/produc...d=155&osCsid=5a2fe2e09abf0e80b03dd1ab27e18b19](http://www.wateranywhere.com/product_info.php?cPath=22_41_61&products_id=155&osCsid=5a2fe2e09abf0e80b03dd1ab27e18b19)

\---

Maybe the situation is different in the US, but even having a counter top system whilst replacing cartridges all the time is way cheaper than trying to buy commercially produced water (5 cents vs 70-80 cents per liter). A lot more convenient too obviously, and now I can make lots of bone broth knowing that at least it's with better quality water.

\---

I'm pretty sure that the tradeoff between CT in fluoridated water vs no CT is going to lean toward getting CT done anyway (provided the water is actually cold).

In winter time, I'll definitely go back to lying in my 700L clark rubber swimming pool (basically everything submerged except for your nose and mouth), and then doing some stretching in the garden.

But personally, my preferred winter time CT strategy is taking lots of cold showers through the day, and then standing around doing work on my computer while I air dry (air temps are usually 10-14C). I definitely feel cold, but don't actually shiver too much, and can definitely feel myself put out lots of heat. The net result is that I can actually get stuff done without feeling miserable in the cold.

\---

Talking about heat, it's 39C / 102F here in Melbourne right now ...

### Josh — 2014-01-28T19:20:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-107185>

The main issue with R/O is keeping the membrane intact. Membranes for smaller outputs (up to 100 gallons per day) are not very large. They can easily fit on a counter top. It is the extra elements housed in 10 inch housings to pretreat the water before it goes in a tank and any post treatment that takes up space. Of course the tank takes up space too. Membranes are also happiest when one runs alot of water through them, so I fill a 2 gallon urn a couple times per day giving my membrane a good long run. My friend actually runs low output membranes continuously into large holding tanks. Ultimately, you would actually need to test the water for dissolved solids and contaminants to see if your membrane was still good. It seems that everything lasts much longer than advertised if used optimally. One can build one's own unit that attaches to the faucet and then just run it overnight to fill a holding container of some sort. I have seen people use an undersink unit without a tank for this and or just buy the parts and hook them up themselves. In america, I use [http://www.appliedmembranes.com/](http://www.appliedmembranes.com/) for parts, etc.. I am sure there are sumilar supply houses in Australia.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-01-30T11:43:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-107429>

Another hot day above 30C / 86F, and again I'm getting these symptoms of slightly reddish skin on my feet and a little swelling.

I've found thought that symptoms decrease in intensity when I'm not eating that much in general. As Jack would say, my problem isn't with electrons, it's with Protons.

It's also on days that I get my ketone levels up to greater than 1.5mM before 12pm (6 hours after waking) that I see the least symptoms. For me, that usually means skipping a big-ass breakfast (BAB), and instead having only some fat (about 500kcal worth) from waking until 12pm.

\---

Another problem that is annoying but not seriously pathological is the urge to eat without actually being hungry. I grew up a fat kid, and went through various stages of dieting from age 15-21, so having a weird relationship with food now doesn't surprise me.

I guarantee that I'm still having some lingering neurotransmitter issues which I need to address.

That said, I'm still very easy maintaining weight where I'm at, which is already relatively lean (13-15% body fat). It's when I purposefully cut calories (during experiments described in the last mini-section) that I respond with an urge to eat more stuff.

Also, this urge to eat increases the more creative work I do; the days which I'm absolutely pumped and can easily program computers, draw sketches, and make music all in a 12-hour burst, also tend to be the days which I want to eat the most (and which I have moments where I actively try to suppress it).

A final note is that this urge to eat ALWAYS comes in the first 6-7 hours of waking, and never after 3pm. This related comment by Jack gets me thinking ... [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?9395-How-I-got-to-here&p=107363#post107363](http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?9395-How-I-got-to-here&p=107363#post107363)

\---

Sleep was much better last night that it finally got down to 15C.

That said, I still can't tolerate that temperature sleeping without a blanket. Last night I managed to fall asleep (bed at 10pm) without covering up, but woke up just after 3am, which should put me 5+ hours into sleep, and maybe having gone through 3 sleep cycles. Covered up after that and slept until 6+am.

Air temps in the 15-19C range seem to be the least tolerable for me. My skin remains cold to the touch at that temperature, and I just can't keep my mind off the feeling of cold.

Below that temperature range however, and major goosebumps start setting in, and my body starts to heat up all over (except my hands and feet, which is something I'm trying to fix).

### yewwei.tan — 2014-02-16T23:02:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-110663>

I tried both the Upgraded Force Glutathione from Dave Asprey's UpgradedSelf ([http://www.optimoz.com.au/collections/supplements/products/bulletproof-upgraded-glutathione](http://www.optimoz.com.au/collections/supplements/products/bulletproof-upgraded-glutathione)), as well as LivOn Lab's Liposomal Glutathione ([http://www.iherb.com/LivOn-Laboratories-Lypo-Spheric-GSH-30-Packets-0-2-fl-oz-5-4-ml-Each/25232](http://www.iherb.com/LivOn-Laboratories-Lypo-Spheric-GSH-30-Packets-0-2-fl-oz-5-4-ml-Each/25232))

I didn't really feel much difference between the two given the same dosage (1 serving of UpgradedSelf GSH is 100mg, while 1 packet from LivOn is 450mg).

LivOn is the more cost effective supplement, but contains some soy-derived phospholipids, with no guarantees that it's non-GMO soy. I don't feel the difference though, so I'll probably stick with the LivOn stuff.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-02-17T00:14:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-110667>

I should take stock of the supplements that I'm taking now just to have a look at the "nutritional dependencies" that I have:

-   Vit C -- 1g or 2g twice a day (2g to 4g daily)
-   N-Acetyl Cysteine -- typically 500mg once a day in the morning
-   Lugols -- 6-12 drops daily (usually taken with seaweed)
-   Magnesium Citrate -- 200-400mg daily
-   Magnesium Malate -- 400-600mg daily
-   Vitamin K2 -- MK4 3 times a week at 5mg dose, MK7 100mcg every other day

-   Zinc Glycinate -- at present it's 30mg daily, but I can probably reduce this pretty soon
-   On days with higher protein intake: Bitter melon extract (1000-1500mg) around meals
-   Liposomal Glutathione whenever I feel like I need it
-   Have some ginger root, oregano oil, and curcumin handy whenever I think I'm facing a higher stress scenario

\---

Diet-wise, the basic template on most days is:

\- one meal in the morning which is probably 600-1000kcal.
\- one meal at night of approximately the same size

Protein intake on such days is probably in the 80-120g range depending on whether or not I decide to eat a lot of protein with breakfast. Today's breakfast for example, was decently high in protein, with about 250g of fish eggs in a seaweed broth, then a big cup of cocoa with MCT oil.

Protein wise, of the 14 meals I have, the rule is to get at least 7 with some form of seafood, which usually turns out to be shellfish (prawns usually) and fish eggs given the season (fish spawning season is coming to a close now though). That probably turns out to be 1.25-1.5kg worth of seafood protein a week. The rest of the meals consist of beef, lamb, and pork. I'd say I would be going through anywhere from 3-4kg of animal protein a week.

\---

Current weight is just a hair above 84kg, which is probably 1kg below when I started this journal.

Been pretty busy, and actually taking the opportunity to try some Intermittent Fasting.

So far there hasn't been any hunger, but now that there's been some colder mornings (12-13C or 53-55F), I've been getting some weird symptoms during the mornings:

-   I'd wake up at roughly 6:30am feeling very warm, and not feel cold at all when doing my morning stretching routine in the garden (only wearing shorts)
-   for the next 1-2 hours, I'd be standing around doing work shirtless, have mild goosebumps, be radiating heat, and be sweating from my armpits and back of the neck. I don't feel cold at all, but hands still remain cold.
-   then I'd go back to feeling "normal", but **if I have any form of MCT oil during this fasting period**, **I start simultaneously radiating more heat, but feeling really cold, and the feeling persists for hours. Hands and feet remain cold.**
-   Normal temperature perception only seems to recover after either some exercise, or a hot shower

Eating MCT oil then feeling really cold is a symptom that I experienced all last Winter. All through that time, I was relatively lean (say anywhere in the 10-13% body fat range).

CT 6 kept mentioning that MCT oil wasn't ideal in the winter because it didn't help fluidity of membranes -- [http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-6-the-ancient-pathway/](http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-6-the-ancient-pathway/)

I still don't know what's up with my case of course, but I should probably try more experiments.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-02-21T02:07:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-111440>

Definitely tried chicken liver pate, but I could never really tell how much of it was actual chicken liver flavour (as opposed to the other ingredients). To be honest, I like duck or goose liver better

Thanks for sharing the the experiences caroline.

\---

Seems like any medium or short chain fats have that effect, but not the long-chain ones. So either distilled C6 and C8 MCTs, or a rich MCT source like coconut oil gave me the effect. I never tried excessive amounts of butter due to potential AI issues. Most other animal fats didn't have any noticeable effect.

It's got to be related to my autoimmune issues in some way, I'm just not sure how yet.

The major symptoms I had in the past were always:

-   a redness of the hands and feet,
-   along with shunting of blood away from the hands and feet (they get really cold)
-   and simultaneous radiating of heat from everywhere else while feeling colder than usual (even the region of the forearm just above the wrists gets really warm, so blood shunting is literally exclusively restricted to the hands)

Nowadays the redness is completely gone, but the other 2 symptoms obviously remain, and only surfaced when temperatures fell below 14C.

\---

Another interesting and possibly related note is that I've decided to give daily Glutathione supplementation a shot since 3 days ago. Literally the day I started, I went from 1 bowel movement to 3 in that day, and now back to 1 a day. Bristol score was 4 all the way. I've always thrown around the term "detox symptoms", but this funny issue with MCT oil may have something to do with that as well.

Anyway, I'll continue experimenting!

### Josh — 2014-02-21T02:29:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-111451>

My hunch, I know I must seem like a broken record. It is cheap and there are few downsides....if you follow the suggestions of 250mcg-1mg doses 2-3x/day.

> Molybdenum mimics certain insulin actions in vitro. We have investigated the effects of oral administration of Na2MoO4 (Mo) for 8 wk on carbohydrate and lipid metabolism in streptozotocin-diabetic rats. Mo decreased hyperglycemia and glucosuria by 75% and corrected the elevation of plasma nonesterified fatty acids. Tolerance to glucose loads was improved, and glycogen stores were replenished. These effects were not due to a rise of insulinemia. In liver, Mo restored the blunted mRNA and activity of glucokinase and pyruvate kinase and decreased to normal phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase values. Finally, Mo totally reversed the low expression and activity of acetyl-CoA carboxylase and fatty acid synthase in liver, but not in white adipose tissue. In conclusion, Mo exerts a marked blood glucose-lowering effect in diabetic rats by an insulin-like action. **This effect results in part from a restoration of hepatic glucose metabolism and is associated with a tissue-specific correction of lipogenic enzyme gene expression**, both processes being essentially mediated by reversal of impaired pretranslational regulatory mechanisms. These observations raise new therapeutic perspectives in diabetes, particularly in the insulin-resistant condition.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8779958](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8779958)

> Taurine, like carnitine, is synthesised from methionine and cysteine. It, too, is found only in animal products. A deficiency in intake of these three amino acids, or a metabolic defect in metabolising these sulphur amino acids may lead to a deficiency of taurine creating numerous symptoms, **including poor digestion of fat. A supplement of molybdenum enhances sulphite oxidase activity and helps convert potentially harmful sulphites into taurine.** For 36%, this reduced urinary sulphite loss and improved symptoms. This improved enzyme activity enhances detoxification of the very toxic cyanide ions improving oxidative phosphorylation and cellular oxidation increasing ATP (energy molecule). Supplementing molybdenum or the amino acid L-taurine (500 mg daily, shortly reducing to 100 mg) will then improve the function of the liver, producing better quality bile (darkening of the stool), protecting against gall stones, and improving the digestion of fats. Taurine is vital in preventing cataracts. It spares potassium and calcium in the heart, preventing arrhythmias, aids in detoxifying the body, and serves with GABA and glycine as inhibitory neurotransmitters in the brain.

[http://www.autismfile.com/diet-nutrition/improved-nutrition-relieves-bowel-problems-infections](http://www.autismfile.com/diet-nutrition/improved-nutrition-relieves-bowel-problems-infections)

### yewwei.tan — 2014-02-28T11:52:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-112622>

> [Sammy said:](/goto/post?id=112582)
>
> Yew-wei,
> How is your experimentation with the Upgraded Brain Octane Oil?? I am considering getting it if it really does deliver - i.e. superior mental performance.
> I have a 7 hr exam coming up(1 hr break in between.....) and I need all the edge I can get....

Right now I'm giving MCT a break until I get my Molybdenum supplements and give those a run, but I've basically gone right up to 6 tablespoons without any GI upset.

I do think that the Upgraded Brain Octane is more potent in getting you into a state of ketosis. (Any other pure C8 substitute should work just as well) So if I ate lots of protein during breakfast, then followed that up with some MCT oil, I could get back to registering >1.0mM values quicker, and the cognitive benefit came back quickly as a result. However, I didn't really see much benefit if I was already in a highly ketotic state.

In other words, it helped me get in a state of ketosis quicker if I was out of therapeutic-grade ketosis, but if I already was in a deep state of diet-induced ketosis, then I didn't see much added cognitive boost.

You're going to have to do an n=1 experiment with that, but hopefully the above helps. Best of luck for that 7hr ordeal!

Minor update regarding Molybdenum supplementation.

So far, I've been doing the 2-3mg of molyb with 20mg manganese combo for 2 days. I can't tell if it's having a major effect yet, but one of the immediate observations I did make is that I'm in a deeper state of ketosis for more of the day. eg: the last week I've been hovering only in the 0.7mM to 1.3mM range, but today I'm seeing consistent values above 2.0mM without any change in dietary, sleeping, or stress patterns. Hopefully this is a good clinical sign.

I'll need to wait for some colder days to test my response to the cold, which I view as the most reliable indicator of my mitochondrial function.

\---

Speaking of mitochondrial function, I went ahead an re-listened to the mitochondrial Rx (Jan 2013 webinar), and am thinking about implementing some of the supplementation protocols from that Rx. In short, it's:

\- CoQ10 (ubiquinol): at least 400mg a day
\- 3 types of magnesium => malate, threonate, and orotate
\- Acetyl L-Carnitine => 600mg does 4 times a day (total of 2400mg a day)
\- D-ribose => as much as you want, but an effective dose probably starts at 2g, and maxes out at 20g
\- Taurine => 1g to 2g a day

I'll see how I react to the Molybdenum experiment, but in all likelihood I'll get at least some of the supps in that Rx to reboot my Mitochondrial function (and not have to depend on those supplements).

Autumn is here, and the goal is to get through this winter with minimal shivering and strong mitochondria.

\---

Just gave a quick first-pass read of EE13. Despite lots of grammatical errors, this blog makes a ton of sense, and very decisively describes why Protons are so important, and how Water and Collagen (and to a certain extent, DHA) work together to make this energy for free.

I've made some initial notes here: [http://tanyewwei.com/notes/jk-ee13-water-chemistry](http://tanyewwei.com/notes/jk-ee13-water-chemistry)

Some misc "a-ha" moments:

\- "Hydronium Ions can be stored indefinitely" -- I always wondered what concretely was the thing that "captured energy for later use", I now have my answer
\- EMF and the disruption of the EZ producing ROS -- page 97 of Pollack's 'Fourth Phase of Water' talks exactly about this, and the mechanism is clearly described in that chapter and other chapters of the book

Last miscellaneous point, as a programmer, I am reminded everything that **Simple things are always difficult to accomplish**. Why? Because you can't afford to make a single mistake -- try to flip an egg in a frying pan, you have to accomplish it with a single, smooth, back-forth motion. Mess it up a little, and you end up either with egg all over your clothes, or an unflipped egg. Flipping the egg depends on you executing a single, specific action correctly, with little room for error. The only way of learning to flip the egg is to try and try and try again, until you've nailed just the right cues, just the right motion given your arm strength and limb length, and the right knowledge of your cooking surface to accomplish the egg flip. That is Spretzatturra.

Now imagine having to coordinate millions of "egg flips" simultaneously -- welcome to my world of Distributed Software Systems.

Now imagine having to coordinate trillions upon trillions upon trillions of "egg flips" -- welcome to Mother Nature and the Quantum Cell.

Thank God nature did all the hard work of experimentation for us, and gave us the 3-legged stool.

(Obligatory egg flipping mastery video:

)

### yewwei.tan — 2014-03-27T03:12:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-118192>

I've been handling cocoa better and better over the last week, and today, I decided on an experiment and had my first cup of coffee in months. So far so major symptoms yet after 3 hours, though I am feeling more wired than usual. At my worst, I'd get finger rashes after a cup of coffee, so fingers crossed that this one experiment isn't going to lead to too much.

But things have been going well so far, and I can now also check butter off the list of past food sensitivities. No issues with MCT oil for the last week as well despite high doses (up to 60g a day). Redox potential is definitely getting better.

The supplementation list is getting pretty long though. In addition to those from my previous posts, I've added:

-   PQQ => 20mg a day
-   ECCg => 400mg a day

I've also got some Methyl-Folate and Lithium sitting around after seeing some of my SNP results. Those supplements may warrant an experiment some time pretty soon. Currently I'm thinking of starting on something like 5mg of Lithium a day and seeing what happens after 3 weeks.

\---

In terms of exercise, it's just been hitting the weights room 3 times a week, the occasional bike ride, and then daily stretching and handstand practice.

In terms of diet, I've actually been pushing a more protein. I'm getting between 300-400g raw weight a day of a protein source for 5 days of the week, which should translate to a protein intake of roughly 100-120g a day (up to 1.5 \* BodyweightKG). Nowadays all the protein is consumed at dinner time, within the 4-6pm window. I only eat fat through the rest of the day, which typically means one breakfast meal. As a measure of food quantity, calories on these days are about 1700-2000kcal.

Then 2 days of the week (always workout days), I'm taking one meal to basically eat as much of a lean protein as I want. Example: Last Saturday was about 1kg of pork shoulder cooked together with a couple of pork trotters . The meal structure follows what I wrote in the previous paragraph. Calories on such days are still only around the 2000-2400kcal range, with calories up until dinner being only around 400-800kcal, and then dinner being the balance.

I always have my lowest weigh-ins after a high-protein day like this. Thirst increases a lot after such a meal, and I usually end up drinking 3L of water post dinner. I'm not sure if this is a good sign -- that the body is trying to retain extra water.

Current weight is around 81.7kg to 82.3kg (around 180lbs), and waist is around 82cm (32.2 inches). Keep in mind that I'm around 189cm / 6'3''.

So weight is down about a pound since my last update on 14-Mar (13 days ago). The interesting thing is that it was stuck for 3 weeks in the 82.2-83.0kg range despite the same amount of calories consumed as at present. Weight only started to drop (and drop fast) on the 22nd of March, when I actively stopped protein intake in the morning, and actively restricted fat intake in the evening. Today's weigh-in was at 81.7kg, so I'm expecting the weight loss to continue. There goes to show again that calories are a crude measure at best, and the way that nutrients prime circadian biology is a more significant factor in the utilisation of said nutrients.

In the fitness community, John Kiefer and Borge Fagerli have been touting similar concepts of high fat in the morning, and then actively restricting fat in the evening (though they add carbs, which I disagree with). They have gotten many athletes really lean using this approach without having to compromise caloric intake that much.

The more important question to me of course, is what does this observation mean in terms of cellular energetics? It seems like protein is a more important signalling nutrient than fat as the day progresses, which actually up-regulates fat metabolism somehow (which I think is a good thing WRT Quantum Sleep)

\---

Been listening to 'Mana' from 'Equilibrium' on infinite loop for the past 3 days while programming :

I should note that for the last 3-4 weeks now, my sleep schedule has been 10pm to about 7am. So a good 9hrs most days, with lots of (dirty) dreams to boot. The quantity would have something to do with my age as well (not yet fully myelinated).

I've been recording sleep duration since about early 2013, and I definitely saw a decrease in sleep quantity during the September to November period last year, but that only shortened from 9hrs to 8hrs, and then stayed there through Summer.

I keep reading in Jack's blogs that CRON and Winter naturally go together, so I have this nagging thought that the need for electrons from food in general should decrease during Winter. If for any reason, it would be that water becomes a more significant electron supply as it gets colder. (EE13 -- [http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-13-quantum-water-chemistry-2/](http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-13-quantum-water-chemistry-2/)) But right now, it isn't exactly that cold, and Melbourne rarely goes into the low 30s Fahrenheit even in the middle of winter. It is starting to get cloudy though ...

### yewwei.tan — 2014-04-04T13:12:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-119768>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=119763)
>
> to be on the computer can make my hands cold... I blame the computer

That's true  I've done the measurements, and the ELF magnetic field right below my keyboard is horrible. I've just got a wired external keyboard though to put some distance between, so I'm slowly making the transition -- years of adjusting to the compact design of a laptop have given me a crutch, whereby I type and navigate around my codebase the fastest when I have muscle memory of my keys.

Another interesting note is that I get cold hands almost exclusively between 10am to around 8pm now, which coincides with the current photoperiod (sunrise at 7am, sunset just after 7pm). After 8pm and before 10am, my hands can actually stay decently warm, and those are the coldest times of the day too ...

I always tolerate cold the best in the morning, which should come as no surprise, since I've been chemically reduced for 8 hours (sleep) before that. The nighttime increase in feelings of warmth probably also has something to do with the overall dimming of light. Anyway, this is something I should continue to monitor as the days get shorter.

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=60691)

> Raynaud's in my opinion is a lack of bio-energenics in the vascular smooth muscles causing a lack of extracellular adenosine which cause reactive vasoconstriction to any stimulus. Anything tied to the MG ATPase function improves it. Mg, avoidance of calcium, D-Ribose, Phosphate, L-carnitine, DHA and Iodine help reverse it.

I also had an issue with Iron deficiency which Jack mentioned in that thread as well, which is mostly fixed today.

I should also say that I'm not feeling particularly cold, and don't really shiver in cold weather, and definitely don't shiver in a cold tub (skin gets all pink). So for now, I'm hoping that with my continual signs of improvement, my hands will eventually improve over time.

Hehe, I'm pretty it's the coffee doing it's magic. I get the appetite suppression even if I drink it black.

Speaking of coffee, after 4 days of coffee drinking, I'm finally starting to see negative effects. Today my cold tolerance was markedly worse, leaving me actually feeling cold at an ambient temperature of only around 17C / 63F (while wearing a t-shirt). Yesterday was roughly the same temp but I was feeling just fine. Sleep was good last night too. Other than that, general energy, libido, etc ... all were still as per optimal.

Going to give coffee a break for a week, then maybe allow myself 1-2 cups a week just for hedonism sake (being wary of any possible side effects of course).

Still, I went from 1 cup of coffee causing me to lose sleep last week, to being able to handle 3 consecutive days without any negative side effects, so obviously things are moving in the right direction.

It's definitely the coffee, and I'm experiencing a milder version of the exact symptoms described in my prior post in late February -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-4#post-111440](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-4#post-111440)

All of those symptoms started going away once I started the Molybdenum and Manganese supplementation, and only started to return a little bit today, likely due to the Xanthines found in coffee. If you believe what Josh has said many times, then molybdenum deficiency is really common. For me, just turns out that coffee is a definite trigger.

You could probably give coffee avoidance a shot. And maybe even give Molybdenum supplementation a shot at the appropriate dosages (500-1000mcg doses 2 to 3 times a day, up to a max of 3mg a day)

Hello Adelaide!

I've got an Optium blood ketone monitor -- [http://myfreestyle.com.au/products/freestyle-optium-blood-glucose-monitoring-system/](http://myfreestyle.com.au/products/freestyle-optium-blood-glucose-monitoring-system/). For some reason I can't find one which doesn't have a package deal (with 100 glucose test strips), cheapest is this seller on ebay -- [http://www.ebay.com.au/itm/40-Compa...Blood-Glucose-Monitor-No-Coding-/150984153091](http://www.ebay.com.au/itm/40-Company-CASHBACK-OPTIUM-FREESTYLE-Blood-Glucose-Monitor-No-Coding-/150984153091). Ketone strips are nowhere near cheap. Amcal has the cheapest I've found so far -- [http://www.amcal.com.au/en/amcal/freestyle-optium-blood-ketone---10-strips-093815991137](http://www.amcal.com.au/en/amcal/freestyle-optium-blood-ketone---10-strips-093815991137) , and I've probably spent $200+ to get all the testing information so far.

I'm not a home owner, so I guess I'll to resort to portable RO units for now .

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=121227)

> I have been on and off.. but I quit yesterday again because I am sure it does not fit in my current situation ! Our body will tell
>
> Yew, have you realized that stuff tastes sweeter at times, and have you come to any connection? I have not had 100% chocolate in some time and a few days ago I bought me some. It is a very nice organic pure chocolate from France.
> Weird is, it now tastes so sweet to me, I mean really sweet! Like the 90% chocolate used to taste.. i cannot believe it.. it is almost too sweet...?! tastes like sugar in my mouth

Hmm, I can't say for sure. I've never had a sweet tooth, nor a liking for sweet foods in general. I have always loved starch though (the Chinese would call you a "Rice Bucket"), and always thought that even "bland" crackers were tasty enough to be even plain. So I guess I'm not the right guy to ask , even the raw cacao nibs that I have right now taste decently "sweet" to me -- and I'm comparing that to the D-ribose I've got at the moment, which is basically sugar-sweet.

I do notice though that as my redox potential has improved over the last few months, things taste more "salty". I've always been able to tolerate (and usually enjoy) really salty foods that most people can't, like overly-salty smoked salmon or charcuterie just in a plain state, and of course, I love some salted, waxed duck every Chinese New Year. I also remember crushing every Vegemite challenge I've been suckered into ...

More accurately, I now don't need to, and don't have much desire to add salt to meat to make it taste meaty. Of course meat still tastes better with some added salt, but not _that_ much better like it did in the past. Maybe the glutamate receptors on my tongue are getting more sensitive

### yewwei.tan — 2014-04-16T05:35:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-121782>

Amazing how a few cups of coffee is affecting me so drastically. Last week I was doing very fine, but after those 4 consecutive days with coffee, it's been 5 days, and I still haven't fully recovered. Libido isn't 100% back yet, and cold tolerance is still sub-optimal. On top of that, I've been eating about 2000kcal a day, and losing weight just fine until that coffee experiment, and have gained back a little bit of weight while looking less defined over the last few days. Fortunately, sleep has not been disrupted at all (still getting the needed hours, with presences of recallable dreams). Gut health is also still good (judging by bristol score and my own subjective feelings).

I did an Inger today and went to the market to get some food . Picked up 6 lamb brains for $9, and 2kg of beef liver for $3, then a couple of oysters at $11/dozen. Already had 2 lamb brains and some oysters, and then going to eat some of that beef liver for dinner. Picked up some pork too.

sidenote: apparently NOLA has oysters at like 25 or 50 cents. Damn I need to get down there for an oyster binge one of these days.​

Well, at least my definition of "poor cold tolerance" isn't poor by most standards -- all the people at the market today were wrapped up like crazy, at it was only about 13C / 55F (not much sunshine though), while I was feeling fine in a t-shirt, shorts, and slippers. My hands were still cold of course , which is why I say cold tolerance is sub-optimal. But again, another reminder that you just can't set your standard based upon what is common.

\----
**Hypothesis on My Poor Coffee Tolerance**

I'd better learn something from this experience. My symptoms seem to indicate an issue with water. I just remembered the recommendation from Dr Kruse that you should not be drinking coffee if your BUN/Creatinine ratio sucks.

Conventional knowledge says that coffee would exert an antidiurectic effect, but perhaps coffee's effect on cortisol, vasopressin, and aldosterone could affect water chemistry in a way that diminishes EZ in certain regions, which would then lead to lessened ability to make semiconduction happen. If that's the case, then it's a problem with utilising the electrons that I currently have, rather than a major deficit of electrons.

Then there's also the explanation that coffee depleted the enzymes needed by the body to metabolize fat, and therefore dramatically drop my ability to make ATP and expose water binding sites, which would basically lead to the same electron shortage scenario. That could also be partly why fat loss is stalling, which indicates a mitochondrial issue to me.

Well, let's see if getting the necessary mitochondrial support is going to turn things around. Some beef liver, grounding, and sunshine are going to be scheduled all-through today.

Time to recovery should also tell me something about the nature of the issue.

\----
**Optimal is for anyone who wants it ...
**
Provided that you have money  Or at least, that's the case when living in Australia. Oh well, I'm well on my way to getting the financial security needed to get all the resources I need anyway. Good thing about Australia is that you don't really need that much money -- $1mil total would do just fine to get you a nice house in a decent location (houses on the peninsula to south Gippsland are only 400k for a couple of acres), and all the money you need to get good food and the needed health diagnostics (which you shouldn't need much of if you're doing things right).

I also need to get some better hobbies. A home on the Mornington Peninsula that's a short drive to the beach sounds like a great way to build a decent surfing habit, which is probably the most "socially-acceptable" way of getting year-round grounding and CT all in one shot (especially during winter of course).

### Christos — 2014-04-16T06:48:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-121790>

Why discard a thing as good as a brain!? Our ruminant and pork offal are actually pretty cheap over here. Pork liver is the most expensive, $3-4/kg. Beef heart is quite easy to get at $2/kg. Same with lamb's heart. I really should start buying more organ meats.

To be honest, I've never had lamb brains cooked like I did yesterday (that is, not much cooking at all). Just a quick 10 second sear on each side, leaving most of the brain pretty much raw. Mom immediately winced at me eating it, since she doesn't like it at all. I can see why -- it's got a gloopy and creamy texture, and is guaranteed to stick to the back of your throat and stay there with a long aftertaste. I like it , and it tastes almost like an oyster too, just with a lot more creaminess without any taste of brine.

I can't honestly say that yesterday's meals made me feel that much better. But that's because I got knocked down a little bit by that coffee experiment. But one thing for sure, oysters always lead to improved libido in the 1 or 2 days following

### yewwei.tan — 2014-04-20T04:34:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-122632>

Easter Sunday. Going to have a token celebration with family later though we're in no way affiliated with any religious institution. (My only religion is to ensure that I don't treat the dead as though they were never born).

\---

CPC#9 was a great read. The "Radical Idea Alert" was the best section by far. The Software Developer in me is excited by the fact that Nature plays with "software" using EMF. The optimist in me says that software is malleable once understood. I'll need to give that a couple more reads.

I went ahead and took a look at my COMT SNPs, and I've got:

-   rs4680: AG (risk allele A)
-   rs4633: CT (risk allele T)
-   [rs769224](http://www.snpedia.com/index.php/Rs769224): GG (risk allele A)

I've also got homozygous risk alleles for MTRR rs2287780 and rs162049 , which was what prompted me to look into adding more B12 into my diet. (my B12 levels are around 800, which isn't too far short of the target 1000, but still can be improved). Regarding MTHFR, I have a couple of single risk allele pairs, but not too much else.

\---

**Vit C
**
All of a sudden, I've developed a Vit C "intolerance" these last 5 days. Anything more than 1.5g leads to immediate loose stools. This may actually be a good sign, indicating decreased need for Vit C. I've also been drinking more seaweed water in place of coffee or cocoa, so there may be some extra Vit C coming from there too, leading my body to reject synthetic ascorbic acid.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-04-28T04:48:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-123617>

Took a 2 hour trip in a floatation tank yesterday. Quite a unique experience, and definitely very effective in getting one to a transcendental meditative state. 2 hours flew by for me, and I can see why Joe Rogan has his own float tank in his basement.

That said, people without meditation experience would probably not benefit as much from it. I consider trying to sit still while controlling your breathing in a cold tub as a powerful form of meditation.

\---

Speaking of CT, I've been starting CT with hot showers. I seem to need some heat to jump start CT, and tolerating the cold becomes much much easy after a warm shower. After that, I can just go cold all the way without much issue.

That basically tells me that I'm running on a thin line, with my semiconductors not being pre-loaded with enough energy. The only reason I'm still feeling fine right now is probably because I'm taking some measures to add back electrons. A divergence from my current lifestyle will probably lead to a major electron deficit, so that's something that I need to fix (become robust against aberrant behaviour)

The fact that I'm currently restricting calories while already being in a relatively lean state also means that I'm not getting enough electrons from food. I've lost weight in the last 2 weeks as well, down about 1kg over that time to about a weight of 80.3kg / 177lbs.

Well, let's see how things pan out in the next 2 months.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-01T03:47:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-124184>

Cold tolerance was not good last night (ambient temp about 10C / 50F).

This probably has something to do with my latest experiment, which was to increase protein to at least 200g a day for the last 9 days now. Satiety is obviously very good, but I'm not in strong ketosis whatsoever. I'm waking up at 0.2-0.3mM and then only getting as high as about 0.5-0.6mM, which is higher than most people, but certainly not the >1.0mM values that I'd normally get with my previous eating plan.

(Previously I was eating about 90-120g protein a day for 5 days of the week, and then 200+g of protein for 2 days. Blood ketones would drop after the higher protein days, but would rebound immediately the next day to >1.0mM)
​

So I'll probably go back to that prior eating pattern, and have a largish fat-only breakfast upon waking, and then another meal at maybe 4-5pm which is mostly protein.

> Inger what I have I been teaching you? Food is electrons and it increases the charge on the inner mitochondrial membrane. What comes out of mitochondria? O2 and Protons from mitochondria. Protons have a positive charge and can make heat. When your mitochondria are really good......you uncouple ox/phos that makes electrons flow and you make free heat..........from the excess protons. Those who are LR can not do this

I'm starting to feel a little bit warmer, though definitely still not where I'm at, after 2 days of reverting to high fat and moderate protein (about 100-120g per day).

I have always noticed a pattern though, that I start feeling really cold right after eating too much protein, which continues until I get a surge of warmth about 5-6 hours after the meal.

If I'm not wrong, protein catabolism provides even less ATP per mole that carbohydrates, since they basically have to go through the Nitrogen pool and possibly the Urea cycle before hitting the TCA. That may explain some of the energy deficit from a diet largely based around protein, and may explain why I'm at a sudden energy deficit by having to process all that protein without getting any energy back for a period of time. Of course, the over-arching cause is that I'm running an electron deficit.

In any case, proteins should be used as binding sites for water, and not as fuel to generate protons. I'll have to ensure that I keep my fat intake higher from now on.

Looks like cold is good for weight loss. The last 2 nights have been below 10C, and all of a sudden I drop another 0.5kg. 79.8kg (176lbs) this morning, with a waist measurement of 80.5cm / 31.7''

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-05T11:54:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-125253>

One day after that beef crackling, feet remain swollen today. Looks like my margin of safety really is really thin. I'm basically at only maintaining at electron equilibrium right now if I'm doing everything right in my current environment. The fact that the number of sunny days is now down to a much lower amount than a month ago also probably contributes to a decrease in the amount of electrons in that I'm getting.

Well, at least despite the setback, mental acuity is still top notch, and dreams are still vivid and recallable. At least everything hasn't gone down the tubes.

For the future, I need to play it pretty safe when it comes to external stressors like problematic foods.

On the electron collection front, I'll continue with what I'm currently doing, with 2 changes:

-   I just got my Vielight 810 today ([http://www.vielight.com/product/810-infrared/](http://www.vielight.com/product/810-infrared/)) so I'll be putting that to use.
-   I'm also going to make a commitment to getting some oysters into my system on a daily basis. Just had 2 cans of oysters today, and I'll get to the markets to get some fresh ones ASAP.

In other miscellaneous notes, I'm getting a little too jumpy after morning CT these last few days, meaning that it's hard to sit down in front of a computer and write code. Right now, I only seem to be able to generate lots of body heat through shivering thermogenesis, which is very effective at really heating me up, but is very distraction to my work. Well, looks like I'll have to make some tradeoffs one way or another.  Unfortunately, that's likely going to be favouring work over CT for the next 1-2 months while I get this company I'm starting up and running.

My EMF situation at my workstation is decent, though not ideal. Currently, it's basically an external monitor about 0.5m away from my head, with the laptop being wired up with an Ethernet cable and any transmitting devices turned off (bluetooth, wifi, etc ...). No other devices are on in the room as well other than an iPod with the wifi and bluetooth turned off as well (cellphone is either off or in another room). Then I've got a couple of amethyst and black tourmaline crystals sitting right around head level which may offer some benefit.

EMF levels where my torso is at peak at 0.5mG for the ELF magnetic field (not that good), and about 0.02 uW/cm2 (decent) for the higher spectrum (>1500Hz).

Then the problem is that there's Wifi in the house due to family members not wanting to wire up, so there's that concern.

Well, the bright side is that I'm still young, and I picked a profession where the ability to help a lot of people (and therefore get paid a lot) is pretty great. ### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-09T07:29:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-125987>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=125985)
>
> I have always just done a cold shower .....didn't mind them at all - and better for my hair too! LOL
> so you think a hot and then cold shower is the way to go? better than just cold?

That's basically a way to utilise the Fournier effect that Jack always talks about

-   Hot => EZ expansion through Infrared Radiation
-   Cold => Hall effect

Though "better" depends on what you're trying to do ... eg: if I'm trying to get cold adapted ASAP, then I'd want to do straight up cold.

For me, contrast showers (at least 5 cycles of 1min hot then 1min cold) have been incredibly helpful to recover when I've done something wrong health-wise.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-10T01:54:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-126119>

> [Danco3636 said:](/goto/post?id=126087)
>
> I usually wear my cool fat burner vest and gut buster no shirt.
>
> But wonder if a wet t shirt will enhance the thermal effect? Then again the vests will get wet and when I go to referee will be frosted and frozen over.
>
> Any experience with that?

Yup, I've tried wearing the cool gut buster over a wet shirt, and it's really good and drawing heat away from your body. As a result, you need to watch out for parts that have both tight contact with the body, and poor blood flow.

It's particularly a problem with the cool gut buster, because you can really get full contact between the vest and your skin. I found that the water right above my hip bones was starting to freeze! No problem with the rest of the body parts though, since there was adequate blood flow. Basically, any part of my body that flushed pink during CT was pretty fine, and any part that wasn't was prone to producing blocks of ice, which could potentially lead to skin damage if you didn't watch out and kept the thing on for too long.

Had at least 4 dozen oysters this weekend

\---
As a weekend fancy, I was looking at places which had the most sunshine. Arizona is off the charts  No wonder Josh is doing so well. As a benchmark, Tucson gets as low as 245 hours of sunshine a month in December, and as high as 400 hours in May. Melbourne Australia gets a low of 108 hours in June, and 279 hours in January. So Arizona is really one of those places where you can Cold plus Sun at the same time (December still drops to 4C / 39F). No clue about how good the water and EMF is though.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-13T05:07:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-126580>

**Initial impressions on VieLight 810**

So it's been more than a week since I've been using the thing daily, especially at night.

So far, the 2 biggest changes are:

-   I'm getting very sleepy by 9pm, in spite of staring at a computer screen (with blue blockers though)
-   no night time trips to the bathroom. Usually it'd be like 1-2 days a week that I'd get up and head to the bathroom, but this week there was none. Because I'm decently lean, I could also tell that my muscles were more full than usual.

The only real metric I want to see improve regarding sleep is the time to sleep onset. Right now I still take maybe 20-30 mins to get to sleep. Hopefully the red light can get that time down.

### caroline — 2014-05-13T07:33:39+0300

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-126600>

Right now, as much as I can. You can't use it simultaneously while eating/drinking though, so I tend to use it more at night.

### caroline — 2014-05-13T08:33:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-126605>

Yew ..... you paid $450.00 - is that the one?

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-13T10:07:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-126611>

Yup, the vielight 810, where the 810 means that it emits light centered around the 810nm wavelength. There's one company here selling it at AU$485 and free shipping -- [http://www.brain2.com.au/products/vielight-810-infrared-pulsing](http://www.brain2.com.au/products/vielight-810-infrared-pulsing)

The one that Josh is using is the Bionase 630 -- [http://www.syrolight.com/bionase](http://www.syrolight.com/bionase) (about $70 + $9 shipping)

The difference between the 2 is the wavelength used. Technically speaking, the bionase still shines visible red light, while the vielight model that I have shines invisible infrared light. It's likely that the 2000nm to 3000nm band is best for expanding the EZ of water, and the closer to that band the better. Though expansion of EZ isn't the only good thing about shining red light up your nose (as the May webinar on brain optics shows).

I guess any amount of red light in the nose is good, but since money wasn't an issue for me, I decided to get the device that got me as close to that 2000-3000nm band as possible.

\---

I'm just using it as a general means of increasing redox potential (hopefully in the brain) as per the Redox Rx -- [http://jackkruse.com/redox-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/redox-rx/) . Trying to get as many sources of free electrons as possible (which hopefully leads to photons in the brain)

\---

Diet has roughly remained the same, but sleep duration has decreased a by about 30 mins, and my weight has basically been hovering anywhere in between 80-81kg. This is despite what should be an energy deficit for me (I was losing weight prior to this period), and probably indicates that I'm getting more electron steal than normal from some source. Could also do with the fact that I had something akin to food poisoning last Monday, with persistent nausea (but no actual vomitting) for a good 3 days. Could have been some bad seafood I ate

I'm going to head up to Cairns for 2 weeks in June, to see if the sun and the beach actually aid in recovery. I'm definitely looking forward to the sun.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-20T03:09:05+0300

What a world this is, where anyone can leverage the internet to help lots of people and be rewarded for it (the converse is also true of course) ...

Well, I turn 23 in 4 days, and made a resolution this year to settle the money problem and start living life to the fullest again

\---

Starting to feel better in general again. Don't know if it's the daily oysters, the nicotine gum, the IR nasal device, the anticipation of an upcoming holiday in June, the extra sunshine we've had this week, the extra probiotics (fermented veg), or just a reduction in overall stress. **Random Notes
**
Just some random observations and plans:

-   I'm finding CT to be easier when I literally dive in face first, meaning that I start with getting my face cold. Must have something to do with the nervous system activation that it gives.
-   For me, the only 2 places that ever feel cold during CT, are the rear deltoids, and just behind the ears. Hands, feet, everywhere else always feel and look normal

Also going to start eating a little bit more carbs. Looking back at my old personal logs, I lost fat best on something like 50g carbs, as opposed to something like 20g which I'm eating now. Time for more onions and garlic, still staying away from the starchy carbs, and I should still be able to maintain strong ketosis even at a 50g carb intake (we'll see though).

That said:

**(1)** I'm vain as hell, and wouldn't mind being leaner

**(2)** I think that being able to maintain a lower fat mass with optimal health is indicative that I'm doing things right regarding lifestyle -- my body will only let me get rid of fat if it already has enough electron carrying capacity to not need that fat (Prahlad Jhani seems to derive all his energy outside of food, and doesn't seem to need body fat).

**(3)** Electron density of foods is almost not related to caloric density at all; it is possible to get a lot of food electrons without getting a lot of calories.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-29T02:46:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-128766>

Well, yesterday I had about 250g of almost-raw bonito tuna, 150g of prawns, the heads of the tuna and 4 horse mackerels, and maybe in between 400-600g of pork knuckle. Definitely felt a little warmer after that. Today's meals will be the other half of that tuna, a small can of oysters, and maybe another 400g of mackerel. Still not Inger-level quantities of seafood, but it should be well enough to feed my brain

\---
The weather here has not been good at all  Not cold enough, yet not much sun. 6 out of 7 days are cloudy, daylight hours are decreasing, and the temp is only between 10-20C (50-68F). Looks like I have to rely a lot more on grounding and food for my electrons.

That said, most signs look to be improving pretty well. In the last 2 weeks, I've put back lots of egg yolks, as well as lots of chillies, to no negative effects at all.

Another random "redox potential assessment" I've been doing is to take 1000mg of niacin once a week. The first time I took it over a month ago, I got some pretty bad flushing symptoms in my feet for a good few hours. Yesterday, my niacin dose led to no flushing at all. I hope this means that redox potential is improving. Also, my blood ketone levels were still around 0.5mM after the very high protein intake yesterday (>200g protein for sure).

\---
Buckethead's various albums (instrumental guitar) has been the music to code to for the last few days:

### yewwei.tan — 2014-05-31T03:03:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-128983>

All of a sudden, my hands started feeling colder than usual. I mean, we just had a morning where the temperature was down to about 5C/41F, but I was still colder than I ought to be.

Then I came across this post by Josh:

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=128974)
>
> I hate to say it....N=1, you do not have a clear picture of whether you can handle sulfur well. If your redox is trashed and or you have CBS and other related SNP's, you may not thrive on a high sulfur diet without molybdenum and other methylation support. Even your infection could put you across the redox line. Tread carefully when eating a high sulfur diet until you are sure you can handle it.

I mentioned that in the last few days, I upped my onion and garlic consumption considerably. Maybe about 2-3 medium sized onions and 5-6 cloves of garlic a day. I've had issues that were fixed with molybdenum supplement before, and it may very well be that I'm not dealing with sulphur well.

Another confounding factor may be the time that I've been spending coding (and writing 1000+ lines of code a day), which may cause some extra emotional stress. Exposure time to the computer hasn't changed that much, so blue light exposure and EMF exposure isn't that likely to have changed that much either. My HRV readings are still always green, but HRV may be a lagging indicator.

Anyway, an easy experiment should follow -- cut back the sulphur rich veggies for a few days, and see how I react. More cold days are coming anyway, so it's a good way to gauge response.

Once again, Thanks Josh!

### yewwei.tan — 2014-06-03T03:56:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-129445>

Been working way too much and getting too much tunnel vision; Yew-wei dreams of clojurescript  Need to broaden the scope of my daily efforts to more realms to facilitate creativity.

\---

I'm starting to think about that living in the Temperate South of Australia vs the Tropical North. Looking through EE4 again -- [http://jackkruse.com/energy-and-epigenetics-4-light-water-magnetism/](http://jackkruse.com/energy-and-epigenetics-4-light-water-magnetism/)

Excerpt:

> **Physical factors affecting apparent dielectric strength of water:**
>
> 1.  It decreases slightly with increased sample thickness of water. (The ocean around Australia are very deep)
> 2.  It decreases with increased operating temperature. (Oz is arid and hot, another major non hormetic benefit for CT.)
> 3.  It decreases with increased frequency of the EMF found in the field a cell lives in. (hello, donut hole)
> 4.  For gases (e.g. nitrogen, sulfur hexafluoride) it normally decreases with increased humidity. (Tropic effect is here: think the cassowary bird)
> 5.  For air, dielectric strength increases slightly as humidity increases (Australia regions with lowest humidity have the highest rates of melanoma and AI’s; 45 percent of Australia is desert in its center where there is no water.)

If I'm understanding this right, the basic issue with the donut hole is:

1.  extra high frequency ionising radiation (basically causes all the issues with Non-native EMF, only more so due to higher energy of the waves)
2.  halogen and EMF "contamination" of the entire water supply, leading to poorer quality water that cannot charge separate as much (and also the factors listed above, which actually make living closer to the equator more favourable in the case of Australia)

I get that (2) would affect basically the entire land mass of Australia, South America, and Africa, but surely the extent of the ionising radiation would taper off above some latitude. I would think that the North of Australia receives less of the solar wind, but I can't be sure unless I go there and measure it myself (direct measurements of ionizing radiation, and measurements of my own redox potential while being there).

### yewwei.tan — 2014-06-06T06:47:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-129921>

Not really been eating that ketogenic these days. Still 30g carbs or less, but protein is definitely very high. For example, yesterday I had around 300g of fish eggs and 225g of frozen raw oysters (half a 1lbs bag) in the morning, and then 500g of bonito tuna later in the day. Basically seafood and oysters every single day now, with some occasional pork being thrown in. Seafood is a 50/50 split between prawns and fish. Prawns are eaten head, shell and all, fish I usually fillet out, cook Japanese 'Tataki' style (seared briefly on one side only), and then boil up the heads into a fish stock.

Despite a heavily electron loaded diet, energy levels have not been ideal, and show a downward trend from last week. I feel like I'm only functioning at 80-90% capacity. None of my other habits have changed, so the only thing I can chalk it up to is a sudden change in the electromagnetic environment with winter now setting in. This change would be both natural (earth's magnetic field + changes in solar radiation), as well as unnatural (neighbour turning on their heating more often).

Now's about the time where I'm seriously contemplating moving up north to the tropics for 1-2 years to get a full disease reversal with all of that free sunshine. It's much harder to get IR radiation in temperate zones short of being next to a hot spring or owning an infrared sauna. But in the tropics you can still easily do CT with some simple ice blocks and a tub of water, and then couple that with an Epi-Paleo ketogenic diet to really push up the electron.

\---

Now is also about the time where the 'Does Where You Live Actually Matter' blog comes back to mind -- [http://jackkruse.com/does-where-you-live-actually-matter/](http://jackkruse.com/does-where-you-live-actually-matter/)

The factors raised in the article:

I used temperature, pressures, ocean presence, sunlight, darkness, and depth (altitude) as my first variables to test myself.​

My ancestry comes from Southern China, from GuangZhou in the GuanDong province -- [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guangzhou](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guangzhou) . Which is pretty warm, getting only as cold as 10C/50F, pretty humid (70-80%), pretty rainy with not that much sun, and right next to the ocean. Obviously my current location in Melbourne is far far away from there, and to be honest, I have never felt completely optimal living in Melbourne, except for the months of late February to April -- this must have something to do with seasonal variation in climate.

I would love to actually do a biohack and go and stay in GuangZhou for awhile, but levels of pollution are just so high in China that the option is completely out of the question. Basically anywhere in central or south-east Asia is not a viable place to be chasing optimal health.

That leads me to question: Where is the ideal place for me to be?

Logistically speaking, the easiest thing for me would be to remain in Australia, hence the thought about giving northern Australia a try for the coming year or two. But knowing what I know now about the Donut Hole and other factors, I don't have high hopes that Australia would constitute an ideal place to live for me. If anything, it would be in the sub-tropical north.

But in the future, I'm very likely going to be making enough cash to afford to put myself wherever in the world I want to be. I'm definitely going to give living in a couple of Northern Hemisphere locations a shot, with some places in the US south being the most likely candidates. I'm also thinking about Japan as a place that is "as close to my ancestral homeland as habitably possible", but I need to sort out how bad the EMF issue is on that island before further consideration (maybe some of the smaller southern prefectures away from the megacities)

Leaving for Cairns next monday (16th June), and definitely looking forward to the trip.

I have not been feeling optimal these last 2 weeks that's for sure. The most markedly decreased parameter is libido, which is usually a leading indicator for me that things are starting to slide. I should note that health parameters have consistently been poorer during the months of June all the way until October for the last 5 years. This is also when the ozone hole is at it's largest, and the light cycles are at their shortest, so I'm not surprised.

Depending on how this trip goes, I may actually camp out in the tropical north for the months of July to November in attempts to recover my redox potential. Whatever it is, I've tried a lot of stuff at my present location that just didn't lead to optimal recover. It's pretty obvious now that **your zipcode matters much more than your genetic code**. I'm definitely eager to see how the 4870 zipcode treats me.

Diet wise, I've actually turned up the animal protein and seafood intake dramatically, in an attempt to get extra electrons from seafood. This puts me out of ketosis, but I also try to ensure that I'm in a calorie deficit (easy to do with that much protein) to hopefully still keep some upregulated fat metabolism. Example of yesterday's diet would be a pound of frozen raw oysters for breakfast, and then 500g of small black tiger prawns for dinner.

During my trip in Cairns, I'm probably going to focus heavily on the seafood, but tone it down to my old protein intake of 100-120g a day, while staying in a slightly calorie-restricted state to promote ketosis.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-06-16T09:32:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-130992>

More issues have surfaced in the past week, mainly to do with my mental state. Ability to focus for prolonged periods on a single task has gone down dramatically, and I'm feeling the urge to do risky and novel things. A scattered mind is great for deciphering JK blog posts, but is horrible for writing computer programs. This is definitely some slight ADD combined with dopamine dysfunction that's leading my brain to want to engage in more thrill-seeking behaviour.

I also had the constant urge to be constantly drinking/eating something through the day. Fortunately that just meant many cups of seaweed and mushroom broth (mainly laver with enoki). The constant urge to snack is a sure sign that something is screwing with the reawrd systems in my head.

I ended up with an oyster and tuna mini-binge on Saturday (3 dozen oysters and 700g of tuna) -- just felt the need to eat "something good" for no reason whatsoever. Not the worst thing in the world, but this is yet another sign that seasonal changes are affecting me in a big way.

Anyway, I arrived in Cairns this morning, and it's definitely a different place compared to Melbourne. I'm staying decently close to the ocean, though in an urban area with WiFi signals around (though I'm typing this while being barefoot in my host's outdoor dining area). Can't say that I feel anything different about Cairns yet, other than the fact that it's 12C warmer than Melbourne. Well, I've got 2 weeks here, so let's see what the effects are.
