### yewwei.tan — 2014-06-18T10:38:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-131244>

Relevant comment I made in the "Skin Blushing" Ask Jack Thread -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/sun-exposure-and-skin-blushing.10569/page-2#post-131243](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/sun-exposure-and-skin-blushing.10569/page-2#post-131243)

> This note contains lots of personal context, but I decided to post it here instead of my log because it may be related to the question.
>
> Today I was in 25C Sunshine for about a 1.5 hours stretch while walking on the beach and around town. As expected, hands, feet (which were covered), knees, forearms, and upper chest started flushing 10 mins in.
>
> THe more worrying symptom to me was that I get pitting edema in the feet, and sometimes the fingers whenever there's lots of bloodflow to the region. For example, my feet get slightly swollen after walking around for awhile, and some pitting edema is present.
>
> As far as I'm concerned, this is indicative of a like of tensegrity of the collagen matrix in the affected regions (hands and feet). Collagen is unzipping and extracellular fluid has a larger room to roam. The red skin flushing simply serves to make that fact obvious, and isn't the cause. I should also note that I can get rid of the edema immediately by putting my feet at a level about my chest, which obviously tells me that the cause is what I described earlier in this paragraph.
>
> Now I'm left with the question of why is the collagen in my hands and feet and preferentially affected. I'm also left more confused because the quality of the skin in those regions is good, and my circadian rhythms are spot on (with good sleep too), so I'm assuming that yoking of Vit A and D to the natural light cycle isn't the main contributing factor to this issue.
>
> More random questions: What kind of collagen is present in the hands and feet, and why would they be more prone to? This is likely a systemic problem of electron deficit, but I'm left wondering if localized solutions like spot CT of the feet, foot massage, and compression socks, will be effective at treating the issue?

### yewwei.tan — 2014-06-25T07:30:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-132166>

Random observation. My vision sucks when I'm in a shopping centre. I almost can't read signs that are 10m away. But yet in sunlight, I can easily read signs that are at least triple that distance. Fake light isn't good for me ....

This metric also seems very sensitive to improvement and regression. One day I can have really good eyesight, and then 2 days later it regresses tremendously due to some stressor, and then it's back to good levels again in another 2 days.

There obviously is some sort of plasticity going on with the focal mechanisms in my eye that is being drastically affected by EMF (blue light). I should note that I'm -6 in the left eye, -7 in the right eye, and have a significant degree of astigmatism (can't remember the exact axis now).

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-07T13:45:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-133377>

Cairns Trip Short Summary

Trip time was a total of 14 days, and the usual routine was to wake at roughly 6:30am, sleep at 10pm, and spend at least 2-3 hours on a beach somewhere with my body fully in contact with the ground or seawater.
**\- Massive Weight Loss:** Started at 81.5kg with a 81cm waist. Ended at 78.4kg with a 78cm waist. 3kg loss in bodyweight and 3cm reduction in waist measurement, all within 3 weeks. This is despite eating more and not moving enough to account for the extra calories (if you still subscribe to the CICO model)
**\- Gut health suffered:** Bristol score went in between 2-4, with a lot of 2s.
\- **Skin quality suffered**: Dry skin started appearing, especially on forearms and ankles
\- **Sleep quality suffered:** as expected in a higher EMF zone (house with lots of WiFi), sleep wasn't as good. Duration was still 8hours, but incidence of dreaming declined. I still felt rested though.
\- **Edema in Feet and Hands reduced**: this likely means that collagen tensegrity of peripheral tissues has improved
\- **Sex Drive Back Up to Good levels**: always a good sign

So some improvements, some regressions, and overall a fun and very informative experience. I'll eventually write up a more detailed explanation of each observation, and the needed intervention strategies.

Mental clarity improved in some respects. The trip was a great dopamine jolt that helped me put some things in perspective, and to more clearly think about what I need to do to stay healthy and happy.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-08T07:35:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-133460>

Start of the Cairns trip recap

**Cairns Conditions:**

\- Temperature range from about 14C (57F) to 27C (80F).
\- Great sunshine (>5 hours) for 10 out of the 14 days I was here
\- 11 out of 14 days at the beach for at least 2 hours, with lots of time spent with at least my feet in the ocean
\- stayed in a small house in the city. WiFi was the only option for internet
\- Still had seafood every day, but it was mainly canned sardines, canned oysters, and boiled queensland prawns.
\- More physical activity than baseline. On average 2 hours walking around a day, and then I took the bike out for 6 longer rides (>30KM) and 5 days with shorter rides (<20KM)
\- Ate more than usual, about 500-700kcal more per day
\- Water was a 50/50 split between tap water (which thankfully isn't fluoridated here), and bottled RO or Spring water. Lots of bottles of San Pellegrino and San Benedetto were drunk.

**Cairns Pros:**

\- Lots of sunshine almost year round (low of 220hrs a month, high of 270hrs a month, as compared to lows of 100hrs/month and highs of 270hrs/month in Melbourne)
\- Easy access to beaches almost everywhere

**Cairns Cons:**

\- seafood selection nowhere as good as Melbourne

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-08T07:37:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-133461>

**Gut Health and Skin Issues**

These two always seem to be linked for me; whenever I see gut issues, skin issues soon follow.

I found this to be highly dependent on my current diet, and very easily reversible. I've been back only 3 days, and all the gut issues and skin issues are completely gone. All I needed to do was to pop 600mg of resveratrol a day, and eat lots of prawns (with shells) and raw oysters.

The regressions during the trip was likely a combination of poorer-than-usual diet, and the dehydrating effects of being around too much WiFi.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-08T07:45:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-133462>

**Sex Drive Increase and Reduction in Feet Edema
**
To me, this is likely a sign of peripheral collagen electrifying and becoming more structured and energy efficient. I'm still not at a stage of full recovery, but the trip definitely put me in the right direction. Now I just need to maintain the improvements. The general strategy is to:

-   localised massage to the feet (mostly by wearing massage slippers and doing foam rolling)
-   figure out some yoga poses that direct energy to the limbs
-   added cold exposure specifically for the hands and feet (submerge them in a bucket of cold water)
-   shine red light on hands and feet at regular intervals

This may be a cortisol issue, since I was getting up and getting sun exposure pretty readily in Cairns, but not necessarily doing it that much in Melbourne. I have since bought a 275W near infrared red light bulb to get morning IR exposure regardless of the weather.

sidenote: I tested the bulb with 2 meters, and it seems to give off about 0.2-0.3mG in the <1500Hz range, and negligible amounts in the >1500Hz range. Hopefully this means that the risk of harmful non-native EMF is reduced. I will continue to monitor my response regardless
​

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-08T07:47:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-133463>

**Endurance Exercise Likely Does Not Harm Me
**
I did a lot of cycling and walking during the trip. I felt physically tired by the end of the day, but easily recovered the next day.

Not much to make of this, except for that I'm likely not broken to the extent that moderate amounts of endurance exercise is going to hurt me.

I'm not going to incorporate endurance exercise either, other than cycling down to the beach 1-2 times a week to be in the ocean.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-08T07:52:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-133465>

**Carb Consumption
**
There were a total of 4 days whereby I ate carbs during the trip:

-   sushi with caroline and Nick
-   3 bananas on <25-Jun-2014>
-   3 bananas on <24-Jun-2014>

-   3 sushi rolls on <23-Jun-2014>
-   1 banana on <21-Jun-2014>

The maximum carb load would have been maybe 120g on the 24th and 25th of Jun. Other than that, it was high fat and usually moderate protein (about 120g/day).

Light cycle was from 6:30am to about 6:45pm in Cairns.

I have no clue whether or not this had any impact at all. If it did, then it was to stimulate more autophagy through production of ROS, while in the presence of sufficient electron supply.

Sidenote: this leads me to wonder whether some carbs even in winter time can be beneficial if your redox isn't completely trashed. I may run an experiment whereby I ensure that I remain in full ketosis for 6 days of the week, and then "spike" carbs to 100g on one day.​

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-08T08:08:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-133470>

**Random Thoughts about Current Disease State
**
My 3 outstanding health problems:

-   cold tolerance not up to par - I want to be able to deal with the Melbourne winter (average low of 4C/39F) in a T-shirt. I heat up really well after CT, but normally don't throw off body heat
-   cold hands and feet - these never warm up, except for a brief time at around 6-7pm every day, lasting about an hour
-   gut health is very flaky - coffee and chocolate still cause immune system over-activation, leading me to feel cold

Interestingly, I never lose the ability to think about and analyse the situation. I guess I'm lucky to shunt electrons to my brain at the expense of my peripheral tissues.

In any case, I still need more electrons, but need to figure out how to concentrate them especially in the collagen of my hands and feet. I also probably have an issue with sub-optimal mitochondria in these tissues, and need to replace them with good mitochondria.

I suspect that movement (a la Jeremy) is going to be a key factor in recovery. The same goes for grounding.

For mitochondrial health, I'll have to rely on ketosis and autophagy. I'm thinking of experimenting with some fasting schemes, or at least something like that which I described in this Ask Jack question -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/maximising-autophagy-on-the-leptin-reset.11227/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/maximising-autophagy-on-the-leptin-reset.11227/) . Again, I'm left wondering if small and infrequent doses of carbohydrates can improve this process despite it being winter here.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-08T08:15:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-133475>

**Random Thoughts About Weight Loss
**
I lost 3kg in bodyweight over 3 weeks that I did the trip. This is despite eating more than I'd expect.

The major differences in my conditions of existence during that time:

-   lots more sunshine in Cairns vs Melbourne
-   more EMF exposure in Cairns vs Melbourne from blue light at night and WiFi signals
-   much more grounding in Cairns (3+ hours a day) vs Melbourne (probably 1 hour a day)
-   and of course, different location (different natural magnetic field, barometric pressure, higher humidity, higher temperature, etc)

I still don't know why and how I lost the weight, though it most likely means that my cells were more energy efficient, and probably correlates to the improvements that I saw in my hands and feet.

The next step right now is to increase the level of grounding that I'm getting here in Melbourne and watch for the effect. I'm currently experimenting with sleeping outside while grounded, and I just purchased a grounding mat and grounding sheet for the bed (both of these are connected to a wire and stake that is supposed to be dropped out a window and plunged into the earth).

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-08T08:19:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-133476>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=133471)
>
> do you think being a programmer has an affect on the hands?

100%. I'm not getting that much EMF to my head and feet right now, but my hands are constantly poking into a 8mG magnetic field that extends above the keyboard.

Sidenote: interestingly, the trackpad, which is only an inch away from the keyboard, has readings <0.5mG. So it really is the processor under the keyboard that is shooting out all those scalar waves.​

The solution is to use an external keyboard, but it reduces my coding efficiency tremendously (almost half the speed). I promised myself that once I launched this business I'm currently building, I'll stop this bad habit and switch out to an external keyboard (which I measured to be pretty safe -- <0.4mG)

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-08T08:47:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-133482>

**Dopamine
**
My motivation to pursue optimal health was waning a little bit after experience the May decline in various parameters. That's when I decided to take the Cairns trip.

When you're down, you need to jolt your brain out of its funk by doing "crazy" things. The vacation was probably the best thing I could have done at the time, and I'm now filled both with optimism, and the clarity of thought needed to take the next steps.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-08T08:56:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-133485>

**Interventions**

-   Get grounded as much as possible. Learn to sleep outside, and experiment with grounding mats directly connected to the earth
-   Red light exposure right thing upon waking, and then at brief intervals through the day
-   Direct physical stimulation of the hands and feet -- massage slippers, foam rolling, reflexology, etc ...
-   Get to the beach at least once or twice a week
-   Daily stretching and yoga interspersed through the day
-   Keep up my daily meditation practice
-   Get high every day (listening and marking music, physical play, and other pleasurable activities ...)
-   Keep endurance activity to a minimum
-   Apply ice soaks to hands and feet. Likely experiment with fournier effect, with hands and feet in tub of ice while basking in a warm shower.
-   no more than 2 heavy weights session (>70% intensity) a week, ideally just 1. Grease the groove the rest of the time (submaximal movement patterns). The goal is to move in every possible way that a human can move, and do it as frequently as possible
-   Keep protein in the 100-120g a day range, and carbs below 30g to ensure ketosis. Eat lots of fat
-   Concentrate protein and carb intake in the last 2 hours before sunset. This corresponds to about 3:30pm to 5:30pm
-   2 meals a day -- fat-only breakfast upon waking at around 7:30am, dinner at around 4-5pm
-   Experiment with 100g of carbs one day a week, always after a fasting window
-   Still eating seafood every day, massive quantities of oysters (today's dinner is a pound of oysters)
-   Work no more than 5 hours a day (to curb mental stress). Never work past 8pm

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-10T04:50:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-133807>

Yesterday I had around 5 tablespoons worth of coconut oil. Not good  Felt like electron overload -- got jittery, felt cold, and pushed out a lot of body heat. Heart rate was around 80 all day.

This morning I had the equivalent amount of fat from butter and beef tallow instead. Now I'm feeling a steady warmth. Decently cold for Melbourne standards too -- about 11C / 52F now

I was doing really well on coconut oil in Cairns, which currently has a 12 hour daylight cycle, vs Melbourne which has a 10hr daylight cycle (which is much weaker in terms of power). Eating seasonally matters!

\----

It's now 6 days that I've been exposing myself to red light (or sunlight) upon waking and before bed, typically 15 mins each time. The hand edema that I've been complaining about for so long is almost gone now, though my hands still get cold.

Red light zips up collagen well!

Reproducing the comment here ([http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/#IDComment852911080](http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/#IDComment852911080)):

**Melanie**
_I am a fat freak. I am like Peter Attia. I know he wears a t shirt that says "I love the Lard." I don't like lard but I love fat especially before bed as it induces such amazing sleep and dreams. Here you warn against fat in the morning for people who are leptin resistance. What are your fat recommendations in general? Can we in general love the lard and get Attia like performance benefits or do you suggest fat moderation? I know you talked about coconut oil as your diesel fuel when losing weight. Then, you have discussed the caveats of coconut oil use. Then, you discussed in the July Q and A fat and one's cytoarchitecture. In OSF 6, you discuss loading the mitochondria with fat to improve the integrity of the inner mitochondrial membrane. I obviously stalk this blog anytime you mention fat as I find fat and DHA to be the holy grail. Like all alchemists, you leave breadcrumbs about this elusive elixir everywhere. I just can't put this magical puzzle together. I can't close the gap. Is there a one stop shop on the blog where we can get "fat RX"? Luvin the lard, ur blog's biggest fan, Mel_

**Jack:**
_Melanie.......**.fat is great as long as you realize it too is seasonal**.

The more healthy you get the more fat you eat but there is a down side to excessive tropical fats in the winter.......and **there is a downside of excessive animal fats around the summer solstice.

T**he irony is that modern humans have not yet approached those problems because our pendulum is now pointed to the high carb fake carb template of the last 60 years and because those fake foods have been made cheaply by the modern food corps........animal and tropical fats are not cheap so most humans avoid them because of cost.
_​

_\====_

Thinking about mechanics again, this post in the 'Any news on the biohack' thread contained some of my thoughts regarding the different fats -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/any-news-on-the-bio-hack.11066/#post-133825](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/any-news-on-the-bio-hack.11066/#post-133825)

This post also sounds important -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/osf-7.11299/#post-133938](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/osf-7.11299/#post-133938)

I keep thinking of the quote "we are designed to be oxidized by sunlight" as it pertains to the long light cycle in summer time.

Still no clue about why things work, but for now, at least I know how to rotate fats seasonally.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-12T05:28:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-134060>

**Minor Grounding Update
**
I've been attempting to get grounded while sleeping in two ways.

-   Sleeping outside in the garden
-   Making use of a grounding sheet (bought this pack for AU$225 -- [http://www.earthingoz.com.au/online-store/sleep-systems/starter-pack-basic](http://www.earthingoz.com.au/online-store/sleep-systems/starter-pack-basic))

Sleeping in the garden has been tough. Problem now is that I don't have a tent, so I definitely feel the wind. Nights have been roughly around 5C/41F, and decently windy.

The grounding sheet is connected to the earth plug of a power outlet, and at least in the short term, feels like it's working. The last 2 nights have been spent sleeping on the grounding sheet, under a thin blanket, and while in my underwear. I do not feel cold at all with this system, when I used to require at least a t-shirt under a sheet at 5C.

Another observation is that sleep time has shortened. Instead of 9hrs, I'm getting just under 8hrs, but I feel just as energetic, and have just as vivid dreams during sleep. I don't wake up to go to the bathroom either.

Staying grounded is definitely the most significant electron-addition strategy that I've used so far, and we'll see if I can graduate to sleeping naked without covers anytime soon. For now, I'm going to continue with the grounding sheet and see how my sleep and redox potential responds over the next 2 to 3 weeks.

**Diet**

After reading OSF7, re-reading OSF6, and then some of the Hyperlipid blogs, I really saw that trying to add carbs right now is foolish. Good thing I hadn't really started yet.

I need to make use of this winter to ramp up autophagy as much as I can. For me, this will mean being calorie restricted (FIAF + mobilise SubQ fat), practicing some intermittent fasting, being ketotic, and avoiding blue light like crazy. Side benefit is of course that I'll probably get way leaner before winter is up (Vanity can be a driver if used properly).

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-12T05:38:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-134067>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=134062)
>
> I don't understand why you are using a grounding sheet connected to an outlet?
>
> My imagination is running away with me ..... All these naked guys sleeping in the garden

The earth outlet in a properly wired house should be plugged directly into the earth and not be connected to any power source.

There is a risk that your wiring is not done properly, and you end up with a lot of dirty electricity getting through a supposedly earthed outlet.

For myself:

-   Use a power outlet that is switched off (earth is still earthed)
-   I used a socket tester to ensure that the socket is wired properly
-   I used my 2 EMF meters to ensure that there wasn't a field given off by my earthing sheet
-   I used a multimeter to ensure that when I'm in contact with the sheet, that I didn't have any skin voltage on me (without being grounded, skin voltage was a very high 8-10V, when grounded, skin voltage went down below 0.2V)

Hopefully those are enough precautions, but ultimately, I'm going to watch my sleep quality closely, and if things start to go south, then the grounding sheet is a no-go.

The place we went to was Masuya Japanese Restaurant -- [http://www.urbanspoon.com/r/70/751461/restaurant/CBD/Masuya-Japanese-Seafood-Sydney](http://www.urbanspoon.com/r/70/751461/restaurant/CBD/Masuya-Japanese-Seafood-Sydney) and [http://www.masuyainternational.com.au/en/masuya/](http://www.masuyainternational.com.au/en/masuya/)

There are 4 restaurants under the Masuya group. The one that we went to is a more formal restaurant. There are more casual options, like a dedicated sushi place, a casual izakaya (think Japanese Tapas), and another high-end place I think.

\---

Raw fermented meat definitely counts as a great probiotic source. Get your gut flora diversity on!

\---

Doing decently well, but I can tell that the collagen issues in my feet aren't improving at all. Brain power is still doing pretty good though, so at least I've got electrons in my CNS now.

Cairns really did me well as a whole. If it weren't for the EMF where I stayed, I think I wouldn't have had any negative symptoms at all. Lots of sun, easy access to beaches, and probably some magnetic field differences I think were the biggest reasons. In any case, rents are dirt cheap up there, and one can easily get a 3BR place for $350-400/week (around $1400-1600/month) by the sea. Next experiment is definitely to live in Cairns for a year with all the optimal health measures that I currently employ here in Melbourne.

\---

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-17T08:44:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-134717>

Cycled to St Kilda Beach today and took a walk in the ocean. Then cycled to Victoria Market to pick up 4 dozen coffin bay oysters and 3kg worth of salami. Then came home at 1pm and ate maybe 300g of salami and a dozen oysters. Going to finish off the rest of the oysters soon.

Total time on the bike was about 2hours, with about 40km (24 miles) covered.

After eating all that salami, ketones were at 3.2mM. That's exercise-induced ketosis for you . Didn't eat that much protein before the ride though -- just a cup of cocoa with about 3 tablespoons of butter in it.

**Endurance Exercise Considered Harmful**
I definitely saw a worsening in the issues in my feet over the last 3 days. Mental drive isn't 100% either. Even had a reaction to cocoa yesterday, whereby my ankles and toes started itching. Rubbed in a whole bunch of coconut oil + olive oil + iodine, and symptoms went away after about 2 hours.

The decline started the day after my 2-hour bike ride, and is only now starting to improve back to baseline. I felt great the day of the ride, which I probably attribute to the high post-exercise ketone levels and the adrenaline release.

Gotta be stricter with endurance exercise avoidance and only stick to Higher Intensity exercise. No cycling for while, and now I have to keep both the diet and circadian rhythms strictly in check until I start to feel optimal again.

\----

Somehow or rather I got away with 11 straight days of cycling nonstop in Cairns, and after that feeling great and having improvements in my feet collagen issues. Yet again another reminder that where you live is incredibly important.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-20T10:39:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-134976>

Non-seasonal Carbs Biohack

This is an experiment that I'm going to run in the coming weeks.

**Summary**:

-   At most once a week (can be less frequent), 100-120g of carbohydrates
-   Only starchy carbohydrates that digest quickly. Examples: white rice (sushi!), sweet potatoes. The idea is to get a large insulin spike followed by glucose clearance as quickly as possible (as per Kiefer's recommendations in Carb Backloading)
-   Only eat carbs when rate of protein synthesis is at its circadian peak (typically the last 2-3 hours before sunset)
-   Only eat carbs immediately after a high intensity workout. (high intensity defined either as a HIIT session, or as working with weights above 70% 1RM)
-   Keep fat intake low, and protein intake moderate in this meal. Sushi is the ideal meal.
-   Diet must be high in DHA and Cysteine. (what I'm already doing now)
-   All other days must have afternoon ketone readings exceed 1.0mM

**Assumptions and Precautions**:

-   Redox potential is not thrashed. If I start noticing huge energy dips, or start getting sensitivities like a niacin flush, food intolerances, gut issues, etc ... it's back to strict keto
-   I must be back in deep ketosis within 24 hours. If I do not see ketones above 1.0mM after 24 hours, it means that my body couldn't handle the carbohydrate load
-   High Intensity exercise is possible and not harmful. I generally do a mix of bodyweight, kettlebell, and (sparingly) some barbell work. I've been training in the gym since I was 13, and know how to set up the conditions needed for a protocol like Carb Backloading to work
-   Any major regression in my current redox assessment will lead me to abandon the experiment.

**Motivation**

This is an experiment to increase autophagy using carbohydrates. The idea is to get a significant but transient Reactive Oxygen Species spike to stimulate autophagy, while taking precautions to ensure that I get back into ketosis as quickly as possible.

It is predicated on my body's ability to generate the appropriate amount of ROS (without hitting the fenton reaction), to signal the autophagy mechanisms within the target cells (collagen in my feet especially), during the time of the day whereby protein synthesis is at its most efficient.

Weight training is done to increase muscle-cell-specific glucose uptake via non-insulin mediated mechanisms (GLUT-4). Weight training is done during the time of the day with maximal rate of protein synthesis, which also tends to be the time when fat cells tend to be insulin resistant. In theory, this allows carbs to be shuttled preferentially to muscle tissue (though Bill Lagakos has some refinements -- [http://caloriesproper.com/?p=4962](http://caloriesproper.com/?p=4962))

Time to return to ketosis is going to be my main metric for determining if this experiment is harmful to me. If I can't get back into ketosis, it means that fat burning has been blunted, which has all sorts of negative consequences.

The ideal outcome of the experiment is to have only a very brief 8-hour window once a week whereby I am "out of ketosis". This theoretically allows for high cellular NAD+ all the time in the tissues that I want, while increasing the likelihood of autophagic repair of my broken peripheral collagen during the carb feeding window.

**Misc Notes
**
I wouldn't do this if my redox potential was shot. In that case, ROS signalling is broken, and will only lead to cell apoptosis (of tissue cells which I don't want apoptosis in).

Those familiar with weight training terminology could see this as a micro-cycle during an "off season for autophagy".

### ssj3 — 2014-07-20T11:23:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-134979>

Nice, I will be following this... will you only be training once per week? Are you going to be training fasted or consuming some type of amino acid source peri workout? Any supplements like curcumin? How are you going to judge pre/post stress levels and recovery? Training volume?

And our mate Josh will probably want to know if you will be joining the Monday crew training the bench press in the sagittal plane, lol.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-20T13:41:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-134983>

> [ssj3 said:](/goto/post?id=134979)
>
> Nice, I will be following this... will you only be training once per week? Are you going to be training fasted or consuming some type of amino acid source peri workout? Any supplements like curcumin? How are you going to judge pre/post stress levels and recovery? Training volume?
>
> And our mate Josh will probably want to know if you will be joining the Monday crew training the bench press in the sagittal plane, lol.

Actually I'm basically doing some form of training on a daily basis. Something like:

-   morning stretching
-   random mobility work through the day. My favourite is to do a quick cycle of:
    -   thoracic bridges -- [http://www.t-nation.com/free\_online\_article/most\_recent/the\_30\_second\_mobility\_cure](http://www.t-nation.com/free_online_article/most_recent/the_30_second_mobility_cure)
    -   maxwell daily dozen -- [http://www.maxwellsc.com/articles.cfm?art\_id=3000&startrow=1](http://www.maxwellsc.com/articles.cfm?art_id=3000&startrow=1)
    -   some of the exercises from Eric Goodman's 'Foundation' -- [http://www.amazon.com/Foundation-Redefine-Your-Conquer-Confidence/dp/1609611004](http://www.amazon.com/Foundation-Redefine-Your-Conquer-Confidence/dp/1609611004)
-   random bouts of pushups (usually 15-20 reps) and pullups through the day. Typically get maybe 5 total sets done through the day
-   main workout block at 3-4pm
    -   about 3 times a week, I'll actually go for max effort type sets taken to RPE 8 (as defined by Mike Tuscherer's reactive training manual -- [http://reactivetrainingsystems.com/](http://reactivetrainingsystems.com/)). Something like 3 sets of 5-6 reps with a basic pull and push (usually rows, pullups, dips, and shoulder presses)
    -   the rest of the time, it's just some random stuff:
        -   kettlebell work, usually with a 24kg bell. The usual stuff, snatches, presses, swings, etc ... but usually pretty low volume. Something like 20 snatches, 20 clean and presses, and then maybe 40-50 high pulls and that's it. If it takes longer than 5 mins, then I know it's too much effort
        -   pullups, hanging leg raises, dragon walks, bodyweight squats, pistols, handstands, etc .... very low volume -- just want to make sure that I continue to have proficiency in all the movements
        -   sometimes I'll do a proper 15 min conditioning circuit. Something like a barbell or kettlebell circuit (row, cleans, press, squat). Maybe once or twice a week at most
-   some agility work almost every day, usually right before my main workout block. Short sprints, vertical leaps, bounding, skipping, standing broad jumps, etc ... This takes only 5-10 mins, so very low volume too.

The philosophy is to have frequent bouts of activity that move the body through all planes of motion, done at an intensity level that never leads to fatigue.

So the metrics for success will be:

-   RPEs during max effort work must not increase with the same weights
-   I'm not losing speed or leaping capacity on agility drills
-   I'm not losing mobility
-   IMPORTANT: I'm not losing the motivation to continue with the activity plan above

Meals are usually just upon waking, and then post workout, with potentially some snacking in between. But it's usually just fat, like an egg yolk blended with beef tallow, or some nuts. So no pre nor peri workout nutrition (I've given up with that concept after obsessing over it for 7 years).

No bench presses for me .... got injured one to many times already

\----

Hope that was useful information

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-29T12:29:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-135911>

> [marcus said:](/goto/post?id=135448)
>
> Nice to read your blog. Thx.
>
> What kind of supp do you take? I am using "Life Extension Super K". It contains 1.000mcg of MK4, which I believed to be a high value?!

Well, how much K2 you should take depends on your personal context. Jack wrote about K2 dosage in the top 10 paleo supplements post -- [http://jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/](http://jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/)

I just pop one capsule of this Carlson's Lab K2 MK4 a day, which amounts to 5mg (or 5,000mcg) -- [http://www.iherb.com/Carlson-Labs-Vitamin-K2-Menatetrenone-5-mg-180-Capsules/14812#p=1&oos=0&disc=0&lc=en-US&w=k2 carlson labs&rc=166&sr=null&ic=2](http://www.iherb.com/Carlson-Labs-Vitamin-K2-Menatetrenone-5-mg-180-Capsules/14812#p=1&oos=0&disc=0&lc=en-US&w=k2%20carlson%20labs&rc=166&sr=null&ic=2)

I have a history of gut issues, hence the 5mg dose. If I didn't have any such issues I'd probably be dosing 5mg MK4 once a week, and then maybe 100mcg MK7 on a daily basis (or maybe not at all).

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-29T13:41:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-135913>

**Veggies
**
Tried to add in some vegetables, and anything above about 2 cups of vegetables leads to stomach bloating and changes in bowel habits. I'll stay away from veggies like I've done in the past. A little bit doesn't hurt, but doesn't help with the healing process either.

\---
**Dreaming**

Dream incidence and sex drive has improved in the last 2 weeks. I probably attribute this to the combination of Red Light therapy, and the grounding sheet that I'm sleeping on all the time.

\---
**Epson Salt Baths
**
Took one of these before bed yesterday. Sleep was amazing, with very vivid lucid dreams . Gotta try this again soon.

\---
**Carbs**

Ate a 700g sweet potato on Sunday after a workout, for a total of about 150g of starchy carbs. No other carbs through the day (not even vegetables).

I'm lucky that I don't ever get carb cravings; I ate the sweet potato just because I needed to in the name of experimentation. It did taste good of course...

Felt fine afterward, no brain fog or what not. Bodyweight and visual appearance remained the same the day after too, which meant that there wasn't any carb spillover. Ketones got up to about 0.8mM the day after, which is still indicative of high fatty-acid oxidation rates, and to me, that's a good sign.

For now, I'll probably keep that one day a week where I introduce carbs, and try to push it up as high as possible without affecting fat metabolism. Again, I'll use ketones as an indicator of how well I'm burning fat, and ensure that I always have a 24-hour turnaround time to get back into ketosis (at least 0.5mM, and ideally above 1.0mM). The rest of the week will remain super low carb as always (0g carbs).

\---
**General
**
Collagen issues in my feet still show no signs of improvement. Still getting some edema on a constant basis the moment I get out of bed. The positive sign is that my diet and other behaviours (like exercise) aren't making it worse. Skin quality is also excellent, so it's just the collagen on the inside of the feet and ankles that's getting unzipped somehow.

Mental clarity and work capacity has been excellent. I've been writing maybe an average of 800 lines of code changes a day within about a 5-6 hour span, up from my typical 400-600 line average over the same time span. I've been both more eager to tackle more complex programming tasks, and more able to deal with them in a quick fashion. I'll take this as a good sign that my brain is getting a good electron dose.

I've always seen acute improvements in my feet issues after I'm in the ocean, that last for maybe 6-8 hours. I suspect that being next to the sea and walking in the water on a daily basis would help fix this issue. The implication is that I don't think I will improve on that condition until I can change my current living arrangement to be right next to the sea.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-29T15:59:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-135916>

the other effect of cold? It increases your own magnetism. This helps you retain light in your mitochondria. In eukaryotes, Fritz Popp has shown that all organelles emit some for of light that he calls bio-photons. In fact anything alive emits light. It turns out the amount of light emitted has to be optimized by the cytochromes and the delta psi of the inner mitochondrial membrane. Photons are controlled by electric and magnetic fields. This is why a photon created in the sun can take up to one million years to be released from the sun's core. The same process is at work in a mitochondria. This is why circadian signaling is key in the cytochromes. Here is where the phase transition of energy transfers from the electron occurs in all eukaryotes. Photons are liberated from electrons at cytochrome 1,3, 4 but not at cytochrome 2 where FAD+ occurs. This is how the photoelectric works. You need electrons to generate photons. All food is broken down to electrons. Cytochrome 2 is also where electrons from FFA and the ketogenic diet feed into the ETC. cytochrome 1,3, 4 are where light is liberated from a mitochondria.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-31T12:14:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-136291>

**Ozone
**
Ozone hole is getting bigger as expected -- [http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/](http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/) . Getting as low as 200 dobson units now. Note that the northern hemisphere is currently sitting at 300 dobson units -- [http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/NH.html](http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/NH.html)

\---
**Light Cycles
**
Current daylight time is about 10hrs 10 mins -- [http://www.timeanddate.com/sun/australia/melbourne](http://www.timeanddate.com/sun/australia/melbourne) . Actual solar power only averages 6.8MJ m-2 in July -- [http://www.bom.gov.au/jsp/ncc/cdio/...artYear=2014&p\_c=-1480599678&p\_stn\_num=086038](http://www.bom.gov.au/jsp/ncc/cdio/weatherData/av?p_nccObsCode=193&p_display_type=dailyDataFile&p_startYear=2014&p_c=-1480599678&p_stn_num=086038)

Comparison to my target city, Cairns, right now the daylight hours is 11hr 20 mins -- [http://www.timeanddate.com/sun/australia/cairns](http://www.timeanddate.com/sun/australia/cairns) . Solar exposure sits between 14.6MJ m-2 and 16.6MJ m-2 -- [http://www.bom.gov.au/jsp/ncc/cdio/...&p\_startYear=&p\_c=-195003374&p\_stn\_num=031222](http://www.bom.gov.au/jsp/ncc/cdio/weatherData/av?p_nccObsCode=203&p_display_type=dataFile&p_startYear=&p_c=-195003374&p_stn_num=031222)

\---
**Vegetables. Mass and Energy. Which System Fails First?
**
I'm feeling better in terms of energy levels, sleep, cold tolerance, sex drive, HRV, and other measures of redox potential. The one metric thing that has been screwed up these last 2 weeks has been my gut health, thanks to some of the experimenting that I've been doing with re-introducing certain vegetables into my diet.

Experiment went very poorly, and basically I'm not well enough to tolerate many vegetables. The list of vegetables that don't give me issues is limited to: Onions, Garlic, Mushrooms (all kinds), and some herbs. (seaweeds are still all good).

Weight has gone up and fluctuated significantly thanks to this experiment. In the last 2 weeks, weight has swung anywhere from highs of 83kg to lows of 79kg. I definitely look bloated today .... Cabbage and turnips really made me bloat (weight could spike 2kg in a day). Bristol score has been all over the place, mostly 3 and 4, but some bouts of 6 or 7.

Starchy carbs like sweet potatoes don't seem to give me issues.

Going to stop and revert back to my low-veggie diet which has gotten me this far, and not bother to re-introduce anything of the sort until.

Short term recovery plan is to have lots of water, some magnesium citrate (400mg a day), resveratrol, and then do some 20hr fasts in the next 2 days to get the flora back to baseline.

\---

In terms of the failure cascade, my gut is almost always the first to be robbed of electrons and give me all sorts of funny issues. I need to watch my diet a lot more closely until I can systemically increase redox potential by a large margin.

On the bright side, I should count myself lucky that I don't seem to lose electrons from my brain until shit really hits the fan ....

\---
**Planning to Move
**
Speaking of getting to optimal, far north Queensland definitely seems to be the right place to start experimentation based off my Cairns trip. The only thing stopping me right now is financial uncertainty, since I don't want to have to worry about extra rent at the moment. I'm currently starting up a software business, and will probably move once it gets off the ground with some minimal revenue. Pessimistically, it's probably going to be around either late December this year or January next year.

In the meantime, I'll just have to try and continue being optimal at my current location. Wonder how the increasingly large ozone hole is going to affect me ...

The donut hole is now beginning to cause effects of methane release in the North pole........If you re- read QB 5 I spoke about how the ice of each pole affect one another using quantum mechanisms.......now we are seeing the effects in reality via the combined effects of non native/cosmic EMF and CO2.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-08-03T02:45:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-136568>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=136307)
>
> The donut hole is now beginning to cause effects of methane release in the North pole........If you re- read QB 5 I spoke about how the ice of each pole affect one another using quantum mechanisms.......now we are seeing the effects in reality via the combined effects of non native/cosmic EMF and CO2.

Some weather systems in the Antarctic is pushing more ozone toward the end closer to Australia (bottom of the map here -- [http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big\_image.php?date=2014-07-30&hem=S](http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big_image.php?date=2014-07-30&hem=S)). Ozone over the pole itself is around 300+ dobsons, and then there's this high density band on the side closer to Australia that's 500+ dobsons.

Northern hemisphere patterns are much more evenly distributed, 350+ dobson units all around -- [http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big\_image.php?date=2014-07-30&hem=N](http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big_image.php?date=2014-07-30&hem=N)

In fact, even when the Ozone hole was at its biggest on 24th September 2006, there was this high density band I described above that was still 400-500 dobson units -- [http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big\_image.php?date=2006-09-24&hem=S](http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big_image.php?date=2006-09-24&hem=S)

What does that mean?

In terms of water, it's pretty clear that all the Southern Hemisphere water will be affected. Australia will get the water from the band stretching from the South American continent to the indian ocean. It should be roughly the same for Africa. South America itself apparent doesn't get precipitation from Pacific winds thanks to the Andean mountain range, so I'd assume most of their water comes from equatorial winds bringing precipitation from the south of the continent down to the rest of it.

In terms of the actual amount of ionizing solar radiation that people are exposed to, I would think that South America gets more exposure than Australia due to the shape of the donut hole. But I have no clue about the mechanics, and intuition may not be the best guide.

In any case, I wonder how this affects a human's electron loss risk being in different places in the Southern Hemisphere. Let's say you manage to put genetically identical persons in:

-   Melbourne, Australia
-   Chubut, Argentina (west of the Andes, and the southmost province with more than 500,000 people) -- [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chubut\_Province](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chubut_Province)
-   Santiago, Chile (east of the Andes, different water cycle from Argentina) -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santiago,\_Chile](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santiago,_Chile)
-   Cape Town, South Africa (as south as you'll get on the African continent)

Which place is "worst" from a solar radiation perspective? What metrics can we look at to ascertain this risk? ..... Something for me to eventually look into ....

### yewwei.tan — 2014-08-12T03:35:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-137611>

**Grounding Sheet and Red Light**

Both of these have combined to make sleep generally very good, and mental performance very high. Going to continue as per usual.

\---
**Carb Experiment**

Going to end this once-a-week carb experiment now, with no intention of returning substantial amounts of carbohydrate at least until summer time (Dec 2014). I'm taking too long to get back to a decent level of ketones, which is a great proxy for fat oxidation in me. 3 days after the last carb feeding, and I'm still registering only 0.2mM on the ketone strips.

Yesterday was a warning sign, with sleep quality suffering a significant amount all of a sudden. Specifically, heart rate was higher than normal, and body temp stayed high while trying to get to sleep, preventing sleep onset even after 30 mins. I got up, went to take a 10 min contrast shower, and that got me to sleep pretty well. Still, it was at least an hour of sleep lost, and definitely poorer quality sleep (autophagy compromised).

Sidenote: supposedly it was a full moon yesterday (11th August) -- [http://www.timeanddate.com/moon/australia/melbourne?month=8](http://www.timeanddate.com/moon/australia/melbourne?month=8) , which can do wonky stuff to both magnetism and gravity. It was also a decently cold day, with the outside temp being about 4C, and the ambient temp of my room being around 10C / 50F. None of those factors should be issues if you are healthy.​

\---

I lost 3kg in the 3 weeks I was on holiday in Mid June, and now I've basically gained it back with no change to caloric intake. I definitely still look leaner than I was before I left though, so it's not a 100% fat gain.

I don't think the weight gain has much to do with the addition of carbohydrates, since I basically put it on entirely in the first 2 weeks after returning, when I still wasn't eating any carbs. Continuing to eat carbs now however, can lead to further weight gain from the energetic inefficiency.

It's still interesting to see how I seem to revert to a specific baseline weight and body comp based on my environment. ie: the "Melbourne Winter" version of me is going to settle at 81-82kg, while the "Cairns Dry Season" version of me is going to settle at 78kg (or maybe even lower given more time spent there).

I view being able to maintain a lower body weight without issue as a sign of energetic efficiency (vs a higher body weight). In other words, if I'm doing things right and collecting more electrons and photons, I will naturally maintain a lower body weight.

\---

With all that said, I still see the value of experimenting with spiking caloric intake while in the presence of high insulin levels. I'm going to use protein to create that insulin spike instead of carbs.

I will keep track of calories consumed, because I can, and because I will generally want to stay in a caloric deficit for 5 days out of the week. ItsTheWoo talked a little about that topic recently -- [http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/08/calorie-counting-is-insulin-suppression.html](http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/08/calorie-counting-is-insulin-suppression.html)

2 days a week will be high protein, and higher calories. This frequency was pretty good for me back in Feb-April, so let's see how I deal with it today.

Just to put numbers to it:

-   5 days of <2000kcal. Protein intake below 140-150g, carb intake as low as possible
-   2 days where I bring protein intake to 250-300g, carb intake below 50g (some "carb budget" to get some quercetin via caramelised onions). Basically eat 1kg of a dense protein source twice a week. Calories will probably be in the 2500-3000kcal range

\---

Fasting is doing me well (better bristol score). I will continue eating within a 4-6 hour afternoon-to-sunset window.

\---

P.S: carb loads with unprocessed carbs like potatoes or sweet potatoes are NOT fun . I definitely went to bed feeling stuffed and way to amped-up.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-12T04:13:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-137618>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=136568)
>
> Some weather systems in the Antarctic is pushing more ozone toward the end closer to Australia (bottom of the map here -- [http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big\_image.php?date=2014-07-30&hem=S](http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big_image.php?date=2014-07-30&hem=S)). Ozone over the pole itself is around 300+ dobsons, and then there's this high density band on the side closer to Australia that's 500+ dobsons.
>
> Northern hemisphere patterns are much more evenly distributed, 350+ dobson units all around -- [http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big\_image.php?date=2014-07-30&hem=N](http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big_image.php?date=2014-07-30&hem=N)
>
> In fact, even when the Ozone hole was at its biggest on 24th September 2006, there was this high density band I described above that was still 400-500 dobson units -- [http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big\_image.php?date=2006-09-24&hem=S](http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big_image.php?date=2006-09-24&hem=S)
>
> What does that mean?
>
> In terms of water, it's pretty clear that all the Southern Hemisphere water will be affected. Australia will get the water from the band stretching from the South American continent to the indian ocean. It should be roughly the same for Africa. South America itself apparent doesn't get precipitation from Pacific winds thanks to the Andean mountain range, so I'd assume most of their water comes from equatorial winds bringing precipitation from the south of the continent down to the rest of it.
>
> In terms of the actual amount of ionizing solar radiation that people are exposed to, I would think that South America gets more exposure than Australia due to the shape of the donut hole. But I have no clue about the mechanics, and intuition may not be the best guide.
>
> In any case, I wonder how this affects a human's electron loss risk being in different places in the Southern Hemisphere. Let's say you manage to put genetically identical persons in:
>
> -   Melbourne, Australia
> -   Chubut, Argentina (west of the Andes, and the southmost province with more than 500,000 people) -- [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chubut\_Province](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chubut_Province)
> -   Santiago, Chile (east of the Andes, different water cycle from Argentina) -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santiago,\_Chile](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santiago,_Chile)
> -   Cape Town, South Africa (as south as you'll get on the African continent)
>
> Which place is "worst" from a solar radiation perspective? What metrics can we look at to ascertain this risk? ..... Something for me to eventually look into ....

Look deeper.....................If I can so can you.
Maunder Minimum’s of the solar cycle is the key piece for Oz. Each cycle is 11.11 years. 2014 is at the genesis of a severe grand Maunder minimum. This is when the Sun magnetic activity is extremely low and is characterized by low sunspot action. This allows more cosmic radiation with high energy photons to energize the atmosphere. Excessive cosmic radiation is what allow thunderclouds to grow in the atmosphere which act to discharge the high energy photons and electrons to the ground. Anyone in this path can get massive amounts of radiation in **_less than one second_** to cause massive health changes. During these times, water inside a cell acts differently than water in a glass. The reason is because the Earth’s magnetic field is weakest over the poles and it exposes humans to the most cosmic radiation. At the end of a sunspot cycle, about all you have left are magnetic fields at the solar poles. We just came out of the sunspot maximum of Cycle 24 in 2012-2014. It has been the smallest sunspot cycle in 100 years and the third in a trend of diminishing sunspot cycles.

Most people do not understand the connection between the Earth, sun, and cosmic radiation. The connection is the electromagnetic force. Cosmic radiation is high energy electromagnetic radiation that interacts with water in our atmosphere to form a lot of infra-red radiant energy. When you have a donut hole this allows a chronic amount of cosmic radiation in. Radiant energy is also electromagnetic energy. It may encompass a broad range of wavelengths, with each wavelength exhibiting different physical characteristics. For example, light waves can be seen. Microwaves can cook your food or blow up a star. Radio waves can help us communicate. Xrays can see inside our body to show doctors your skeleton. Infrared waves keep us warm. These waves interact big time with water. All of these features of seem so different to our perception sense that we easily forget that all of these waves belong to a single electromagnetic spectrum contained in our Sun.

During Maunder minimum’s the Earth faces its most extreme climate events because of changes in how the electromagnetic spectrum is split in our atmosphere. **_How waves continue to persist depend upon the medium through which the wave must propagate._** This explains why the architecture of an object or a mass is critical in understanding how a fundamental force acts on it. More solar radiation affects the Earth than usual. With each passing season, the weather seems stranger and more extreme to modern humans because they are clueless about these links. This is why we recently experienced the recent sudden outbreak of the “polar vortex” phenomenon in the US; the unprecedented winter drought in California that is current; and summer temperatures so torrid Down Under that even play at the Australian Open was halted.
During the last grand Maunder Minimum in 1645 — 1715, sunspots basically disappeared and as documented in paintings from the era, Northern Europe suffered unusually cold winter temperatures. The Maunder Minimum coincided with the middle part of the [Little Ice Age](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Little_Ice_Age), during which Europe and North America were subjected to very cold winters in 1645-1715.
No one currently suspects that the Earth’s native field may effect the sun’s output...........but this is what I think.

Maunder Minimum’s primary climate effect is cooling of the atmosphere by reducing electrons and their energies in the atmospere. Predicting weather becomes tougher and the weather can change on a dime. Commodity prices however are very volatile and lead to large swings in price due to the uncertainty of the weather. This cooling effect is good news for Gulf of Mexico residents as well. Ironically, hurricane’s are not as common at these times because the atmosphere is cooler. They tend to occur at solar maximum’s when cosmic radiation builds huge thunderstorm clouds over western Africa and the atmosphere has more negatively charged energy within it. The donut hole does this to OZ constantly now........and the result is felt in water chemistry and cloud formation.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-08-19T12:22:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-138288>

**More Protein
**
I tend to feel really great after a protein refeed. Did 1.1kg of pork neck on Sunday, felt nice and warm for a long time (until the next afternoon), didn't gain any weight, generally felt more alert, and by 1pm the next day (19 hours post meal), had serum BHB back at 0.7mM.

On the days that I drop protein to around 100+g, I don't feel as warm, despite keeping fat intake high. What I'm going to do instead is to still keep food intake lower most of the week (200kcal or less), but probably aim to eat around 150-180g of protein a day, while still doing some days with protein as high as 250-300g. This is still a high fat diet by any definition (65% of calories during the lower intake days)

\---
**Clouds in the Poles
**
I asked a quick question about cloud formation due to cosmic radiation in the CO2 thread. Jack's reply was here -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...o2-is-added-with-emf.10509/page-6#post-138259](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/what-happens-when-co2-is-added-with-emf.10509/page-6#post-138259)

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=138259)
>
> Around the poles the donut hole and the south atlantic anomaly. This is where you can expect some serious cloud formation.

I'm posting some initial thoughts here first, and then probably more in that thread when I get some more coherence about the topic.

But off the bat, it's bound to be a complex topic, and the effects of the further weakening to the solar magnetic field is going to depend on where specifically you are.

If you live where I'm currently at in Melbourne Australia, which is more than 37deg south of the equator, the likelihood is that direct radiation effects from the ozone hole will become greater. Decreased solar magnetic field and increased polar stratospheric cloud formation will lead to further degradation of the ozone hole. See wikipedia for the mechanism -- [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polar\_stratospheric\_cloud](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polar_stratospheric_cloud) . These clouds also tend to form more readily in colder temps.

The water in Melbourne would then come from the south-east winds all the way from South America, which blows across the entire south of the Australian Continent. South America is also over the South Atlantic Anomaly. I'm not sure if more clouds over this region means more rain in the south of Australia.

I'm planning on moving to Cairns, which is 17deg south of the equator, and therefore in the tropic of Capricorn. If I'm not wrong, it gets rainwater from the northern hemisphere during the monsoon months of Sep-Apr, and then is relatively dry, whereby rain from south-east winds. Theoretically speaking, a colder North Pole would mean stronger pressure and more rain during the monsoon season on the Asian continent, leading to a higher likelihood of flooding. I don't know if that would directly affect the Indo-Australian monsoon though ...

Therefore my hunch at the moment is that being further north exposes you to a stronger magnetic field, less cosmic rays, and gives you access to slightly better water.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-08-26T03:03:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-138891>

Thanks guys. Haven't had the time to watch that vid yet, though I may get the opportunity to do regular walking on rocks pretty soon ...

\---
**Spring Transition
**
Melbourne crossed 11 hours of daylight yesterday, as we get closer and closer to spring. Jack has said that seasonal transitions are always more stressful, and I'm definitely feeling the effects. Sex drive is lower, physical endurance is affected, and my feet are a little more swollen.

Add to the fact that the ozone hole is now only 200+ dobson units on most days. September and October will just bring worse symptoms.

\---
**Time to Get Out of Melbourne**

This last bout of issues was the "fuck it" moment that I needed to commit to a permanent move away from Melbourne. I'm flying up to Cairns in 14 days (9th Sep), with the likelihood being that I'll stay there for the foreseeable future.

In the intervening 2 weeks, this is where supplements are going to help. I've added:

-   taurine 500-1000mg a day
-   acetyl L-carnitine 1200-2400mg a day
-   quercetin about 800mg a day
-   resveratrol 800-1000mg a day
-   curcumin 1600mg a day
-   N-Acetyl Cysteine 500mg a day (with either lots of oyster consumption, or a little bit of supplemental zinc -- 30mg / day)

\---
**Cairns
**
This is about 17deg south of the equator, and gets pretty decent sunshine year round, so that's a plus point. I'll be staying on the beach, so that's another plus. Temperature ranges are decent, typically 15-27C during the dry season, and 22-31C during the wet season. Humidity usually as low as 50-60% during the dry season, and 80+% in the wet season. It's a coastal town, so basically at sea level.

The wet season is from Dec to Mar, whereby we'll probably get less sun, but that's when the place gets most of the rain. Water supply to Cairns then comes from the Copperlode Dam and Behana Creek, which are anywhere from 10-20km southwest of the city. Current water levels are good (overflowing), and I obviously hope that it stays that way -- daily report [http://webs.cairns.qld.gov.au/Filestore/water-quality/CRC\_Water\_Summary.pdf](http://webs.cairns.qld.gov.au/Filestore/water-quality/CRC_Water_Summary.pdf)

I also have a hunch that the water, which mostly comes during the Indo-Australian monsoon, is more capable of energy transfers compared to the water from where I'm at right now in Melbourne. (37 deg south, water from the South Hemispheric easterlies). No fluoridation of the water supply too, since the government thought it too expensive.

Most of the vegetation in Cairns is either tropical rainforest, or mangrove, both of which are evergreen of course. I guess more rainforest is always good, and so far there hasn't been much incentive to destroy them for other development. Rainforest tourism is the major industry here, and not many people in Australia actually want to or are able to relocate to Cairns (hard to find work, etc ...), so it has one of the lower city expansion rates in Australia -- [http://profile.id.com.au/cairns/population-estimate](http://profile.id.com.au/cairns/population-estimate) .

It is of course, still growing, though the current count of about 157,000 people is pretty manageable. Well, at least the current migration dynamics make it such that young people are moving out, and older people are moving in -- [http://forecast.id.com.au/cairns/net-migration-by-age](http://forecast.id.com.au/cairns/net-migration-by-age) . Older folk hopefully means a slower rate of technology adoption.

Mangroves are interesting. I've always known that they are specifically adapted to low oxygen conditions, so I wonder if they would be the last to die when the ocean turns into a hypoxic hell. Supposedly they are great at sequestering atmospheric carbon as well -- [http://www.jseejournal.com/JSEE 2012/PDF file\_JSEE 3(2) 2012/12. Carbon Sequestration pp. 87-92.pdf](http://www.jseejournal.com/JSEE%202012/PDF%20file_JSEE%203\(2\)%202012/12.%20Carbon%20Sequestration%20%20pp.%2087-92.pdf) . What I don't know is how much extra O2 they put out into the environment. Well, I guess I'll find out via first-hand experience, since the beach suburb I'm relocating to is basically surrounded by mangroves.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-01T11:48:18+0300

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-01T12:31:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-139541>

I was going to ask this as a question, but still have not formed a specific question to ask, so I'll dump some thoughts here first.

\---

Since my lifetime is probably going to be characterised by progressive hypoxia of all of the world's systems, so naturally I've been thinking a little about this topic.

This train of thought is stimulated by the latest Tensegrity 4 blog post, as well as this comment in the 'Fasted Stated Training Adaptations' -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...-training-adaptations.297/page-23#post-117964](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/page-23#post-117964)

Key quote from the above thread:

If you want to turbo charge performance.......here is another clue.......Fasted, hydrated, hypoxic training is the key.
​

This got me thinking about exercise-induced ketosis. It's been my experience that when I'm eating a ketogenic diet, I get higher and higher blood BHB the more exercise I do. So far, the longest bout has been a 2 hour bike ride, whereby my ketones started at 0.6mM, and after which my ketones were above 3.0mM for at least a few hours.

Peter Attia's recent blog post about exogenous ketones showed that in himself, systemic oxygen consumption decreased when the body can rely on a large source of ketones for fuel -- [http://eatingacademy.com/personal/experience-exogenous-ketones](http://eatingacademy.com/personal/experience-exogenous-ketones)

Well, the first though that I have is that the more hypoxic our environment gets, the more we'll have to rely on a ketogenic diet to spare more oxygen on the brain (by having the rest of the tissues not require as much).

I'm also thinking about exercise induced autophagy, which is something that I really need to first my collagen issues. Naturally, I'm thinking about the safest and most efficient way to do this. I'll probably be testing this theory once I make the move to Cairns next week, by modulating the type of exercise that I do. The hypothesis so far is that moderate bouts of higher intensity endurance activity is the most beneficial. An example would be a 15 minute bike ride on as high an intensity as I can, while being ketotic and properly hydrated (and ideally at 4pm when protein synthesis is greatest).

Then I remembered Dr Kruse talk about his hypoxia experiment over the last few months, and whereby he was avoiding CT on purpose, since CT would increase oxygen when he was trying to be hypoxic. Anyway, I'm wondering how spot CT would work out.

\---
(end of random thoughts)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-09-01T17:02:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-139549>

### Jack Kruse — 2014-09-01T17:27:14+0300

When you understand Tensegrity 4 you will understand how I see the world now: When I see snow laying on the ground and draped over trees, I see great heaps of protons quietly precessing in the earth's magnetic field. You begin to see the world for a moment as something rich and strange is the private reward of many new discoveries for regaining your health.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-06T10:24:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-140103>

From -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/august-6th-2014.11652/#post-140086](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/august-6th-2014.11652/#post-140086)

Higher hospital cardiovascular and cerebro-vascular accident mortality
Higher number of deaths of outpatients from acute myocardial infarction (heart attacks the next week)
Higher diastolic blood pressure in healthy blood donors and hypertensive patients
Higher number of blood platelets, prothrombin index, and platelet aggregation (risk of coronary thrombosis) Taking aspirin the next week would be wise.
Greater human blood plasma viscosity (with the risk of clotting)
Use CT levels massively this next week. DO NOT FLY AT ALL THE NEXT 5 days.
Higher levels on human prolactin and 17-ketosteroid levels for those of you who are biohackers.
​

Oh well, looks like I picked the worst time to fly (Tuesday 9th Sep 2014). At least it's not a long flight -- 3.5hours from Melbourne to Cairns, and at least I'll be flying northward away from the poles. I better look for some beach time once I land.

### prAna303 — 2014-09-06T10:28:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-140105>

**FILAMENT ERUPTION, EARTH-DIRECTED:** On Sept. 2nd, an enormous filament of [dark plasma](http://spaceweathergallery.com/indiv_upload.php?upload_id=101279), which had been snaking across the face of the sun for days, became unstable and erupted. NASA's Solar Dynamics Observatory recorded the blast: [movie #1](http://spaceweather.com/images2014/02sep14/20140902205957_512_aia_0304.mp4?PHPSESSID=7fuig7u0nlml81johog3mm0ej7), [#2](http://sdowww.lmsal.com/sdomedia/h264/2014/08/31/SSW_cutout_20140831T0000-20140902T1700_AIA_304-193-171_0250.mov). Soon thereafter, a lopsided CME billowed away from sun: According to NOAA analysts, the CME will deliver a glancing blow to Earth's magnetic field on Sept. 6th. This is not a particularly fast or powerful CME. Nevertheless, the coming impact could spark auroras. The last two minor CMEs that struck Earth in late August triggered beautiful displays of Northern and Southern Lights. The reason: it's [aurora season](http://science.nasa.gov/science-news/science-at-nasa/2002/23sep_auroraseason/). High-latitude sky watchers should prepare for Sept. 6th.

Source: [http://spaceweather.com/](http://spaceweather.com/)

Nah, Internet here is even better than where I was back in Melbourne! 15+MBps download speeds pretty easy here. I guess it's the same quality infrastructure as Melbourne, but with 3% of the population

Currently stuck with WiFi now that I'm in a sharehouse while getting my own place, so I've been using the internet sporadically and mainly for work purposes. Then the day time has been spent mostly on beaches reading and writing (with pen and paper). Great weather for being on the beach that's for sure, 17-27C (63-81F) every day with lots of sun and a 50-60% humidity range (September is the dryest month). Looks like we've got good weather for the G20 summit -- [http://www.timeanddate.com/weather/australia/cairns/ext](http://www.timeanddate.com/weather/australia/cairns/ext)

Thanks for all the compliments!

\---

Thinking about how to draw something with the line "Water: A single drop contains an ocean of information". Perhaps I'll sketch something while on the beach tomorrow.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-13T10:45:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-140841>

Also drew this random symbol when I thought of the phrase "Not all (hydrogen) bonds are built to last", which I fancied as an apt header to a section explaining ways in which the hydrogen bonding structure of water could break down (with non-native EMF and heating to high temperatures probably being the most common)

Again, I think it's too much colour -- I should probably just draw everything in black and white, then distinguish the oxygen and hydrogen atoms by the pattern which I fill them with. The pattern for oxygen should be non-intersecting lines (denoting negative charge), while the pattern for hydrogen atoms should be intersecting lines (denoting positive charge). Anyway, I'm just thinking aloud as usual ....

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-13T10:59:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-140845>

Random topics since it's Saturday:

**1.** For some reason the EMF field in my room right now is <0.001mW/cm2 in the radio range, and <0.03mG in the ELF range. Surprisingly good considering the WiFi in the house

**2**. Beach time is always good

**3.** When I was 17, I wanted to be either a musician, a comic book artist, or a concept artist for movies/games.

I became a programmer instead  partly for the money (and I made a decent chunk despite only working 1 year), and partly because computer science seemed to describe some aspects of nature pretty well (and I still think it does).

I've thrown away dreams of becoming a digital concept artist, but eventually I'm definitely going to draw comics. Japanese manga style, meaning only black and white, and accomplishable on pen-and-paper (example -- [http://www.onemanga.me/Tokyo\_Ghoul/143/16/](http://www.onemanga.me/Tokyo_Ghoul/143/16/))

As for music, I'll probably have to buy a special acoustic guitar which has the thin neck of an electric guitar to further my musical aspirations.

All in due time ... for now, continue with the project of starting an online business, create some good software (read: software that gets the job done quickly, so that you need LESS software in your life), make some cash, and buy the freedom to do whatever is needed for optimal health.

Back to coding

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-14T10:57:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-140941>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=140854)
>
> Love it Yew  you rock
> you could just draw by hand at the beach, take pics and upload online

Got some drawing done today!  Still needed a lot of digital touch-ups though.

\----

Probably a little too much exercise today. 20km bike up to Palm Cove and back. Well at least I got to sit on the beach, draw some sketches, and read some X-rated fan fiction .

Drew a total of 3 sketches, but only colourised 2 of them so far.

First one was of the aforementioned line '**Water: Every Drop contains an Ocean of Information**'

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Second one was a phrase that I had thought up a few weeks ago, but never got around to drawing. The phrase was "**We Needed Philosophy, but all we got were Visionaries"**.

Put one way, we need wise philosophers who know why they are building certain technologies, instead of narrowly-focused visionaries who just want to see their vision fulfilled at any cost. I'll probably write a full (300 word) blog about this sometime.

Last sketch isn't coloured in yet, and I haven't decided on the colour scheme (though I think it should be only 5-6 colours). This was done with the working title, "**Not all (Hydrogen) Bonds are Built To Last**"

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-14T11:03:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-140942>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=140884)
>
> [http://www.dailypaul.com/318405/study-guitarists-brains-are-different-than-others](http://www.dailypaul.com/318405/study-guitarists-brains-are-different-than-others)

Maybe that's why I'm such a hippy

I do remember telling myself 7 or 8 years back that I would only become a professional musician if I could come up with 100 songs before I turned 18. Figured that the only way I could make it as a musician was if I could prove that I was actually really good. I only got to about 60+ ideas, all written from when I was 16 to 18 years old. Not being able to generate enough ideas, I didn't end pursue that path, foreseeing nothing but years of strife without much reward if I did.

Well, that turned out to be a good decision  I still have all the music ideas lying around in powertab or guitar pro format though. One day I'll turn those into fully fleshed out songs .... That said, I still like the way some of those idea sound. My idea was always to make fast-paced instrumental ambient music, which worked fine as instrumentals, but could also be re-purposed with vocals to become something completely different. Maybe I'll convert some of those rough cuts into MP3s and post them somewhere

### caroline — 2014-09-14T11:15:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-140944>

you are a man on a mission Yew!

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-14T22:48:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-140990>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=140944)
>
> you are a man on a mission Yew!

Not really  .... I just want to create and consume great art for as long as I live (a healthy and long life).

All I can do is try to align my selfish desires with the greater good of humanity.

\----

6 days in Cairns so far, and I'm starting to see changes already.

First thing is that diet is higher in carbs than usual, but not particularly high carb. Maybe 70-120g of carbs a day. Lots of dark chocolate, but only 2 occasions with starchy carbs (both of which were days with 40+km bike rides).

Wake up is immediately synchronised to the 0-30 min window before sunrise, which is why I'm awake now at 5:46am, whereby sunrise is about 6:15am. We've got 11hr 59min of sunlight today.

Sleep needs have gone down dramatically, immediately cut an hour of each day, to get just over 7 hours of sleep a day. Yet I feel more rested than I was in Melbourne.

Overall, I'm definitely feeling much better than I was in Melbourne. I would love to see the result after 8 weeks, after I'm gotten a more permanent place to stay, and am visiting the beach multiple times a day if possible.
