### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-15T10:26:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-141040>

Digged up some of my old music compositions ... I like looking back sometimes

First up was one that was just called '**Idea6**', and is dated **<13-Aug-2006>**, so I was just over 15 years old when I wrote that. All I have is a MIDI version of the tabs, so it definitely sounds awful -- [https://db.tt/BwWjFM53](https://db.tt/BwWjFM53)

Fast forward one year, and I wrote "**idea34 - Rushed Goodbyes**", dated **<16-May-2007>**. I intended this to be just 2 guitars and some vocals, but never ended up writing the lyrics . MP3 recording of the MIDI track -- [https://db.tt/quPxtQOm](https://db.tt/quPxtQOm)

Fast forward 6 months, I have "**idea39 - Don't Believe In Angels**", dated **<18-Dec-2007>**. This was meant to be a piano with a guitar, and I also wanted to write lyrics, but it seems this was never my strong suit, and I never got those written either ... MP3 file converted from MIDI again -- [https://db.tt/3ohsSvRX](https://db.tt/3ohsSvRX)

Then sometime in early 2008, I heard 'Michi to you all' by Aluto -- (link:

), and decided in my pompous youth to write a "better" version of that. Guitar Pro 5 also just came out at the time, so this was the first time I decided to use the new "Realistic Sound Engine' which came with the software. I called the song "**idea54 - If I could have one more chance**", dated **<16-Mar-2008>**. MP3 -- [https://db.tt/v3t6hG2Y](https://db.tt/v3t6hG2Y)

Then I decided that I wanted to write something with lyrics, so I did "**idea56 - Coin in the Wishing Well**" on **<19-May-2008>**. I liked the way this turned out, one of these days I need to find a good singer (or learn to sing), learn to add some orchestral background chords, and record this for real. Lyrics -- [https://db.tt/6ffM1KnC](https://db.tt/6ffM1KnC) . MP3 -- [https://db.tt/uMajmjjd](https://db.tt/uMajmjjd)

Then the last full song I wrote up until now was "**idea62 - After the Grey**", which was done on **<28-Dec-2008>** (almost 6 years now!). This was a purely instrumental piece, and I also liked the way it turned out. I need to do an actual recording of this sometime. MP3 played using the guitar pro software -- [https://db.tt/Bn9sWcys](https://db.tt/Bn9sWcys)

Then I left my childhood and teenage stomping grounds (Singapore) at the beginning of 2009, went to Melbourne, gave up making music, and focused hardcore on becoming a professional software developer

At least I had some fun with music along the way, and I never actually stopped practicing the guitar ...

### caroline — 2014-09-15T11:16:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-141046>

Hi Yew ....love the music!

What do you thing will be the differences living in Cairns as opposed to Melbourne for the summer?
Is it mostly latitude and water? or will the biggest differences be mainly in the fall/winter?

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-16T00:45:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-141103>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=141046)
>
> Hi Yew ....love the music!
>
> What do you thing will be the differences living in Cairns as opposed to Melbourne for the summer?
> Is it mostly latitude and water? or will the biggest differences be mainly in the fall/winter?

I think the higher latitude is going to decrease the Ozone Hole problem somewhat. The hole is pretty big as of September 12, being only 100+ dobson units in some places (350-400 dobson units is normative) -- [http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scr...S&source=IOMI\_PAURA\_V8F\_MGEOS5FP&section=HOME](http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big_image.php?date=2014-09-12&hem=S&source=IOMI_PAURA_V8F_MGEOS5FP&section=HOME)

I think water is going to be better in the summer months, since monsoon rains bring water from the Northern Hemisphere.

I think water's dielectric constant will be better in Cairns during summer. Referencing EE4's section '**Physical factors affecting apparent dielectric strength of water':** \-- [http://jackkruse.com/energy-and-epigenetics-4-light-water-magnetism/](http://jackkruse.com/energy-and-epigenetics-4-light-water-magnetism/)

-   ocean around Cairns (reef) is not as deep as around Melbourne (Bering strait)
-   Cairns is actually comparable to Melbourne in terms of temperature. Cairns is 24-32C in summer with rarely any days above 34C, while Melbourne can get 34-40C days a lot
-   Cairns has wet summers vs dry summers in Melbourne, so humidity is higher => higher dielectric strength
-   Cairns likely has lower EMF risk thanks to being further from the Donut hole, and having 3% of the population spread over a larger area

Random sidenote: people here in Cairns complain that summers get very hot and humid. I think after living for more than half my life in the constant 100% humidity and 26-36C of Singapore, I'll feel just fine

>
> I think water is going to be better in the summer months, since monsoon rains bring water from the Northern Hemisphere.
>
> I think water's dielectric constant will be better in Cairns during summer. Referencing EE4's section '**Physical factors affecting apparent dielectric strength of water':** \-- [http://jackkruse.com/energy-and-epigenetics-4-light-water-magnetism/](http://jackkruse.com/energy-and-epigenetics-4-light-water-magnetism/)
>
> -   ocean around Cairns (reef) is not as deep as around Melbourne (Bering strait)
> -   Cairns is actually comparable to Melbourne in terms of temperature. Cairns is 24-32C in summer with rarely any days above 34C, while Melbourne can get 34-40C days a lot
> -   Cairns has wet summers vs dry summers in Melbourne, so humidity is higher => higher dielectric strength
> -   Cairns likely has lower EMF risk thanks to being further from the Donut hole, and having 3% of the population spread over a larger area
>
> Random sidenote: people here in Cairns complain that summers get very hot and humid. I think after living for more than half my life in the constant 100% humidity and 26-36C of Singapore, I'll feel just fine

The length of hydrogen bonds depends on bond strength, temperature, and pressure. The bond strength itself is dependent on temperature, pressure, bond angle, and environment. This is usually characterized by local [dielectric](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dielectric) constant. Water has a very high dielectric constant but it is quickly adjustable by the environment.
If the hydrogen bond is substantially bent then it follows that the bond strength is weaker. This is why bonding angles have massive effects on the thermodynamic abilities in water. This is the deep science behind why the people in Australia have water that has a less favorable energy profile that lead to more disease. The main criteria to determine the strength of hydrogen bonds are their (relatively inaccurately determined) intermolecular distances and the (more precise) wave numbers of their stretching [vibrational modes](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/vibrat.html) and those of the donor hydrogen covalent bond.

When I think about the times when I have carb cravings, it is only after eating a bunch of carbs  I think I'm pretty lucky that going full on keto with less than 0g carbs a day is really easy, and I don't get any want for carbs at all.

I wish we had a mitochondrial blue light meter to compare the relative contribution of excess blue light of a piece of bread compared to 60 minutes of staring at a phone screen. (ie: which source of blue light is the most significant in altering mitochondrial efficiency)

\====

Haven't washed my hair with anything other than water in 3 weeks  Yet I'm not getting any of the problems that I used to get from not washing it. Usually I'd get dandruff and excess oil buildup within 3 or 4 days. Doesn't even smell bad either. I guess I've been "washing" it during my bouts of ocean swimming

But speaking of ocean swimming, we only have just over a month before stinger season hits in November. If there's one thing I'm hoping for from this impending "Maunder Minimum", it's cooler oceans, and therefore less cyclones and less jellyfish-infested seas.

IMO only protein is excess contributes to excessive blue light load. I'm not entirely sure of this as well, since protein breakdown products can enter the TCA cycle, or can be shuttled through the Urea cycle, or can undergo gluconeogenesis (which basically means protein ends up as carbs). That said, I don't know enough about mitochondrial mechanics to comment if the electrons entering here are high powered blue light electrons.  So we'll have to wait to someone else to chime in.

The tricky part is in determining what is considered excess for a particular individual. I have no idea how to go about doing this except for monitoring my own measures of redox potential in response to high protein intake.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-23T08:19:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-141698>

My version of intermittent fasting these days has been to eat somewhere between 6am and 12pm. Odd eating schedule, but I just find that I'm never hungry in the afternoon or night time. This is even the case on my current low calorie diet (maybe around 1500kcal a day).

The only drawback I could think of was that I'm taking advantage of the window of maximum protein synthesis (the last 2 hours of daylight). Though I'd think that because high protein meals take hours to fully digest anyway, that a meal eaten at 12pm would still be providing amino acids for growth at 5pm.

Bill Lagakos seems to support this style of eating though -- [http://caloriesproper.com/afternoon-diabetes-and-nutrient-timing/](http://caloriesproper.com/afternoon-diabetes-and-nutrient-timing/)

-   **DHA is not an electron source, it is merely the conductor that directs the orchestra.** The electron input when I was in Melbourne was insufficient to overcome my issues, whereas the bright sunshine and ample beaches in Cairns provide more than enough electrons for DHA to do its job. So I'm still thinking that there is some geological feature of that region that helps the Japanese people preserve what DHA and electrons that they have.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-24T13:12:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-141824>

Reading through the last post by Bill Lagakos again -- [http://caloriesproper.com/afternoon-diabetes-and-nutrient-timing/](http://caloriesproper.com/afternoon-diabetes-and-nutrient-timing/) . Summary for the purpose of my experiment is basically:

-   Skeletal tissue insulin sensitivity is high in the morning and gets lower through the day
-   Adipose tissue insulin sensitivity is low in the morning and gets higher through the day
-   "these data suggest that skewing more calories earlier in the day, when insulin sensitivity is high in muscle and low in adipose, bodes better for[body composition](http://caloriesproper.com/carb-back-loading-take-ii/) and blood glucose control"

I think I'm going to do a biohack whereby I do 4 weeks of almost only breakfast eating in a calorie-restricted state, with the goal of maximal fat loss even down to single digit body fat percentage, while maintaining a good redox potential. I think the only time where I'll eat later into the day is when I do a significant workout later in the day (like a kettlebell circuit, some pullups, leg raises, etc ..). We'll see how this goes.

-   Sites like this talk about the effect of magnetism on water -- [http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/magnetic.html](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/magnetic.html) . **But what is the effect of water on magnetism**? When each mitochondria is surrounded by a large sphere of EZ water, how does that enhance the magnetic effect? Does this magnetic enhancement effect need other proteins to work (to exist as a topological insulator)
-   Water is diamagnetic. How does this property interact with other diamagnetic matter (many crystals like black tourmaline are diamagnetic) to enhance or decrease the magnetic effect of water?

Plenty of derivative questions follow based on those 2, like the effects of temperature (which the mitochondria can alter), but I'll have to think a little more ....

I definitely continue with Mag malate supplementation too. Though I wonder whether having my feet in the ocean every day reduces the need for supplementation by virtue of magnesium absorption through the skin.

\-----
**Random Haiku
**
Saw this Haiku from the water research site: [http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/haiku.html](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/haiku.html)

水の音
光の遊び
生きている​

The given english translation is:

Sound of a stream,
Sunlight dancing on waters,
Life wakes up again.
​

The romanji (how you pronounce it) for the Japanese text is:

mizu no oto
hikari no asobi
ikite-iru​

The literal line-by-line translation, in the same order as the Japanese text (which is not grammatically correct in English) is:

water's sound
light's playfulness
living exists (no subject is given, so we assume that this refers to either life in general, or the speaker of the sentence)
​

\----
**Personal DHA Intake Target**

Totally random, but just to keep myself accountable, the goal now is to consume at least 3g of DHA a day.

The salmon that I buy for $30/kg has around **400-1000mg of DHA per 100g** depending on the type of salmon. A 100g can of sardines for $1.50 ($15/kg) has **600+mg of DHA per 100g**. The "lousy" Woolworths homebrand canned mackerel (which no-one buys) that comes at $1.60 a can for just over 400g of mackerel ($4/kg) has **1400mg of DHA per 100g** (USDA ref -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4072/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4072/2) . You have to select a 100g serve, and then expand the 'Fats and Fatty Acids' profile to see this information. DHA's shorthand is 22:6(n-3)).

I've got 22 cans of mackerel in the closet right now, and have been eating a can a day. Should probably stock up on more . Only 550kcal and about 60g of protein for those who still think that macronutrients matter a lot.

I can hit my target with a single can of mackerel, 5 cans of sardines, or anywhere from 300-800g of salmon. I need to make a reminder to eat lots of Aji and Saba when I'm on holiday in Japan. Also goes to show the wisdom being drinking mackerel head smoothies I guess

Don't care about the issue with canned food either -- the DHA will buffer any issues

> i think i will never use a hat in my life anymore....... if it is not extremely cold like -30 degree C.. but it does not get so cold here....

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=142767)
>
> Funny that you learn Japanese.... love it  each new language has its own world.. so if you speak many you have a way broader world
>
> I speak 5 languages pretty well (Norwegian, Swedish, Finnish, German and English.... the last being my worst... I know a bit Estonian and Russian too, but just a little),
> and it is weird how some words to describe something does not exist in the other language at all..
> I actually know several wordslike that - it is sometimes annoying...lol you want to say it in the other language... but it is impossible as the word does not exist... uh!

So very true!

\*Programmer's rant\*

Similar situation with computer languages. I definitely have benefitted greatly from being able to program is basically every type of programming language at a professional level (with the except of stack programming languages. Damn you Factor and Forth).

But every programming environment brings with it it's own language and constraints. The way I would render a list of blog posts on the iPhone using Objective-C or Swift is vastly different (yet similar) from the way you would do it on an Android device in Java.

The way you get a simple app server running in a systems programming language like C is totally different from an actor model (Erlang), which is totally different from some purely functional style (Haskell), which is again somewhat different when you're passing continuations (Racket), which is completely different when you run a flow-based paradigm.

And let's not get into meta-programming and DSLs (convenient ways to force a new way of doing things into existence, many a time with bad consequences) ....
​

Having experienced a good breadth of computer languages, I really want to explore the full range of human languages.

That means I want to learn languages that have different grammar, different sounds, and of course, different cultural contexts.

So far, English is by far my best language, and Chinese I can read and write to quite a high level (eg: I am able to comprehend both the cultural context and the more advanced language used in political discussions).

I can't speak Chinese well though .... and it's not because of the pronunciation (Chinese has many more sounds than English, and is definitely harder to pronounce correctly), but mainly because of the grammar. I'm too used to thinking in English, and sometimes the order in which thoughts arise in my mind (in English) usually don't translate very well to Chinese speech, and thus there's an extra lag time translating English thought to Chinese speech in my head.

The way I'm going to fix that is to be able to think in Chinese itself, which I've always found to be very difficult.

By "Chinese" I'm referring to 普通話, or the "common chinese dialect". Some chinese dialects are world's apart. My grandfather was Hokkien, and my grandmother was Cantonese. Those 2 languages sound nothing like each other, and even the vocabulary used to refer to the same things is different thanks to different cultural contexts.

Example (thanks to the Singapore government ). Here's the same words spoken in a couple of different Chinese dialects

-   Hokkien --

-   Cantonese --

-   Teochew --

Well, my Mum has Hainanese and Peranakan (Malay + Chinese) blood. Her sister married a Teochew. Dad's eldest sister married another Baba-nonya. I happen to be 189cm and dark-skinned (thank you grandma for making Christmas island your a home and feeding all your kids with lots of fish). Whatever, we're all "Southern Chinese" anyway ​

I'm finding Japanese to be a very interesting and fun language to learn. Much more verbose than Chinese despite sharing common character subsets, and has a prefix/suffix system with word stems, which is not really present in Chinese.

I have no problems pronouncing Japanese either. The Japanese don't really differentiate between some rounds like R/L and F/H. So 'Hi-ka-ri' can be pronounced as 'Hi-ka-li', and usually the R/L sound is a mix of R/L/D. Another example is that 財布 (literal meaning 'wealth-cloth', which refers to a wallet) can be pronounced 'sai-fu' or 'sai-hu'.

Reading Japanese is tough though, since just like Chinese, there is no phonetic alphabet (at least when it comes to kanji), and there are both kunyomi and onyomi readings, and kunyomi readings usually are different for different conjugations. So basically if you don't have a certain character in memory, you have absolutely no clue how to read it. Good news is that the meanings of the words usually don't change too much between Chinese and Japanese, so even if I see a word which I can't read in Japanese, I usually know what it means.

\----

Well, that's the "oriental languages", which I view as one extreme of the language spectrum (if you believe the US Foreign Service Institute's ranking of how difficult a language is to learn for a native English speaker -- [http://www.effectivelanguagelearning.com/language-guide/language-difficulty](http://www.effectivelanguagelearning.com/language-guide/language-difficulty))

Tentatively, the next languages (in order) that I want to learn within the next 3-5 years are:

-   Spanish. Supposed to be very similar to English, so I want to use this to test some language learning techniques to help with future language leraning
-   Cantonese. It's weird that I'm a cantonese that can't speak it . Pronouncing this is the hardest part. Each reading in Chinese has 4 tones, while each reading in Cantonese has 8. Meaning that there are 4 ways to say "shi" in Chinese, but 8 ways to say "shi" in Cantonese. And then there are sounds in Cantonese that don't exist in Chinese ...
-   Some form of Nordic language. Probably Finnish first, then Swedish. Finnish is remarkably easy for me to pronounce for some reason. Swedish has some sounds that I'm unfamiliar with. Then I hear Icelandic and think that is beautiful too --

-   German. Feels and sounds close enough to English, so why not
-   French. Everyone says that this is closely related in grammar and semantics to English, but it sounds completely different. I want to learn new sounds!
-   Russian. "Middle of the line" language between the 2 extremes of Chinese and English (which I already know). New character set too,
-   Vietnamese. More new sounds in a language that seems to be related to Chinese.
-   Hindi. This I think will be difficult. I have no idea what to expect. At least pronouncing it is pretty easy (I have no issues with rolling my 'R's)
-   Arabic. Also no idea what to expect, but I want to see why people say that Arabic poetry is so beautiful

Probably is pretty ambitious. But don't underestimate the power of DHA (Inger is proof!)

> BTW, hope that you're starting to feel much better after switching back to Optimal Living since October 1st
>
>
>
> Pretty much
>
>
>
> I definitely continue with Mag malate supplementation too. Though I wonder whether having my feet in the ocean every day reduces the need for supplementation by virtue of magnesium absorption through the skin.
>
> \-----
> **Random Haiku
> **
> Saw this Haiku from the water research site: [http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/haiku.html](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/haiku.html)
>
>
> 水の音
> 光の遊び
> 生きている​
>
>
> The given english translation is:
>
>
> Sound of a stream,
> Sunlight dancing on waters,
> Life wakes up again.
> ​
>
> The romanji (how you pronounce it) for the Japanese text is:
>
>
> mizu no oto
> hikari no asobi
> ikite-iru​
>
>
> The literal line-by-line translation, in the same order as the Japanese text (which is not grammatically correct in English) is:
>
>
> water's sound
> light's playfulness
> living exists (no subject is given, so we assume that this refers to either life in general, or the speaker of the sentence)
> ​
>
> \----
> **Personal DHA Intake Target**
>
> Totally random, but just to keep myself accountable, the goal now is to consume at least 3g of DHA a day.
>
> The salmon that I buy for $30/kg has around **400-1000mg of DHA per 100g** depending on the type of salmon. A 100g can of sardines for $1.50 ($15/kg) has **600+mg of DHA per 100g**. The "lousy" Woolworths homebrand canned mackerel (which no-one buys) that comes at $1.60 a can for just over 400g of mackerel ($4/kg) has **1400mg of DHA per 100g** (USDA ref -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4072/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4072/2) . You have to select a 100g serve, and then expand the 'Fats and Fatty Acids' profile to see this information. DHA's shorthand is 22:6(n-3)).
>
> I've got 22 cans of mackerel in the closet right now, and have been eating a can a day. Should probably stock up on more . Only 550kcal and about 60g of protein for those who still think that macronutrients matter a lot.
>
> I can hit my target with a single can of mackerel, 5 cans of sardines, or anywhere from 300-800g of salmon. I need to make a reminder to eat lots of Aji and Saba when I'm on holiday in Japan. Also goes to show the wisdom being drinking mackerel head smoothies I guess
>
> Don't care about the issue with canned food either -- the DHA will buffer any issues

Thanks for giving me my new sig!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-142923>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=142858)

> OMG Yew... that was a TON of information....lol you are on speed
> Interesting stuff.. languages and words for sure! French is maybe the language I think is the most beautiful........ but I cannot speak it. Maybe I will one day!
> I once went to a Chinese course, in Finland in my late teens.... but I quit after a month or so because everyone could speak some and i could nothing and it was just a bit hard to hang on
> but I learned some words that I still remember ! I though Chinese was funny to learn... and totally hard to sing in Chinese IMO  because each word has to be "sung" in a different tone, like a word can mean 4 different things depending on how you pronounce it.. so how on earth can you make a melody...? pretty hard to me! lol
>
> I have never been good in learning languages. All languages I know I have gotten "for free". just from living and hearing it around. To sit and learn from a book have never been easy for me.....

Nah, I'm on the beach often, and finally fixing my DHA-steal syndrome by being in Cairns instead of Melbourne.

Oh yeah, Chinese is definitely a language which you have to sing. Makes for some interesting conversations, and definitely different dialects bring with it a different vibe when spoken (Cantonese and Hokkien are f\*\*\*ing good languages for swearing ).

\---

Relatedly, I found a pic from 11 years ago (when I was 12 years old) when I used to write loads of Chinese Calligraphy. The large 舞 character is written by me, which basically means "To Dance". The words on the left side are 有維十二歲 (Yew-wei, 12 years old) followed by a inkstamp (chinese version of a signature).

The words that I am in the process of writing in the picture are 浩然正氣 , which literally mean "magnanimous and steadfast composure".

I tried to write that word again this year with a brush pen instead of an actual brush. To be honest, I like real brushes better (but I left them all in Melbourne ) -- [http://scrivle.com/posts/do-your-dance](http://scrivle.com/posts/do-your-dance)

If the Chinese language isn't complex enough, Chinese calligraphy can get even more complex. Like most calligraphy students, I started with the standard 楷書 ("regular script"), mainly imitating the calligrapher 颜真卿 -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yan\_Zhenqing](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yan_Zhenqing) . But honestly, I like 行書 ("walking style") better -- feels more organic. I never liked writing nor looking at the official 隸書 script -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clerical\_script](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clerical_script)

Chinese is a beautiful language both when spoken and written, but honestly, it's way too old, way too hard to learn, IMO not suited for the modern environment.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-10-12T05:29:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-143574>

What the heck, let's write a quick random thought I had about the encoder before I forget it.

The "reduced sleep needs" turned out to be just me getting 45-60mins less sleep. Only started feeling the effects after about 4 days, but it's definitely a negative effect on sleep. When my dad used it in Melbourne, he reported excellent sleep.

The positive effects during wakeful activities are still very profound though. Balance and physical coordination are greatly improved. Ability to get into the zone mentally is slightly improved.

So I'm thinking what "being able to sense the native EMF field" actually means in the case of having a quartz crystal strapped to your wrist.

First off, differences between me and dad can be attributed to anything from a difference in his environmental EMF exposure, to the fact that his brain should be completely myelinated, while my 23-year-old brain is still 2-3 years off.

But anyway, I was reminded of one fact which I think was stated in the October 2013 Webinar about sleep, whereby brain cells actually need to lose energy and swell in order to transfer information and store memories. Kinda similar to the mechanism by which we wake up using cortisol to cause cell swelling, we see again that negative feedback mechanisms are present in everything from an intracellular level (eg: ROS and RNS signalling) to inter-cellular communication, to global viral epidemics.

This is totally unsubstantiated and is just a hunch, but I'm thinking that the crystals in the encoder help us sense and respond to the environment by un-condensing our cells. When this is done when you're awake, it is essentially a hormetic effect at the cellular level.

Maybe you are more sensitive to gravity (which is EMF), and therefore your collagen network gets "more disrupted" by changes in EMF strength when you have this crystal on. More disruption implies more counterbalance measures and therefore a stronger response from the entire system (muscular, fascia, nervous system, etc ..) which manifests itself as a better sense of balance. Over time, this leads to structural improvements in the system, provided that it is capable of mounting those responses in the first place.

So these crystals would only work if your body could respond to these increased stressors in a constructive way. Maybe this is a bad idea for people with trashed redox potentials.

That might also explain why this would suck out my ability to sleep well, since the crystal would act as an un-condensing force against the vortices in the brain that are trying to condense and cool water during sleep.

Another theory as to why this isn't good for sleep is that some frequencies are enhanced more so than others. So for example, the encoder may promote alpha waves in the brain, but not the delta waves needed for deep sleep. There may even be interference.

Enough random thoughts. Time to continue with the testing, though my hunch now is that the encoder is a "wakefulness enhancing device" that has negative impact in the sleep state.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-10-16T23:59:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-144048>

**Morning Fasted Exercise
**
Going to disobey the "don't do morning exercise" rule and start doing some high-intensity circuits in the morning.

This is based off the observation that every time I get morning fasted training done, my problem with feet enema goes away and stays away for most of the day. Usually it would start good and then by 4-5pm I'd start to notice some pitting enema.

I suspect my collagen network is strong enough to handle morning exercise at this point. I'm also ensuring that I wait until sunrise to actually get outside and swing around my kettlebell, so some of the red light is good. I also tend to put the Vielight 810 to use once I wake up for the added red light boost.

Favourite kettlebell circuit right now is a modified version of the 'Man Maker' by Keith Weber -- [http://weberphysiotherapy.ca/](http://weberphysiotherapy.ca/)

Original is:

-   10 thrusters per side
-   10 clean and press per side
-   10 overhead squat per side
-   10 windmills per side
-   10 snatches per side

It's modified because:

-   I think high rep windmills are too technical for me in a fatigued state
-   My mechanics mean that the overhead squat puts a lot of strain on my shoulder (which has to be held very far back)

Therefore I'm doing this instead:

-   10 thrusters per side
-   10 clean and press per side
-   10 squats per side (holding the kettlebell in the rack position)
-   10 snatches per side
-   10 high pulls per side -- [http://www.maxwellsc.com/articles.cfm?art\_id=3130](http://www.maxwellsc.com/articles.cfm?art_id=3130)
-   finish with 20 pushups

Currently takes me just over 6 mins with a 24kg bell, so it's a pretty quick workout.

That sounds great! Amazing how people think that the fish roe is "trash to be discarded".

I can't find fish roe up here in Cairns unfortunately, especially since we're just out of spawning season (usually in the middle of the year here). Oh well, I just have to deal with DHA-laden mackerel for now.

While Melbourne is definitely a crowded high-EMF mess, the fact that there are so many people means that lots of fish come in from all over Australia. It's perpetual spawning season so long as you're not picky about the type of fish roe that you get

But anyway, I don't mind anyway. Any amount of DHA I seemed to eat didn't seem to keep up with the rate of DHA depletion that I experienced down in Melbourne. And this was me eating literally 500g of either oysters, fish roe, or other high DHA food every day for months on end to no avail. Again, environment matters so much.

Sidenote: I'm still pondering why the Japanese don't get the level of DHA depletion that I had -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-24#post-141706](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-24#post-141706) . Sure they eat DHA, but I was eating more DHA, exposing myself to less non-native EMF compared to most of the Tokyo-ites, and still not improving. There is something about the Magnetism in that Earthquake-prone region called Japan that is helping them somehow.​

### yewwei.tan — 2014-10-18T02:02:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-144141>

**Change to Morning Exercise for my n=1
**
This morning I somehow managed to tweak my back a little while getting up from a squat position. I didn't do any weight bearing exercises either.

The location of the twinge was exactly where I had my disc herniation about 4 years ago now -- right side of L3-L4. Right now (2hrs post), it's a dull ache which hurts when I bend too deep on my right side. Left side is completely fine though.

SI joint was completely locked up after this, with the right side sitting higher than the left. Had to do lots of myofascia release on the glutes to get it to unlock, and then re-adjust it back to neutral. If I had my inversion table I would be hanging from it right now.

This is a good early warning sign that my collagen networks still can't put up with excess stress. I'm pretty what precipitated this incident was my foray into practicing kettlebell windmills these last few days. From now on, I'll flag that movement as a high-risk movement for my build and current redox status. I'll probably only ever re-visit it when my collagen redox is really good (ie: can run a marathon without training)

Not going to stop morning fasted exercise, because I'm seeing great systemic effects on a day-by-day basis. Edema in the feet is markedly reduced, and skin quality is much better (used to get dry skin on the feet at times).

Also, it seems that the higher intensity I can go (as measured by max heart rate), the better the effect.

What I do need to do now is to:

-   only do bodyweight training in the morning -- this will be core strengthening type exercises (ie: yoga), and then bodyweight circuits to get the heart rate up as needed (burpees, bodyweight squats, mountain climbers, etc ...)
-   shift any weight training to the afternoon when the collagen network is more tensile
-   avoid any high risk movements for my body type and current level of flexibility (another way to say collagen tensegrity)
-   high fat diet (>80%) for at least a couple of days

\----
**Total sidenote: on the word "Tensegrity"
**
@[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) always says "Tensegrity" as 'tense-gri-ty' (3 syllables). I've always pronounced it as ten-se-gri-ty (4 syllables).

The 'ten-se' in my mind always seems to denote 'Tensile Strength', which is basically the ability of a material to withstand being stretched -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ultimate\_tensile\_strength](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ultimate_tensile_strength) . This makes sense for piezoelectric collagen of course, since it being stretched and deformed is precisely what generates charge across those structures

Therefore 'Tensegrity' meant "Tensile Strength Integrity" white denotes "ability to maintain and enhance the tensile strength of the tissues of the body"

### yewwei.tan — 2014-10-22T01:26:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-144650>

Oh no, looks like I've got a new productivity tool  thanks to @[Josh](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/8791/)

Rainsong S-OM1000N2. Bought from Big Music in Sydney. It's a full carbon guitar. Only thing that isn't carbon I think are the ivory neck and body bridges, and metal truss rod.

I immediately switched up the strings for 0.009 inch Ernie Ball strings (super slinky, low E string is 0.042 inch), and lengthened the truss rod to give as low a fretboard clearance as possible.

Plays very (very!) well. Almost feels like an electric guitar, and sounds very good too. No buzzing despite the low action. Carbon body means that this guy is going to last a long time

>
> Plays very (very!) well. Almost feels like an electric guitar, and sounds very good too. No buzzing despite the low action. Carbon body means that this guy is going to last a long time
>
> [View attachment 995](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/995/)

If you really want the full acoustic sound while playing solo, pullout the fishman controller and just put a grill there. It opens the sound up when playing unplugged. I put a passive pickup in mine, or you could keep the wire for the under the saddle pickup and wire it to the jack for use with an external amp. You could mike the soundhole to get the dual mike experience and use an external controller and or amp. I never plug mine in and love the extra sound that comes out of the side where the controller was. I just put Martin Ultralights SP's on mine: 0.47-0.10. The owner told me the sound would not change in 150 years. I had mine without a case in a river valley in India for 4 years in an unheated/uncooled room with no issues. I have the older one without the truss rod. Enjoy!

### yewwei.tan — 2014-10-24T12:47:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-144939>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=144663)
>
> If you really want the full acoustic sound while playing solo, pullout the fishman controller and just put a grill there. It opens the sound up when playing unplugged. I put a passive pickup in mine, or you could keep the wire for the under the saddle pickup and wire it to the jack for use with an external amp. You could mike the soundhole to get the dual mike experience and use an external controller and or amp. I never plug mine in and love the extra sound that comes out of the side where the controller was. I just put Martin Ultralights SP's on mine: 0.47-0.10. The owner told me the sound would not change in 150 years. I had mine without a case in a river valley in India for 4 years in an unheated/uncooled room with no issues. I have the older one without the truss rod. Enjoy!

Now that I've got a few days with it, I can definitely say that I'm enjoying both the feel and the sound. Probably will keep it in it's current state for now.

Picking technique definitely has to change on the acoustic though. I usually pick pretty similarly to Michael Angelo Batio when playing the acoustic -- last finger on the guitar body, 40 deg picking angle, 90 deg thumb bend to modulate picking angle by extending/retracting the thumb. The acoustic is a lot more unforgiving when it comes to the tone, so I almost need to pick nearly parallel to the strings if I want to sound the high notes well when picking fast. Had to revive my old picking technique that I picked up from George Benson and also used by Tosin Abasi, which is a completely arched thumb.

Anyway, all technicalities aside, it's fun playing acoustic again.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-10-28T01:11:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-145358>

Bleh, still not back to optimal after being on the bike for 2+hours in a fasted state on Saturday. The ride itself was easy since I'm pretty fat adapted at this point, but the aftermath was a lack of mental energy, a reduction, and a worsening of my feet issues again. Endurance exercise seriously demands a lot of electrons from you

4 days and lots of fish and sunshine later, the brain issues are all fixed, but the collagen issues in my feet aren't still where they were before I took this ride. Another reminder that I'm not as healthy as I'd like to think, and I that I need to make much smarter exercise choices. No more hard endurance exercise until I'm well.  If I have to cycle to buy groceries, it's going to be easy pedalling for 20 mins.

Random: cliff diving must be the best exercise ever. Sprint/Climb up to a high place, dive into the water, laze around for a minute or two in the (electron dense) water, and then repeat.

Then the title of best likes-to-posts ratio shall me mine! . Right now at 327/525 = 62.2%

\---

**Random
**
I'm hungrier today . Major difference is 2hours of almost full-body sun exposure while on the beach. That's probably an indicator of higher FFA oxidation as a result of the grounding and sun exposure.

Brain is working well though. Seems like this is always the first system to refill for me  (lucky me?). Managed to get some important questions answered in today's Q&A too, so my brain is obviously in a state to make the right connections.

Took off the superhuman encoder while trying to recover from this latest bout of messy unwound collagen. If my thoughts in this previous post is in the ballpark, then wearing that thing could put me at risk of "overcharge" within unwound collagen fibers that can't handle the issue -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-26#post-143574](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-26#post-143574)

Every time I partake in major electron-robbing activities, it really does take at least a week to recover. Once you're healthy, you're a brick wall that doesn't crumble unless it's slammed into really hard, but when you're trying to rebuild that wall, the damage threshold is way lower, and you never know what exactly causes a collapse. ie: strike the top-most brick, and all you need to do is put that single brick back in place. Strike a foundation brick, and you'll have to rebuild a whole section of the wall.

Figuring out the appropriate damage threshold has been a complete hit-and-miss endeavour these past 2 years. I think I'll just have to go back to militant Optimal Quantum Health mode until my redox potential is strong enough to sustain a big hit.

More worrisome is that I had no indication that this bout of endurance exercise would be beyond the damage threshold. I was recovering very well just before that ride, feet collagen issues almost gone, etc .... I was feeling good during the ride, I felt fine after the ride, and then the next day I was shot ..... Too bad, the only way to learn from a lesson like that is to actually suffer the consequences and then become very cautious of any symptoms that signal problems arising. (My redox potential measurement resolution must be on the order of hours and not days).

Fortunately for me, my peripheral collagen is always the first to fail, and I can pretty easily track metrics like feet soreness, presence of edema (big issue for me), etc ... all on a minute by minute basis. That logical approach, along with my gut feeling of the situation, should eventually help me intuit the right course of action.

Sidenote: **You gotta get lucky to work hard. You can't work hard to get lucky.** Oh well, looks like I don't have permission to push myself physically speaking like someone like Danco3636. Just have to count my blessings and think hard with the gift of a good frontal cortex redox potential.​

As expecting from something like this, I gained 1kg over the last 3 days as cells swell and become less energy efficient. This came along with some minor gut issues. Probably will drop off the weight in 4-5 days as redox potential restores itself, but again, fair warning that mass is controlled by energy.

A lesson I learnt from Nassim Nicholas Taleb:

Man can strive for Money, Knowledge, or Love.

Pick two.

There be dragons at the intersection of the three.
​

Between trying to fix my money issues once and for all, and learning all the skills and new knowledge that this world has to offer, I can't seem to find the time to pursue what a young man naturally would .... ### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-02T01:43:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-145899>

**Risk Assessment
**
Finally got a chance to fully listen to the October 2014 Q&A, which got me re-thinking my own personal risks.

It seems like my only real risks right now are Blue Light and Food.

My nnEMF scenario is far better now than it was in Melbourne thanks to the low population density and higher latitude. With the Ozone Hole finally starting to get smaller, the impact from that should also start decreasing in the following weeks. No WiFi in the house, and the largest source of nnEMF is my computer. Measurements of nnEMF in my house definitely put it much lower than it was in Melbourne (<0.001 mW/cm2 in RF range, and <0.01mG in the ELF range).

Blue light is a major risk, since I rely on the computer for so much of my activities. I'm starting up another software business right now, which means that I work however long (or short) as I want. I can easily say that my all-or-nothing obsessive personality has been the greatest tool in acquiring knowledge and getting stuff done, but it has also been responsible for all of my existing health issues.

With the way my body is built, it seems that my brain is a hog for any DHA or electrons that I can put in. In many ways, this is a very good thing, because my brain is the last to fail; I can think myself out of a bad situation. On the other hand, it also makes me less aware of just how much the rest of my collagen structures are suffering as they get their energy stolen by the brain. The result of overworking myself for the last 4 years while staring at a computer screen has led to really shitty peripheral collagen and a weak gut.

I've fixed most of the other energy sapping issues in my life, and blue light is the last to fall. Time to commit to sealing off that leak.

I have to bring up food, not as a cure for my issues, but to prevent any additional harm that it can cause. Again, a good diet isn't necessarily going to cure my disease, but a bad diet can sure destroy my health. Time and time again I prove to be sensitive to this aspect, so the diet has to be pretty strict Epi-paleo EPCOTx, especially when I engage in compromising activities like endurance exercise or excess work-related stress.

At the times when diet cannot be ideal, I suspect that keeping calories low is the right fix. First off, that means eating less of the shitty food, but it also means intermittent fasting (quorum sensing tool), and also having more free FFAs despite carbohydrate consumption. No binges on shitty food that's for sure (oyster binges are welcome of course).

\----
**Blue Light Mitigation**

The only way to reconcile my goals of starting a successful software business and getting less blue light is to become more productive, and spend less overall time on the computer to achieve the same result.

I'm going to put this 2011 article from Ryan Carson into practice (on working 4 days a week as a Company policy) -- [http://qz.com/197060/to-grow-your-company-and-make-millions-start-working-four-days-a-week/](http://qz.com/197060/to-grow-your-company-and-make-millions-start-working-four-days-a-week/) . Work days are now officially restricted to the Monday-Thursday interval. This leaves 3 days free to do any energy-recharging activities while avoiding blue light.

Work hour modifications also need to be made in light of the new information about sunlight in Tensegrity #7. On a high level, my schedule will probably look like:

-   Wake at 6am, head out to the beach
-   Work from 7am to 10am
-   Get out into nature (and the sun) from 10am to 12pm (also good for subconsciously mulling over work done prior, making the next work block more productive without having to stare at a screen)
-   Work again from 12pm to 3-4pm
-   After that 2nd work block, exercise, get out into nature, etc ....
-   Limit computer activity massively after sunset

\----
**Random Sunday Bullshit
**
I am as typical "hairless Asian who can't hold down his alcohol" as it gets ........

I think I get drunk after 2 glasses of wine, and then take a whole day to recover. Even caroline was fine after multiple glasses . I guess it's a good thing, since it discourages electron-robbing behaviour.

Tried to grow my beard out for a MONTH, all I get are these little barely-visible streaks. Forget about Movember ([http://au.movember.com/](http://au.movember.com/)), I can never pull off a 'stache. At least I got double eyelids (google "double eye lid surgery" to see the mad obsession over cosmetic surgery in the Asian community)

### Lahelada — 2014-11-02T14:13:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-145917>

Lol,you made me laugh with the Nassim Taleb quote. No mean feat this morning so thank you ! I am working on the K and L word whjch I have in my life ,the K being work in progress. The M
intersection is miles away.
. Consoling myself with the idea that dragons are scary...

Regarding off plan diet I can possibly give you my experience.I just lived a period of hicarb lo protein hi fat diet,not by choice at least initially until the gut critters were multiplying unfavourably again. What helped me is intermittent fasting and as careful selection of carbs as possible. Once back on track I lost my appetite for fish completely again. I never had much of that in the first place.So watch what you are craving.Some is telling you something is healthy for you,others is a majority vote by unbalanced gut critters.

A pleasure to read your journal and your blog as always. Don't worry about the young lady. She is out there. Waiting for you to build your happy place so that she can join you,maybe. That way you will not need to make allowances for different lifestyles but find someone who fits in.
Beard or no beard you are a goodlooking young man,smart to boot.Keep plodding away you will get there.

> Beard or no beard you are a goodlooking young man,smart to boot.Keep plodding away you will get there.

 Sending you an e-hug from Cairns to wherever you may be sailing right now.

Total geeky sidenotes:

When I wrote about dragons, I was thinking about a few things.

Obviously "here be dragons" is a play on the medieval saying -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Here\_be\_dragons](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Here_be_dragons)

Then I always think of the infamous "Dragon Book" by Aho and Ullman -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compilers:\_Principles,\_Techniques,\_and\_Tools](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compilers:_Principles,_Techniques,_and_Tools) . This was supposed to be the bible of how to build a computer compiler. I got through maybe 200 pages before giving up , and going for the much easier to understand 'Parsing Techniques' -- [http://www.amazon.com/Parsing-Techniques-Practical-Monographs-Computer/dp/1441919015](http://www.amazon.com/Parsing-Techniques-Practical-Monographs-Computer/dp/1441919015)

After trudging through those Parsing books for 8 weeks, I proceeded to use basically none of that knowledge thus far. Robert Heinlein was right when he said that specialisation is for insects.

Then I'm always reminded of the traditional Chinese saying: 望子成龙，望女成凤

"Wish for your son to become a dragon, Wish for your daughter to become a Phoenix". There is lots of symbolism behind each creature, but I think a fair generalisation is to say that the dragon represents strength and authority, while the Phoenix represents an everlasting capacity (for beauty, love, etc ....)

I've always thought, "Why can't I have both? I want my partner to be a dragon with the head of a phoenix that always rises from the ashes" ​

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=145956)
>
> well.... there is something special about falling in love at ripe age............... it is better
> I agree Yew is a very sexy dude........ uh! And that beard of his looks very sexy too........ mmmm

My ego has been stroked far too much today

I'll have to trust my elder for good judgment here , and expect some great times to come as I age. Though I suspect it's probably more accurate to say that the amount and variety of love you receive is directly proportionate to the amount and variety which you can put out -- a skill that takes time to develop.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-03T09:13:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-146008>

It occurred to me that given that all my problems are collagen related, I probably don't have an issue of a lack of DHA. My cognitive function and other parameters related to any neuroectoderm-derived tissues (skin included) is pretty solid. On top of that, I've been dosing pretty high amounts of DHA for quite some time now. We're talking >3g a day for at least a year. I've definitely seen cognitive improvements, but have not improved the collagen in my feet that much.

Reading EE12 again makes me think that my problem will be better solved by adding more cysteine into my system -- [http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-12-battery-charged/](http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-12-battery-charged/)

TBH, I haven't been eating a whole lot of cysteine rich sources of protein. Had a look at nutritiondata.com for foods high in Cysteine (assuming that you trust the numbers reported by that site of course):

-   Mackerel only has about **200mg of cysteine per 100g** -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4072/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4072/2)

-   Prawns have about **220mg of cysteine per 100g** \-- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4172/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4172/2)

-   Farmed Atlantic Salmon (the type that we get here in Australia) has 2**20mg cysteine per 100g** \-- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4258/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4258/2)

-   Wild Salmon is about the same (**200mg cysteine per 100g**) -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4102/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4102/2)

-   A lean cut of pork (loin) has about **270mg cysteine per 100g** -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/pork-products/2115/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/pork-products/2115/2)

-   Chicken is actually not too bad in the cysteine department, **270mg per 100g** \-- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/poultry-products/650/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/poultry-products/650/2)

-   A lean cut of beef (top round) has about **290mg cysteine per 100g** -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/beef-products/7468/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/beef-products/7468/2)

-   Eggs are really good, about **290mg cysteine per 100g** (1 egg has about 50-55g edible portion, so 4 eggs would deliver close to 600mg of cysteine) -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/dairy-and-egg-products/117/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/dairy-and-egg-products/117/2)

-   Quail is surprisingly good, **380mg cysteine per 100g**, but I can forget about getting it anytime soon -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/poultry-products/797/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/poultry-products/797/2)

-   Wild Duck has **300mg per 100g** \-- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/poultry-products/784/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/poultry-products/784/2)

-   As expected, fish roe clocks in high at **390mg per 100**g -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4098/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4098/2)

Right now most days I'd probably get anywhere between 300-600g of a protein source (measured raw). If that's anywhere from 600mg to 1200mg of cysteine if it's all from my common seafood sources. I probably need to switch things up.

\----

On another note, after 5 weeks, the early-eating experiment, where I concentrate my eating early into the day, doesn't seem to be yielding any positive or negative effect. Time to switch things up.

\----

So I'll probably run with 2 interventions for the next 4 weeks:

-   Eat at least 500g (raw) of protein sources a day. This would typically deliver 120g of protein. Always make this a 50:50 split between seafood and a higher cysteine source.

    Example: 200g of mackerel and 300g of lean pork would deliver 2500mg of DHA and 1200mg of cysteine

    Example: 3 cans of sardines (about 300g) and 8 eggs (400g edible) would deliver 2000mg of DHA and about 1800mg of cysteine

    The goal is to bump my cysteine intake up by 40-50% through means of food. I should ensure that I hit at least 1200mg of cysteine a day.

-   Shift my eating window to the window of maximal protein synthesis. This would likely mean eating anywhere from 3-6pm, given that sunrise is now 5:45am and sunset is 6:20pm

Only 4 weeks for now because I'm going to Japan for 2 weeks after that (carbs galore ).

Probably will hop back on to the measures above once I get back though.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-11-04T04:55:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-146072>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=145899)
>
> **Risk Assessment
> **
> Finally got a chance to fully listen to the October 2014 Q&A, which got me re-thinking my own personal risks.
>
> It seems like my only real risks right now are Blue Light and Food.
>
> My nnEMF scenario is far better now than it was in Melbourne thanks to the low population density and higher latitude. With the Ozone Hole finally starting to get smaller, the impact from that should also start decreasing in the following weeks. No WiFi in the house, and the largest source of nnEMF is my computer. Measurements of nnEMF in my house definitely put it much lower than it was in Melbourne (<0.001 mW/cm2 in RF range, and <0.01mG in the ELF range).
>
> Blue light is a major risk, since I rely on the computer for so much of my activities. I'm starting up another software business right now, which means that I work however long (or short) as I want. I can easily say that my all-or-nothing obsessive personality has been the greatest tool in acquiring knowledge and getting stuff done, but it has also been responsible for all of my existing health issues.
>
> With the way my body is built, it seems that my brain is a hog for any DHA or electrons that I can put in. In many ways, this is a very good thing, because my brain is the last to fail; I can think myself out of a bad situation. On the other hand, it also makes me less aware of just how much the rest of my collagen structures are suffering as they get their energy stolen by the brain. The result of overworking myself for the last 4 years while staring at a computer screen has led to really shitty peripheral collagen and a weak gut.
>
> I've fixed most of the other energy sapping issues in my life, and blue light is the last to fall. Time to commit to sealing off that leak.
>
> I have to bring up food, not as a cure for my issues, but to prevent any additional harm that it can cause. Again, a good diet isn't necessarily going to cure my disease, but a bad diet can sure destroy my health. Time and time again I prove to be sensitive to this aspect, so the diet has to be pretty strict Epi-paleo EPCOTx, especially when I engage in compromising activities like endurance exercise or excess work-related stress.
>
> At the times when diet cannot be ideal, I suspect that keeping calories low is the right fix. First off, that means eating less of the shitty food, but it also means intermittent fasting (quorum sensing tool), and also having more free FFAs despite carbohydrate consumption. No binges on shitty food that's for sure (oyster binges are welcome of course).
>
> \----
> **Blue Light Mitigation**
>
> The only way to reconcile my goals of starting a successful software business and getting less blue light is to become more productive, and spend less overall time on the computer to achieve the same result.
>
> I'm going to put this 2011 article from Ryan Carson into practice (on working 4 days a week as a Company policy) -- [http://qz.com/197060/to-grow-your-company-and-make-millions-start-working-four-days-a-week/](http://qz.com/197060/to-grow-your-company-and-make-millions-start-working-four-days-a-week/) . Work days are now officially restricted to the Monday-Thursday interval. This leaves 3 days free to do any energy-recharging activities while avoiding blue light.
>
> Work hour modifications also need to be made in light of the new information about sunlight in Tensegrity #7. On a high level, my schedule will probably look like:
>
> -   Wake at 6am, head out to the beach
> -   Work from 7am to 10am
> -   Get out into nature (and the sun) from 10am to 12pm (also good for subconsciously mulling over work done prior, making the next work block more productive without having to stare at a screen)
> -   Work again from 12pm to 3-4pm
> -   After that 2nd work block, exercise, get out into nature, etc ....
> -   Limit computer activity massively after sunset
>
> \----
> **Random Sunday Bullshit
> **
> I am as typical "hairless Asian who can't hold down his alcohol" as it gets ........
>
> I think I get drunk after 2 glasses of wine, and then take a whole day to recover. Even caroline was fine after multiple glasses . I guess it's a good thing, since it discourages electron-robbing behaviour.
>
> Tried to grow my beard out for a MONTH, all I get are these little barely-visible streaks. Forget about Movember ([http://au.movember.com/](http://au.movember.com/)), I can never pull off a 'stache. At least I got double eyelids (google "double eye lid surgery" to see the mad obsession over cosmetic surgery in the Asian community)

Ha, I'm gonna go full Otaku mode!  Akihabara here I come. I'm certain I'm gonna blow $200 to get the full 'Tokyo Ghoul' Tankobon collection . Maybe even pick up some of the Naruto tankobons now that come next week, it's closing out a 700 chapter run spanning 15+ years (you can literally say that I grew up reading this comic)

(I've got Track 3 'A-0pictures' and Track 6 'アZ-Kat' from the 'Aldnoah Zero' soundtrack on continuous loop all day while coding)

\----
**Bringing Up Japan Again (Loooooooong read )
**
I stated my amazement with the 4% obesity rate in Japan here -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-24#post-141698](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-24#post-141698) . Smoking, Blue light, huge EMF risks, etc .... really bad behaviour from a Quantum health perspective, and yet they didn't suffer as much as I did when I was still in Melbourne.

At the time, Jack claimed that it showed the importance of DHA. My sceptical response here -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-24#post-141816](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-24#post-141816)

I said that it can't be just a DHA story (Jack didn't deny it either -- usually means he has something to hide). There is something about the magnetic effects of the region we call Japan that allows its denizens to have favourable energetics somehow.

As usual, it's a case of not having enough information at the time. Now that I've uploaded some of the Electric Universe (EU) perspective into my head, some new hunches are brewing. My comments in the 'Biohacking Eye Color' thread show my thought progression -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/bio-hacking-eye-color.12033/page-2#post-145988](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/bio-hacking-eye-color.12033/page-2#post-145988)

So here's a statement that probably isn't controversial here: Gravity really matters in yoking our bodies to circadian rhythms. Yoked circadian rhythms => leptin sensitivity.

In the EU model, gravity is nothing more than electrostatic attraction between the particles that make up bodies of matter (these particles form aligned dipoles) -- [http://www.holoscience.com/wp/electric-gravity-in-an-electric-universe/](http://www.holoscience.com/wp/electric-gravity-in-an-electric-universe/) .

I was also looking at some basic Japanese diabetes figures vs the USA (assuming those figures are accurate):

-   Japan 2007 figures -- [https://www.med.or.jp/english/journal/pdf/2010\_01/036\_040.pdf](https://www.med.or.jp/english/journal/pdf/2010_01/036_040.pdf)

-   USA 2012 figures -- [http://www.cdc.gov/diabetes/pubs/statsreport14/national-diabetes-report-web.pdf](http://www.cdc.gov/diabetes/pubs/statsreport14/national-diabetes-report-web.pdf)

To me, it looks like Japan suffers from similar diabetes rates as the US and many other diabetes afflicted nations, but onset only happens after you hit 40 years of age, whereas rates of teenage and young adult diabetes are much higher in other countries. For some reason this feels like the ability to recycle proteins is enhanced somehow in the Japanese population.

**And here's my hunch (and where I being talking out of my ass )**: Electrostatic forces are not heavily affected by non-native electromagnetic forces, at least not when it matters.

TL;DR -- strong native magnetic fields makes you more sensitive to gravity. Being sensitive to gravity corresponds to circadian sensitivity, which is the basis of leptin sensitivity and mitochondrial wellness.​

What I'm referring to by "when it matters", is of course the period that we are asleep.

Tensegrity #4 talks about how Melatonin condenses cells to allow them to become "more sensitive to microgravity" -- [http://jackkruse.com/tensegrity-4-magnetism-electrons-sleep/](http://jackkruse.com/tensegrity-4-magnetism-electrons-sleep/) . Sleeping on a magnetico, or having a strong native magnetic field (like in Japan or the US Gulf), also serves to make the cell more sensitive to gravity by aligning electron spins.

"More sensitive to gravity" here means a higher likelihood to undergo phase transitions as a result of the gravitational (electrostatic) effect which circadian controllers like the sun impart upon the cell.

More concretely, it occurred to me that a cell which has all it's electrons aligned is now in a "ground state". Perturbations from external forces will disrupt this ground state by tugging on the electrons, either electrostatically, or electro-magnetically. This disruption is "information", and can be carried by water and interpreted by the cell, and by the body as a whole.

We know that the Electromagnetic force is stronger than the electrostatic force (gravity), but I also suspect that the Electrostatic force can be assumed to be independent of the electromagnetic force. ie: non-native EMF "doesn't really affect" gravity given the right circumstances.

Technically speaking, this is called "electrostatic approximation", which assumes that the magnetic environment is static. We can get this static magnetic environment by having strong native magnetic fields, which simultaneously enhances the magnetic field of the cell itself (all mitochondria are magnets). While not immune to heavy non-native EMF bombardment, I suspect that this magnetically aligned structure is more robust to those insults.

Now that your cell can accurately sense these perturbations coming from the celestial bodies that correspond to circadian cycle, your cell is now "yoked to the circadian rhythm". It knows better when to engage in more autophagy, when to turnover, etc ...

You still need electrons, and you still need raw materials like DHA, cysteine, and water, but the "blueprint for wellness" is imprinted into the cell thanks to this information transfer as a result.

Of course, this system can break down. The natural reduction in redox potential as aging sets in can diminish the local magnetic environment of a cell, and render the assumptions I made above invalid (about a static magnetic environment). Once that happens, you can't sense gravity anymore, and the subsequent decline continues because people don't know how to engage in electron-replenishing behaviour, while continuing to engage in electron-robbing behaviour.

Another important thread that I have yet to read: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...nd-magnetic-sensing-of-circadian-cycles.9584/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/cryptochromes-and-magnetic-sensing-of-circadian-cycles.9584/)​

Anyway, that's just my speculation. I'll be in Japan pretty soon, and will be eating some carbs for sure. Armed with this new knowledge, I'll definitely be making observations about how I feel and react to the trip.

>
> TBH, I haven't been eating a whole lot of cysteine rich sources of protein. Had a look at nutritiondata.com for foods high in Cysteine (assuming that you trust the numbers reported by that site of course):
>
> -   Mackerel only has about **200mg of cysteine per 100g** -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4072/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4072/2)
>
>
> -   Prawns have about **220mg of cysteine per 100g** \-- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4172/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4172/2)
>
>
> -   Farmed Atlantic Salmon (the type that we get here in Australia) has 2**20mg cysteine per 100g** \-- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4258/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4258/2)
>
>
> -   Wild Salmon is about the same (**200mg cysteine per 100g**) -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4102/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4102/2)
>
>
> -   A lean cut of pork (loin) has about **270mg cysteine per 100g** -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/pork-products/2115/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/pork-products/2115/2)
>
>
> -   Chicken is actually not too bad in the cysteine department, **270mg per 100g** \-- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/poultry-products/650/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/poultry-products/650/2)
>
>
> -   A lean cut of beef (top round) has about **290mg cysteine per 100g** -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/beef-products/7468/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/beef-products/7468/2)
>
>
> -   Eggs are really good, about **290mg cysteine per 100g** (1 egg has about 50-55g edible portion, so 4 eggs would deliver close to 600mg of cysteine) -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/dairy-and-egg-products/117/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/dairy-and-egg-products/117/2)
>
>
> -   Quail is surprisingly good, **380mg cysteine per 100g**, but I can forget about getting it anytime soon -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/poultry-products/797/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/poultry-products/797/2)
>
>
> -   Wild Duck has **300mg per 100g** \-- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/poultry-products/784/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/poultry-products/784/2)
>
>
> -   As expected, fish roe clocks in high at **390mg per 100**g -- [http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4098/2](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/finfish-and-shellfish-products/4098/2)
>
> Right now most days I'd probably get anywhere between 300-600g of a protein source (measured raw). If that's anywhere from 600mg to 1200mg of cysteine if it's all from my common seafood sources. I probably need to switch things up.

A loss of a electrons is tied to cysteine at one level you're right, but the 30,000 ft level says you're focusing on the 3 ft level thinking you're collagen is bad because you need more cysteine... Your feet is bad because of the way you're reading the information. We have two ears one mouth (thanks to jacks wife).

Yup. And what I wrote referred specifically to my personal context.

Where your body decides to put electrons is very important. I can pretty safely say by now that I'm one of the lucky few people whose body actually steals from my peripheral tissues and gives to the brain rather than the other way round (which is more common).

My symptoms when faced with energy-depleting stressors are always unwinding of the collagen in my feet then hands, followed by symptoms of leaky gut. This is congruent with poor glutathione recyling, as elaborated by the EE12 -- [http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-12-battery-charged/](http://jackkruse.com/energy-epigenetics-12-battery-charged/)

My brain functions pretty well, my skin doesn't burn from lots of sun exposure, etc .... any nervous system function, which we would expect to deteriorate quickly given a DHA deficiency.

We should also be reminded that DHA isn't a source of electrons per se , but rather, it is a transducer between electric and light signals, as well as a medium to create topological insulators (an information transfer mechanism). This makes it very important for directing energy supply in all tissues, but more specifically within skin and nervous sytem tissue. It plays a much lesser role in being the energy director in bone tissue for example (which uses copper and apatite to form its SQUID).

When we say nature works off a fractal energy plan, what we mean is that energy system distribution is scale independent. I subscribe to this notion, hence all my discussion about mitochondrial energy dynamics over their dual membranes as being homologous to plasma double layers in a star -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/bio-hacking-eye-color.12033/page-3#post-146077](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/bio-hacking-eye-color.12033/page-3#post-146077)

Also, more electron supply isn't always better . You still need to be able to capture those electrons, and transport them to the mitochondria and other proteins in the form of a currency which they accept (eg: charge carrier molecules for mitochondria, along with good EZ water surrounding the mitochondrial micelle). Josh has spoken about instances of "charge overload" that he sees in his medical practice, and the disrupted collagen structures that result.

I asked myself if the tissues which rely on DHA for catching electrons were functioning poorly, and the answer was generally, "Not really". So I had to look elsewhere for answers.

It's accurate to say that more electron supply across the inner mitochondrial membrane is always good. In my case, I need to ask how do I get more electrons to the collagen structures in my feet so that they remain hydrophillic, piezoelectric, and tensile?

The question is one of tradeoffs -- given a set amount of food that I can eat a day (500-600g of a raw protein source), how do I distribute that in the best way possible to help me with my personal context? The conclusion from that was to experiment with a modification to my diet to incorporate more raw material for glutathione recycling and collagen restructuring.

The decision based on the information presented above is that instead of eating the 4-5g does of DHA which I've been doing daily, I'm going to back that down to 2-3g a day, which is still a lot, and likely enough for my personal context. This will afford me the freedom to add an extra 600-900mg of cysteine a day. It's a small tweak, but I'm confident that it's based off sound principles from what I've learnt from all the optimal principles presented thus far, so I'll see where this brings me.

I should note that the dietary tweaks are just there to supply more raw materials. Other collagen restructuring activities to deliver more energy to the tissues continue to be put in place.
