> It's accurate to say that more electron supply across the inner mitochondrial membrane is always good. In my case, I need to ask how do I get more electrons to the collagen structures in my feet so that they remain hydrophillic, piezoelectric, and tensile?
>
>
> The question is one of tradeoffs -- given a set amount of food that I can eat a day (500-600g of a raw protein source), how do I distribute that in the best way possible to help me with my personal context? The conclusion from that was to experiment with a modification to my diet to incorporate more raw material for glutathione recycling and collagen restructuring.
>
> The decision based on the information presented above is that instead of eating the 4-5g does of DHA which I've been doing daily, I'm going to back that down to 2-3g a day, which is still a lot, and likely enough for my personal context. This will afford me the freedom to add an extra 600-900mg of cysteine a day. It's a small tweak, but I'm confident that it's based off sound principles from what I've learnt from all the optimal principles presented thus far, so I'll see where this brings me.
>
> I should note that the dietary tweaks are just there to supply more raw materials. Other collagen restructuring activities to deliver more energy to the tissues continue to be put in place.

Dha is used to activate autophagy (light signal, I remember specifically a protein on the inner mitochondrial membrane jack talked about that uses IR to repair and re tailor.). You need GOOD autophagy to repair you feet. This means you're dealing with excess blue light and other forms of non native emf. Dha comes from the sea, so it comes with electrons...EMF 2!!! Dha is the boat!!! Natural package... I agree with jack. I'm not saying what you're doing isn't helpful because I know it is, I was just saying you might be isolating scale. I remember jack saying that making your mitochondria magnetic removes toxins and induces glutathione recycling, one reasons cold works. And how does cold work ? Dha lets us convert light to electrical to condense our matter, not like a tree. We do have a plan for your feet!!!!!

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-04T22:27:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-146137>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=146095)
>
> one huge issue........you don't know how many holes you have in your flower pot so the amount of DHA you need is always a moving target.

More details about the progression of symptoms should explain my thinking

I've always been trying to look for causes for energetic leaks and to plug them. I couldn't do so in Melbourne, because I tried all the necessary things, but there were just too many holes. My conclusion was that it was something about the location that was too disruptive to my biology. While in Melbourne, the hand and feet collagen issues were almost consistent through this last winter.

After the move to Cairns 2 months ago, the number of holes obviously decreased dramatically, since cognitive function went up, mood improved, and the collagen issues in my hands disappeared and have not come back.

What has followed regarding my feet in the last 8 weeks has been a cycle of symptoms disappearing completely for 1 week, then I'd do something that aggravates it again, and symptoms come back, which then requires 1-2 weeks to recover from again .

The acute and sudden swings between symptoms appearing and disappearing makes me think that I likely don't have a problem with supplying electrons to those tissues. For me, it's an issue about holding the charge within the collagen structures of my feet, and about not stealing energy away from those tissues.

The 2 things that would always destroy my feet collagen in the past are either:

-   Prolonged endurance exercise spanning more than an hour, and only when I keep rating of perceived exertion above 7 or 8. This is likely just
-   Some food mediated issue (eating something that causes worsening of symptoms)

Since moving to Cairns, the food-related issues are gone. eg: Chillies don't worsen symptoms anymore, even stuff like dairy, gluten, and alcohol, which would destroy me before even with small exposure, now don't pose any issues (of course, I wouldn't go out of my way to eat those). The fixing of my gut-mediated issues, and being able to maintain that fix, seems like a good indicator that I am capable of increasing and holding charge. This is another sign that the amount of DHA I'm getting is sufficient to fix my gut -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...-training-adaptations.297/page-32#post-146135](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/page-32#post-146135)

The endurance exercise related issues still remain, as evident by my most current bout of exercise 10 days ago causing another bout of issues (which are almost recovered from now).

The things that improve my feet collagen best are:

-   Intermittent fasting
-   Higher intensity exercise, as well as shorter bouts of endurance activity (0 min)

No surprises there , both are measures that increase tissue-specific autophagy. The fact that I tolerate those measures so well, without any compromise to energy levels, sleep quality, etc .... again looks to me like I have the capacity to generate good redox, but lack the capacity to maintain that good redox (specifically in my feet).

My good exercise tolerance for anything other than prolonged endurance exercise also makes it likely that any calcium efflux issues that I do have are not very bad (at least, not until exercise hits a certain threshold).

So I need to ask again: Why am I unable to maintain charge in the collagen structures of my feet?

I don't think it's a simple matter of increasing autophagy from a DHA-mediated pathway; I still need to ensure that the recycling that is going on creates proteins specifically within my foot collagen structures that are hydrophilic and robust against redox insults.

DHA can increase autophagy on a systematic level, which should in theory, eventually "spill over" to my feet. However, my hunch is that improving direct glutathione-mediated autophagy is going to be more effective with my specific symptoms; the marginal effect of increasing DHA is lesser than the marginal effect of increasing cysteine (and thus direct gluthathione synthesis)

So what's the issue here? It's "electrified" collagen and water that's needed to fix my issues. The collagen needs to come first, and the water issue fixes itself after, so I need to think about why my collagen isn't optimal. EE12 made the cysteine-glutathione-collagen link clear, and is something that I need to explore (with continual addition of lots DHA of course, just not lots and lots of DHA at the expense of not enough cysteine )

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-07T03:31:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-146408>

Finally recovered from the last bout of feet issues. 13 day recovery time . I should have more free time this weekend though, and it's gonna be sunny again, so more beach time is warranted.

\----

**Fat Loss, BAT, Exercise, and Ubiquination
**
This note is motivated by trying to figure out what exactly happened during my last visit to Cairns back in mid June 2014. Yet again, this note is long ....

I arrived in Cairns on <16-Jun-2014> and left on 0-Jun-2014>, 15 days total. I recorded the experience in pretty good detail. Some notable points:

-   average of 2100kcal eaten per day

-   eating pattern was almost the same on every day except for <28-Jun-2014>, whereby I only ate about 800kcal in one meal.
    But the rest of the days, I woke up, ate most of my calories at breakfast (50-70% total calories) at around 6-7am, went out for the day, and ate a second small meal when I got back around 3-4pm. This second meal was usually just some protein source (eg: 3 cans of sardines)

-   Diet was high fat, moderate protein, lots of canned seafood. Carbs were eaten on 4 consecutive days -- <22-Jun-2014> to <25-Jun-2014>. around 200g from sushi on <23-Jun-2014>, and then about 80-120g on the other 3 days (bought a bunch of bananas and was eating 2-3 a day). Interestingly, this is when I noticed that I was losing a lot more fat than I expected. Whether or not the carbs was a cause of increased fat loss, or just a trigger for the common "whoosh" effect, is something that I give some thoughts below.

-   I biked around for a total of 9 days, doing around an average of 15km a day. Longest ride was about 35km (Cairns to Yorkey's knob and back). I did walk around a fair bit (2-3 hrs a day)

-   **Weight dropped from 82kg to 78kg (-4kg / 8.8lbs). Waist measurement dropped from 81cm to 78cm (-3cm / 1.2inches)**.

    This is something I just couldn't explain. I wasn't fat at all at 82kg (pic -- [http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/20140524-p.png](http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/20140524-p.png)). During my dieting attempt in mid 2011 (still in CW-thinking mode), it took me 3 months to lose the same amount of fat in Melbourne eating less than I did on average, as compared to those 2 weeks in Cairns

    I had to have some major energetic efficiency gain for this drastic fat loss to have occurred. I give some thoughts about this below.

Prominent environmental conditions at the time:

-   11 hour daylight cycle. Sunrise at around 6am, sunset just after 5pm
-   low temps of 15-16C (60F), highs of 26-28C (80F). Very sunny for 12 days, cloudy for 3 days
-   stayed at a the same place for all 15 days. It had WiFi 24/7, and I used my computer for around 4-5 hours a day with WiFi on

Naturally, since I'm back in Cairns, I tried to isolate the cause of this crazy fast fat loss over the last 6 weeks that I've actually had a place of my own and can control variables. There are 3 big differences between then and now though:

-   Temperature range is now 20C (68F) to 31C (88F).

    The colder temp would have had an impact on fat loss. How big? I don't know, and that's something I want to figure out now.

-   The ozone hole has been big these last 6 weeks, and remains large -- [http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big\_image.php?date=2014-11-01&hem=S](http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big_image.php?date=2014-11-01&hem=S) . It's only 200 dobson units in most places (should be >400), and it won't recover to the 400+ range until late November (if history is to be trusted. Ozone Hole last year on <20-Nov-2013> -- [http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big\_image.php?date=2013-11-20&hem=S](http://ozonewatch.gsfc.nasa.gov/Scripts/big_image.php?date=2013-11-20&hem=S))

    The energetic loss that this would cause is unknown to me. I know the principles behind how the ozone hole causes energetic loss. At the same time, I am doing mentally and physically better now than I was then.

-   Almost 13 hour daylight now vs 11 hour daylight then. Cairns has no daylight savings (good!). Sunrise is 5:35am, and sunset is 6:25pm.

    Sleep periods are almost the same now. But I cannot overlook the effect of 2 hours of "extra inflammation" and the way my body interprets those signals.

Any experiments I run now will be under hotter conditions

I have not been exercising as much as I did back then. This is out of precaution of all the collagen related issues that I've had and mentioned in recent posts in this log. I do suspect however, that doing as much of the right type of exercise as I can tolerate is one of the keys to both recovering the Tensegrity of my collagen structures, as well as achieve the same fat loss feat I did the last time round.

\====

Continued in next post

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-07T03:31:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-146409>

Exercise turns on genes that upregulate Irisin. With my current understanding, this is advantageous for the body because Irisin will convert WAT to BAT, and BAT creates extra IR heat that drives charge separation of water to power more exercise.

I gave a brief look at the research concerning Irisin and exercise. Nothing useful .

-   90min at 60% VO2max upregulated irisin by 20%, but 90min after exercise, it dropped back to baseline -- [https://www.thieme-connect.com/products/ejournals/abstract/10.1055/s-0033-1355381](https://www.thieme-connect.com/products/ejournals/abstract/10.1055/s-0033-1355381)

-   another study with 45min at 70% VO2max acutely increased irisin related genes, but gene expression dropped after 12 weeks (could they be getting more sensitive?) -- [http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/...sCustomisedMessage=&userIsAuthenticated=false](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/febs.12619/abstract?deniedAccessCustomisedMessage=&userIsAuthenticated=false)

-   12-week Resistance training showed no effect on irisin in untrained women -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24906447](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24906447)
-   Yet again, mixed results -- [http://jp.physoc.org/content/591/21/5393.short](http://jp.physoc.org/content/591/21/5393.short)

It feels to me that researchers do not appreciate how PGC-alpha works. The function of PGC-alpha was explained in this blog -- [http://jackkruse.com/how-to-find-your-inner-masterpiece/](http://jackkruse.com/how-to-find-your-inner-masterpiece/) .

Again, we see a gene that is heavily coupled to the environment (both internal and external to the body). **You will get poor PGC-alpha signalling if you are Leptin Resistant. Exercise won't stimulate PGC-alpha in this case.** (and I don't even think that PPQ would help either)

Sidenote: PPAR gamma and PGC-alpha are strongly coupled.

PPAR, Vit D, and RXR are strongly coupled. Bah, there goes the importance of circadian yoking once more.

Then DHA and PPAR-gamma are strongly related. I wrote about DHA and PPAR-gamma in this post -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/50-grams-or-protein-no-can-do.11940/#post-144495](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/50-grams-or-protein-no-can-do.11940/#post-144495)​

**Tensegrity #7** ([http://jackkruse.com/tensegrity-7-black-hole-sun-come/](http://jackkruse.com/tensegrity-7-black-hole-sun-come/)) **talked about PGC-alpha again**:

AMPK regulates PGC 1 alpha, which is also a signal to set off mitochondrial biogenesis. But the central link, the activation of AMKP, is to do one thing: make a mitochondria generate a superoxide signal.​

I suspect that my carb feeding in June was just another way to enhance this superoxide signal, and that my body was capable of responding favourably to that signal back in June.

**AMPK** was covered in the Intermittent Fasting blog -- [http://jackkruse.com/intermittent-fasting-and-leptin/](http://jackkruse.com/intermittent-fasting-and-leptin/)

\----

This post from Jack is very relevant here -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/50-grams-or-protein-no-can-do.11940/#post-144311](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/50-grams-or-protein-no-can-do.11940/#post-144311)

Then I quote Jack from the Quantum Health Facebook group:

[https://www.facebook.com/groups/872...d=883110075035221&offset=0&total\_comments=103](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/881516561861239/?comment_id=883110075035221&offset=0&total_comments=103)

The worse your mitochondria are the higher the fat version of the keto diet has to be.

As your mitochondria improve the lower the fat content needs to be as the protein levels can increase.

As you improve further the protein levels in your diet decrease because your ubiquination pathways improve so you don't not need to replace a lot of protein.

As you improve you begin to be able to exercise more as well and then you can add seasonal carbs back to your template. **The more you exercise the less protein you actually need.** This is another thing that the paleo kids do not understand because their "leaders" have no idea how ubiquination rates are tied to the amount of exercise you do. Ask [Bill Lagakos](https://www.facebook.com/bill.lagakos) if this is bad advice. I do not want anyone buying what I am selling. I want you to all know why I say what I do. My understanding is based upon a different scale of science.​

The appropriate exercise to do is HIIT. I will define HIIT as having bouts of exertion of at least 20 seconds long and above 90% VO2max. You're basically in pretty high glycogen utilisation at this point.

How does aerobic exercise fit in? I don't know. It clearly screws with my redox potential when I push it over an hour. At the same time, I am clearly fat adapted enough to continue riding at a solid pace (70% max HR) for a full 2 hours in a fasted state, which I just did 13 days ago. The day after that ride, I had dropped 1.2kg, which I then proceeded to gain back over the next 2 days as collagen issues re-surfaced.

It seems like I don't have an issue with energy metabolism, but something required for the redox-recovery process after a bout of endurance exercise is fundamentally broken.

This makes me think that there is something fundamental about the Ubiquitin pathways that I don't understand. It feels as though the damage from an endurance bout happens over time, and only 1-2 days post exercise do my collagen fibers suddenly decide to quit and get marked for replacement (leading to swelling and other nasty shit).

I'm reflecting on the statement '**The more you exercise the less protein you actually need**'. I also re-read Tensegrity #4 -- [http://jackkruse.com/tensegrity-4-magnetism-electrons-sleep/](http://jackkruse.com/tensegrity-4-magnetism-electrons-sleep/) .

I think my mistake when trying to recover from this last bout of endurance activity was that **I succumbed to conventional wisdom thinking, and ate more protein and more calories. What I should have done was to eat only a little bit of protein (30-50g), eat lots of fat, and keep calories reasonable instead of trying to eat more.**

Excess protein can definitely be a stressor -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...e-actually-looking-for-nad.10598/#post-122687](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/best-measure-of-ketosis-are-we-actually-looking-for-nad.10598/#post-122687) . **I suspect that one of the key functions of dietary protein is to un-condense/un-fold our own proteins through the addition of protons for cellular signalling to occur**. Insulin is just another "cellular un-condenser".

The corollary is that excess dietary protein basically pushes excess positive charge to your proteins, and excess positive charge leads to the over-active ubiquitin cycle expression that I experience only 1-2 days post exercise.

\----

I also think there is something special about the eating schedule that I naturally fell into in June. Eating a big-ass breakfast (BAB) is basically the prescription of the Leptin reset, and kept me yoked to the circadian rhythms of my location.

Then I think the small but important bolus (30-50g) of protein at the window of maximal protein synthesis actually enhanced autophagy. Eating at this window of low cortisol (a cellular un-condenser) buffered any "damaging un-condensing effect" of any protein stimulus. See these 2 threads that I started awhile ago for the explanation:

-   [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/protein-refeeds-instead-of-carb-refeeds.9295/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/protein-refeeds-instead-of-carb-refeeds.9295/)

-   [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...ly-window-of-maximal-protein-synthesis.11241/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/seasonal-variation-of-daily-window-of-maximal-protein-synthesis.11241/)

\----

**What changes should I make now? (on top of everything I'm already doing regarding DHA, cysteine, etc ...)**

-   Experiment with the same eating timing strategy I did in the past. Large-ish breakfast, exercise during bright sunshine hours, small protein feeding in the window of max protein synthesis (4-6pm now)
-   Be very wary of adding excess protein after exercise. Keep intake to less than 50g from whole foods.
-   This is something I will ease into, but I will try to slowly increase the amount of HIIT exercise I do, and be sure to keep it in the window of max protein synthesis.

I wish I could do more CT now, but I'm restricted to my cool-fat-burner/cool-gut-buster combo that's only good for about an hour a day. So there's that, and then I can become more consistent with morning dips in the ocean when the temp is still in the low 20s (celsius). Some CT is still better than nothing, and the sea gives free electrons anyway.

Experimenting with carbs is something I also want to do, but I think I will try to tweak one variable at a time for now, so no carbs as of yet.

\====

END

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-13T10:01:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-147238>

Random observations from the past week.
**\----
Hair
**
What little hair I have on my forearms and calves is starting to bleach . Some of the hairs on my forearms are literally transluscent, as they give up all their pigment thanks to all that Sun exposure I'm getting. (the calves and forearms are the areas which are most exposed of course)

I also don't think I'm imagining the fact that my head hair is growing faster than usual.

Probably signs that my body is saying "enough Vitamin D dammit!!"

\----
**Sex Drive
**
This is starting to get annoying now . Spring time mating season is real ....

\----
**Carbs and Sleep
**
Well, went for a little excursion on Monday, and probably ate 400g of carbs through the day. Mostly (70%) starches instead of sugars. Lots of potatoes.

Sleep was:

-   Fine on Monday.
-   Very poor on Tuesday. I went back to <50g carbs, and only slept 6 hours that night (waking at 3+am, >2 hours pre sunrise)
-   Got better last night (Wednesday). Woke up once, felt very hungry. No idea about the time, but got back to sleep fairly quickly, and woke up again at sunrise (just after 5:30am)

Poor sleep did not seem to affect my logical reasoning capabilities and ability to assimilate and manipulate new information. Monday and Tuesday was when I was posting all that nonsense in the Tensegrity #8 and Infrared and CT threads. When I woke up in the middle of the night, I was hungry, and thinking about the topics in those threads  (green-tinted dreams of prokaryotes floating around a hypoxic sea of spontaneously forming molybdenum-catalyzed iron-sulfur clusters .... amongst other things)

The carbs definitely affected my mood. I was a lot more impulsive, and felt very jumpy. I found it hard to focus on programming, which to me right now is "work", and not exactly fun, on top of requiring lots of dedicated focus. Instead I spent much more time obsessively reading varied research papers and pestering Jack with questions . Reward hormones were obviously affected by the carbs , at least I got something useful and creative done.

Waking up hungry and earlier than usual was a common theme. Sounds like altered cortisol signalling due to overactive VIP secretion if you ask me. All of that driven by excess blue light to the gut microflora to stimulate excess VIP, which probably led to an earlier than usual cortisol burst.

Collagen issues came back a little for one day, and then were resolved pretty quickly in a day. This should hopefully mean that the cortisol spike was sharp but transient.

As you'd expect, weight loss has halted these last 3 days. Caloric intake increased with increasing hunger. Calories are around a 2500kcal average these last 3 days.

Obviously too big a dose all of a sudden. I haven't had issues with 150-200g before.

I should also try to mix up the glucose/fructose ratios. Should also probably avoid major nightshade doses like potatoes. Eat some bananas to increase the fructose content and see how that goes

.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-19T05:19:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-147779>

OK, so like I said yesterday, I took a long-ish walk on the beach today -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...ing-the-epi-paleo-rx.12169/page-2#post-147691](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/resistance-training-while-following-the-epi-paleo-rx.12169/page-2#post-147691)

Basically biked up about 6km to Clifton Beach, walked along the Clifton beach to Palm Cove shoreline for about 3km, then turned back and returned home the same way I came. Took about 2.5hrs total, including breaks.

\----

On the way back from Palm Cove, I took a quick break under a Palm Tree. Feet were starting to ache a little.

Next to me was this dried-up and hollowed-out branch of a palm tree. You could fill it with sand, and none would leak out.

I immediately thought of using it for some weird form of meridian point tapping. It was shaped perfectly to hold in my hand, and I could hit all parts of my feet from all angles to send that piezoelectric effect to the tissues and get them stronger again. There was also a stray thought that the branch would give off a vibration in the Schumann range to further aid in the effect.

Obviously I can't say if the result is positive, but the principles I'm basing those stray speculations on are proven true, and I did feel slightly better afterward. Obviously I took the branch home with me

Dear Yew, I know you are smart clocks and I know autophagy is your favourite topic. I wanted to ask you if you could recommend what from your autophagy arsenal would be suitable for a pregnant lady. ### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-19T23:08:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-147855>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=147806)
>
> Hi Yew .....just travelling around Coff's Harbour. I really like this area. Lots of oysters!!!

 Are you actually eating the oysters

> [kovita said:](/goto/post?id=147817)
>
> Dear Yew, I know you are smart clocks and I know autophagy is your favourite topic. I wanted to ask you if you could recommend what from your autophagy arsenal would be suitable for a pregnant lady. As well I have a topic, where maybe you could through some ideas on me...imagine all blood tests in very good ranges. As for hormones... Excellent progesterone, excellent thyroid panel, lower third range estrogen, midrange testosterone, top range dheas and igf.... but still borderline low pregnenolone. Or falling asleep within max 2 min from closing eyes, but waking up during the night. Blood melatonin very low (!), blood serotonin very low. I think it all ties together in some way.

Best autophagy is from good sleep . I think Jack has recommended CT as well.

I wouldn't say that melatonin is an issue based solely on a blood test, especially since it's more likely that the blood test was done in the day time. Saliva tests are better for melatonin -- more accurate, and can be used at the relevant times.

Testing melatonin is really a pain though, since the only real way to test for the melatonin rhythm is to get one before bed, and then another one after being asleep for some time, while ensuring that artificial light exposure is kept as low as possible (amber bulbs are the only fix here ).

More important questions are that despite the fact that you're waking at night:

-   Are you getting back to sleep easily?
-   What time are you waking up? If it is 4 hours post sleep, you may already have gotten the benefits of the prolactin surge, which sets the stage for autophagy in the 2nd phase of sleep. I wrote about the 2-stage human sleep pattern here -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/is-too-much-sleep-harmful.11323/#post-134462](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/is-too-much-sleep-harmful.11323/#post-134462)
-   Are you waking up feeling refreshed?

Then the more important question is: What is the ideal signalling environment for each stage of pregnancy?

Is maximal autophagy optimal for sending signals to the foetus?
What is the appropriate levels of inflammation?
What about the season? (I asked a question about 'Human year-round mating season' vs the spring-summer season for most animals. The key still seems to be leptin sensitivity, but achieving that is going to differ based on season -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/human-year-round-mating-season-implications.11016/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/human-year-round-mating-season-implications.11016/))

I'm totally ignorant about how labs will respond while pregnant. For example: is lower plasma pregnenolone normal is this case, since you'd be shunting more of it into hormone production really quickly? (and therefore the pregnenolone measurement may be lower despite hormone signalling being normal).

Unfortunately I'll need to know more before commenting further, but I think I'll have to add this to my topics of interest anyway

..

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-19T23:33:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-147859>

**Lean, Hungry, and Performing Awesome
**
One of the things that I've been trying to explain is why my collagen tensegrity is so much better when I am almost at unhealthy levels of leanness.

I was probably 77-80kg in High School, and performed pretty damn well athletically. Examples: Bench press 100+kg, easily dunk a basketball on a 10-foot rim, could sit in full front splits, threw a 5kg shotput about 15m, 19 mins for a 5km run, etc .....

I had already reached my current height of 189cm (6'2'') by the time I was 15.5 years old. This was a picture from that period in Hong Kong -- [http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/hk-indie.png](http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/hk-indie.png)

Then I left home at 17, and got heavier through that period. Never really got fat (probably at most 15-16% body fat at one point, and mostly at 12-14%), but wasn't my <10% bodyfat level that I had in High School. That was also when my collagen issues began, though I didn't notice at the time, and only took notice around the time I was 20 years old.

As part of the body image obsession back then, I'd thought I try a hand at getting as lean as possible (just because I could). Did that through your typical leangains calorie-restriction with resistance training and a little cardio thrown in.

I did manage to get pretty lean (most would say way too skinny). 3 Pictures from that time, which was about 3 months after I turned 21 (pics are of me in my underwear, and when I still had dreadlocks ):

-   [http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/lean-front.png](http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/lean-front.png)

-   [http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/lean-back.png](http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/lean-back.png)

-   [http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/lean-abs.png](http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/lean-abs.png)

I got down to about 75kg / 165lbs still at my current height of 189cm / 6'2''. Waist measurement at navel went down to basically 76cm / 30''.

My cold tolerance was really bad, and I was pretty damn hungry, but all my prior athletic ability came back, I did not experience any of the collagen issues I'm experiencing now, and my brain and mood was pretty good. Had some basic labs taken at the time too, testosterone was decent (600+), CRP very low, HDL at the time was 89mg/dL, etc, ..... essentially my lab panel was decent.

Now that I think back, the cold and hunger issue was an independent problem -- not enough DHA. Somehow or rather forcing myself to get that lean actually improved my collagen tensegrity (of course, the fact didn't click until all the Tensegrity blogs came out here, 2 years after the fact).

Because of the hunger issues, I got back up into the 80+kg range within 6 months, but there was definitely a period of 4 months when my bodyweight was below 78kg that my collagen issues inexplicably disappeared.

I still have no clue why that's the case, but that's been one of the driving motivations to get as lean as possible using the protocols that I know now (sufficient DHA, minding light cycles, etc ....), as well as the reason I've been looking into autophagic repair mechanisms so much as of late in an attempt to explain this observation.

Regardless, as of this morning I'm sitting at 82.6kg, with a waist measurement of 80.5cm, and I think there's a calorie restriction biohack coming up in my near future

..

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-21T02:52:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-148041>

**More Random Thoughts (note: I wrote this before reading Tensegrity #9)**

The period from age 20-22 was by far the highest stress. Lots of (over)work, simultaneously doing a day job while trying to start a company of my own. Got a fair bit of cash out of that time, and took a big toll on health. This did coincide with that period when I was very lean and the collagen issues went away though ....

Right now I'm in a more optimal location, work stress is much less, there's enough resources to last awhile, the ability to acquire resources is better, and the ability to deal with stress is better. So yes, there's is much less stress now compared to then.

My gut issues started around late 2010, after what I suspect was acquiring a parasite from some kangaroo meat. That's also when my collagen issues started.

Then the gut issues persisted until I started adding massive amounts of DHA about mid last year, and then took a really positive turn after I moved out of Melbourne to Cairns.

The collagen improvement seemed to be completely independent of gut issues. It could get worse because of diet triggers, but it got better purely by virtue of the weight loss experience.

It's of course, impossible to tear apart the two, because I was obviously being calorie-restricted at the time. (almost no DHA in the diet I should mention) Fatty acid oxidation is bound to be higher.

An important detail is that I took 3 months to go from 78kg to 75kg, and my diet at the time involved huge oscillations in caloric quantity. eg: one day 1200kcal, and one day at 2700kcal. 7-day averages for caloric intake was 2200-2300kcal, which is actually not that low.

I also ate in the leangains fashion, only from about 11am to 4pm every day. Carbs were present -- 75g for 5 days of the week, and then 2 refeed days at 150-200g (consumed in a single meal). Prior to this, I had failed to lose weight at 78kg for 2 months, while trying standard dieting approaches.

Again, this definitely introduced a fasting effect, and I wish Jack would elaborate on how that involves the gut flora.

I definitely think there is a big key to quorum sensing, and the relative levels of L-cysteine and D-cysteine in the gut. Jack has said in the Nov2014 Q&A that blue light basically turns L-cysteine to D-cysteine. The isomer of cysteine would then drastically affect the function of the Lysyl oxidase (LOX) active site, which depends on these cysteine residues for its function. LOX function would then also be rate-limited by proton tunnelling as I wrote in this post -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...ing-the-epi-paleo-rx.12169/page-2#post-147678](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/resistance-training-while-following-the-epi-paleo-rx.12169/page-2#post-147678) , and the overall production of allysine to form collagen crosslinks will be limited by these factors as well.

So I'm asking questions like: How do I increase proton tunnelling? What is the right way to drive the correct isomer of cysteine for LOX function in the tissues that need it most?

Calorie restricted fasting of some sort seems to be yet another way to increase proton flow (body fat is a proton source), but I don't know if this is a way to increase proton tunnelling (what other conditions must be met?) I do suspect based on my past experience that this is the way to go though ....

.....

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-22T09:57:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-148148>

> following along Yew....... this weight loss stuff is baffling to me.
>

>
> But I feel massively clunky and chunky and beach ballish ...and not the least bit optimal.
>
> At the weight I am now - I used to be just fine ...... now I am just way too heavy for my frame and feel very uncomfortable in my skin. My body composition was just so much better after the leptin reset altho my weight didn't go down much .....and then when I dropped some K's - I felt awesome.

Hmm, I wonder if in both of your cases, if it was the case of energy loss that then caused the weight gain. That's definitely what I experienced in the past when say I did something to majorly aggravate my gut issues, and I'd immediately gain weight that was really hard to lose afterward.

I'm a bit perplexed by my case of forced weight loss to <10% body fat though, because it seems like the arrow of causality goes:

I forcibly lose weight through non-optimal means => my collagen improves
​

My cold tolerance and other factors did not improve, so I wasn't gaining energy efficiency systemically in any way. I was just somehow getting some sort of efficiency at the peripheral collagen level, and I have no clue if this is just a happy side effect, or something about the notion of excess proton supply through body fat loss that I do not understand yet.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-22T15:19:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-148164>

Our being is so full of quantum magical things just waiting for our senses to catch up to this reality. Too often we are absorbed in our own lives and fail to see we constantly find good places in dark spots. We can touch with fingers but it is strange to remember touching more than thought. But our fingers can still tingle with it the passion of our memories. We can hear tranquility in a crashing waves at sunset. We can smell passion in our lover's trail, and the taste of the obscure breeds the greatness of our intuition. Become astonished at what you are unaware of in life and those who affect your DC current in your cells. Ultimately, it is not what we don't know that hurts us.......it is what we "know" that just ain't so that does.

i know I am best between 13 and 10 percent body fat. i am 1.70cm and my optimal weight seems to be around 56 kg. I am quite muscular for a woman, partly genes and partly history of athletics. Ot is very easy for me to build muscles overnight. This is the weigth my body always seeks and comes to. I eat a lot, at least this it what people usually admire, but I had times of diet and low fat diest and my body composition was worse and my weight higher. I do not seem to be optimal on very low carb though. I do not make any countss as Yew does, but I estimate on average 75 carbs a day (real carbs that could be used) makes the level for me. This is as well something my body would ask for. I put myslef to 'eat as much and what you want' regimen now and curiously i do not seem to cross 100g a day, never and I tend to eat quite high fat and moderate protein. I always crave fish, but would eat much less of if it is not fatty. my current weight is about 59 kg at 16 w pregnancy.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-22T22:43:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-148184>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=148164)
>
> Our being is so full of quantum magical things just waiting for our senses to catch up to this reality. Too often we are absorbed in our own lives and fail to see we constantly find good places in dark spots. We can touch with fingers but it is strange to remember touching more than thought. But our fingers can still tingle with it the passion of our memories. We can hear tranquility in a crashing waves at sunset. We can smell passion in our lover's trail, and the taste of the obscure breeds the greatness of our intuition. Become astonished at what you are unaware of in life and those who affect your DC current in your cells. Ultimately, it is not what we don't know that hurts us.......it is what we "know" that just ain't so that does.

Most of the time it's Nature I despise; she let's pain consistently reprise.
Nature's contents of unknown comprise, lead most people to find solace in lies.

The lucky few can afford to be patient, because their life is sufficiently abundant.
But we all see patients looking despondent, who lack the energy to be resonant.
How do you pull people out of perpetual man-made summer?
My only answer, is for your soul to shine brighter .....

Nature weaves a symphony, we can be singers and join the harmony,
But playing an instrument is hard; years spent on mastery.
In many ways I don't quest for truth, nor do I need to be understood.
I like confusion, I don't need to know what's under the hood.
I just want to win; one feet in mud and one in the clouds ....
We can touch with fingers; but can we touch with our hearts?

I'm an artist and shaman; my job is to confuse.
To convince through emotion, that perceived comfort is no refuge.
That struggles build character, and vagaries incite connection.
The confused soul is a beauty to beyond -- one who bears a unique complexion.

To innovate we need ambiguity. To consolidate we need clarity.
In either case we need sharp thinking tools -- blades of alacrity.

Information discovery
begets information density
begets information connectivity
begets information utility
begets information harmony.

Damnit I'm high .... the only time when poetry sings to me.
Time to jump in the ocean; nature calls me to be free .....

\-----

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-27T07:37:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-148624>

Took a 6:30am flight from Cairns to Melbourne this morning. 3hr 20min total flight time, arriving at about 11am Melbourne time (1 hr ahead of Cairns thanks to daylight savings)

Drank 1.5L of water on the flight. No real preparations done since this was a relatively short flight.

I definitely saw some dry skin immediately appear in my ankles. Shows you how quickly non-native EMF effects come. No feet swelling though.

After getting back home (parent's place), I lay out in the sun for a 20min nap in the garden with maximal skin exposure. Felt a lot better after that.

\----

Flying to Tokyo on Sunday night. Let's see how I handle that

I'm definitely the most interested in how I handle the cold. Sulfur related pathways seem to be strong enough to help me avoid sunburn, but how quickly can you activate Nitric Oxide production? Can H2S be used in the cold as well? How quickly is H2S down-regulated to insignificant levels after the long-light stimulus is lost?

As usual, only an experiment will lead to the answers. Tokyo is EMF hell, but hopefully the 1C to 12C temperature range (40F to 54F) will make up for some of that damage.

Anyway, had some preliminary "cold exposure" in the 15C morning here. Still having cold hands, but yet the rest of my body burns up .... Let's see how I react to the Tokyo windy cold.

I'm also interested to see how the cold affects weight loss, given that I'm currently in summer mode.

\----

Until then, I'm loading up on the DHA. Back in Melbourne means that I'm back near the big fish markets. I just had 300g worth of fish eggs (mostly raw), and dinner is some rainbow trout

...

Yup, jetlag should not be an issue. I'm synced to UTC+10 with a 5:30am sunrise and 6:30pm sunset (almost 13hrs). Japan is UTC+9 with sunrise at 6:30am and sunset at 4:30pm (10hrs)

As for the cold, so far so good. Cold shower this morning was much easier than I remember. Water is only around 15C though.

Then did some yoga/bodyweight movements in the garden. Outside temp was about 12C, though air was pretty still, so not much wind chill. Still, my body was mostly radiating heat.

As usual, the two places that remain cold as hell are my hands and feet. Everywhere else is pumping out heat . I'll probably just accept icy hands for now, and not care so long as there isn't any frostbite risk.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-11-28T23:06:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-148731>

**Body Composition**

In Cairns, I was eating maybe 2000-2500kcal a day, with lots of NEAT, lots of beach time, and regular (but not excessive) exercise thrown in. While that got my health quotient up quite a fair bit, it did not get me any leaner, and I basically hovered around the 83kg mark for a good 3-4 weeks.

I wasn't hungry or anything, but if I tried to actively restrict food, the weight would not move, and I'd start to see energy levels dip a little, and then would add back some food again to hit a maintenance level. The environment signal seemed to favour fat storage.

So on Tuesday (5 days ago from now), I decided to try a quick refeed experiment. I would have maybe 100g of carbs in a single meal, from the most insulinogenic sources I could find. It had to be dairy plus some pure sugar for maximal insulin. So I went completely un-Epi-paleo and hand a tub of Yogurt and an Irn Bru

Weight and waist changes over the last 5 days has been:

-   <2014-11-25 Tue>: 83.3kg, 81cm
-   <2014-11-26 Wed>: 82.7kg, 81cm

-   <2014-11-27 Thu>: 82.2kg, <81cm // fly to Melbourne at 6:30am

-   <2014-11-28 Fri>: 81.6kg, 80.5cm

-   <2014-11-29 Sat>: 81.3kg, 80cm

Diet over the last 4 days (numbers are kcal amounts):

-   <2014-11-25 Tue> 3000
    \- mackerel + seaweed + herbs: 1400
    \- fat: 600
    \- yogurt + irn bru: 900
    \- misc: 100
-   <2014-11-26 Wed> 2000
    \- fat sources: 1000
    \- mackerel + seaweed + herbs + sardines: 1000
-   <2014-11-27 Thu> 1700 (back to Melbourne)
    \- nuts: 400
    \- fish eggs 300g: 600
    \- 300g trout + misc: 700
-   <2014-11-28 Fri> 3000
    \- fish eggs 300g: 600
    \- oysters 200g + seaweed + anchovies: 400
    \- sushi + dessert: 1500-2000

So I ate roughly the same, had a bunch of carbs, and lost almost 2kg (4.4lbs) and 1cm off my waist over 5 days

Notes: The lower calories on Thursday was due to my Melbourne flight, and I didn't want to eat anything until I had landed, been grounded for awhile, and actually had some good seafood on hand.​

The 2 big changes that have been made was the refeeding I did, and the cold exposure that I'm gotten since I'm back in Melbourne.

For the mechanics of how AMPk and insulin interact during re-feeding to lean you out, see -- [http://jackkruse.com/intermittent-fasting-and-leptin/](http://jackkruse.com/intermittent-fasting-and-leptin/) . Suffice to say, I'm in a position to benefit from these, and will likely to continuing this practices on a weekly basis at least.

The temperature here in Melbourne isn't exactly cold, but it's definitely enough to get a mild cold stimulus. I'm typing this while sitting shirt-off in the 14C (57F) weather. Nights have been getting down to the 12-13C range the last 2 nights. Have been doing cold showers every morning since I've been here too. Last night when I went out to dinner, it was a cool 17C (63F), and at least this time I was doing fine in a T-shirt.

See, calories are a useful metric to track "amount of food" you're eating given that your diet composition doesn't vary too much, but it does literally nothing to tell you about variations in macronutrient type and timing and the resulting effect on mitochondrial ETC to drive the rest of circadian signalling.

This makes me even more curious to see what happens when I get to Japan, and the cold stimulus becomes more significant. CT4 stated that eNOS can uncouple the SCN from light and couple it to temperature instead, which for me would mean that I would continue to see the body composition effects I'm seeing now despite eating carbs (blue light input).

\----

My hands still get cold though. Need to think about ways to improve this.

...

Blocking out some time to read Tensegrity #10 later. It should give me even more answers to the exact situation I'm facing now

...

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-02T00:41:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-148967>

**Japan Day 1
**
7:30am in Tokyo now.

Took the 11pm (UTC+11) flight from Melbourne to Narita airport in Tokyo. Flight time of 10hr 20min, arriving at 7:20am Tokyo time (UTC+9). Got 6 hours of crappy sleep on the plane.

Took the Narita Express Train from Narita Terminal 2 to Shinjuku station, then a Train on the Chuo-Sobu line in the direction of Mitaka, stopping at Koenji station in the Sugimoto ward, which is about 7-8 mins to the main city area by train. Got off and walked 10 mins to our apartment on Airbnb.

Temp yesterday was 14-15C (57-59F) most of the day, so it was T-shirt weather for sure. Walked around a fair bit (4+ hrs), and had some onigiri and tonkatsu along the way . No pics yet because the internet connection here is crappy (should get better on Friday when we're in Kyoto). Had cold hands, but otherwise I was comfortable all the way through.

\----

EMF situation in the apartment is surprisingly good. <0.01mG on the ELF readings, and <0.05mW/cm^2 on the Radio frequency readings.

Of course, given how small the apartment is, once the fridge comes on, the ELF readings go to about 0.5mG .....

\----

**Insulin shuts down uncoupling?
**
I feel colder after eating even a small meal (say 200kcal), which can be explained by falling Free Fatty Acids (FFA) levels due to a sudden spike in insulin. Less FFAs for uncoupling means less heat generation.

Of course, all the food here is predominantly carbs , so obviously I'm curious as to what would happen if I ate just fat and protein instead.

I get back to my usual cold tolerance in maybe 20 mins. Is this reflective of the ability to generate sufficient insulin resistance to keep FFAs high?

...

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-03T09:18:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-149085>

Still have a shitty internet connection, so haven't been really following the Tensegrity #10 thread, and have not listened to the December Webinar. Promise that once I get a good connection again, I'll get back on track and move the discussion forward.

**\----
Japan Day 2
**
Mainly spent running around Akihabara. Better leave this description out for now
**\-----
Japan Day 3
**
Ate too much rice today

Went to Tsukijishijo.
Had sushi at Sushi Yamazaki -- 11 piece sushi set (mackerel sushi was AMAZING!!!!!!! )
Then a stick of yaki-dango, and a stick of red-bean dango (dango are basically glutinous rice balls).
Then a stick of dashi-tamago-yaki. (sweet layered egg)
Then a sea urchin roe steamed bun.
Then a stick of Unagi (sea eel)
Then had 5 different onigiri (ingredients wrapped in rice) -- mentaiko (fish roe), ikura (salmon roe), anago (river eel), unagi (sea eel), shoyu (just soy sauce and then grilled)
Then bought some fried pregnant fish (with roe) from 7-eleven on the way back home.

I've had enough food for awhile

Got pictures of almost everything of course, but don't have the bandwidth to upload yet.

\----

Cold tolerance was pretty good today, and I was walking around Tsukijishijo in a T-shirt despite the 5C (41F) weather.

Again, after all that rice, I immediately felt really cold, and put on a jumper. Need to eat fat to keep up the fat burning!

**Japan Day 4
**
Went to Kappa-bashi, Ueno Park, and the temple down at Asakusa, then Akiba again. Ate some fried stuff , and a really good takayaki, then opted for a quick dinner from the supermarket.

Picked up a 100g bag of raw oysters, 200g of mentaiko (preserved fish roe), 100g of salted fish liver (I think ...), and a pack of natto. Got pics of course, and got a bunch of DHA too. All for about ¥1200 (most expensive was the mentaiko, which was Y680) (we can assume that ¥100 = AU$1, so AU$12.00, maybe about US$10.00)

All the supermarkets here seem to stock fresh fish for a decent price

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-06T13:36:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-149345>

**Redox Potential Drop ....**

As expected, my redox potential is starting to fall with all of this non-epi-paleo eating and blue light exposure (food and direct exposure)

As usual, first place redox potential tanks is in the peripheral tissues, specifically the tissues with the highest turnover -- the skin. Dry skin since day 4, and then yesterday my cold tolerance really tanked. Granted, it's 1C to 9C now (34F to 48F), but I was rocking a T-shirt in 5C weather with only my hands feeling slightly cold on day 3, and today, my hands are slightly swollen from the cold, have reduced dexterity, and I am actually feeling cold and shivering (uncoupling is screwed, and skin can't repel the cold anymore). Probably going to have to strategically rug up for the remainder of my trip, given that it's going to get colder in the coming week.

\-----
**Meal Timing**

Given that I'm eating all carbs now, I've tried to experiment with different carb timings.

So far the writings of Bill Lagakos which suggest that carbs earlier in the day has proven to be the most helpful in terms of cold tolerance -- [http://caloriesproper.com/afternoon-diabetes-and-nutrient-partitioning-2/](http://caloriesproper.com/afternoon-diabetes-and-nutrient-partitioning-2/)

Fasting for prolonged periods with reduced redox potential literally gives me the chills; shivering thermogenesis starts pretty strong after about 20hr of fasting.

Best bet now is probably to keep some dried seafood with me on hand for a protein dose in the morning, and then eat most of my calories before 2pm. One day

\-----

No clue if I'm getting fatter. Don't feel it though . I credit the cold and the 5-6 hours of walking every day.

Will have to spend my 7 days in Cairns before the NOLA trip mostly on the beach to restore this redox state, before trashing it again with 20+ hours of flying to get to NOLA.

\-----

First day in Osaka saw more unhealthy eats

Osaka is obsessed with Kushi Katsu (basically means fried stuff on a stick)

TBH, I don't really like fried food.

Then had some ChaShu ramen later in the day:

\----

Went for Chanko Nabe yesterday (all the ingredients are boiled in a dashi stock, and served with rice)

and also had this pork omelette which was amazingly good (don't know how they make ometlettes taste good ....)

Then had some Pork Katsu curry (fried pork with japanese style curry). Another one of those things that are insanely unhealthy for you.

And if that wasn't enough, we decided to have some rice bowls for dinner

Bro had yaki-gyu-don (charcoal grilled beef with rice)

I had a buta-don (charcoal grilled pork belly)

Both of these were amazing .....

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-10T13:43:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-149686>

Had more sushi today. About ¥6000 for all the sushi below (US$50.02 using the latest exchange rates)

Meshi-saba (steamed saba mackerel) at top, scallop at bottom

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Ikura on top (salmon roe), Uni at bottom (sea urchin roe). Salmon roe was really good. I like Uni, but I think it's a little overrated.

2nd and 3rd best items I had at this restaurant. Braised Anago (freshwater conger eel) on left, grilled Unagi (sea eel) on right.

Dashi-tamago-yaki (sweet fish stock egg)

Akami (lean tuna)

Chu-toro on top (medium fatty tuna), Top-grade Hamachi at bottom. The hamachi was the best thing of the meal. Firm texture, incredibly, incredibly complex and sweet taste. If I were to describe it, it's a mackerel taste which isn't as strong as mackerel, with the texture of salmon without the fat, with a very strong aftertaste like sardines or anchovies.

Chu-toro was melt-in-your-mouth good, but the flavour is very subtle.

Oo-toro (fatty tuna belly). Melt-in-your-mouth as expected. Very good, but I don't get the hype.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-11T01:06:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-149770>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=149690)
>
> Amazing food porn pictures Yew . Do you have time to do anything else besides eat yummy things on this trip? Hopefully you're walking around enough while sightseeing to burn off some of it!

> [Jude said:](/goto/post?id=149755)
>
> Great photos Yew...having fun... ? How's the language going?
> Looks like rice with each meal? Are you limiting carbs? How are your feet?.. sleep?

All we've been doing is walking . Definitely 4-5hrs a day.

Yesterday for example, we went out to the Osaka Aquarium in the morning, then dove back into the Umeda area and explored for a couple hours, before getting lost for another hour while trying to look for the sushi place. Today will be to Osaka castle, and then to search for this good Okonomiyaki place on foot.

Kyoto was lots of walking too. If you really want to explore the Fushimi Inari shrine, it's 3hours of walking at least ... Also went around the entire Gion area at and behind the Gion Yasaka Shrine when we had the Saba sushi, an exploration that took us 3+ hours too.

We definitely got some pics of the aquarium trip, so maybe I'll post those up when I have the time (eventually ...).

Then there were chunks of the trips, like wandering up and down buildings in Akiba, and getting lost in between yoyogi park, harajuku, and shibuya, which were not captured on camera

\----

Fortunately for me, with the exception of one meal (the Tsukemen), all eating has taken place during daylight hours. Circadian cycles shouldn't be screwed up that bad because of that.

And I think it's a lot worse than just "paleo-approved" white rice at each meal . Lots of snacks, sugar, breads, noodles, etc .....

Sleep has been very solid. Definitely at least 8 hrs a night.

So far, the 3 things that have failed are my cold tolerance, my skin (dry lips and ankles), and I'm starting to get lots of fascia tightness, especially in the low back and right calf.

Surprisingly, my feet are doing pretty well. No swelling at all. The things that I did in Cairns (and maybe the lithium and molybdenum supps I introduced about 3 weeks ago), are working to help me hold charge better .

Also surprising is that gut health (a very common problem area of mine), is still holding strong despite all the blue light from those carbs.

As expected though, the brain is still functioning really well, which at least gives me some confidence that the living arrangement that I've made in Cairns is really doing me well.

....

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-11T13:16:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-149816>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=149780)
>
> This bio hack is interesting.........to me anyway. I have some ideas of what will come.

Well, eager to talk about some of that when I'm in NOLA then

\----

Speaking of the flight. I was thinking about how I'd manage circadian cycles on the Eastward flight to NOLA.

**Cairns => Sydney** (3h 5m)
CNS 5:10 am
SYD 9:15 am

Layover: 2 h 25 m

**Sydney => Los Angeles** (13h 50m)
SYD 11:40 am
LAX 6:30 am

Layover: 6 h 1 m

**Los Angeles => New Orleans** (3h 41m)
LAX 12:31 pm
MSY 6:12 pm​

I don't think there's any possible way to make an Australia to US flight work well, but my current plan is to basically live a "30-hour day time" for the duration of my travel. I'm actually thinking that eating nothing but fat during the journey is the best idea, staying as fasted as possible, while keeping insulin as low as possible so as to not throw off lipid homeostasis.

So maybe a bolus of fat sometime during my layover between LAX and MSY, which is day time in the US (but night-time for my current biology), and then a bolus of fat when I land in MSY at night.

I'm planning to forcibly stay awake during the journey, so that by the time I hit NOLA, it's night time, and my homeostatic sleep debt will force me to bed pretty easily. I would then get a bolus of protein and fat the next morning to reset the circaidan cycle as quickly as possible. Note that I'm assuming here that resetting the circadian cycle to be sensitive to that of NOLA as quickly as possible is the healthiest route to take.

Good thing the US allows you carry in dried fish (not beef) and fats for personal use. Canister of coconut oil and some dried anchovies will be my go-to travel meals.

....

LAX 8:44 pm

Layover: 1 h 46 m

SYD 8:40 am +2 days

CNS 3:10 pm

This place is on the opposite side of Shinjuku station. Yodobashi camera is on the east side, and this place is in the basement of the "MY Shinjuku Building", which you get to by walking out the West exit of Shinjuku station and then through Keio mall.

The fish in Nihon was good, but the rice (and carbs) were plenty.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-18T10:43:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-150497>

**Back in Cairns
**
Flew out of Melbourne at 11:15am Melbourne time today, landing in Cairns at around 1:40pm (minus 1 hour in timezones). Spent some time at the beach and got some grocery shopping done, and that's about it for the day.

I'll have 6 full days of re-charging the redox battery before flying again. I wonder how NOLA will treat me health wise.

\----
**Dealing with Lost Sleep
**
So far, my rule of thumb for re-paying sleep debt has been to go to bed earlier, and never wake up later than desired.

The hypothesis there is that the rhythm of "wakefulness hormones" is what is important for circadian yoking. The "sleep hormones" can be active whenever it is dark, and sleep being the default state of life, you can initiate and prolong that cycle so long as it is dark.

Practically speaking for myself, and using the flight to NOLA as an example, I'll probably get to bed at 9+pm NOLA time after lots of flying and being awake. I'll still force myself to wake up when the sun is up the next morning at roughly 7am NOLA time. That day, I'll be active during the daylight hours, but as soon as sunset hits, I'll try to get to bed, hopefully sleeping from about 6pm all the way to 7am the next day.

\----
**Misc**

Damnit, playing the Rainsong guitar again feels good . Thanks @[Josh](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/8791/) again for the great recommendation to pick this thing up.

\----
I'll probably post up some observations from the Japan trip within the next 1-2 hours of this post.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-18T10:53:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-150498>

**The Cold
**
As expected, Japan was decently cold.

Tokyo for the first 4 days hovered anywhere in the 5-15C range (40-60F). Slight wind, and very cloudy. Cold tolerance was excellent these few days.

Headed to Kyoto after 4 days in Tokyo, spending 3 days there. Temperature range was 0-8C (32-46F) all the time. Nice and sunny though. I felt fine the first day, and then after that skin quality declined suddenly, and cold tolerance was gone. Specifically, I experienced dry skin on the lips, ankles, and forearms. Redox potential in the skin was the first to go, and logically so did cold tolerance, given that I didn't have enough charge to repel the cold.

I still had the ability to shiver to get warm, but that wasn't really that fun ... so I put on a jacket from there on out.

Went to Osaka for 4 days after Kyoto. Slightly warmer, around 2-10C (35-50F), and always sunny.

Then in Tokyo for 3 days after Osaka. These days were colder, anywhere from -1C to 10C (30-50F).

\----

I was dropping lots of water (peeing a lot) the whole time I was in Japan. I didn't necessarily feel thirsty, but any water I did drink led to a trip to the bathroom pretty quick (within an hour).

Water intake did remain pretty high through the trip. Maybe 2L in the morning, and then another 2L through the day, and then I'd make sure to drink another 1.5-2L when we got back to our acommodation.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-18T11:07:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-150499>

**Food Intake
**
The pictures I posted don't do justice to the actual amount of food consumed, since I missed many miscellaneous drinks, snacks, bentos, dried goods, etc ....

If you put a calorie count to the trip, I'd probably be anywhere as low as 2,000kcal, to easily above 5,000kcal on some days. An intuitive guess is that my calorie intake was in between 3500-4000kcal most days.

These were basically predominantly carb sources. I'd assume that I'd be getting anywhere from 80-200g of protein a day, and at least 300+ grams of carbs every single day I was there.

Activity was just 4-6 hours of easy walking every day.

All eating was done in the 7am to 5pm daylight window, except for one meal on the 2nd day of our trip, and one day in Kyoto.

No gut issues through the entire trip. I'd get larger bowel movements when I ate excessively, but bristol score always remained a good 4.

\----

I left Melbourne at 81.3kg with a 80cm waist measurement, and the last 2 days I have weighed in at 81.2kg and 80.6kg respectively, with the same 80cm waist measurement.

I feel like there has been some extra fat accumulating in the waist despite the same waist measurement, and I feel like the rest of my body looks "fuller", probably as a result of extra muscle glycogen from eating all those carbs.

\----

I will give another 4-5 days of my body eating my normal Epi-paleo diet before making conclusions about body composition changes.

However, I am pleasantly surprised that I managed to not get fat despite pigging out on completely un-optimal foods for 2 weeks. I was definitely expecting some serious mass increases as a result of the excess blue light (from food and the actual direct blue light exposure in the environment).

Plausible mechanisms for being able to stay relatively weight-stable are:

-   The cold, which would lead to increased thermogenesis, as well as counteracting the energetic lost of blue light exposure
-   Eating at the right times. Almost always ate in daylight hours. Circadian cycles were not affected that much, and both carbohydrate and lipid metabolism was not compromised
-   Activity at the right times. We were active during the day, and never during the night.
-   Japanese magic! -- I found it hard to find fat people here. The sub 5% obesity rate is real. BTW, the people still look sad and unhealthy in general, but obesity doesn't seem to be a major problem. I still don't know if there is something about this place that allows the leptin receptor to work better.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-18T11:34:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-150502>

**Flight Experiments
**
I did 2 experiments regarding managing flights.

\----

When flying back from Tokyo to Melbourne, I decided to try to upregulate fatty acid metabolism as much as possible.

The flight was 12:15pm Japan time on the 15th of December. The last meal before the flight was at about 4pm on the 14th of December. (fasted for 16hrs before taking off.)

Before the flight, I had 4 cans of coffee, and 8mg of nicotine, over a 4 hour period. I was definitely jittery when I boarded the plane.

Then I didn't eat for the 10.5hr flight, and drank about 4L of water along the way. I definitely still felt thirsty despite drinking this amount of water; a total of 4 trips to the toilet during the flight. I also felt pretty hungry about 4hours into the flight.

We reached Melbourne at roughly 1am Melbourne time, and got to bed at 2am Melbourne time, waking up at 6am Melbourne time. (5:50am sunrise)

The first meal was 3 dozen oysters with lots of water at 10am Melbourne time on the 16th of December, after approximately 39hrs of fasting.

\----

When flying from Melbourne to Cairns today, I decided to see what the impact of carb consumption was.

Had a bunch of fruit and snacks early in the day. About 150g of carbohydrate.

I also decided to not drink much water through the 3.3hr flight. I did not feel thirsty at all during the flight, and did not get dry lips/skin during the flight.

I drank lots of water (3+ litres) upon getting home, and immediately had to pee within 15 mins, whereby my piss was pretty dark yellow.

\----

I suspect that keeping high levels of FFA seems to help recycle water, and keep redox potential higher during the nn-EMF assault of flying. The hunger pangs that came are probably the exact symptom of "perceived need for carbs" during high nn-EMF exposure. Despite the harder execution, I think this is the correct way to go for most of the flight.

Increased carb consumption probably keeps water within the cell somehow. This is probably a good thing for short-term redox potential maintenance.

Now, I wonder what would happen if I kept as fasted as possible most of the time, and then introduced carbs at some point during a flight.

Using my travel to NOLA as an example:

-   Last meal at 5pm the previous day
-   Leave Cairns at 5am for Sydney. 2.5hr flight. No food consumed, with lots of water.
-   Leave Sydney at 11:40am for LA. 14hr flight. Lots of water, no food.
-   Reach LA at 6:30am PST, with a 6hr layover before flying to NOLA. Eat some protein and fat during the layover, probably at 7-8am PST, in an attempt to yoke to the light cycles of the new location. (Of course, I need to find an outdoor place to stare at the sun)
-   Leave LA at 12:30pm for a 3hr 40min flight to Loius Armstrong airport. reaching New Orleans at 6:12pm, hopefully getting a cab into my accomodation just after 8pm, by which I will immediately get to sleep, and wake up at 7am the next day.

I'm wondering where would be a possible place to use carbs as a "short term energy booster", or if that would be even a smart thing at all .... My instinct is to say that substrate utilization will be so compromised due to the long periods of staying awake and the craziness of flying.

My current strategy is to simply some fat and protein during the layover at LA, spread over 2 meals:

-   one meal right after I land, which will contain some protein. Ideally 75-100g of protein to get as strong a circadian yoking response while keeping insulin as controlled as possible. Probably at least 50g of fat.
-   one meal right before I get on the plane to New Orleans (roughly 11:30am), which will comprise of fat only (to keep insulin as low as possible and FFAs as high as possible). Probably at least 50g of fat here.

We'll see how that goes. I'll have a couple of days to think about it.

..

Posting this from New Orleans.

Details of trip were:

-   last full meal eaten at 12pm on <24-Dec-2014> Cairns time
-   wake at 2am Cairns Time <25-Dec-2014>
-   had 1 tablespoon coconut oil and some supplements (oil to help with absorption of the fat soluble stuff)
    -   curcumin 1300mg
    -   resveratrol 1500mg
    -   quercertin 800mg
    -   magnesium malate 1200mg
    -   zinc picolinate 50mg
    -   NAC 500mg
    -   Vit K2 15mg
    -   Vit C 2000mg
-   flight from Cairns to Sydney at 5am, reaching Sydney after 3+hr. 1 bathroom trip
-   2.5hr layover in Sydney, then 14hr flight to LA. 6 bathroom trips, lots of water, 2 cups of coffee,
-   6hr layover in LA
-   Felt cold as hell during the LA layover all of a sudden. This is definitely the body sensing a time when it should be asleep. I decided to eat something and see how that affected things. Had a bag of beef jerky (50g protein), a latte, and an americano (and 2mg more nicotine)
-   3.5hr flight from LAX to MSY at 12:35pm PST, landing just before 6pm CST.
-   Drink some water, and probably bed 5 mins after this post (definitely feeling sleepy now)

Tomorrow's plan is to get up during the daytime, and hopefully yoke the circadian rhythms as quickly as possible. Hopefully meetup with @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) too

\----

Sidenote 1: I'm still amazed all the time that airplanes don't crash ..... and that everyone takes for granted that they don't crash.

Sidenote 2: one initial impression about the US vs Australia, holy crap the security theatre and general security risks feels unsettling. In Australia, I get the feeling that nobody gives a crap about me (freedom!), not so here.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-12-29T17:15:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-151136>

On a more serious note, Yes, this was circadian-mismatched to all hell.

I can count the number of times I drank Alcohol this year on two hands (six -- three in the last 3 days), and I got to bed at 3am and woke up at 8am (yay! circadian yoked ).

This morning I woke up, drank some green tea, drank 1.5L (50 oz) of water, had a contrast shower, and took some supplements. Hopefully my redox is high enough to survive another 3 days with Dr K (plus NYE )

Despite definitely getting drunk last night, I am not having any sort of hangover. Resveretrol and oysters doing the work. But in general, a good sign that I can hold my redox charge much better than in the past.

\=======

I should re-emphasise that everything changed for me when I switched my LOCATION from Melbourne to Cairns. Within 2 weeks, I saw massive improvements despite not "doing things as optimally" as I was in Melbourne.

For me, the biggest difference was some funny combination of more sun, better magnetism, less EMF, etc .... that I got from being in Cairns. I was telling Rubin and @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) yesterday that I can't measure this stuff on a meter (eg: I need some really expensive SQUID to measure magnetism), and that the only way I knew what was right was through biohacking.

So, here's to 2015 being a great year of biohacking myself, and helping other people biohack their own lives.  From what I've heard with the conversations with Dr K, we've got some interesting and practical stuff coming up in 2015.
....

>
> Worst case, I'll only start getting real work done from <07-Jan-2015> Aussie time onwards.

Get out to some theme parks while your in town, there are some pretty amazing rides out here. Hit me up if you want to grab an oyster. Cheers mate

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-03T23:43:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-151523>

Went to Daytona Beach with the cousin today.

Had lunch at 'Oyster Pub' down on the beach. I had a total of 39 fresh oysters and 9 chargrilled oysters. @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) the fresh oysters were better than any I had in New Orleans, and guess what, they were Lousiana Oysters (area 5). Chargrilled were excellent.

\----

After that we got to the beach. A little cloudy, but still getting that UV benefit.

\----

Dinner was just a bar of dark chocolate from Whole Foods and some Italian Spring water

\----

Also going to meet up with @[bionaut](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6076/) on Monday. Should be awesome!

....

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-07T22:31:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-152009>

Met up with @[bionaut](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6076/) on my last day in Orlando, and we literally spoke for hours about various topics.

Met up with @[caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) yesterday at the airport. She commented that I was much more hyper-energetic this time than when I met her in July 2014 (before the Cairns move). Redox still holding decently strong after being thrashed by two 30+hr journeys across the pacific

Managing the flight home was decently easy:

-   Last meal at 2pm Orlando time
-   Flight at 6pm Orlando time, arrived at LA 5.5hrs later
-   Flight at 10:30pm LA time, 14hrs total. Slept for 8+hrs on the flight
-   Arrived at Sydney 8am Sydney time.
-   Left Sydney at 1:10pm Sydney time for a 2.5hr flight to Cairns
-   Landed in Cairns 3pm, back at home by 4pm
-   Bed at 7pm, slept until 6am

Currently 6:30am as a write this, and it's back to my usual routine now. Coding in the morning, beach time in the 10am - 12pm window, more work in afternoon, more activity from 3-5pm, dinner, etc .... Will get started with the implementation of some of the ideas I proposed to Jack tonight.

Weight measurement this morning is 79.0kg. Waist <79cm. Which basically means that I didn't gain weight / lose energy during my trip.  Other than some respiritory issues like a runny or blocked nose, I didn't really experience other issues during the entirety of the trip.

### caroline — 2015-01-07T22:59:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-152014>

great to catchup Yew ..... you look awesome - lean and full of energy

and lots of great stories

did the fast on the way home help - do you think???

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-08T05:30:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-152056>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=152014)
>
> great to catchup Yew ..... you look awesome - lean and full of energy
>
> and lots of great stories
>
> did the fast on the way home help - do you think???

Yup, I still think fasted is the best way for me to deal with flying . And by fasted I mean nothing but water, tea, coffee, and nicotine gum

Got home and ate the stuff shown below at 4pm cairns time ...

\----

Keto Epi-paleo eating Bachelor Style.  So this is basically the boring-as stuff that I eat almost every day when I'm back here in Cairns.

Mashed up a can of mackerel (5g worth of DHA), then just dumped in a lot of whatever herbs I have (today was basil, oregano, chives, black pepper, dill tips, marjoram, terragon, coriander, and thyme), then add a whole bunch of hot sauce (and some extra chilli), then mix up and optionally wrap with yakinori (roasted laver seaweed).

For those interested in macros, I'd probably say 30g of fat, 65g of protein, and whatever carbs comes in the herbs. Probably 700-800kcal total. Pretty decent keto-friendly meal which I can easily eat every single day. Sometimes I add carbs if I want (with salsa, on rice cakes, etc ....)

At AU$1.70 a can, you can't beat this shit in terms of price. Maybe when I'm actually making more money I'll upgrade to more seafood variety, but for now, it's the same old boring DHA load on a daily basis.

you forgot to mention chocolate!!! just saying......

What chocolate??  Don't you know that calories don't count if you eat it with someone else .

But yeah, caroline gave me about 40g of 90% Lindt chocolate. I doubt that it blunts fat burning though, since the carbohydrate and protein load in that chocolate is so low; "fasted" really just means "using fat instead of carbs"

Sleep is pretty good, and I'm already yoked to the sunrise here (5:30am)

I'll promise some red speedos when I make it to the cruise

\=====

LOL @ the Cairns roadkill now . Frogs of all kinds smashed up on the road side every 50 metres. At least the kangaroos are deciding to stay off the roads during the rainy season.
