### PaulG — 2015-01-09T02:34:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-152176>

hi Yew - for your mackeral yakinori wraps what technique do you use - I'm thinking this would make a quick an easy snack for the kids lunchbox but maybe a bit 'messy' unless you envelop the mackeral. More pics please.. I have always wanted to run a sushi business and sell healthy lunch boxes to kids as here school dinners are $7 per day - sausage roll, pies, lasagna, fruit juices/soda

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-09T05:44:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-152193>

> [PaulG said:](/goto/post?id=152176)
>
> hi Yew - for your mackeral yakinori wraps what technique do you use - I'm thinking this would make a quick an easy snack for the kids lunchbox but maybe a bit 'messy' unless you envelop the mackeral. More pics please.. I have always wanted to run a sushi business and sell healthy lunch boxes to kids as here school dinners are $7 per day - sausage roll, pies, lasagna, fruit juices/soda

Ha, I'm really sloppy when it comes to wrapping these things up, and don't give a crap about how they turn out . But seaweed, or at least yakinori, will definitely become soggy and not hold up to any moisture.

The one way that I've have success with packaging potential wet ingredients is to use rice paper, like a vietnamese spring roll. Some carb content, but not much at all TBH. You can even pan fry the rice paper rolls afterward to make them more rigid.

That sort of idea probably wouldn't fly that well in a business setting of course, since you'd need to systematise everything. I was helping my Dad plan out a chips and pie business early last year, and we got into some good detail, but ultimately decided that starting a software business was much more profitable. Perhaps we can talk about this some time (PM me if interested )

....

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-11T13:10:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-152395>

**Thoughts After the Optimal Resolution 2015 Trip**

This is a quick mind-dump of some of the things that I gained after the trip that I made to New Orleans (NOLA) for Jack's Optimal Resolution Event.

\======

**Internet Personas**

I am a person who is a child of the Internet; I got on the internet in 1997 when I was 6.5 years old, and immediately dove into it's full uncensored glory.

Almost 17 years of being on the Internet means that the person you see through text on the Internet is pretty close to the real me (minus the cultural-specific adaptation which I do -- y'all appreciate the butchered Southern Twang plus the swearing =P)

Jack is not an Internet native, and as such, in real life he is nothing like the person portrayed on the Internet. This is definitely counter-productive when it comes to expressing certain points, which get mis-construed as him being arrogant, dismissive, etc ... though that is sometimes true, and it's our job to call him out (politely) if so.

Add to the fact that he is a doctor, and has to be wary of writing things that may be interpreted as "practicing medicine on the internet". In that aspect, I ask the community to be a little bit more understanding, and be less demanding when asking questions specifically pertaining to personal health issues. Questions getting at the generic principles of optimal health work best.

\----

Now that I've met Jack and spent some time with him, I understand a lot of his take from both a strictly logical and an intuitive-emotional perspective.

From that, I feel like I know how to better interpret many of his forum posts than I did prior to making the trip. I think I have a better feel as to if I'm on the right track, and hopefully I can help others in that process too.

\==========

**Optimal Resolution Q&A**

The Q&A was a bunch of fun! I didn't expect to say as much as I did, and I hope that what I said was useful.

A summary of the Q&A is forthcoming together with the start of the wiki project.

\----

**Wiki Project**

I've got most of the basic items laid down. It's now just a matter of finding time to get stuff done. I'm still working on starting a company right now, so time is limited, but I'll try my best!

\==========

**Connecting with Jack**

I definitely feel that I can help Jack in many ways now, and that I don't owe him anything except gratitude and a bottle of Grange =D

\----

**Connecting with Jeremy Thomley**

Connecting with Jeremy during the NYE party was amazing. I'll not get into the details in order to respect Jeremy's privacy, but I can definitely say that I share too many things with him despite only having just met, for this to have been anything but a great connection.

There was an eerie amount of similarly to be honest, and you wonder if there's some global-level quantum entanglement at work.

(And if you want to know the details, I'll tell you in person, where I can ensure that Jeremy's privacy is protected)

I think I'm going to go attend one of his yoga and climbing classes this year. =P And of course, to check out his amazing metal sculptures in person.

\==========

**Not Limited By Knowledge**

Another thing that I told Jeremy during the NYE party was that knowing less than some other "smarter" people in the community doesn't make you less useful.

Neither I nor my Dad can tell my Mom to live optimally. But her sister has far more impact. All we need to do is to know enough regarding optimal health principles, and bear the context of the listener in mind when conveying that knowledge.

When it comes to making inconvenient changes in their lives, some people need to hear the right information, from the right person, at the right time.

\-----

Furthermore, we're at a stage whereby more information from Jack is only going to marginally move the needle in helping people achieve optimal health outcomes in the short and medium term.

I want to continuously get more information out of Jack, and I think there are profound implications for how we do things in the future. But I think that we're armed with enough information of how to look after ourselves today.

Most people struggling with health are limited by being able to connect to the right people, and helping them with their specific problems, and not by understanding some new blog post about quantum entanglement.

I thought that it's important that we create a culture whereby everyone can help pull each other up, and not have the benefits limited to the few who can understand Jack's stuff. Innovation in the open is what is required, and a culture of sharing the process and results of biohacks.

Note: one complaint that I have about Jack is not enough details regarding his biohacks. For example, during this last biohack, what exactly did he eat? When was he eating it? etc ..... Those are important details to figure out how our experience might rhyme.

I am a little more open about what I'm trying to do, but not open enough. I'll attempt to change that with this current biohack (described in next post).

There are privacy concerns, and being open about biohacks is somewhat of a learned skill. There are some things that don't need to be shared, and being too open can also make it hard on the reader. Explicitly stating what is and is not willing to be put in the open should be tolerated by the culture, but it starts with the willingness to be open about certain things.

Again, all I can do is try to show what I mean by displaying "a discerning level of openness" in my own log.

Another criticism of Jack: withholding of information without telling us the reason why. Example: when asked how he's increasing the guass rating of his magnetico, the answer was "you'll have to wait for the book". I personally am fine with that answer, but it isn't one that I personally would give if I were trying to foster a discerning culture of openness.​

\=======

**Making the Trip to NOLA**

I do not think that just anyone could have made this trip. I am in a fortunate position to do so.

First off, I'm single, so I don't have the constraints of having to take care of family during the holiday season.

Next, it was on pretty short notice, and I can afford to change or make plans very quickly.

Next, I've got enough cash to make the trip, which isn't exactly cheap coming from where I'm coming.

Next, I was sufficiently involved in the community to make the trip worth it.

Next, I like meeting and being with people (despite liking to be on my quiet beach with people I know most of the time). Being accepted by all of the people that I met there was a great feeling coming basically as a outsider to the US, to New Orleans, and to Jack's family and friends.

Next, I travel well, and I'm a tall, young male who can give the "do not fuck with me" aura if I'm caught in a funny situation -- New Orleans isn't exactly the safest of places, and it gets crazy.

Examples:

I stayed on St Roch Avenue at this place listed on Airbnb, and Jack was like "DO NOT walk back!". It is definitely not what you would call a family-friendly accommodation spot.

Then the cab ride to the airport after the NYE party was me fighting for a cab along side drunk dudes, having to convince the cab driver that I wasn't drunk, paying the fare upfront, having 2 other guys crash along for the ride (nice non-drunk guys), and then having the driver play youtube videos of him showing off his guns while driving (one of the 2 guys was a gun-nut). That was fun =P​

New Orleans is a crazy city. I want to be back for Mardi Gras one day, probably in 2016. Definitely would not want to go crazy for too long, and I still love my quiet beach a lot more.

\----

Don't let the above scare you, since the French Quarter is a safe place. (though Bourbon street is obviously not family friendly). Staying at a hotel in or close to the Quarter rather than being a cheap adventure-seeking idiot like me is completely safe.

The cruise leaves from New Orleans at 4pm on <21-Jun-2015>, so it is possible to plan a flight that arrives that morning, and head straight for the cruise. We get back at 8am on <28-Jun-2015>, and the cruise should offer a baggage storage and transportation service for a fee (I think $25-40), whereby they keep your baggage at the terminal, and then transport it to the airport before your flight later in the day.

\======

That's it for initial thoughts. If anything else crops up, I'll update as needed!

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-11T13:31:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-152396>

**Current Project: Getting Back to a Very Lean State
**
I think I said before that when I forcibly dieted myself to 75kg back in September 2011, I felt really cold, but also experienced no collagen issues.

I think that the cold intolerance is due to a lack of DHA and Sulfated compounds in the skin, and was independent of being that lean.

I suspect that being physically that much smaller is actually a good thing for my collagen issues, and want to see if forcibly dieting myself back down to that weight will help my collagen issues. This time using an Epi-Paleo diet while minding circadian rhythms.

As a visual reference to how lean I was, here are some pictures (me in my underwear )

-   front -- [http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/lean-front.jpg](http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/lean-front.jpg)
-   back -- [http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/lean-back.jpg](http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/lean-back.jpg)
-   abs -- [http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/lean-abs.jpg](http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/lean-abs.jpg)

Stats were roughly 75kg / 165lbs, with a waist measurement of around 76cm / 29.9'' . I was around 189cm / 6' 2.5''

\----

As of today, I'm sitting at 80.5kg with a 79cm waist (up from 79.3kg when I first came back to Cairns) after a little much chocolate yesterday  (likely temporary bloat)

I think it'll be relevant to give information about the exact things that I'm eating, alongside daily weight fluctuations, exercise amounts, as well as reports on how I'm feeling. I'll likely post those summaries up once a week, on every Sunday night.

As for my schedule:

-   It will be completely uneventful for the rest of January, as I knuckle down to do some programming and business development to get my company launched and making money.
-   February I may make a trip down to Brisbane for 4-5 days
-   March I may make a trip down to Melbourne and Sydney for a total of 7-8 days
-   April I will probably make a trip to Adelaide for probably a good week.
-   May schedule is currently unknown
-   June will be the Kruse Cruise, and I will likely be travelling for close to 3 weeks total

That still leaves my schedule relatively consistent through this project, and as such my daily activities should be pretty constant. These few months will likely be another period of "robot-mode" dedication to work, sacrificing all social activities other than those trips described above.

\-----

When it comes to diet, the initial plan is to restrict calories for 2 days, followed by one day of higher calories, and then just repeat that cycle ad-infinitum.

NOTE: let's just use the word "calories" to mean "amount of food consumed". It's still a convenient measure​

It'll be easier to explain this using exact examples of diet logs. First update this time next week!

\----

The exercise protocol is fairly basic, though I think I'll be limiting endurance exercise more so than before. It being the wet season should probably make that easier (hard to ride a bide in a thunderstorm ).

Again, it'll probably be easier to just show the exercise log.

\----

Well, let's see how quickly I manage to lose the weight. It's a long light cycle here, not exactly the best time to be losing weight (we're designed to be leaner in the winter), but I'll try anyway

> I do not think that just anyone could have made this trip. I am in a fortunate position to do so.
>
> First off, I'm single, so I don't have the constraints of having to take care of family during the holiday season.
>
> Next, it was on pretty short notice, and I can afford to change or make plans very quickly.
>
> Next, I've got enough cash to make the trip, which isn't exactly cheap coming from where I'm coming.
>
> Next, I was sufficiently involved in the community to make the trip worth it.
>
> Next, I like meeting and being with people (despite liking to be on my quiet beach with people I know most of the time). Being accepted by all of the people that I met there was a great feeling coming basically as a outsider to the US, to New Orleans, and to Jack's family and friends.
>
> Next, I travel well, and I'm a tall, young male who can give the "do not fuck with me" aura if I'm caught in a funny situation -- New Orleans isn't exactly the safest of places, and it gets crazy.
>
>
> Examples:
>
> I stayed on St Roch Avenue at this place listed on Airbnb, and Jack was like "DO NOT walk back!". It is definitely not what you would call a family-friendly accommodation spot.
>
> Then the cab ride to the airport after the NYE party was me fighting for a cab along side drunk dudes, having to convince the cab driver that I wasn't drunk, paying the fare upfront, having 2 other guys crash along for the ride (nice non-drunk guys), and then having the driver play youtube videos of him showing off his guns while driving (one of the 2 guys was a gun-nut). That was fun =P​
>
>
> New Orleans is a crazy city. I want to be back for Mardi Gras one day, probably in 2016. Definitely would not want to go crazy for too long, and I still love my quiet beach a lot more.
>
> \----
>
> Don't let the above scare you, since the French Quarter is a safe place. (though Bourbon street is obviously not family friendly). Staying at a hotel in or close to the Quarter rather than being a cheap adventure-seeking idiot like me is completely safe.
>
> The cruise leaves from New Orleans at 4pm on <21-Jun-2015>, so it is possible to plan a flight that arrives that morning, and head straight for the cruise. We get back at 8am on <28-Jun-2015>, and the cruise should offer a baggage storage and transportation service for a fee (I think $25-40), whereby they keep your baggage at the terminal, and then transport it to the airport before your flight later in the day.
>
> \======
>
> That's it for initial thoughts. If anything else crops up, I'll update as needed!

Great report Yew…energy, honesty, kindness and intellect

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-11T23:25:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-152438>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=152397)
>
> Great report Yew…energy, honesty, kindness and intellect

Thank you

\---

And now let's start the Project off with some setbacks  ....

The bodyweight log for the last 5 days since returning to Cairns has been:

<2015-01-12 Mon>: 80.8kg, 80.5cm **// (+0.3kg)**
<2015-01-11 Sun>: 80.5kg, 80cm **// (+1.2kg)**
<2015-01-10 Sat>: 79.3kg, 79cm
<2015-01-09 Fri>: 79.0kg, 79cm
<2015-01-08 Thu> (AU time): 79.0kg, 79cm
​

So it's pretty obvious something happened on Saturday

That's funny, because the diet on Thursday and Friday was horrible, but yet I didn't gain any weight.

Thursday was basically some nuts in the morning, my usual mackerel bowl, and then a ton load of chocolate and candy bars. Basically a good chunk of chocolate, plus 4 candy bars, and I think I had a carton of almond milk somewhere in there. Total eaten for the day was around 4000kcal (600kcal in the morning, 800kcal for the mackerel bowl, 1000kcal for the 4 candy bars, and 1600kcal for the rest of the chocolate and misc stuff). The only exercise was cycling to the supermarket and back (20mins each way).

Friday was the same (just with different candy bars ). Cycled up to Clifton beach (20mins each way) and walked around the beach for 30 mins.

The experiment was basically to see how high I could push junk-food carbohydrates. I was just eating according to hunger levels, and stopping when I decided I was full but not stuffed. LOL, that ended up being a lot of food . I basically woke up the next day looking and feeling the same. There was zero brain fog or negative impacts on mental health, not even feeling excessively amped up at all, and I could tell given that I was able to focus and do between 3-4 hours of coding each day.

Saturday was where things went sour, and the diet consisted of some butter and cream with coffee in the morning, followed by my mackerel bowl at roughly 1pm, and then maybe 400g worth of ham along with some sauerkraut. Total calories I'd estimate to be around 2500kcal, and total protein at around 150-160g

Next morning I was up 1.2kg, looked bloated, but feeling normal.

Yesterday (Sunday), I actually dropped the calories even lower, having some butter and cream with coffee in the AM (500kcal worth), then a single meal at 2pm consisting of my mackerel bowl and some more ham and sauerkraut, for a total of 2000kcal in the day, and about 150g of protein again.

This morning, I was up an additional 0.3kg (1.5kg / 3.3lbs gained over 2 days), and looking more bloated, but again, feeling the same.

The fact that I'm feeling mentally sharp (performing well with coding), sleep is still good, and bristol score is still a solid 4 (no gut health deterioration), seems to point to this being an isolated collagen issue, probably mediated by something in the ham and/or sauerkraut. The one negative symptom I'm seeing is a little more edema in my feet, which again signals a collagen issue.

I want to say it's excessive sodium and the inability to deal with it that's causing the bloat, but I don't know for sure. In any case, I do think it's diet-mediated, and it's the damn ham .
​

\----

Given the above, I'll probably just take the ham out of the diet for a week, and continue on with the plan as before.

Not the first time this has happened, and I've gotten over worrying about random weight swings . I'll give another more detailed update in the next 5-7 days

....

### rgp888 — 2015-01-12T00:13:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-152446>

Hi Yew!

I just followed a link to your "notes" - I like them very much - I am new here and almost three weeks into Leptin Rx and having lots of fun! I have started writing about my experiences, and my (limited) understanding of Dr. Kruse's writing too ([http://lyndalehmann.net](http://lyndalehmann.net)) - just because I find that writing stuff out helps me sort it out in my mind. I think it will be a couple of years of reading jackkruse.com before I reach even a basic level of understanding - your "notes" are great reading. My self named website was just a static site ("about me") until I decided to actually take epi-paleo on (the day after I started reading Jack Kruse's website, the day I got the epi-paleo Rx book on kindle and read it cover to cover the same day - everything I have read so far - even though it's not easy to read - has rung true - so jump in feet first!), anyway I decided to turn my "about me" website into a blog and dedicate to writing as much as I can about my thinking and adventures along a new paradigm -not that my website has anyone to read it - that's not the point! - the point is to keep me on track!

I live Gold Coast , QLD - how do you reconcile living in the tropics with the cold theories? I come from Adelaide where winters are colder and summer hot and dry, I struggle with humid heat (I lived in Cairns for a few years when I was much younger - really struggled with it up there). And since I have started CT in the past few weeks I have been having a hard time with the humidity - its been pretty wet and hot here since Xmas - been on the Gold Coast for 5 years now and was almost OK with the humid heat, but menopause hot flushes were unbearable - thanks to epi-paleo and CT no more hot flushes - except when I go outside in the humid heat!!!! It seems that doing CT is making me less adapted to tropical heat (and really Gold Coast is sub-tropical, not like Cairns!) (once when I was in Cairns there was 100 inches of rain in one day, with most of it falling in just 4 hours - it was amazing! - I lived there before you were born, when it was just a fishing village!).

Does the heat bother you? Do you do any CT? Are you native to Cairns (or the tropics?)?
Does you website have an RSS feed? I'd like to read when you make a new post!!

Cheers
Lynda

I like living closer to the equator a lot more than not . I also grew up for 16 years in Singapore, so that's basically equatorial humidity year-round, much more so than Cairns. I actually don't mind the current weather, which maxes out at 35C and 100% humidity (most of the time it's low 30s Celsius and 60-80% humidity anyway).

For context: my ancestry is from South China, in the 23-26 deg latitude band. I was born in Melbourne, then lived in Singapore for most of my childhood and teens, then popped back to Melbourne at age 17, and then moved to Cairns September 2014. So you can say that I'm adapted to the tropics and originate from the low latitudes.​

And of course, the Cairns dry season give some of the best weather possible IMO -- Little rain, daylight for 11hrs a day, bright sunshine for at least 90% of that day, great temperature range from 15-28C, not too humid (usually 40-60%) but never dry, etc .....

Right now, my CT is limited to a coolfatburner and a coolgutbuster, but if I had a house, it would be pretty easy to get a tub and fill it up with ice; CT you can bring with you anywhere

To be honest, I'm living fairly un-optimised right now, and still reaping lots of benefit. Can't wait to get a place of my own and ramp up the optimal health prescriptions.

\----

As for your issues, I'll have to follow along your log and make some assessments regarding your personal context. Watch out for that later tonight

\----

Ha, I didn't bother with RSS for that site. Probably should re-write the damn thing one day (months from now )

\----

Whenever it is, I'll be there. Will keep you posted, and hope you can make it as well

OK, I'll make it up to you by taking pics in a month's time, while wearing red speedos  .... Had to appease grandma @[caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) , and ended picking up a couple pairs of red swim trunks

To your question, WiFi and other nn-EMF signals weren't that bad by conventional standards in both places. That said, "conventional standards" are probably still be too high for optimal health.

When I was in Melbourne, the nearest Power Transmission Pylon (which was about 132kV) was about 1.5km away. Nearest step-down transformer was about 1km away. Here, the nearest Pylon is more than 5km away. Just basic street-side powerlines outside my place now, not even any step-down transformers in sight.

The house in Melbourne had higher ambient microwave radiation, up to around 0.5mW/cm^2, though usually in my room it was always >0.05mW/cm^2. Here is it pretty much always around 0.001mW/cm^2. Both places didn't have WiFi, but obviously the ambient EMF was higher in Melbourne. For one, Victorian houses all need to have smart meters, while here in Queensland nobody has them.

The ELF magnetic field (<1000Hz) in Melbourne was usually less than 0.3mG. Here it is anywhere from 0.1mG to 0.5mG

As for cell towers, I know there was one base station about 1+km away from where I was in Melbourne, and over here I got no clue where the cellular towers are

Hope that was helpful

...

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-18T11:32:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-153239>

**<Weight Loss Week 1 Update>
**
It's 11 days since I've been back in Cairns, and I'm feeling a lot better than when I left.

Values for bodyweight and waist measurements, as well as a rough meal log are quoted below.

Weight fluctuations were massive this week. The main thing that caused me to bloat like crazy were processed meats (ham and beef biltong this week). It seems like this was "legitimate weight gain" caused by energetic inefficiencies, which actually prevented further fat loss, and not just some sodium mediated water-bloat. Something about those processed meats is affecting my gut flora in a negative fashion.

At the end of the week, I basically ended up where I started body composition wise, so I'll call this week a failure in terms of actually losing weight, but I did learn something new.

Metrics of Gut health remain good, with bristol score being a consistent 4, and any bloating clearing up within a day of avoiding the problematic foods.

Brain function still remains high. Mood is stable and good. No new neurotransmitter issues (I'm my usual brand of crazy )

\----

In Japan and New Orleans, I could get away with eating a lot more crap, and still not gain any weight. I was eating at least 4,000kcal worth of food in Japan dammit!

The only explanation is the temperature difference. I already knew that I am going up against a hill by trying to do this weight loss experiment at the hottest time of the year, and the current results again show that hot weather does not favour a lean state.

It's been hot here for sure: 25C (77F) lows, 35C (95F) highs, humidity from 60-90%, rained 14 out of 18 days this month. That's the wet season for you

Sidenote: despite it raining most days, it rains for probably only 3-4hrs in total a day. That means that I've been getting plenty of sunshine (4hrs spent outside yesterday). Also, we're looking at a 86deg sun angle at noon, and what feels like a >50deg angle even just 1hour after sunrise. In other words, you're going to get good UV exposure almost any time of the day here right now (though 9am-12pm is still best)​

Sidenote: "cold" isn't really that cold either; I've seen cold-induced fat loss at a lousy 13-15C ambient temperature, which my previous locale (Melbourne) is still experiencing regularly even in summer time.​

\----

My meal timing pattern is still fairly evenly distributed through the day. I basically re-started eating some breakfast when I got back to Cairns on <08-Jan-2015>, to ensure that I'm yoked to my current light cycle, and haven't changed the eating pattern that much.

Now that I've been waking up just at sunrise for a week, I will switch things up to an intermittent fasting regime, getting as close to 1 meal a day as possible.

Intermittent fasting + calorie restriction will have to be employed for fat loss to occur in this climate. Note that every time you mobilise body fat, you are basically liberating stored protons

In cold, the natural reaction is to uncouple respiration and liberate these protons as IR heat, leaving no net positive charge that can lead to inflammation in excess. In a hot climate, uncoupling is turned off, and forcibly liberating fatty acid stores requires a large electron supply to work. I will have to spend lots of time in the sun and on the beach for this to work out well without compromising my health.

\----

I am eating a very non-Epi-paleo diet 2 days a week. Lots of cereal

(a) My gut health and redox potential in general is good enough now that I'm not suffering from a shitty diet twice a week

(b) If there is any harm, it will be progressive slow deterioration, and not just falling off a cliff ==> I have the opportunity to revert to an optimal diet if I see any bad symptoms

(c) It's a long light cycle, and I've got easy access to an electron source that is the beach

(d) I'm experimenting with Kiefer's main principle of Carb Backloading, which is to eat high-GI carbs that spike insulin very quickly, and don't linger in the system for long. These carbs are consumed during the window of highest protein synthesis (last 2 hours of sunset). I have tried and failed to do this with natural sources like potatoes, and even short-grain white rice. Weetbix still works best here

(e) The main issue with eating processed foods is chronic exposure to processed carbs, and the main issues are:

(1) deprivation of the appropriate substrate for your gut flora
(2) feeding of potential bad agents (like Candida)

The issue with (1) I think is attenuated somewhat given the quick nature of the carb refeed, and so long as the diet on the othre 5 days of the week is good, the gut flora can still get what they need (low intensity light release). Intermittent fasting also allows for good FIAF action.

(2) is not a problem if there aren't many bad agents in the gut in the first place. Again, if I start seeing negative symptoms, I can and will cut out the non-optimal food.​

\======

That's it for now. Let's see what I can achieve in the following week.

\======

> Bodyweight and waist:
>
>
> <2015-01-18 Sun>: 79.7kg, 79cm
> <2015-01-17 Sat>: 80.1kg, 79cm

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-26T09:03:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-153868>

**<Weight Loss Week 2 Update>**

Overall summary of progress this week is:

-   Introduced stricter intermittent fasting (4-6hr eating window)
-   No weight or waist change, with weight remaining at just until 80kg / 176lbs, and waist consistently at 79cm / 31''
-   Major visual body composition changes, primarily in shoulders and chest, which are obviously leaner
-   All other metrics of health un-affected (despite the high-carb junk food refeeds)

So intermittent fasting did not lead to weight loss, but it did improve my body composition.

The goal is to figure out how lost of actual mass will affect my collagen quality, so this week was still a failure to achieve that goal.

Though I have to admit that the issues that I've traditionally faced with hand and foot swelling have diminished tremendously since December, with no major worsen-ing of symptoms throughout that entire period. I can still tell that there is some swelling, which is obviously something that needs fixing.

My copy of the Pavel Kolar 'Clinical Rehab Textbook' ([http://www.rehabps.com/REHABILITATION/Clinical\_Rehabilitation\_textbook.html](http://www.rehabps.com/REHABILITATION/Clinical_Rehabilitation_textbook.html)) is currently in Sydney, and I'll probably get it in 2-3 days time. That is relevant in that learning how to move properly is critical in fixing my issues.

You will see in the diet logs that the copious amounts of late-day junk food isn't doing me any harm at all, and in fact I believe a late-day insulin spike that is very big, and which clears very quickly, in the context of exercise, is a very helpful mechanism for improving body composition in an energetically-favourable manner if you manipulate other variables appropriately (ie: not trying to gain extra mass).

Sidenote 0: I have brewing thoughts about applying refeeds, either with protein, carbs, or combinations thereof, to helping people get back to optimal from a sub-optimal state. I think I'll have a write-up about this at some point in time.

Sidenote 1: Here's a pic representing current body composition taken on Sunday morning, with the appropriate warning that this is me wearing a pair of red swimming trunks that's all too small for me  (NOT wearing these on the Cruise) -- [http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/4u7\_X4swfz-3xIUhSt1-l3QL.jpg](http://tanyewwei.com/static/pics/4u7_X4swfz-3xIUhSt1-l3QL.jpg)​

\-----

**Upcoming Interventions
**
The next week will consist of me both raising protein levels significantly, and attempting to isolate the effects of the insulin spike using a protein-only late-day refeeding scenario.

My protein intake has actually not been very high. On days that I don't do a late-day refeed, it's probably only around 70-90g, which is in the range of 1g per KG bodyweight for me. On days that I do have the refeed, it's still only been up to 120g of protein.

I'm going to raise protein intake to at least 150g per day, and even more on re-feed days.

As for the refeeds, I'm going to try and take carbs out of the picture, and do at least 2 refeeds with just protein. A typical day would probably be:

\- small amount fat consumption in the morning. Cream in coffee, etc ... Maybe 200-300kcal
\- High fat meal at 12-1pm, which will probably be my mackerel bowl. Around 800kcal
\- Exercise of some form after 4pm
\- late-day meal at 5-6pm (sunset is 7+pm here right now). This meal will minimally be 10g of leucine with 90g of whey isolate. This is basically close to 100g of fast-digesting protein that will give a massive insulin spike that should clear pretty quickly. Fat is deliberately kept very low in this meal. Addition of extra lean protein is possible, but I want to cap this meal at 1000kcal
​

Total of maybe 2000-2200kcal eaten through the day, which is a calorie-restricted state if you look at it from a daily perspective, but the point is to figure out how the transient and rapid influx of a lot of insulin-spiking compounds without the addition of carbs is going to affect body composition, and simultaneously help me figure out some thoughts that I'm having regarding the exact function of insulin.

Sidenote: grass-fed and likely A2 protein NZ Whey protein isolate bought from 'Professional Whey' -- [https://professionalwhey.com.au/product/nz-whey-protein-isolate/](https://professionalwhey.com.au/product/nz-whey-protein-isolate/) . Also got the leucine from them, as well as some AU grass-fed whey concentrate (a lot cheaper -- [https://professionalwhey.com.au/product/aus-whey-protein-concentrate/](https://professionalwhey.com.au/product/aus-whey-protein-concentrate/))

Sidenote: Whey concentrate and isolate do contain calcium. I generally try to avoid extra calcium consumption, and I suspect that the body is capable of making it's own calcium . For the sake of this experiment, I'll just have to hope that the added calcium intake isn't going to affect me. In general, I'd assume anywhere from 200-500mg of calcium per 100g of whey concentrate or isolate. Can't say which has more, since it varies by batch of milk, and the ultrafiltration processes used to make these proteins does not remove calcium.​

....

I like your experiment! on the side note i believe that each would need/react to differemt approach depending onthe current context. That wants to say that results of your experiment with yourself may not be generally aplicable for anyone else. I learnt a lot about how the context matters and how it changes myself. I find intersting your experience with late afternoon insulin spikes. I do not want to sound weird, but this is what I sort of naturally started to practice during the last few months (must remember i have unusual internal hormonal environment coming from unusual situation plus the fact I am a woman and you are a man). It did nothing to the state of ketosis I live in. I use some carbs though, but sometimes I naturally leave proteins just for the dinner. Looking forward to your thpughts "about the meaning of insulin, universe and everything". As for myself, it is fixing my sleep plus some more things I massed up with hardcore keto. It may as well be that what I achieved with hardcore keto and thpusands of other things I was/doing to get well again somehow "start to work" if I allow some insulin spikes. I have more and more energy (subjective level) and MUCH more ATP (measurable level, even ATP value is limited).

### Jack Kruse — 2015-01-26T17:37:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-153904>

Leaders never cross the finish line first. They wait for others because they are patient. To follow the leader means the leader is bringing others to a destination. That destination is not a place where followers congratulates the leader, it's the place where the leader applauds those who followed and were able to cross the finish line first before themselves. It is not looks, not height, not fitness, not intelligence, not wealth, not clothes, not humor. It is patience that matters.............Food for thought

Dr Kruse - you often sound so much like the Grandmaster of the Bujinkan Ninjutsu - Dr Masaaki Hatsumi Sensei - whenever he speaks, every word has so much depth and meaning - I often describe his manner of speaking as if he is speaking a quantum field where everything is connected but we (mere mortals) just can't see it - he, like you, has the ability to tie together ideas that appear to be disparate, yet are subtlety the same thing in a different way - it takes a lot of time and patience (and training) to understand him as well!

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-27T03:23:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-153945>

> [kovita said:](/goto/post?id=153898)
>
> I
>
> I like your experiment! on the side note i believe that each would need/react to differemt approach depending onthe current context. That wants to say that results of your experiment with yourself may not be generally aplicable for anyone else. I learnt a lot about how the context matters and how it changes myself. I find intersting your experience with late afternoon insulin spikes. I do not want to sound weird, but this is what I sort of naturally started to practice during the last few months (must remember i have unusual internal hormonal environment coming from unusual situation plus the fact I am a woman and you are a man). It did nothing to the state of ketosis I live in. I use some carbs though, but sometimes I naturally leave proteins just for the dinner. Looking forward to your thpughts "about the meaning of insulin, universe and everything". As for myself, it is fixing my sleep plus some more things I massed up with hardcore keto. It may as well be that what I achieved with hardcore keto and thpusands of other things I was/doing to get well again somehow "start to work" if I allow some insulin spikes. I have more and more energy (subjective level) and MUCH more ATP (measurable level, even ATP value is limited).

See, and this is a pregnant woman managing to find a way to accurately intuit her needs and respond to them with the appropriate diet

Some random un-polished thoughts follow ....

\-----

I will say that full-on keto isn't the best idea for a lot of people. I use "keto" to literally mean a ketogenic diet with at least a 2:1 ratio of fat to protein (and a higher ratio if possible), and with 30g of carbs or less. I'm referring here to Jane Plain's description of ketogenic diets -- [http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2015/01/keto-diet-facts-tm-by-woo.html](http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2015/01/keto-diet-facts-tm-by-woo.html)

There are situations which I think that a true keto diet is the answer, and those are typically situations of serious neuro-degeneration.

I will still advocate a high-fat, moderate-protein, low carb diet as a default. For definition sake I'll call this a diet whereby:

-   carbs are below 75g
-   protein at 1g per KG bodyweight, with a minimum of 60-70g. eg: for a 50kg woman, I'd still advocate at least 60-70g of protein a day.
-   rest of the calories from appropriate seasonal fats

I then think there is a large role for a cyclic approaches that not enough people are taking advantage of: some days with high-fat-low-carb, and some days with higher protein or carbs timed appropriately.

\----

In terms of pure body composition changes, the two standout examples have been John Kiefer's 'Carb Backloading' ([http://carbbackloading.com/](http://carbbackloading.com/)), and Borge Fargeli's success with clients on his Biorhythm diet protocol ([http://forum.reactivetrainingsystems.com/content.php?108-The-Biorhythm-Diet](http://forum.reactivetrainingsystems.com/content.php?108-The-Biorhythm-Diet))

You will find some commentary from Bill Lagakos about Carb Backloading here:

-   [http://caloriesproper.com/carb-back-loading-and-the-circadian-regulation-of-metabolism/](http://caloriesproper.com/carb-back-loading-and-the-circadian-regulation-of-metabolism/)

-   [http://caloriesproper.com/carb-back-loading-take-ii/](http://caloriesproper.com/carb-back-loading-take-ii/)

I cannot deny the positive body composition effects achieved in the performance-oriented community by putting protein and carbs in the evening, while simultaneously limiting fat intake.

\----

To define "fast insulin spike", I'm looking at an insulin increase past baseline that lasts for 1-2 hours, with blood glucose normalising back to baseline within 3 hours. Eating fast-acting carbs tend to achieve this effect, especially when GLUT4 receptor activity is up-regulated after resistance exercise.

I still suspect that the benefits are insulin-mediated, and excess carbs is not necessary for the beneficial effects. Some added carbohydrates are probably useful, and I think a mix of fast-digesting fructose and glucose is beneficial. The appropriate dose is context-specific, but I think somewhere in the range of 10-20g of fructose, and 40-50g of glucose is where effects start to happen. No clue how D-ribose will affect this.

I think insulin's effects on uncoupling proteins likely correspond to the feeling of "throwing off lots of body heat" that people experience when they use Carb Backloading. I also view this as a diagnostic criteria: If you don't throw off body heat in response to high insulin spikes, then you are not Leptin Sensitive.

I do not know to what degree insulin must be spiked (measured in blood insulin concentration) for there to be a pronounced effect, but the value probably doesn't matter that much; I think the body just has to see a "low insulin" signal most of the time, and then a sudden "OMGWTF! insulin signal" for a short period.

\----

Getting an insulin spike purely through fast-acting protein will be: (a) shorter, probably on the order of 1-2hours, and (b) bringing with it a corresponding up-regulation of glucagon.

Glucagon up-regulation during periods of high protein synthesis (which is the case in the last 2-hours of daylight) will increase macro-autophagy rates by quite a significant amount. I personally want to take advantage of this for health optimisation if possible.

\----

My hunch is that the ideal combination for myself for the goal of weight loss is roughly 80-100g of fast-acting protein, coupled with 50g of glucose and 20g of fructose. Of course, I'll eventually put this to the test.

....

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-27T03:48:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-153946>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=153904)
>
> Leaders never cross the finish line first. They wait for others because they are patient. To follow the leader means the leader is bringing others to a destination. That destination is not a place where followers congratulates the leader, it's the place where the leader applauds those who followed and were able to cross the finish line first before themselves. It is not looks, not height, not fitness, not intelligence, not wealth, not clothes, not humor. It is patience that matters.............Food for thought

Readers of this should not mis-interpret it to mean that leaders don't need intelligence, etc ....

(a) Yes, you need to be really skilled in a whole set of particular attributes to lead
(b) No, you don't need to be the best -- but just because I'm not the most intelligent, doesn't discount the fact that a leader needs to be pretty intelligent
(c) Not having patience and kindness is a deal-breaker for a leader .... but that still requires the needed attributes for the task (whatever those are)

\----

Then we get to another (less traditional) definition of "Leadership", which is to provide for the environment needed for the appropriate freedom of interaction. This obviously takes skill, time, and resources (money or otherwise).

The goal is to inspire spontaneous acts of initiative between members in the environment to help each other (hopefully while fulfilling selfish needs).

It is important to remember that a term like "Intelligence" is a relative concept. An 8-year-old elder sister is more intelligent than her 6-year-old sibling, and can teach and lead the younger sibling how to braid her hair (amongst other skills). Maybe the 6-year-old sister can lead her elder sister to dig up earthworms to use as fishing bait

Pieter Hintjens' article 'The Cretan Method', talks about the process whereby leading and learning happens amongst everyone from all ages, regardless of absolute level of expertise -- [http://hintjens.com/blog:81#toc6](http://hintjens.com/blog:81#toc6)

Another book that touches on these topics, which I want to read but haven't, is Peter Gray's 'Free to Learn' -- [http://www.amazon.com/Free-Learn-Unleashing-Instinct-Self-Reliant/dp/0465025994](http://www.amazon.com/Free-Learn-Unleashing-Instinct-Self-Reliant/dp/0465025994) . He recently did an interview with Chris Ryan about this topic -- [http://chrisryanphd.com/tangentially-speaking/2015/1/15/108-peter-gray](http://chrisryanphd.com/tangentially-speaking/2015/1/15/108-peter-gray)

This also still introduces a power hierarchy, in that the owner of the resources needed to provide for that environment has a higher degree of control over what happens in that environment. Part of this is necessary, just like good laws are necessary (some places in Scandinavia like Denmark good a pretty good job ), but the goal is eventually to get as much distribution of power as possible ....

That's a whole other can of worms though .... , and I'll leave that for a face-to-face discussion (come find me during the Kruse Cruise! )

....

### kovita — 2015-01-27T09:52:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-153977>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=153945)
>
> See, and this is a pregnant woman managing to find a way to accurately intuit her needs and respond to them with the appropriate diet
>
> Some random un-polished thoughts follow ....
>
> \-----
>
> I will say that full-on keto isn't the best idea for a lot of people. I use "keto" to literally mean a ketogenic diet with at least a 2:1 ratio of fat to protein (and a higher ratio if possible), and with 30g of carbs or less. I'm referring here to Jane Plain's description of ketogenic diets -- [http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2015/01/keto-diet-facts-tm-by-woo.html](http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2015/01/keto-diet-facts-tm-by-woo.html)
>
> There are situations which I think that a true keto diet is the answer, and those are typically situations of serious neuro-degeneration.
>
> I will still advocate a high-fat, moderate-protein, low carb diet as a default. For definition sake I'll call this a diet whereby:
>
> -   carbs are below 75g
> -   protein at 1g per KG bodyweight, with a minimum of 60-70g. eg: for a 50kg woman, I'd still advocate at least 60-70g of protein a day.
> -   rest of the calories from appropriate seasonal fats
>
> I then think there is a large role for a cyclic approaches that not enough people are taking advantage of: some days with high-fat-low-carb, and some days with higher protein or carbs timed appropriately.
>
> \----
>
> In terms of pure body composition changes, the two standout examples have been John Kiefer's 'Carb Backloading' ([http://carbbackloading.com/](http://carbbackloading.com/)), and Borge Fargeli's success with clients on his Biorhythm diet protocol ([http://forum.reactivetrainingsystems.com/content.php?108-The-Biorhythm-Diet](http://forum.reactivetrainingsystems.com/content.php?108-The-Biorhythm-Diet))
>
> You will find some commentary from Bill Lagakos about Carb Backloading here:
>
> -   [http://caloriesproper.com/carb-back-loading-and-the-circadian-regulation-of-metabolism/](http://caloriesproper.com/carb-back-loading-and-the-circadian-regulation-of-metabolism/)
>
> -   [http://caloriesproper.com/carb-back-loading-take-ii/](http://caloriesproper.com/carb-back-loading-take-ii/)
>
> I cannot deny the positive body composition effects achieved in the performance-oriented community by putting protein and carbs in the evening, while simultaneously limiting fat intake.
>
> \----
>
> To define "fast insulin spike", I'm looking at an insulin increase past baseline that lasts for 1-2 hours, with blood glucose normalising back to baseline within 3 hours. Eating fast-acting carbs tend to achieve this effect, especially when GLUT4 receptor activity is up-regulated after resistance exercise.
>
> I still suspect that the benefits are insulin-mediated, and excess carbs is not necessary for the beneficial effects. Some added carbohydrates are probably useful, and I think a mix of fast-digesting fructose and glucose is beneficial. The appropriate dose is context-specific, but I think somewhere in the range of 10-20g of fructose, and 40-50g of glucose is where effects start to happen. No clue how D-ribose will affect this.
>
> I think insulin's effects on uncoupling proteins likely correspond to the feeling of "throwing off lots of body heat" that people experience when they use Carb Backloading. I also view this as a diagnostic criteria: If you don't throw off body heat in response to high insulin spikes, then you are not Leptin Sensitive.
>
> I do not know to what degree insulin must be spiked (measured in blood insulin concentration) for there to be a pronounced effect, but the value probably doesn't matter that much; I think the body just has to see a "low insulin" signal most of the time, and then a sudden "OMGWTF! insulin signal" for a short period.
>
> \----
>
> Getting an insulin spike purely through fast-acting protein will be: (a) shorter, probably on the order of 1-2hours, and (b) bringing with it a corresponding up-regulation of glucagon.
>
> Glucagon up-regulation during periods of high protein synthesis (which is the case in the last 2-hours of daylight) will increase macro-autophagy rates by quite a significant amount. I personally want to take advantage of this for health optimisation if possible.
>
> \----
>
> My hunch is that the ideal combination for myself for the goal of weight loss is roughly 80-100g of fast-acting protein, coupled with 50g of glucose and 20g of fructose. Of course, I'll eventually put this to the test.
>
> ....

welll...that may be what I observe. I am not able to go a ywhere higher than 100g carbs at least now in the winter. I indeed do exprience to heat up effect (i call it light bulb effect ;-) I would naturally go for the diet you advocate. I am not the counting person, but if I make the counts, these are the numbers I get. Sometimes I wake up and feel like no protein at all. I respect this and get some more carbs with the fat meals. Curiously at the dinner time I ususally "feel" like protein again, but mostly pass this idea and finish the day as very low protein (let's say 15 g a day max). this makes my sleep more profound for the next 3 days. I have a feeling that when you have been doing "right" to your body for reasonable amount of time, you start to act instinctively "smart". It is great to understand what does it mean, but in a way it is enough "to do it". it is a bit like you suddenly understand what to do without employing the intelligence (I may be passed straight to the hands of inquisition for this I think!). take my yesterday...I started to fall down with some virus. I felt very cold and shaking (no fever), but still felt like goint for a swim to Donau. So I went and spent in longer time than usually. After this I craved "blood" so badly, I went home and consume notable amount of bloody lamb liver and some heart, too bad no pork blood sausages anywhere around. No taste for vegetables at all. This morning I woke up craving porc and raw eggs. So I had it and the disease is gone. Well, not exactly a story to tell to my friends or my ob-gyn doc. I have no idea what, why and how....but i did it and it helped. I think I am oficially nuts ;-) btw your new book is a classic in Czech republic where i come from, Kolar is our number one!

### PaulG — 2015-01-28T11:37:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-154080>

> specifically Melbourne which is right under the doughnut hole

Not quite true, according to this [FAQ](http://www.bom.gov.au/uv/faq.shtml)
**

> Does the Antartic Ozone Hole ever come over Australia

**

> No. The ozone hole has only ever been observed to be well south of the Australian mainland and Tasmania. In fact, during springtime, when the hole is in existence, ozone levels over southern Australian cities are at their highest.
>
> However, after the ozone hole has broken up parcels of ozone depleted air mixed with mid latitude air move northwards. These parcels can move over the southern part of Australia and cause a reduction in total ozone values.

and [here](https://uk.answers.yahoo.com/question/index?qid=20070221025340AA0BPGa)

> Ozone depletion takes place over Antarctica in the late winter, early spring as the Sun reappears. Later in the season, the Polar Night Vortex, a stratospheric jet stream, breaks down and some of the ozone depleted air moves north. On occasion it has reached Tasmania but not often.
>
> The UV radiation from the Sun is absorbed in the process of ozone creation and destruction in the mesosphere and stratosphere but not all of it is absorbed. The more radiation coming in, the more gets through with or without polar depletion.
>
> **The solar angle is what is important. In mid-summer, the Sun is almost vertically overhead in Brisbane but still at an angle in Melbourne. The greater the angle, the greater the depth of atmosphere so the more UV is removed. Brisbane with a fully intact ozone layer above it gets much more UV on the ground than Melbourne even with a 70% reduction of ozone over Melbourne.**
>
> Australia has the highest skin cancer rate in the world because northern Europeans are living where they did not evolve to live. Hobart, Australia's most southerly city, is the same latitude south as Rome or New York are north. All of Australia is closer to the equator than there with Darwin being the equivalent of Panama or Sierra Leone. Europeans would not consider being out in the Sahara Desert without protection from the Sun but they do it at the same latitudes in Australia.
>
> There is no ozone depletion over Australia at the moment but the UV Index today is 8 in Melbourne and 16 in Cairns and Darwin. This is normal for this time of year.

There is a [link between Ozone depletion and UV](http://www.epa.gov/ozone/science/myths/ozone_uv.html), personally as a general rule I get Sun between 7-11am and after 3pm (low-mid UV) all year round. During the summer months and when the Ozone hole is largest between late Sept-Feb I cut back on my sun exposure and definitely on days 30 degrees or higher avoid Sun. Generally I wear a large brimmed hat no sunblock and keep exposure to no more than 2hrs, maybe 1-2 times per week. Getting 15mins of morning Light is sufficient from a Vitamin-D level point of view. Don't make the mistake I made thinking that it has to be full on sunshine-even it is overcast get that early morning light (no glasses on) and expose as much skin as possible.

Ozone hole has a greater significance with regards to the potential energy payload of water so I drink Reverse Osmosis Non-fluoridated water or Northern European Spring water.

Check out the [previous monster thread](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/) on this and [this](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/oz-water-chemistry-and-what-to-do-about-it.11351/#post-134796) and then read [Tensegrity 6](http://jackkruse.com/tensegrity-6-hydrogen-bonding-networks-water/)

> **Water is not a homogenous fluid, it is an ionic plasma of small sized cations with small amounts of iodine that becomes able to imprint information and energies from the environment it senses.**
> The hydrogen bonding angle should be around 180 degrees. If the angle is below 150 degrees, then hydrogen bond brakes naturally. Do not confuse water’s angle of bonding hydrogen to oxygen with the hydrogen bonding angles. These are different bonding angles between the atoms that make up water. Normal bonding angles between two H-O bonds in water is 104.5 degrees; in the gas phase or 105.5 degrees in liquid water. Quantum chemistry of the environment of the water cycle is capable of altering water’s bonding angle because of the introduction of a new electronegative elements and the addition of powerful sunlight high up in the atmosphere which alters atmospheric chemistry. The donut hole next to Australia causes an altered bonding angle in water and this is why Oz is not the ideal place for life to live currently below the clouds that make rain.
> It is all about bond angles within the hydrogen bonding networks.
>
> In H2O, only two of the six outer-shell electrons of oxygen are used for this purpose, leaving four electrons which are organized into two non-bonding pairs. The four electron pairs surrounding the oxygen tend to arrange themselves as far from each other as possible in order to minimize repulsions between these clouds of negative charge. This would ordinarily result in a tetrahedral geometry in which the angle between electron pairs (and therefore the H-O-H bond angle) is 109.5°. What we expect is not what we find. Because the two non-bonding pairs remain closer to the oxygen atom, these exert a stronger repulsion against the two covalent bonding pairs, effectively pushing the two hydrogen atoms closer together. The result is a distorted tetrahedral arrangement in which the H—O—H angle is 104.5° in the atmosphere. This is what we call water’s excited state.
> There is a possibility for the main angle of water to deviate significantly from its standard value when water is so called “excited” by a laser or strong electromagnetic radiation (hard UV from the sun for example). This happens in the donut hole in the Southern hemisphere due to a lack of ozone, allowing the UV light to interact with excess of chlorine from human waste gases. This creates a hole in the ozone.

### PaulG — 2015-01-28T12:04:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-154082>

Hmm, looking at the latest [UV chart](http://uv.willyweather.com.au/wa/great-southern/denmark.html) where I live, I should be limiting exposure between 10am-4pm

Longer term view on Ozone recovery
[http://theconversation.com/antarctic-ozone-recovery-will-be-a-long-and-bumpy-road-22429](http://theconversation.com/antarctic-ozone-recovery-will-be-a-long-and-bumpy-road-22429)

> A smaller hole doesn’t mean recovery. In 2012, the Antarctic ozone hole was the second smallest since the early 1990s. The ozone vertical profiles measured by OMPS in the centre of the hole showed severe depletion in the lower stratosphere (below 22km) starting from mid-September. What does this tell us? It means that the area is still sufficiently saturated in chlorine to destroy nearly all the ozone. OMPS readings also show that ozone above 22km increased in October 2012, due to winds. Viewed as a column, the higher ozone levels above 22km masks the lack of ozone below, which gives the false impression that the overall ozone depletion is less severe than it actually is.
>
> So it seems the road to recovery will be bumpy. As chlorine levels gradually drop, wind variations in some years will produce a big enough downward slump in chlorine to reduce the size and severity of the ozone hole. But in other years, wind variations may produce an upward bump in chlorine, **and if it’s cold as well, the ozone hole will be large and deep**.
>
> While this could last until the 2030s, beyond then ozone levels should consistently increase and the hole should be consistently smaller than it is today, setting us on a path to recovery by about 2070, give or take a decade.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-28T12:08:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-154084>

> [Seamus Ryan said:](/goto/post?id=154074)
>
> To all the Aussie members on this forum. I have a question for you (I posed this to Jack on Facebook, he suggested I would get a better response here) - On a different note Jack, can you please give me a specific reply to this question. If you lived in Australia (specifically Melbourne which is right under the doughnut hole) how would you live your life in terms of sun exposure, grounding, seafood etc. ie the sun burns here in literally 12 minutes, am I more protected if grounded, should I limit exposure because of the hole. Give me a typical summers day/ winters day if you lived here. I don't wear sunscreen unless absolutely necessary (ie out golfing for 5 hours) is this the right approach? I don't know what we need to do given our problems here. Your thoughts people?

Hey there . I used to live 10km north of the Melbourne CBD, but as @[caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) said, I move to Cairns in early September 2014, to dramatic health improvements.

Let's go through the stressors one by one.

\----

@[PaulG](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1838/) mentinoed the issue with the water in the Southern Hemisphere being higher-density and thus less capable of charge separation. On top of that, it is fluoridated almost everywhere in Australia (I'm lucky that fluoridation was ended in late 2013 here in Cairns after a brief introduction in early 2013, with the governing bodies citing it as an unneeded cost).

The effect that drinking this will depend on your redox potential. What really matters is the quality of water that is in and around your cells. If proteins have enough electrons, this will naturally cause water to charge-separate, and basically nullify the effects of any dissolved ionic chaotropes. Best bet is still to be safe than sorry, and get some un-fluoridated water.

\----

Theer will be high non-native EMF from man-made transmitters (power lines, cell phones, mandatory smart meters, etc ...), which is a common problem to any big city

No choice here but to try and get some form of EMF shielding, or move somewhere which doesn't have that many of these threats. Some places south of Frankston would probably be much better EMF wise. Where @[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/) is in Torquay is excellent.

\----

There will be higher cosmic radiation during the months of late August to early November when the Ozone Hole dips below 300 dobson units. IMO, there is nothing that can be done about this, since these are heavy ionised protons that will wreck through everything, including the walls of your house. The only defence is a lead ceiling (or similar material that can absorb said particles). I don't know how to implement this .

\----

Regarding the UV issue, I personally never did care that much, and just exposed myself to as much Sun as possible while I was there. I've got darker skin though, and I never ever got a sunburn. Your mileage will vary on this item. I always suggest getting as much sunshine as possible, say 5mins below your burn threshold.

Perhaps a minor point, but try to stick to natural sunblock as far as possible. Using artifical sunblock with aluminium isn't good for you (unbound transition metals generally are not good). The alternative is coconut oil with the possible addition of a magnesium sulfate spray.

\----

Do natural grounding with skin contact against the ground as much as possible. Living next to the beach like @[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/) is ideal.

\----

There are going to be issues specific to a location which are too complex to determine through logical analysis.

Josh figured out through observation that the people in his original home of Bendigo were much sicker than usual.

Personally, while I was scouting for potential places to live in Melburne, I found that the North of Melbourne, including the entire plain from Healesville to Bendigo, and encompassing the more inner suburbs like Eltham, Bundoora, Keilor, etc ... all were home to many people with mental illness and developmental issues. That should be a warning sign that these places are not good to live.

I scouted down south in the Mornington Peninsula as well, and the inland suburbs did nto look good. The beachside suburbs like Mt Martha, Safety Beach, Dromana, etc ...all loked pretty chill, but I had ruled out that region by then.

Here in Cairns, I see a lot of obesity, but nowhere near as much mental illness. That is still a problem, but it doesn't signal as much of a nn-EMF problem as in the big cities in Melbourne.

\----

Finally, I think I'll post up something that I wrote up regarding some reasons that I made the move from Melbourne to Cairns. Coming up in the next post ....

....

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-28T12:09:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-154085>

**Cairns vs Melbourne**

This is going to be long, but I hope that it's concise enough.

\----

**(1) Better water in Cairns**

The water supply here in Cairns is pretty abundant, and the water is lower-density (higher energy) water.

No fluoridation in Cairns since the local governing body thought it was too expensive.

Also, the water comes from the Northern Hemisphere, and all the reservoirs are fed from the Indo-Australian monsoon during the wet season (Jan-Apr).

Northern Hemisphere water isn't exposed to as much cosmic radiation than Southern Hemisphere water (due to the ozone hole), and is generally lower density water.

All of the Australian South, including even places as high as Brisbane, will get water from precipitation on the mountainous regions of the South. The wind comes in from South America and blows across the atlantic to drop water in Australia.

\----

**(2) Variability in amount and intensity of Sunlight is much less**

Sydney gets 14hr 47m in summer (Dec 22) with a 75.6deg angle -- [http://www.timeanddate.com/sun/australia/melbourne?month=12&year=2014](http://www.timeanddate.com/sun/australia/melbourne?month=12&year=2014)

And then 9hr 32m in winter (June 22) with a 28.8deg angle -- [http://www.timeanddate.com/sun/australia/melbourne?month=6&year=2014](http://www.timeanddate.com/sun/australia/melbourne?month=6&year=2014)

Cairns gets 13hr 8m in summer (Dec 22) with a 83.5deg angle -- [http://www.timeanddate.com/sun/australia/cairns?month=12&year=2014](http://www.timeanddate.com/sun/australia/cairns?month=12&year=2014)

And then 11hr 7m in winter (June 22) with a 49.7deg angle -- [http://www.timeanddate.com/sun/australia/cairns?month=6&year=2014](http://www.timeanddate.com/sun/australia/cairns?month=6&year=2014)

Furthermore, Cairns has a 4-month wet season during the longer light months of Jan to Apr, and then a dry season for the rest, meaning that you get enough strong sunlight during the wet months despite the cloudy rainy weather, and then you get lots of sunlight in the dry months due to the clear weather despite the shorter light cycle.

It's pretty much above 220hrs of strong sunshine a month during every month here.

When I was in Melbourne, there was not much sun in winter time, because it was further south, and because it has much wetter winters than Cairns. You're getting <100hrs of weaker sunshine during a typical Melbourne winter month.

\----

**(3) Proximity to the Beach**

Free electrons from the sand and surf just 200m away from me. Of course, I could have been next to the beach in Melbourne, but that wasn't to be in my case.

\----

**(4) Less EMF in Cairns**

Population is just under 200,000 in Cairns, and is about 5,000 in my beach-front suburb (Trinity Beach) --[https://www.google.com.au/maps/place/Trinity Beach QLD/data=!4m2!3m1!1s0x69786e41c05aa08f:0x500eef17f210880?sa=X&ei=2p-tVPnLCIb58QXE5oDQCg&ved=0CJkBEPIBMA8](https://www.google.com.au/maps/place/Trinity+Beach+QLD/data=!4m2!3m1!1s0x69786e41c05aa08f:0x500eef17f210880?sa=X&ei=2p-tVPnLCIb58QXE5oDQCg&ved=0CJkBEPIBMA8)

We all know that Melbourne is a place with about 4million total population, and that more people usually means more power lines, more cell phone use, more cell towers, etc .....

Smart meters are mandatory in VIC, but here in QLD, the government decided not to put them in due to cost issues.
Here in Cairns, all the major power lines are inland towers, so being at the coast puts me at least 4km away from those buggers.

\----

**(5) Likely better O2 levels in Cairns**

I've got the beach 200m from me, and the rainforest about 2km away. In some places like Palm Cove, you're literally living next to the beach, a stone's throw from a swamp, and then having the rainforest a couple hundred metres behind you.

Mangroves and Rainforests make some good O2. Melbourne and Sydney don't really have the same density of within the urban environments. (some Rural communities are decent)

\---

**(6) Maybe a better magnetic field in Cairns**

I'm not 100% convinced about this.

In terms of raw field strength, my old place was higher than here.

Old place in Melbourne was 60 mT -- [http://magnetic-declination.com/Australia/Coburg/118242.html](http://magnetic-declination.com/Australia/Coburg/118242.html)

Current suburb in Cairns ranks at around 47.4 mT -- [http://magnetic-declination.com/Australia/Trinity Beach/125259.html](http://magnetic-declination.com/Australia/Trinity%20Beach/125259.html)

The magnetic inclination is different of course, and that may have an effect, in that I can sense the environment better with my current magnetic field characteristics.

This is something that requires further research, and can be only discerned through biohacks for now.

\-----

Finally, the distance above sea level could make a difference. I don't know how big a difference it would make, since the differences are so small between the places that I've lived in.

In general, you start seeing significant effects at 300m above sea level.

In Melbourne I was at 80m above sea level, and now I am currently at 5m above sea level.

### PaulG — 2015-01-28T12:12:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-154086>

Interesting article here
[http://joannenova.com.au/2015/01/is-the-sun-driving-ozone-and-changing-the-climate/](http://joannenova.com.au/2015/01/is-the-sun-driving-ozone-and-changing-the-climate/)

which talks about the Oct 2014 paper by Andersson et al energetic electron precipitation (EEP)
**[Missing driver in the Sun–Earth connection from energetic electron precipitation impacts mesospheric ozone](http://www.nature.com/ncomms/2014/141014/ncomms6197/full/ncomms6197.html)**

> **Further thoughts on the Andersson “energetic electron” paper**
> The new paper by Andersson et al builds on the hypothesis that ozone is influential and a potential mechanism to amplify solar factors. It adds energetic electron precipitation (EEP) to spectral changes in UV, which is a significant step forward.
>
> Andersson et al describe it as having a short term regional effect, with no implications for global or long term climate change. But if the effect is significant between the peak and trough of a single solar cycle, then surely it is also going to be significant over the millennial cycle of solar variation — such as that observed from the Medieval Warm Period through the Little Ice Age and up to date.
>
> Observations of climate changes across the last thousand years suggest that it must be so. In the Medieval Warm Period, Greenland had agriculture and the Western Isles of Scotland were prosperous with a much larger population than today—which implies more poleward climate zones and zonal jets at that time. In contrast, ships logs from the Little Ice Age show much greater Atlantic storminess and more equatorward mid latitude depression tracks at that time (depressions generally follow the tracks of the jet streams).
>
> **Food for speculation**
> Stephen Wilde’s hypothesis is a possible mechanism for the [notch-delay theory](http://sciencespeak.com/climate-nd-solar.html), in which the TSI drives surface temperatures after a delay of one sunspot cycle (~11 years) and which potentially explains most of the temperature variations over the last few hundred years. This would occur if the [extreme ultraviolet](http://lpce.cnrs-orleans.fr/~ddwit/publications/EUV_strategy.pdf) that drives ozone creation and destruction, and the effects of the energetic electron precipitation found by Andersson et al, both lag the trends in bulk TSI (visible light and normal UV) by one sunspot cycle — that is, by half a full solar cycle (~22 years).
>
> (Quick reminder: The delay of one sunspot cycle in the ND theory overcomes the objection that because TSI and so on peaked around 1986 and surface temperatures kept rising to about 1997, the Sun cannot be driving temperature. The delay can explain this: 1986 + 11 = 1997. The delay also means that the [fall off in bulk TSI](http://joannenova.com.au/2014/06/big-news-viii-new-solar-model-predicts-imminent-global-cooling/) around 2004 presages a fall in surface temperatures around about one sunspot later, around 2017: 2004 + 13 = 2017. The “pause” the believers of the carbon crisis have lately admitted to may turn out to be a “plateau”.)

### yewwei.tan — 2015-02-03T11:08:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-154594>

**<Weight Loss Week 3 Update>**

The main modifications over the past week were an increased protein intake and faster-digesting proteins as the first thing consumed during dinner-time.

The results were pretty dramatic, basically causing 1kg of immediate weight loss over the course of 3 days

Of course, me being the idiot I am, started experimenting with other possibilities for introducing carbs and insulin spikes, including during the early day (not good on me).

The conclusion in my case is that late-day insulin spikes work wonders for fat loss. I personally think it's an insulin-only effect, with no need for carbs, since I could achieve the same effect using just protein.

Addition of carbs did not seem to hamper progress.

No change in other parameters of health were noticed.

\----

The most profound effect however, was a dramatic improvement in collagen quality (I don't have a better word for simultaneous improvement in relative muscular strength, tendon quality, skin quality, etc ...., so "collagen quality" it is)

Once I dipped to or below 79kg, I immediately saw:

-   An increase in flexibility within my tight right hip (past injury)
-   An increase in chin-up strength not proportionate to the weight drop. Specifically, I could do maybe 6 reps "without much effort" at 80kg, but 10 reps with the same sort of effort at 79kg
-   Dramatic improvement in my feet swelling, whereby it was slightly noticeable at 80kg, but almost gone at 79kg

I'm hoping for these effects to continue as I continue to lose weight.

I have ZERO clue as to the mechanism behind this. All I can say now is that fractals be real , and that macroscopic mass reductions somehow either reduce distances between or increase hydrophilic potential of collagen fibrils on a microscopic level to produce energetic advantages (more quantum mechanical effects)

\----

Anyway, for now, I'm done with some of the experiments. I already know what works, and that seems to be a "carb re-feed day" consisting of:

\* Alternating non-refeed and refeed days

\* During the refeed days:

\- Some small fat intake during the morning (about 200kcal)
\- High fat intake from about 11am to 1pm. Usually I'll have my mackerel bowl here (about 800kcal)
\- Some form of exercise at about 4-5pm
\- 100g whey protein + some form of additional carbs or protein. Sugar intake here doesn't seem to affect results so long as I don't go above 100g of sucrose.​

\* During the non-refeed days, I just concentrate food intake to the 12pm to 5pm window, and mostly keep things high fat. Food intake on these days is about 2000kcal​

\----

For now, the goal is to get as low as 75-76kg as quickly as possible, and validate the hypothesis of lower mass => better collagen in my case.

Once I hit that weight, I'll probably stabilise my weight there for a little while and see what happens. The practice of Insulin spiking will be reduced to probably at most once a week.

\----

Now it's time to boost my understanding of Pavel Kolar's stuff -- I need to find ways of monitoring my "collagen quality", and movement and stability analyses is a great way to do that

....
