**Red LED**

I definitely feel this thing almost instantly. Sat in front of it with the setup pictured above with my back basically fully illuminated for exactly 15 minutes. The ambient temperature is about 18C / 64F here in the morning, and once I was in front of the light for about 30 seconds, there was a noticeable feeling of both alertness, and goosebumps all over my body. Then I started radiating a little bit of heat, an indicator that I'm getting some metabolic uncoupling.

Otherwise, there's not too much of a difference before and after on this first day of use. Report back in 2-3 weeks

> [CTforlife said:](/goto/post?id=164729)
>
> If asking about our coupled evolution is causing trouble in the world. Well then the world is dead. Sleep on that. If you think it's a get dha and run well I have another thing coming. Called emergence and evolution, something not absolute like get dha and run.
>
> Do you know what happens when you condense concentrated dmt? If you hit dmt with a hammer it releases light every hit.... That dmt and light is in your calcified pineal, fluorides or the governments main target.
>
> Do you think we are still evolving yew? Actually it's a waste of energy to type that you are dying not evolving.

What about Water? When water is condensed and cooled to 5 degress above absolute zero, it still retains an intrinsic proton disorder (ie: intrinsic increase in entropy, and thus evolution) -- [http://arxiv.org/pdf/1504.04158v1.pdf](http://arxiv.org/pdf/1504.04158v1.pdf)

This disorder will allow any life that is in contact with water to take advantage of the Electromagnetic Force to create innovation -- [http://www.jackkruse.com/tensegrity-11-exotic-atoms-matrix/](http://www.jackkruse.com/tensegrity-11-exotic-atoms-matrix/)

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-24T02:42:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-165156>

**24 Years Old**

Well, today I turned 24 years old. It's been a mix of mostly shitty circumstances and some good times for 22 years, then consolidation of both skills and wants in the last 2 years. Most importantly, I've probably already experienced most of what life has to give (minus being to a war zone or getting married ... some people would say that both are pretty similar ). Not going to discuss all those circumstances here, but nothing I wouldn't share in a face-to-face conversation

More importantly, I've come to a point whereby I know what I want to achieve, and what I need to achieve it. From this point on, I'm pretty content at just preserving Tranquility, rather than seeking Thrills.

Big thanks to Dr Kruse for giving me a paradigm shift in thinking and giving me the perspective to seek health in today's crazy world.

Obligatory birthday red speedo selfie taken with a red cellphone  (technically those are AussieBums, not Speedos)

....

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-24T07:07:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-165176>

Thanks for the well wishes Everyone!

> [JMO said:](/goto/post?id=165168)
>
> Happy Birthday Yew! You are so smart and wise for your age...unbelievable (well a testament for all the DHA you grew up with). My sons are your age...maybe starting to listen a little but it would be so cool if they could teach me this stuff-- I learn so much from your posts. Have a great day and don't party too much!

I got lucky . Despite only having (caged) eggs as a DHA source for most of my life, I somehow managed to store enough in the right parts of the brain (I've been depressed, but I always think logically).

That is despite taking my first plane ride when I was 2 months old (10 hour flight), being born via C-section, not being breast fed, living next to major power lines for most of my childhood, growing up in a negative household environment and society in general (Asian elitism and perfectionism), having mostly shitty food, being myopic by age 5, living in a big city with lots of Internet access and Blue light 24/7 for the majority of my first 17 years (Singapore), etc ....

I can also look back at all the people I used to call friends stuck in the same old cycles that afflict any nn-EMF-overdosed population. All my cousins (I have more than 30) are screwed up either mentally or physically from the diseases of modern life, and even my brother didn't get dealt the same brain structure that I have.

Sidenote: We've got people on the forums from Europe, South American, etc .... but no one from the Asian nations on these forums, and I don't think it's as much a linguistic issue as much as it is an issue of culture and pollution -- the culture does not allow you to think thoughts that make you likely of landing on the Kruse blog, and the entire Asian region is over-population and polluted with nn-EMF, further preventing the brain-plasticity needed to break out of that cycle.​

So I've been nothing but lucky , and I don't intend to let that luck go to waste. My gut and peripheral collagen may have failed earlier than most people, but my brain stayed pretty functional. (Never got a migraine in my life BTW, another indicator that my brain can somehow control water flows well)

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=165158)
>
> Happy birthday Yew! I hope you're having a great day .
> Oh, to be 24 again!

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=165164)
>
> Will it be an oyster day today?????

> [cinnamon said:](/goto/post?id=165174)
>
> Happy Birthday, Yew!

> [Jenny S said:](/goto/post?id=165175)
>
> Happy Birthday Yew, hope you have nice weather for a good beach day!

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=165171)
>
> Happy BD go eat a fish..........

TBH, I was more like "Don't do anything to fuck this day up"  I did eat lots of fish though.

Instead of explicitly doing something special, I spent some time on the beach (and about to head out again), and gave a first-pass read of this paper by Roger Penrose and Stuart Hameroff, '**Consciousness in the universe: A review of the ‘Orch OR’ theory**' -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1571064513001188](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1571064513001188)

Thanks to @[David Limacher](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/11045/) for introducing me to this guy. Hameroff comes the closest to explaining consciousness IMO, and his model is 100% compatible with everything you'll find on this site.

One lecture:

A probably more approachable interview:

In this model:

\- Consciousness is not an emergent property of computation, but is instead an intrinsic Qualia of the Universe. Microtubules organise these qualia.

\- **Cognition is not Consciousness.** Cognition refers to being able to observe and react to stimuli, while consciousness means being able to perceive and choose which cognitive paths to take.

\- Microtubules are the site for quantum computation, and all their associated proteins (including the tau protein and actin) determine when a computation starts and terminates

\- **Hexagonal** Calcium-activated CaMKII (calcium-calmodulin kinase 2), when activated, binds perfectly to the **microtubule hexagonal lattice,** and is responsible for reifying a computation as a concrete neuronal impulse.

Sidenote: note that Saturn's north polar hexagon is likely a vortex phenomenon due to electrical forces in conducting fluid -- [https://www.thunderbolts.info/wp/2014/03/28/saturns-hexagon/](https://www.thunderbolts.info/wp/2014/03/28/saturns-hexagon/)

The hexagonal configuration is energetically efficient somehow, and represents a low energy state. In biological molecules, hexagonal rings also allow lots of delocalised pi-electrons to exist in various superpositions (likely taking advantage of the energetically favourable hexagonal configuration).

DNA is dependent on the Nitrogen containing Purine and Pyrimidine groups, which modify the energetic structure and C-N-C bond angle slightly in the hexagonal ring. I immediately suspected that Nitrogen "decides whether or not" to make the Pyrimidine group perfectly hexagonal (long enough for information signalling to occur), and thus allow very specific expression of proteins.​

\- The model presented in 'Tubulin dipoles and anesthesia' is perfectly congruent with why high magnetic fields stop consciousness by disallowing the "act of making a choice of dipole" in the tubulin qubit.

\- I happen to think that τ≈ħ/EG actually doesn't just imply that Orchestrated Objective Reduction (Orch-OR) creates consciousness, but it also creates time itself. This process happens everywhere in the Universe, but it just so happens that microtubules allow for a speeding up of time T by increasing the denominator EG (gravitational self energy -- which Dr Kruse has given many examples which affect this, like pressure, density, external magnetism, cold, etc ...).

Note: the important structures here are the microtubules, not necessary a brain. Consciousness occurs everywhere in the body, since there are microtubule-like structures everywhere (though I still think tubulin is the perfect structure, allowing consciousness to be concentrated in the brain).​

This note on gravity is also where I see the biggest flaw in the paper -- the assumption that gravity is constant, for example, a quote:

Ec=Gm2/ac, where _G_ is the gravitational constant, _m_ is the carbon nuclear mass, and ac is the carbon nucleus sphere radius equal to 2.5 Fermi distances. We calculated that roughly 2×1010 tubulins displaced in coherent superposition for 25 ms will, on this basis, self-collapse in that time period, and elicit Orch OR. For a _τ_ of 500 ms, ∼109tubulins would be required.
​

Big G is not constant, and can be modified by the body to allow for more favourable quantum computation. This means that Hameroff's model is even more correct BTW.

\----

I only briefly skimmed the paper, and as usual, the devil is in the details. I will need to spend the next week slowly investigating each point and figuring out how each component works. I have a gut sense that Hameroff is missing large chunks on how the electromagnetic force affects the action of these microtubules (through water), but I think that his model is spot on.

I'm also reading Nick Lane's '**The Vital Question: Why is Life the Way it is?**', and after 2 chapters, I am positive that there is stuff in here that is relevant to the Orch-OR discussion -- [http://www.amazon.com/The-Vital-Question-Why-Life/dp/1781250367](http://www.amazon.com/The-Vital-Question-Why-Life/dp/1781250367)

Fun times!  Kruse Cruise in 3 weeks, gonna have some fun conversations on that trip.

....

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-05-25T20:48:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-165320>

Yew: even more 'belated' Happy Birthday from me. I really like the way you talk about yourself too. We know you have a great brain but it's interesting to hear a bit of your personal story.

BTW I believe you got the book "Light in Shaping Life" by Van Wijk. I am curious if you have read and if so what you think of it. I am about 2/3 way through and I find it very very good, seems to 'explain' so much. I found his stuff on cancer and genetics very convincing. The idea of cancer as just another 'metabolic' disease like diabetes makes all the sense in the world and I though he conclusively debunks the whole emphasis on 'genetics' and shows how mitochondrial genetics are a bigger deal for cancer than nuclear genetics

BTW I am now almost done with the 3 books Dr Kruse has strongly recommended "The 4th Phase of Water" "Life at the Edge: Quantum Biology" and now the Van Wijk one. To me 3 brilliant books that 'explain' so much...................I think being here is worth the price of admission just for Dr K's recommendations on what to read. In that way he is shining his flash light as he says. But just to be introduced to books like that is truly valuable. In today's world there is so much 'noise' just for someone to guide me like that..............well I really appreciate it and it is worth a lot..............

To me 3 brilliant books that 'explain' so much...................I think being here is worth the price of admission just for Dr K's recommendations on what to read. In that way he is shining his flash light as he says. But just to be introduced to books like that is truly valuable. In today's world there is so much 'noise' just for someone to guide me like that..............well I really appreciate it and it is worth a lot..............

Well, if you decide to make it to the cruise in 3 weeks time, I can talk a lot about my personal story . Maybe over a bottle of Grange (and maybe even sucker @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) into a trip to Doris Metro to drink that Grange ). Don't want to write it out here because I need to mention a lot of people who may not want to be mentioned on the Internet.

\----

Regarding the Biophoton book, here were some notes I made in a private conversation (only my comments included):

Finished with an initial skim of 'Light in Shaping Life'. Very detailed review of the existing research out there. Formatting is very good grin emoticon . Also useful in describing exact experimental techniques used.

As expected, it's doesn't emphasis a lot of analysis, so putting things together is left to the reader.

Seeing great graphs of biophoton emission spectra is great though.

Hand biophoton analysis is going to be really promising in the future.

Now I need to learn accupuncture, because that's one of the topics which was mentioned, but then you're left in the last chapter with a couple of "what if?"s

The images in the last chapter of body part biophoton emission are pretty cool

Finger biophoton emission as a diagnostic criteria was something mentioned last December to me, and I wonder how far along that has come along since then.

Pretty easy to get a dark environment too, since covering the hands is pretty easy.

Unless the patient doesn't have hands, or is missing a hand

Emission could be different though. Huge concentration in the fingers, especially the middle finger.

Big difference between the left and right hand too.

Generally less emission at night, and more during the day, and more from the right hand.

Makes sense that you emit less light as you sleep.

There was also description of meditation experiments where trascendental meditation led to drops in light emission by about 20-30% (page 350-356)

Probably, but these are just observations for now. Not much discussion on what exactly it means.

But since you see greater charge gradients during periods of injury and growth, it's probably safe to say that biophoton emission is correlated with "having to do work to continue living".

I still don't have the answer to my question: How much red light therapy is too much? And how do I tell?

I was having some random riffing with Rub about how your (osteo) comment about "Skin turns red when it has absorbed enough red light" should serve as a context-dependent metric for light therapies.

That is when energy addition results either in significant temperature rise, or when the applied light is being reflected / not absorbed to a significant degree, then it is time to stop therapy for that patient.

Measurement is going to be difficult.

My simplistic mind says: We're using visible 650nm red light, so we should be able to get back visible feedback. But I don't think it works that way.

ntuitively, it sounds right though, and is a natural reaction of at least the skin to sunlight.

Sunburn is basically a negative feedback signal to "get the fuck out of the sun you idiot"

\----

But see, that's where I get confused, since stuff like massage also drives localised "inflammation" to the spot being manipulated.

"inflammation" meaning positive charge.

And hence photon release due to movement of that charge.

So is adding more exogenous red light is a good thing in that case?

ie: Exogenous red light of the correct wavelength is mimicking the body's own capability of "pushing electrons toward the site of inflammation", and thereby allowing some restoration back to the negatively charged tissue baseline, and thereby allowing further physical manipulation by the osteopath.
​

And then, the Biophoton Book talked about how DNA emits lots of UV light during the process of mitosis.

What would happen if you provide exogenous UV during the process of something like osteopathic manipulation, where rates of mitosis are upregulated due to the external "injury stress" (which is meant to be hormetic).

Is it going to overload the system and cause havoc? Or is it going to enhance signalling?

That's a dangerous experiment to run on humans, but it's something that I would love to see further research into (starting with plant, and then animal models)

I'm wondering how to determine if UV and blue light can be used. Obviously works with healthy people for preventing jetlag.

Not sure the effect will be the same with sick people.

Red light I suspect that you can monitor more easily via colour and temperature change, and is in general not very harmful anyway.

Larger margin for error with red light is what I mean.

You making a trip to frozen Canberra tongue emoticon

Sidenote: still not convinced Canberra is a good spot to be in, being far from the sea and >550m elevation in most places. Wonder why Wallace Thornhill still stays there. Might have some good magnetism?
​

This was before I wrote my UV biophoton notes -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/neurons-protons-and-lactate.14517/#post-164393](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/neurons-protons-and-lactate.14517/#post-164393)

\----

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=165326)
>
> @[yewwei.tan](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6824/)
>

Only doing low doses (3-5g), and the motivation was to improve eyesight. I'm definitely seeing better, but that's more probably due to the sun exposure and walking on rocks without my glasses (have to spot the cracks properly, and the barnacles ....). The bicarbonate is not harmful, and probably has a small beneficial effect, but nothing significant I can directly attribute to it.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-06-16T07:44:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-168335>

**Minor Update Before the Cruise
**
Been busy with my software business, while trying to stay as healthy as possible for the cruise.

Mostly a lot of reading and staying away from the Computer while I can (which explains the relative lull in postings from me)

\----
**Travel Management**

I'll only be leaving Australia early morning of <Fri-19-Jun-2015>. The flight path will be:

\- 5:50am Cairns to Brisbane (2 h 10 m)
\- Layover 2 h 50 m
\- Brisbane to Los Angeles (13 h 5 m) => reach LA at 6:55am local time
\- Layover 2 h 25 m
\- LA to NOLA (3 h 51 m) => reach NOLA 3:11pm local time​

Total travel time of 24 h 21m starting from the flight from Cairns.

However, I'll probably be up by 3am Cairns time, so that makes 27.5 hours.

Then I'll be waiting at the airport (likely with Josh Ferber) until about 5pm when Inger arrives. Then we'll make our way to the place we've rented. I expect to be at the at the place latest by 6:30pm, which puts me at about 31 hours door-to-door.

Then I'll probably catch-up with Neil, Gretchen, Josh Lamaro, etc .... , hope I'm not too sleep deprived by that time.

Given the above, the general strategy will be to approximate the sleep state as much as possible during the flight over the Pacific; I'm landing in LA in the morning USA time, so anything before that should mimic sleep as much as possible.

\- This means no food once I start my travels at 3am Cairns time, with introduction of food only once I reach LA (No nicotine during this period either)

\- More likely than not, I'll chew 2mg of Nicotine once I hit LA, and drink as much water as a can. I may decide to eat something at this point, but will have to decide what exactly to do based on how I feel at the time.

\- No coffee or tea during the entire trip. Just something that I find personally to work out with long flights

\- Probably going to grab some dinner the night of <Fri-19-Jun-2015>

\- Likely grab just a little bit of protein the morning of <Sat-20-Jun-2015> to yoke myself to the US daylight cycle. Pre-cruise party is that night too, which will inevitably lead to some alcohol intake . I personally suffer more ill effects when I put alcohol with lots of food, so I will also limit food intake during that day after breakfast.​

\----

I'll be bringing the supplements: Trans-resveratrol, Curcumin, Vit K2 MK4, PQQ, Ubiquinol

Plus a small canister of coconut oil, some oregano oil, and some lavender oil (insect repellent).

\----
**Red LED Light Experimenting**

The light I have is a combined 630nm and 660nm LED light, and I can definitely feel the effects. Most days I will just use it for 15 mins in the morning, and that's it.

I have tried pushing the limits of exposure on this device, with the rule being that once I start to feel a little drowsy/sleepy, I'll stop any further exposure.

Using that rule of thumb, I've gone as far as 5 x 20 min sessions, only to feel better and better each time.

This proved to vary with the seasonal effects stated below, and I think the "expose until sleepy" rule is a good rule of thumb for me. That said, I'd rather be cautious, and stick to 1 session a day -- more seems to add a little bit of added benefit to my joints, but the effect on my mental capacity tampers off.

\----
**Seasonal Effects
**
This last moon cycle seemed to have major effects on myself and many other people I questioned during that period

From about early May to mid June, I had a period of higher-than-normal mental focus and overall energy in general. This was despite decreasing light levels where I'm at (though it's still >11hrs each day)

I experienced a downturn back to normal energy levels at about the first week of June. Asking a bunch of people in Australia, they reported the same sort of downturn in health.

The full moon was on 4th June, and where I was at I got another week of very tall high tides (3m) that pushed the sand line a little further up on most of the beaches close to where I'm at. If the moon has that sort of effect on the ocean, you can bet that it has a significant effect on your brain.

Still, the only 2 things I noticed were a gain in a little bit of weight (hovering around 77.5kg), and a drop back to normal levels of mental focus.

\----
**Current State of Health
**
Feeling decent. Still getting good sleep, good diet, lots of grounding, etc ...

Sidenote: being grounded on the beach is by far the biggest factor in impacting my bodyweight. Living like a plant really impacts fat stores ​

Still skinny  -- 76.6kg this morning, 76.5cm waist, and not feeling hungry at all. Hope I don't shrink too much during the trip .

Collagen quality has been very slowly improving. Front splits getting closer, knees not as tight, etc .... I expect this to take a longer time to fix though, on the scale of at least 3 years. Not very easy to re-model neural patterns that have been ingrained since birth, but the direction I am moving in is roughly correct.

\======

I can guarantee that the cruise will be a fucking fun time , and I am certain that I will be stressed out as hell during and after the trip

Next 2 days will be spent in tranquility while getting prepared for the hectic journey.

....

Regardless, I gained a lot from the trip, and met a lot of interesting people. Some health interventions are incoming for myself, and some further discussion into magnetic monopoles and fundamental truths of the Universe are likely to surface.

What I gained in insight I lost in bodyweight . I left at around 76.5kg with a 77cm waist. Got back home at 1:30pm local time yesterday after 30+ hours of travel. Weighed myself at 76.3kg, and this morning, I had dropped to 74.2kg with a waist measurement less than 76cm . Time to eat.

More details on what is to come in the following 1-2 weeks.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-07-08T08:10:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-171354>

**Misc Update
**
Doing well, but busy with lots of thoughts , plus having to deal with my business.

I've been stuck in a bunch of research, and there's too much mind-blowing shit I'm reading / listening to, and I'm not yet at a point whereby I can make a full synthesis of the information. I think it'll take me slightly over a month to come to useful conclusions

\----

In terms of health protocols, I'm going to give a protocol that Josh recommended a shot. The testing of both Josh's, as well as Andrew Ferber, seem to indicate that my liver is under stress (likely caused by parasites).

What I am doing starting today is:

-   **PRL Parasta**t: 4 caps a dose, 3 times a day, for a total of 12 caps a day, dosed for 30 days ([http://www.netpharmacy.co.nz/default.aspx?catid=7283&pdid=2533](http://www.netpharmacy.co.nz/default.aspx?catid=7283&pdid=2533))
-   **a few rounds of PRL Medi-Body Pack and PRL Detox ND treatment** ([http://www.netpharmacy.co.nz/prl-medi-body-pack-333g?nav=7283#](http://www.netpharmacy.co.nz/prl-medi-body-pack-333g?nav=7283#) and [http://www.netpharmacy.co.nz/prl-heavy-metal-nano-detox-237ml?nav=7283#](http://www.netpharmacy.co.nz/prl-heavy-metal-nano-detox-237ml?nav=7283#))
-   **Methylation Support**:
    -   Molybdenum: 150mcg dosed 4-6 times a day
    -   Manganese: 15-30mg a day
    -   Lithium Orotate: 10-20mg a day
    -   5MTHF: 400-800mcg a day

This is in addition to my usual supplement regime:

-   Trans-Resveratrol: 300-600mg a day
-   Curcumin: 600-1200mg a day
-   PQQ: 20mg a day
-   Vit K2 MK4: 5mg a day

We'll see what the next 30 days bring. I have a bunch of markers that I can track, and will report back after the experiment is done.

### Richelle Jones — 2015-07-26T08:26:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-173027>

Hi Yew
I have read with great interest your posts re moving to Cairns. I am looking to move away from Canberra. Now that you have lived there for a while do you have any other advice on the pro and cons of that location? I have 2 dogs, 2 cats & 2 children..what about box jellyfish, crocodiles and cane toads? Can u swim at the beach?

### yewwei.tan — 2015-07-26T13:23:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-173039>

> [Richelle Jones said:](/goto/post?id=173027)
>
> Hi Yew
> I have read with great interest your posts re moving to Cairns. I am looking to move away from Canberra. Now that you have lived there for a while do you have any other advice on the pro and cons of that location? I have 2 dogs, 2 cats & 2 children..what about box jellyfish, crocodiles and cane toads? Can u swim at the beach?

I'm gonna be biased , because I'm of tropical ancestry, and like hot and humid climates. 35C and 100% humidity was the environment I grew up in (Singapore), and Cairns barely reaches that ever (and likely not with Solar Mininum coming in 2030-2040).

I don't worry about any of the critters here. Stay away from croc territory, don't swim in the ocean during the warm periods, try not to go wandering in the bush, make sure all your doors and windows are either closed or have fly screens, and you're fine. TBH, I'm more worried about running into a kangaroo while on the road (there are tons of them in the fields in my suburb )

To cut to the chase, there are too many pros and cons to list. The only way that you will know is to actually visit this place for at least 2 weeks during both the extreme seasons (dry in July, and wet in February), and then decide whether that treats you well.

A decently objective comparison of Cairns vs Melbourne can be found here -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-40#post-154085](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-40#post-154085) . Canberra basically shares the same issues as Melbourne, except that it isn't as crowded (good), but is higher up (likely bad).

\-----
**Longer Term
**
Naturally, I'm looking at good options to meet the likely impending Solar Minimum, which already started in some ways, though we probably won't see the effects until 2018, and then an abnormally low solar peak in 2022, with a full on minimum by 2030.

'Dark Winter' is probably a good book regarding this matter -- [http://www.amazon.com/Dark-Winter-Causing-30-Year-Spell/dp/1630060356](http://www.amazon.com/Dark-Winter-Causing-30-Year-Spell/dp/1630060356)

So is the JoNova blog -- [http://joannenova.com.au/](http://joannenova.com.au/) . Specific blog post -- [http://joannenova.com.au/2015/07/is-a-mini-ice-age-coming-in-2030-and-does-the-sun-have-two-dynamos/](http://joannenova.com.au/2015/07/is-a-mini-ice-age-coming-in-2030-and-does-the-sun-have-two-dynamos/)

I'm currently for the argument of decreased solar activity, more cloud cover all over the world, decreased Schumann resonance, and colder temperatures in general. Assuming this is true, Cairns specifically will probably experience colder temps as well, and likely more rain (which adds to flood risk), and less cyclones (not 100% sure of this, usually you need warm oceans for cyclones, but La Lina events may precipitate more cyclones)

In any case, the real major long term risk factor up here in Far North Queensland is going to be too much rain, and therefore flooding.

I personally do not want to be in any major population centers if the Solar Mininum really does come to pass. Too much potential for unrest.

Also, I'm biased to say that I don't want to have to face sub-zero degree South Australian winters

Also, I don't like the growing conditions in the South of Australia. I much prefer the prospects of FNQ's tableland regions and their volcanic soils.

Also, I don't like the idea of extra cosmic rays coming into a place which already has a degraded ozone layer (above Antarctica). I'd rather be as close to the Equator as possible in an attempt to reduce cosmic ray exposure.

Also, I don't want to have to deal with the huge swings in sunlight between summer and winter down South, especially when solar activity is already going to be decreasing.

Also, I like the sharp differences in natural zones here in Cairns. I've got ocean on my doorstep, rainforest behind me, swamps on certain beaches, and the elevated tablelands (400m above sea level) just a 30 min drive away. Gives me a nice range of environments to experiment with.

Obviously, there is no real city life here like you would find in the larger Capital cities, but I'm an Internal Markets kind of guy who gets his kick out of the Internet, and likes his real life to be surrounded with peaceful natural environments. Some people won't like that of course.

\----

Well, I could go on , but I think I covered the main resaons already. Enough for the night!

### Jenny S — 2015-07-27T06:46:25+0300

> [Richelle Jones said:](/goto/post?id=173027)

### Richelle Jones — 2015-07-27T08:06:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-173189>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=173039)
>
> I'm gonna be biased , because I'm of tropical ancestry, and like hot and humid climates. 35C and 100% humidity was the environment I grew up in (Singapore), and Cairns barely reaches that ever (and likely not with Solar Mininum coming in 2030-2040).
>
> I don't worry about any of the critters here. Stay away from croc territory, don't swim in the ocean during the warm periods, try not to go wandering in the bush, make sure all your doors and windows are either closed or have fly screens, and you're fine. TBH, I'm more worried about running into a kangaroo while on the road (there are tons of them in the fields in my suburb )
>
> To cut to the chase, there are too many pros and cons to list. The only way that you will know is to actually visit this place for at least 2 weeks during both the extreme seasons (dry in July, and wet in February), and then decide whether that treats you well.
>
> A decently objective comparison of Cairns vs Melbourne can be found here -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-40#post-154085](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/take-it-slow.9428/page-40#post-154085) . Canberra basically shares the same issues as Melbourne, except that it isn't as crowded (good), but is higher up (likely bad).
>
> \-----
> **Longer Term
> **
> Naturally, I'm looking at good options to meet the likely impending Solar Minimum, which already started in some ways, though we probably won't see the effects until 2018, and then an abnormally low solar peak in 2022, with a full on minimum by 2030.
>
> 'Dark Winter' is probably a good book regarding this matter -- [http://www.amazon.com/Dark-Winter-Causing-30-Year-Spell/dp/1630060356](http://www.amazon.com/Dark-Winter-Causing-30-Year-Spell/dp/1630060356)
>
> So is the JoNova blog -- [http://joannenova.com.au/](http://joannenova.com.au/) . Specific blog post -- [http://joannenova.com.au/2015/07/is-a-mini-ice-age-coming-in-2030-and-does-the-sun-have-two-dynamos/](http://joannenova.com.au/2015/07/is-a-mini-ice-age-coming-in-2030-and-does-the-sun-have-two-dynamos/)
>
> I'm currently for the argument of decreased solar activity, more cloud cover all over the world, decreased Schumann resonance, and colder temperatures in general. Assuming this is true, Cairns specifically will probably experience colder temps as well, and likely more rain (which adds to flood risk), and less cyclones (not 100% sure of this, usually you need warm oceans for cyclones, but La Lina events may precipitate more cyclones)
>
> In any case, the real major long term risk factor up here in Far North Queensland is going to be too much rain, and therefore flooding.
>
> I personally do not want to be in any major population centers if the Solar Mininum really does come to pass. Too much potential for unrest.
>
> Also, I'm biased to say that I don't want to have to face sub-zero degree South Australian winters
>
> Also, I don't like the growing conditions in the South of Australia. I much prefer the prospects of FNQ's tableland regions and their volcanic soils.
>
> Also, I don't like the idea of extra cosmic rays coming into a place which already has a degraded ozone layer (above Antarctica). I'd rather be as close to the Equator as possible in an attempt to reduce cosmic ray exposure.
>
> Also, I don't want to have to deal with the huge swings in sunlight between summer and winter down South, especially when solar activity is already going to be decreasing.
>
> Also, I like the sharp differences in natural zones here in Cairns. I've got ocean on my doorstep, rainforest behind me, swamps on certain beaches, and the elevated tablelands (400m above sea level) just a 30 min drive away. Gives me a nice range of environments to experiment with.
>
> Obviously, there is no real city life here like you would find in the larger Capital cities, but I'm an Internal Markets kind of guy who gets his kick out of the Internet, and likes his real life to be surrounded with peaceful natural environments. Some people won't like that of course.
>
> \----
>
> Well, I could go on , but I think I covered the main resaons already. Enough for the night!
>
> ....

Thanks so much for your comprehensive insights. I will check out those links.

Did Cairns have something that Darwin didn't?

### yewwei.tan — 2015-07-27T10:25:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-173195>

> [Richelle Jones said:](/goto/post?id=173191)
>
> Did Cairns have something that Darwin didn't?

Many, many things

\----
**Latitude**

Darwin is definitely closer to the Equator, at around 12deg South. Where I'm at is 16.5deg. That's one advantage that Darwin has over Cairns.

\-----
**Water -- Humidity**

Darwin's humidity varies from 70-80% during the wet season, and 37-60% during the dry season -- [http://www.bom.gov.au/climate/averages/tables/cw\_014015\_All.shtml](http://www.bom.gov.au/climate/averages/tables/cw_014015_All.shtml)

Cairns' humidity varies from 65-78% during the wet season, and 55-68% during the dry season -- [http://www.bom.gov.au/climate/averages/tables/cw\_031011.shtml](http://www.bom.gov.au/climate/averages/tables/cw_031011.shtml)

Cairns gets more rain, about 2,000mm a year, with a variance between 400+mm in wet season months, and 26mm in dry season months. Darwin has 400+mm wet season months, and virtually zero rain in the dry season months.

Higher humidity is good -- better dielectric constant of water, which is in the air that you breathe in, and are constantly exposed to. Darwin's dry months are too dry for my liking, and it's wet months are really wet, and uncomfortably humid.

\-----
**Water -- Drought**

If I'm not wrong, the Cairns coastal and Tablelands regions have never experienced a drought in the last 100 years. (Go further inland, and yes, lots of drought). There have been dry spells where waterfalls dry up, but I don't think we ever saw reservoirs go dry (may be wrong).

Darwin is largely arid, and doesn't have as much protection against droughts.

In any case, Solar Minimum is likely to bring more rain, not less. So we're worried about floods, and both Darwin and Cairns are equally susceptible to that risk. In Cairns though, I'd like to get something higher up on the Tablelands just in case (high up, you are flood proof, and if you construct your homes in a certain way, you are wind proof too).

\-----
**Sunshine, Growing Conditions, Geography**

Cairns gets you roughly 6 hours of bright sunshine during the wet season (when the sun is at a 90deg noon altitude anyway, so tons of UV), and right now is a fucking beautiful 8+hours of bright sunshine at a 55deg noon altitude (still lots of UV).

Darwin gets you just under 6 hours of bright sunshine during the wet season, and then 10.1 hours of bright sunshine during the dry season (which is unnecessary, and can work against you given the drier conditions).

One of the reasons why it's "carb season all year long"  here is because you can literally grow plants all year long here in Cairns. Coastal areas yield great stuff in the wet, and the Tablelands are producing some pretty damn fine produce now (everything from avocados, to coconuts, to sweet potatoes, to various greens, to apples).

I've hinted at the Tablelands, which are 400m to 1,200m above sea level (1,300ft to 4,000ft), but with sharp cliffs and the coastline only 30-40 mins away depending on where you are on the Tablelands. (Note: the tablelands receives the same, and in some places like Mareeba, more sunshine and rain, as compared to the coastal regions)

The Darwin metro area is very flat, and to get to the higher regions, it's a bit of a drive. Note that these high regions are dry regions, whereas the tablelands near Cairns are rainforests and growing regions.

Sidenote: all of these conditions will allow for more availability and more flexibility in renewable energy sources. If I'm going to build an off-the-grid house, and grow my own food, I'd want to do it here in FNQ rather than NT.​

\-----
**Economy, Politics, Infrastructure**

Large-scale mining operations are closer to Darwin than Cairns. I'd rather be as far away from those as possible.

Darwin's governing structure is a little different, though I think that's not too important of a point of consideration for me.

Darwin's real estate prices are stupidly high . (like 50-100% more than Cairns)

10km north of me is a protected zone where no construction can occur ... rainforests for miles until you hit Port Douglas, and then drive a little further, and they didn't even allow the Highway to be constructed through the Daintree rainforest. There is likely going to be political inertia in keeping these regions as conservation zones.

Cairns itself is a bit more populous, so if you head into the city, there's still something to do. If the Equis casino gets built here, I wouldn't mind the influx of tourism. (while staying far away from it of course)

Cairns Airport to any of the major cities is a decently short flight (3.5 hours to Melbourne, 2 hours to Brisbane, about 2.5 hours to Sydney). Darwin is going to take longer, and has less frequent flights.

Postage to Darwin is going to be slooooooow. Your Optimal Health supplements, books, and equipment is going to take a long time to be delivered.

\-----

Well, that's enough for now. You can tell that I like this place

....

### Jenny S — 2015-07-27T12:02:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-173199>

Good detailed answer Yew! Think you definitely like Cairns. Didn't realise humidity is good for you. I was trying to find somewhere not too humid - bad idea obviously.
Wouldn't living in the Tablelands be not so good because of the altitude even tho not that far to coast?

### yewwei.tan — 2015-07-27T12:38:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-173202>

> [Jenny S said:](/goto/post?id=173199)
>
> Good detailed answer Yew! Think you definitely like Cairns. Didn't realise humidity is good for you. I was trying to find somewhere not too humid - bad idea obviously.
> Wouldn't living in the Tablelands be not so good because of the altitude even tho not that far to coast?

Firstly, I would like to spend most of my time on the coast, because I like the beach . Would also like another Tablelands home for occasional change in environment, and for extra disaster recovery plan, and for experiments in growing stuff.

**(a)** I'm not 100% convinced that altitude is inherently bad
**(b)** Tablelands are not that high -- say you get in Kuranda, which is around 330m above sea level, it's still not like Denver at 1,500m, or Ecuador at close to 3,000m. Even Canberra is at >550m
**(c)** Oxygen concentration would be lower at altitude, but Tablelands are generally rainforest regions, which offsets the oxygen loss a little.
**(d)** Intermittent change of environment can be hormetic

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-07-27T18:47:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-173233>

There are pretty convincing socioeconomic/political arguments for the increased suicide rate in Utah, which would explain why it is largely an outlier. Some regions in Colorado are known to have rather excellent happiness/contentedness scales, and mountainous region Ecuadorian shamans, Sherpas, Tibetans, etc. are all known to be rather robust, content, and capable of easily accessing "alternative states of consciousness". Geneticists believe they simply have genetic adaptations, but I believe it is more epigenetic and capable of being expressed in most individuals.

The ubiquination series has made me think about altitude a lot more, though, and also the nearby environment one is exposed to. To me, it appears as though coastlines, or at the very minimum, being near a river or lake, waterfalls, etc. would be optimal -> negative ions, naturally vortexed water laden with minerals and exposed to a high magnetic field, etc.

The "Potato Earth" perspective indicates that there are areas of increased/decreased native magnetism at all different altitudes, though. There are also some very deep rivers/lakes, "oceanic-like" bodies of water in some mountainous regions, and it has become an adrenaline junkie-type activity to do mountain range free diving in those bodies of water. I wonder how adaptation to moderate altitude + free diving in the same area would play out...

### yewwei.tan — 2015-08-10T03:31:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-174319>

Heh, 30 days of Parastat, some PRL Medi-body pack treatment, and some changes to diet, and I'm in the best physical condition I've ever been in.

Whatever liver weakness I'm testing for has been eliminated with the Parastat, and I have the tools and knowledge to monitor myself from now.

The main dietary changes involved ensuring that BOTH eating and digestion happens during times of strong daylight. This will not be the same prescription for everyone, but for me it worked out really well -- better cognition (very stable and very high), good sleep, bristol stool consistent #4, etc .....

This meant eating fast-digesting foods earlier in the day. What I'm doing is basically eating from 8am to 12pm, and getting lots of sunshine.

"Fast-digesting" means stuff like eggs, avocados, dried fruit, (sugary) chocolate, sticky rice, whey, honey, heavily caramelised onions, etc .... Foods that don't sit well with me are stuff like raw fruit, vegetables, meat of almost any form (raw or not), hard cheeses. Doesn't matter if I eat "junk food" like wheat biscuits or candy in this time either -- improved liver function, and fast digesting substances that relieve all GI activity by sunset was the key.

Body composition has been affected in the same time period.

Trying to pull off a Kane Sumabat pose at around 74.0kg (163lbs) bodyweight at a 74cm (29.1'') waist measurement.

Dietary changes have awakened the Skinny Asian genes

Haven't been eating that much seafood . Testing incoherent for it this last month, and feeling incoherent after eating too much as well. DHA intake has also been lowered at this point to whatever is in my typical 4-eggs-a-day breakfast. (Probably 200mg DHA)

Having loaded for so much DHA for so long, I have probably reached a level where I am at DHA saturation, and only need a small dose to maintain whatever stores I have. Seems like every 5-7 days I feel I need a can of sardines. This has correlated with coherence testing as well, and so that's when I consume the needed seafood.

Requirements will change with varying times and contexts of course, which is why I like the accuracy and speed of coherence testing.

When I say "coherence testing", I'm referring to the use of a Biotensor rod to interpret weakness through various acupuncture points in the body. I have ZERO clue how this works, but know that it works reliably again and again. Probably something to do with field disruptions which can be detected along the collagen meridians.

In any case, I can reliably say, hold a piece of fish and test my liver point through finger contact, and the biotensor rod goes incoherent. Mechanistically, it would make sense that to process all that DHA and EPA, you need strong liver function (p450 system), as I have noted here -- [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...on-fish-oils-and-dha.14227/page-2#post-163657](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/some-perspectives-on-fish-oils-and-dha.14227/page-2#post-163657)

Not being able to process DHA essentially makes it an added stressor. I also did not test well for seafood on many of the brain and sleep points. It certainly didn't help me, and could possibly hurt me in my current context, so I will wait until I start testing positive again before pushing up my seafood intake.

Was working on 2 software projects .... 1 is finished now, but may have another coming up in a week's time

> [Joce\_ said:](/goto/post?id=179059)
>
> How did you know you had a parasite?

I should have be more accurate and just say that my Liver was not functioning well.

I knew this through testing and monitoring of Liver associated acupuncture points, and through monitoring of emergent symptoms due to liver stress. Example: check for digestive issues with high fat meals, or symptoms like vasocontriction of the blood vessels in the hands after eating seafood (indicator of some form of stress due to eating seafood -- and yes, eating seafood can be stressful if your liver is shot).

Whether or not it was caused by Parasites is unknown. All I know is that the adequate supplements and herbal formulas fixed the issue, and it has yet to return. I'm still monitoring on a constant basis of course.

\----
**Health is Doing Well**

More tweaking of diet composition and timing, more testing of various supplements targeted at my issues, etc .... more experiments. That's basically what I've been doing in my spare time.

Health is the best it's been for sure.  The key issue for me was really the liver, once I fixed that, a lot of issues resolved itself.

If methylation is off, Leptin function is screwed. Just look at all the downstream products which require methyl transferases, and which feedback directly to Leptin (dopamine is one of these). So it makes sense that once these pathways were fixed with stuff like Molybdenum, etc ... I suddenly saw a vast improvement in function.

Some refinements still need to be made. Fascial issues from my back injury 5 years back still are present. Other than that, everything is either maintaining (like eyesight), or has seen vast improvements (like brain function and exercise tolerance).

\----
**Re-consideration of Everything I have Written Here**

While I have been busy with writing software, there's always some time in the day for research. What I have discovered over the past 3 months has made me re-consider everything I know about science, and has definitely broadened my perspective on the forces which affect one's state of health.

The list is too long to list here, basically everything I know has been overturned . I'm still in the process of researching, but I'll likely be doing some writing about some subjects in the coming months.

Confusion rules the day!

### yewwei.tan — 2015-10-14T23:14:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-179488>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=179450)
>
> Nice to hear from you Yew
> is it....... that in the end it is all about love...?
> I am so curious now........

Nope . If we're talking about Human well-being, then it is all about Uniqueness and Differentiation.

I don't want to pay lip service to the term "unique" either. ie: I don't look at someone today and then attempt to offer the same homogenized fix like "eat DHA, obey light-driven circadian cycles, etc ..."

Since learning how to test various meridians of the body to monitor in real-time a person's energy transmission and mobilisation status, I've had the opportunity to test a bunch of people. I can't find a uniform response .

Example:

Put me in front of a halogen bulb after sunset, and my occipital lobes blow out. Eye strain sets in, and it's very clear that I am reliant on visible light as a signalling medium.

I tested a friend of mine -- exposure at halogen lights and at night, no negative response in any of the meridians. Tested his food response to eating at night, no negative response. Play high-pitched heavy metal music into his ears, many points go incoherent  ..... I had to admit to myself that Sound Acoustic frequencies were a larger signalling factor than the Visible Light frequencies.

Should I tell him to "eat during the day"? Well, he tried, and started feeling worse and gaining weight .... Well, kinda makes sense, since if sound affected his digestive and signalling process, and he worked in a busy and noisy office during the day, that would screw things up. Eat at night, digest your food in a quiet environment, everything is fine. I even recommend technology for this guy -- extremely good noise cancellation headphones.
​

Another random anecdote, when the "Blood Moon" approached Perigee on \[28-Sep-2015\], I noted the specific geometry of the moon, along with a bunch of other factors I will not talk about yet , and then noted who had sleep issues, and who didn't. Everything matched my predictions, and some people got affected tremendously (poor sleep, poor immunity, etc ...) while others did not. I felt it, waking up at stupid hours for days in a row. My friend mentioned above felt nothing. My dad, who shares many characteristics with me, got thrashed. My Mom, who is unlike me, did not feel a difference at all. Also had some reports of random animal death (like pet goldfish).

Whatever the case, it's obvious that the Moon being closer to the Earth exerts a force on it, electromagnetically and gravitationally. How this affects a particular person is going to vary wildly, and is going to be very, very significant. Imagine someone trying to do a particular dietary experiment (say eating extra carbs) through this period, and they get jolted up by the Moon. How can they blame the experiment for the poor results, when it was really the moon?

If you want to go further, the Suspicious Observers guys have convinced me through their observational data that Planetary alignments and conjunctions can lead to extra force being applied on Earth, enough to cause extra earthquakes. These events will have an impact on humans as well. How big? You have to test.

\-----

My ideas on Quantum Physics have been completely throw out and re-examined from first principles , meaning looking again at the experiments that seem to prove quantum phenomena. Lots and lots and lots of inconsistencies. Thank you Miles Mathis for some great debunking

No conclusions yet from me, I just know what is wrong, and do not yet have an adequate model of what is correct.

.....

### Jack Kruse — 2015-10-15T19:27:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-179586>

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-10-15T23:39:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-179621>

Well, it depends on what model your speaking of. As I said, there is no one and only "QED" - there are many different "quantum ideologies" with radically different interpretations, sometimes even of the same experiments, all with solid mathematical and logical proofs, and the observations of scientists to back them. Much of what people tend to think is "QED" is based on the Copenhagen interpretation, and there is a more realistic interpretation known as "Quantum Information Theory".

As Yew has pointed out, Miles Mathis has logically and mathematically broken down some major flaws in quantum theories. I recently watched a lecture on QED that was very straightforward, it was in particular about Quantum Field Theory, and more specifically, the electron and proton fields. The lecturer kept mentioning how important the Stern-Gerlach experiment was. Well, as Miles Mathis opens up in his article, "The Stern-Gerlach experiment was performed in 1922 on silver atoms passing through an inhomogeneous magnetic field. This is the experiment that gave us the current ½ spin values for fermions. I will show that the experiment was badly misinterpreted, and still is, compromising all of QM and QED in many ways." [http://milesmathis.com/stern.html](http://milesmathis.com/stern.html)

Then, there is the physics of the individuals I listed above, some of whom had predecessors that wrote about and "proved" many of the things now discussed in "QED", only 100+ years ago. So, in effect, are they 100+ years ahead of QED?

### yewwei.tan — 2015-10-16T00:56:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-179623>

Agreed with @[bio-fractal-soul-self](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/12496/) , when I read his paper about the CSHS Bell tests -- [http://milesmathis.com/chsh.pdf](http://milesmathis.com/chsh.pdf) , I thought, "Oh well, looks like that was wrong ... and now all of superposition and entanglement theory, which has basis in such experiments, are likely wrong too "

I also found his debunking of the use of **π** in many physical equations to be satisfying obvious in hindsight. **π** is a scalar, how can you use it in vector equations?!  Yet all physicists use it to represent circular motion in 3 dimensions, and assume that the direction of the vector doesn't need to be accounted for -- [http://milesmathis.com/pi2.html](http://milesmathis.com/pi2.html)

As for mechanics, there is no way to say for certain that the model Mathis provides is 100% correct. The experimental results look promising though -- [http://milesmathis.com/elec2.html](http://milesmathis.com/elec2.html) , and I very much like the fact that his models relies purely on real particles (a photon is nothing but a particle with mass), with real spin (no 1/2 spin, just real spin in real 3D space), etc .....

I also looked into this paper 'Unexpected Stable Stoichiometries of Sodium Chlorides', which seems to find "the laws of chemistry to be violated" -- [http://www.sciencemag.org/content/342/6165/1502.short](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/342/6165/1502.short) (it was mentioned in Suspicious Observers' Starwater series).

I immediately thought of Mathis' model of electron "bonding" -- [http://tanyewwei.com/notes/mm-magnetism/#bonding:039a7b2502c2645bade1578b1d3c5e57](http://tanyewwei.com/notes/mm-magnetism/#bonding:039a7b2502c2645bade1578b1d3c5e57)

Elements don’t bond through the electron configurations, they bond through focused charge channels, and it is the nucleus that create these channels.

All bonding is determined by nuclear configurations, not electron configurations. The electrons do have configurations, as we have seen, but these configurations simply follow the proton configurations. The electrons are only along for the ride.​

Mathis claims that bonding is due to particular charge configurations, and that the ability to channel charge is dependent on both the E/M force and Gravity. This means that molecular and ionic bonding will be different at different places where the force of E/M and Gravity vary quite a bit. eg: Water Hydrogen bond strength is different on Earth vs Mars vs Jupiter vs any other body in the solar system, etc ..... If there is any place to start looking for a coherent model, it is one like Mathis', which forces us to consider all forces on all objects (from photons to galaxies) as a variable product between E/M and Gravity.

What we can say is that QED has many, many holes, to which it cannot answer for.

\----

Sidenote: So far, I have only detected one piece of "magic" in Mathis' theories, which he fully admits to -- [http://milesmathis.com/gravmag.pdf](http://milesmathis.com/gravmag.pdf) . He knows that the Gravitional acceleration Vector must point outwards from the body in question, but cannot mechanistically explain why. He admits that one explanation is that the entire Universe is spinning in 3 dimensions, which would produce a uniform outward acceleration vector on all bodies, and would make all his mechanics match his predictions. There is obviously no way to prove that the entire universe is spinning

That said, his mechanics have proven to be far more predictive of phenomena than QED has been, which is why I am working to understand his models, and then to see if I can falsify them

\----

As for CT, I use the cool gut buster regularly (almost daily) for 30-60 mins . Spot CT in specific regions (like some ice packs or the cooling glove), or acute CT (like a short stint of cryotherapy), isn't exactly biologically stressful to most people IMO, and has some cool benefits. So why not add it it.

As for light. Not really anything special. Just sun exposure as much as it seems necessary. It's so sunny here to begin with. 81deg angle for noon time sun now. UV light in excess all the time. Even a dark skinned person would not be able to fully utilise the amount of light where I am.

Magnetic stuff is kinda in the wash too. I don't understand magnetism anymore.

I have to admit, now that Mathis explained some stuff, and reading some interpretation by Peter @ Hyperlipid regarding Denise Minger's proposed "carbosis", I am forced to admit that Peat has some excellent points about CO2.

As a result I take back everything I said about altitude being inherently bad. I can't buy the argument that gravity bends light after Mathis' debunking of the Pound-Rebka experiment (notes here -- [http://tanyewwei.com/notes/mm-great...a-experiment:b51106c8b712b195e8bac2a40ab3fd18](http://tanyewwei.com/notes/mm-greatest-error-gravity/#debunking-the-pound-rebka-experiment:b51106c8b712b195e8bac2a40ab3fd18) , original article here -- [http://milesmathis.com/pound.html](http://milesmathis.com/pound.html)).

I read Peat's take on altitude, it makes sense. Now it is time to test it in the coming years. Unfortunately, the only "high altitude" places I have near me are about 300-400m (1,000-1,300ft) above sea level. Should make for an interesting experiment nonetheless, though I'm not likely to have the resources to do a prolonged experiment any time soon.

....

> [smart cat said:](/goto/post?id=179714)
>
> Yew, I like your blog/notes. I read CT as hormesis and DHA and more. The summaries about Kruse and Pollack were helpful. Miles Mathis not so much. Because that goes way over my head. So I didn't read it.
> Lots of authors, models and theories about all this quantum physics stuff. Sigh.
> Lots of bloggers say Mathis is a total crackpot. I don't know. I am not a physicist or mathematician, so I have no clue who is right about what.
> Even physicists biochemists, MDs don't know everything about their scientific discipline. Let alone laypersons. So it's all a lot of hypothesis, models, speculations.
>
> Nobody knows "the truth". But your learning activity is interesting. So keep us informed about your experiments and thoughts.

Unlike the mystical truths that I have been given , which are un-falsifiable, models proposed by Humans come with their assumptions, and can thus be proven wrong if the assumptions are not met.

All I am saying is that the models of quantum physics which gave us concepts of Spins, Hilbert spaces, Tensors, etc .... have had all their assumptions proven wrong by Mathis.

Then, we have empirical evidence. Any model which can accurately describe empirical data should be taken seriously, with all it's assumptions stated up from, and rigorously tested against empirical data. Quantum Physics has failed that test (hello Large Hadron Collider ).

Mathis' models are very appealing, because not only do they describe empirical data exceedingly well, but also mesh together many concepts from many disciplines (including the Mystical ones ), and has clearly shown me mechanically how to define a Lewis Acid and Base -- [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lewis\_acids\_and\_bases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lewis_acids_and_bases)

Explaining terms like "electron donor" in terms of movement of real particles of light with real spin suddenly makes Gilbert Ling's work make a lot more sense. I am re-reading some of Ling's primary work now to make connections.

This also suddenly makes Ray Peat's talk about CO2 make mechanical sense, and the empirical observations fit the model of CO2 being a very important substance ([http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/co2.shtml](http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/co2.shtml) and [http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/lactate.shtml](http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/lactate.shtml))

And once you see CO2 in terms of charge channeling, suddenly Fructose takes on a different role, and "carbosis" can be explained both Physically and Biologically.
\- [http://rawfoodsos.com/2015/10/06/in-defense-of-low-fat-a-call-for-some-evolution-of-thought-part-1/](http://rawfoodsos.com/2015/10/06/in-defense-of-low-fat-a-call-for-some-evolution-of-thought-part-1/)
\- [http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com.au/2015/10/protons-and-ultra-low-fat-once-more.html](http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com.au/2015/10/protons-and-ultra-low-fat-once-more.html)

Peter explained how the efficacy of extreme high carb could actually be explained in terms of decreased insulin levels and massively increased insulin sensitivity, all using mitochondrial mechanics. I am still thinking about the Physical explanation, but I have an idea where to start.

This opens up many doors for personal experiment in order to figure out what works best for an individual. Coherence testing of meridians has helped me tremendously in deciphering what is good for me at a particular moment in time, and over a period of months, I will have enough personal data to make better decisions for myself.

Is Mathis correct? Dunno. Like I said, I need to understand his models better, and then see if I can falsify them.

Can a layperson deal with all this information? Frankly, I don't concern myself with that. I do things for myself, and occasionally write things on the internet.

.....

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-10-18T03:17:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-179767>

A friend on facebook (who I believe is friends with you) sent me a document with all of his "Townsend Letters" from the 70's upwards, probably over a hundred or more articles that aren't on his site; also, part of his graduate thesis on William Blake, mysticism, neoplatonicism, etc.

It's helpful to understand that a lot of his ideas are shaped (1) by Soviet biology, physics, physiology (Pavlov), and literature (Tolstoy and many others) and (2) the predecessors to a lot of individuals Dr. Kruse has written about; for instance, Budenborg De Jong (pre-Ling and A.I Hypothesis), A.I Oparin ("The Origins of Life" book), V. Vernadsky (geophysics, biospherology, the noosphere), Walter Drost-Hansen and Needham et al on water (pre-Ling and Pollack), etc. Some of the works he discusses in interviews or over an email (if you email him) are sometimes not even found on pubmed or similar databases, and if they are, need to be translated. His books go into some good detail. I can maybe send you his book Generative Energy if you'd like.

He has some interview (maybe "A Self Ordering World", but I'm not sure) where he discusses the history of physics, and describes it in a similar manner to Miles Mathis in his articles were he describes where physics went wrong and how it started to be corrupted; Dr. Peat outlines, quite succinctly in fact, the many various models of E/M, gravity, electron structure, redshift, etc. Interestingly, although many will call him a "reductionist biologist", he taught graduate level physics courses at Oregon University after a short stint of being a historian and tour guide at a National Park, lol.

> [Christos said:](/goto/post?id=182566)
>
> "The Biology of Carbon Dioxide" was very interesting. I immediately thought about CT and free diving and its relation to CO2. In this blog, [http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/co2.shtml](http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/co2.shtml), Ray talks about how mitochondria uncoupling increases CO2 production and the relation of CO2 for hibernation, thus linking to CT. Hows Lings response to Ray about Lings accomplishment.... "you didnt realize science was about money and prestige"... that sums up academia I suppose.

I'll only talk about CO2 here

First, it is safe to assume that the bulk of CO2 comes directly from metabolism, as well as exogenous sources (eg: high CO2 concentration in air, sitting in a High Bicarbonate concentration hot spring, etc ...). Contributions from any other sources (like the gut flora) are not significant.

Sidenote: hyperventilation can cause loss of CO2. It is unknown how significant correcting a chronic problem like non-diaphragm driven breathing will be at maintain good CO2 levels.​

Acute CT itself is a stressor which can induce increased metabolism, and through increased metabolism, increased CO2.

The observations where O2 and CO2 solubility and thus concentration increase in serum are only in cases of actual hypothermia. There are times when this is usual (eg: trauma), but in what is termed 'CT' as practiced by regular folk, hypothermia is exactly the opposite of what occurs (body temp should increase or be maintain for benefit from cold exposure).

Sidenote on Nitric Oxide -- [http://tanyewwei.com/blog/ct-hormet...nitric-oxide:4a0d882b4041cd3dcd2c154b80d1e593](http://tanyewwei.com/blog/ct-hormetic/#update-wed-25-nov-2015-nitric-oxide:4a0d882b4041cd3dcd2c154b80d1e593)​

No, I do not tie CT to CO2 . Assuming that increasing CO2 is a desirable thing (this is another long discussion, but the research seems to point to the answer being "YES"), and someone wants to increase levels of CO2 available to their cells, then the real way to go about it will be to:

(a) Metabolise a lot of sugars. RQ of glucose is 1.0 and fat is 0.7, and metabolism is where most people will get their CO2
(b) Learn how to breathe properly. (bag breathing can be useful too)
(c) Don't exercise excessively
(d) Control their environment to be more CO2 rich. eg: bathing in CO2 or bicarbonate rich water, hyperbaric chamber with lots of CO2 (some have done up to 10% concentration), living in a CO2 rich air environment (which isn't polluted)

Again, that is if you are trying to maximise CO2. Not saying that everyone should go about doing that

\----

**Personal Experiment**

As for me, my experiment is based off some really good results by eating as low fat as possible during the 2 weeks before heading to Melbourne. Basically something like 50-70g of protein from lean sources, and then rest of diet from mostly sugar, but fat intake was easily <15g. Eating was still done in a small window from about 7am to 12pm.

Many benefits -- better vision, sustained energy, higher caloric intake (close to 3,000kcal), sustained body temp, etc ...

No issues with hunger despite fasting 19hours while eating lots of carbs. Insulin sensitivity is obviously good enough at this point to have proper insulin control. And as Wooo! sez in article -- [http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2015/11/hyperinsulinemic-blood-nutrient.html](http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2015/11/hyperinsulinemic-blood-nutrient.html)

Conclusion: high insulin, and likely low FFA and disruptions of beta oxidation of fat induced by high insulin, are the real cause of being hungry. NOT low blood sugar.​

In any case, with the success of the pilot experiment, it's probably time to run a longer experiment, on the order of at least 3 months with as low fat as possible. Pretty doable given my current circumstances, with only a few days whereby experimental conditions will not be maintained

The sudden increase in metabolic rate hints at many factors, most likely suppression of PUFA mobilisation, and acute improvements in Thyroid function. In any case, the main goal of the experiment is to deplete stored PUFAs to as low levels as possible. We shall see what results I get

.....

### seanb4 — 2015-12-21T14:09:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-184669>

Hey, I can't find the post but I remember you saying you don't tollerate CT as well as people who have heritage in colder climates, that most likely is true but I read that high vitamin D is unhelpful to uncoupling. Could this be whats going on with you and a few others?

You have a lot of sun/uv-b/tan/vit D. So maybe this makes uncoupling and thus CT harder?

### yewwei.tan — 2015-12-28T01:38:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-185026>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=184669)
>
> Hey, I can't find the post but I remember you saying you don't tollerate CT as well as people who have heritage in colder climates, that most likely is true but I read that high vitamin D is unhelpful to uncoupling. Could this be whats going on with you and a few others?
>
> You have a lot of sun/uv-b/tan/vit D. So maybe this makes uncoupling and thus CT harder?

If I'm objective, my cold tolerance was/is good by most standards. I had no problems wearing a T-shirt in 0-4C weather in Japan (at least for the first 4 days before holiday stress kicked in), have and probably can still manage 20 min cold winter ocean swims, can tolerate ice packs, etc ..... all with the appropriate flushing mechanism.

Vit D cannot be said to be "unhelpful to uncoupling" in the context of cold stimulus. It only inhibits UCP2 (and some UCP3) activity. In this study by about 50%, **'1alpha,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3 inhibits uncoupling protein 2 expression in human adipocytes**' (Shi et. al., 2002) -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12223452](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12223452)

UCP2 and UCP3 are, (a) Expressed mainly in non-thermogenic WAT, and (b) dependent on UCP1 activity in BAT, along with other factors like Irisin (for WAT->BAT conversion)

UCP1 is of course, strongly activated by cold, Non-shivering thermogenesis is usually only mediated by BAT, and the studies below would indicate that all cold-induced thermoregulation is dependent on UCP1 activity as a start signal.

\- '**Only UCP1 can mediate adaptive nonshivering thermogenesis in the cold**' (Golozoubova et. al., 2001) -- [http://www.fasebj.org/content/15/11/2048.full](http://www.fasebj.org/content/15/11/2048.full)
\- '**High Incidence of Metabolically Active Brown Adipose Tissue in Healthy Adult Humans**' (Saito et. al., 2009) -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2699872/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2699872/)

All such experiments with "Vitamin D" actually expose cells to Calcitriol, which is the active metabolite of the Cholecalciferol which we synthesise (or consume) and store. We do not know how a particular person regulates the pathways needed to activate Vitamin D, and we certainly do not know how a particular person would regulate these endogenous systems in the face of cold stress.

Even if we get lots of Calcitriol-induced suppression of UCP2 and UCP3, the net effect on thermogenesis is unknown. eg: UCP1 activity may dominate regardless of UCP2 and UCP3 activity, or even perhaps the down-regulation of UCP2 is favourable in some fashion (eg: what if we discover UCP2 activity to be antagonistic to Irisin?).

Whatever, there are no conclusions to be had in this regard.

\----

As for my own cold exposure, right now, it is nothing, and that is because:

(a) It is not necessary for me
(b) It may have counter-productive effects given my current PUFA depletion experiment. (There is some suggestion of cold-induced elongase activity to make new PUFAs)

As for the PUFA depletion experiment, it is going very well.

\- Skin and hair quality drastically improved
\- Body temperature regulation drastically improved (especially in response to extreme heat)
\- Metabolic rate drastically improved. Before I would maintain weight on about a 33 x BW\_kg multiplier, so 75kg \* 33 => approx 2475kcal of food a day. 1 month into the experiment, I'm not even bothering to count most days , but it's easily in excess of a 40x multiplier (so at least 3,000kcal of food a day).
\- Accompanying this is a general good feeling most of the time

Bodyweight is stable at just over 75kg for the entire month.

Downsides come in the form of side effects from PUFA release. For example, skin elasticity and tone is better (no dry spots like before), but incidence of isolated spots of Acne has increased (which is something I always experience => indicative that it is a response to mobilised PUFA)

Adherence was pretty good, with only 2 days out of the month having more than 8-10g of fat (kept food intake lower on those days too, so it most likely didn't affect PUFA depletion progress).

Given the current good result, the experiment continues on .

\[**NOTE:** This is not a recommendation of the PUFA depletion diet. You need to know what you're doing here (supplements are needed for maximal depletion rates), and need to know how to monitor oneself. Fast PUFA depletion also has side effects, which not every one can handle\]

....

### JanSz — 2015-12-28T16:02:35+0200
