My results for getting back to a low carb diet this winter were mixed. I had some liver/gallbladder issues that could have been due to other issues besides just increased fat consumption. My ?/thought is... is high fat w/o ketosis tougher on the liver than high fat w/ ketosis? thanks Yew for sharing this.

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=185373)
>
> @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) , @[bio-fractal-soul-self](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/12496/) , @[Inger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1057/)
>
> I have discussed Resolvins and Protectins before (should add it to my article though ). One study that discussed the mechanisms in good detail is, '**Resolvins and protectins: mediating solutions to inflammation**' (Payal Kohli and Bruce D Levy, 2009) -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2785519/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2785519/)
>
> Take a look at all the figures, especially figure 3 -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2785519/figure/fig03/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2785519/figure/fig03/)
>
> No one will dispute the potential benefits of Resolvins and Protectins. The more important question is: **If DHA is going to be broken down, what products are going to be formed?**
>
> Then we need to look at the studies that I cite in the 'Isoprostane and Neuroprostanes' section of my article -- [http://tanyewwei.com/blog/dha/#isoprostanes-and-neuroprostanes:be30353b6977b7107e48806b5457b119](http://tanyewwei.com/blog/dha/#isoprostanes-and-neuroprostanes:be30353b6977b7107e48806b5457b119)
>
>
> -   Isoprostanes (IsoP) and Neuroprostanes (NeuroP) are formed **automatically** in the face of free-radical stress, **and** can be formed through enzymatic intermediates (mediated through the COX-2 enzyme)
>
>
> -   Resolvins and Protectins must be formed enzymatically through the COX-2 or P450 pathways. ie: formation of these products are under endogenous control.
>
>
> Anything that is endogenously regulated like this, we presume is bound by tight feedback and control mechanisms, AND requires those enzymes to be working in the first place. What happens with high stress? I find it much more likely that endogenous systems break down, and you get more free-radical induced IsoP and NeuroP synthesis.
>
> More DHA isn't going to solve this problem -- it does nothing to resolve the enzymatic inefficiencies that result from stress, be it excess blue light, or having to chronically worry about paying off the mortgage. More DHA in the face of uncontrolled stress will more likely make IsoP and NeuroP formation much more likely, and only serve to supply more potentially oxidisable substrate.
>
> Also keep in mind that free DHA itself is regulated by the liver, which is the organ that "decides" to put DHA in the Lyso-PC-DHA transportable form to be delivered to tissues. Stress usually hits the liver hard, and prevents it from performing optimally.
>
> \----
>
> Bazan's studies seem to support this viewpoint. If anything, he has documented dys-regulation in DHA control under stressful conditions (like how the brain seems to lose DHA under stress).
>
> His work is critical is showing us that the body has endogenous systems and compounds to ensure that just the right amount of DHA gets to just the right places (his work on the RPE is great )
>
> Sidenote: nobody will dispute that Neuroprotectin-D1 is very useful, but again this requires enzymatic formation via phospholipase A2. Moreover, most of it is formed in Peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMC), which are basically immune cells that come under strict regulatory feedback loops.
>
> I have some commentary in my article about how DHA affects Lymphocytes in a negative fashion, but have not made commentary on the effect of PUFAs (including DHA) on PMBCs. This shall be put on the TODO-list
>
> But in any case, the point remains the same -- that Endogenous regulation is absolutely essential for DHA to be used appropriately, and if said endogenous functions are compromised, DHA becomes harmful.
>
> I have then seen no evidence that DHA directly impacts the ability of these endogenous functions to operate properly. Fixing these endogenous pathways require something other than DHA, and usually the common denominators are **stress removal** and **increasing energy to the cell**.
>
> \----
>
> I have discussed the amounts of DHA needed in various tissues. The brain needs very little (at most 5.5mg a day), and Retinal DHA flux is unknown (though I did comment on how it cannot be very high).
>
> As for DHA in cell membranes, it is still present mostly as phospholipids in the membrane. While they do affect lipid raft mechanics (something which I will eventually get on in my article), they do this while in the phospholipid form, and will be be subject to the same level of breakdown stress seen in other conditions where DHA is bound as a phospholipid -- free-radical damage will cause DHA to breakdown.
>
>
> Sidenote: A good starting point for the discussion of DHA-rich domains in cell membranes, and their likely functions, can be found in, '**Polyunsaturated fatty acid–cholesterol interactions: Domain formation in membranes**' (Stephen R. Wassalla and William Stillwellb, 2008) -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0005273608003337](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0005273608003337)​
>
>
> The only thing that will protect DHA in the cell membrane is likely going to be some sort of "electromagnetic barrier" / high Membrane Potential that is induced via membrane proteins.
>
> This is a function of ATP, and the ability for the membrane proteins of a cell to regulate Action and Resting potentials in such a fashion as to preserve function amidst stress (including preventing the breakdown of DHA).
>
> More ATP has nothing to do with DHA, and in fact, I wrote a section (largely influenced by Peter @ Hyperlipid's work) showing how PUFAs (DHA included) will hinder the formation of ATP.
>
> \----
>
> Lots of the stuff that @[bio-fractal-soul-self](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/12496/) mentioned above is probably going to be worth reading too . Thanks for the recs!
>
> \----
>
> So yes, environment will have a systemic effect on the ability to deal with seafood and DHA. But in fact, you can argue for the idea that under a stressful environment, processing this compounds becomes hindered -- they become "more harmful" than if consumed under low stress conditions.
>

### yewwei.tan — 2016-01-10T01:03:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-185877>

@[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) I do not understand what you mean by "less dependent" on the above variables?

Are you implying:

**(a)** these conditions were present, and we need them in the quantities previously existent for good health
or **(b)** these conditions were present, but we actually don't need them in the quantities previously existent for good health

As for some of proposed observations you list, I cannot say for sure if that is 100% true.

eg:

-   Because we do not know how much energy to the energy comes from radiation from the plasmasphere, from charge channels into the poles from the plasmasphere, from direct incidence from the previous sun, the ancestral "night" is still undefined. Earth probably still rotated, and would still be experience variances between the direct irradiance from the sun. As far as the ancients in what today is the West-Asian/East African region were concerned, the night sky was a dim purplish hue, but never quite pitch-black like we seen during a new moon today.

-   The shielding from outer system forces by the heliosphere of the previous sun is probably true, but it can never be completely shielded. Case in point, Scholz's star passed into a distance that would correspond to the Oort Cloud today ([https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scholz's\_star](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scholz%27s_star)). That timing coincided with the Toba Volcanic eruption. Whether or not there was a link is debatable, but it is plausible (given how solar activity today affects volcanic activity)

    We also have no clue how the previous solar system looked like. Dr Kongpop U-yen's models of emergent orbital stability at particular resonant frequencies clearly show a huge role for E/M forces in maintaining the existing orbits of planets. E/M isn't the only force, so previous solar system configuration changes will not be quite as dramatic as he suggests, but the factor is there, and a huge star system like Scholz's (binary) star passing so close will affect us dramatically on Earth.

    We must also not forget the very clear record of geomagnetic reversals on Earth, and all the ice ages that follow. Even if Proto-Saturn were our original star, and protected us somewhat from outside forces, we would still be stuck in a cage where she can do whatever she wants

Regardless, life was supported on this planet for quite some time, and under many differing environmental conditions. My personal assumption is that so long as there is enough energy being supplied to a biologic system for continued adaptation to take place, then the organism will continue to exist. I do not discriminate between sources of energetic input, though food is clearly the most significant input in terms of raw energetic amount.

There are some people out there who claim that we are past our golden age, and that the Human species has been in decline for many thousand years, and that we were "better" in the past. I do not necessarily buy into the romantic notion of this narrative, but I can see how it is very plausible given sudden environmental shifts, plus all the ancient tales of Golden Civilisations before the founding of the new world (what we call the "ancient world" of 5,000 years ago isn't ancient)

Sidenote: one example of such a tale .... Plato told of the destruction of Atlantis "9,000 years before the time of Solon", which would have been about 9,600 B.C.E. As Graham Hancock notes, this isn't a mere fairy tale, but has plausible roots in the flooding of Sundaland ([https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sundaland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sundaland)), which is traced exactly to this period, and is likely due to the catastrophic events of this time. Whether it be Solar events, as I think, or an asteroid event, it doesn't change the fact that this area was once a land mass, and is now a sea bed, AND haboured advanced structures like Gunung Padang.​

I do believe that the current conditions, even devoid of all technology, are suboptimal for our existence. Moreover, without technology, forget about ever being able to exist in certain environments; there is no way for humans to naturally survive in the cold places of this planet without the ability to make clothing, shelter, fire, hunting tools, and storage space.

However, we must be careful with statements like, "current (suboptimal) conditions reduce our longevity". I have stated in previous posts in this thread that lifespan and function of biologic systems is dependent both on the function of components, as well as the emergent higher-level control mechanisms to govern those components. ie: suboptimal components, so long as their function is above some threshold, can keep the higher level control functional enough to maintain the state of being alive. Obviously we have the technology to live longer today, and Mystically speaking, there are narratives to suggest that the current planetary geometry "allows" us to live longer.

In any case, for now we only understand bottom-up death scenarios (eg: poisoning, grievous injury, failure to produce ATP, etc ...). Until we know enough possible cases that bring about the death state, "quantity of life lived" is something that can only be pursued as a hopeful side effect of providing enough energy to the system as a whole.

That said, biologic adaptations have definitely occurred in the past 10,000+ years. We assume that there is some intelligence or at least suitability-to-the-environment drive in these adaptations, such that the organism tries to obtain the most amount of energy given the environment that it is placed in.

I have linked to research (in prior forum posts) proposing that fair skin only fully evolved about 7,000 years old, again suggesting that the UV stress from the environment was much greater even at high latitudes in periods not too distant from that time.

Sidenote: I will stress again that UV light is definitely harmful without the appropriate biological apparatus to deal with it. We must have specific pigments like melanin in order to modulate incoming UV radiation for useful purposes. Plants suffer the same constraint.​

As for the historical Schumann resonance frequency, I have no clue, and I don't think anyone understands the factors that give rise to the specific frequencies. There is no way to say if it is likely or not that the Schumann resonance is the same as what we have observed in the last 10,000 years.

If the Schumann frequency is primarily a result of interactions of Earth-specific materials -- that is, that energy comes into the Earth, and then the materials in the core absorb and re-emit the Schumann resonance -- then it is likely the same so long as the Earth's composition remained fairly consistent. If this is true, then it favours the argument that the Schumann resonance would be roughly the same once the majority of Earth's components were consolidated millions (if not billions) of years ago.

If the Schumann resonance frequency is determined primarily by external forces, then all bets are off, and we don't know the historical variance. (Note that energy still comes from external forces, like solar X-rays)

\----

Regardless of all of this discussion, the goal is still to obtain as much energy as possible to sustain the living state -- enough ATP to maintain and control resting and action potentials of the cell, which predicates all cellular and organism-wide decisions.

It is upon that basis that I'd support strategies like preferring carbohydrate as metabolic fuel and avoidance of PUFAs (which diminish mitochondrial respiration rate).

No discussion of the mechanics of their magic "anti-oxidant" formula. No discussion of how prior medications could interact with the issues. No discussion of other environmental stressors. No discussion of dietary changes. No discussion of other potential failure modes. And only to go from "statistically completely fucked" based on MELD score to "statistically not so fucked" (which is still a statistical score, not an actual clinical observation).

This is crackpot science which only looks at potential upsides without any qualification of context nor the possible downsides (which I can name many )

From my book:

Bio-hacking tip #237 LOCATION CONTEXT WITH RESPECT TO LIGHT: lowering your DHA intake should only be done when you increase your UV/IR exposure WHILE simultaneously limiting your blue light hazard in your eye. This hack requires strong UV/IR to do correctly. Key point of context. Why? The photo-electric effect is why. Light needs electron clouds on surfaces to collide into to offload its energy and momentum to our atomic lattice. So the two variables are amount/power of light and amount of pi electron cloud. If you overwhelm the system with strong UV you won't need as many pi electron clouds to get the job done. This explains why equatorial zones are easier to recover in than regions outside the tropics. It is also why seafood is not found as commonly in warmer water in nature. Everything is yoked and connected in nature. It is our job see the threads that connect these things. When we are healthy good time prevail but this makes biohacking more difficult because the results can be deeply camouflaged by the fundamental physical effects. ### yewwei.tan — 2016-01-10T23:21:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-185938>

The sun will never provide a significant source of energy to the body

Let's say I use the standard Du Bois formula to calculate my **total** body surface area ([https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Body\_surface\_area](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Body_surface_area)):

0.007184 \* Weight\_kg ^ 0.425 \* Height\_cm ^ 0.725
\= 0.007184 \* 75 ^ 0.425 \* 190 ^ 0.725
\= 1.77172 m^2​

Then I go find the average solar irradiance for where I am (have to search for "Cairns") -- [http://www.energymatters.com.au/climate-data](http://www.energymatters.com.au/climate-data)

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Let's use the high value of 6 kW/m^2/day. Note that this is a value of "peak sun hours", meaning that 6 kW/m^2/day is the average energy of being exposed to the sun for 6 hours at 1 kW/m^2. That means that **if I expose myself to ALL the sunlight there is in an average day**, I would get 1.77172 \* 6 = **10.63032 kJ of energy**

**Edit \[12-Jan-2016\] MAJOR ERROR:** the above calculation is an amateurish mistake on my part . 1 kilowatt hour corresponds to 3,600 kilo joules. It's more like 1.77172 \* 6 \* 3600 = 38,269 kJ worth of energy. This is indeed a lot of energy, albeit that it is an unrealistic expectation.

Let's try something more realistic. I manage to expose half my body surface area (lying down on my back means that half my body is not exposed) for 2 hours of sunlight a day. I assume 12 hours of daylight, which is roughly correct for Cairns.

That's 1.77172 \* 0.5 \* (6/12\*2) \* 3600 = **3,189 kJ**, or around 760 kcal, which is about 1/4 of my daily caloric needs.

Note that this assumes me lying down, with full exposure to the sun, for 2 hours a day, on a regular basis. A truly realistic estimate for free-living conditions is probably a quarter of this (30 mins of full body sun exposure, and even less if I'm just walking around in the sun), or around **800 kJ / 190 kcal** a day.

This is not that insignificant a figure, but it is still assumes 100% efficiency of utilisation of Sunlight for metabolism (the true efficiency is unknown), and is still less than 10% of my caloric intake.

Thus, this is still a minor player at best in terms of raw energy input.

Note that I have pretty dark skin, and people of a lighter complexion will not even be able to reach the 800 kJ a day figure without having skin issues (making raw energy from the sun an even lesser player).

This is enough energy to send some major signals to the body, and certainly enough to give you major sunburn. But it is nowhere near enough to support the metabolic requirements of the body.​

I go eat one tablespoon of sugar (15g), and that's 4 \* 15 = 60 kcal = **251.04 kJ**

There is no comparison there ... **the energy received from the sun is negligible compared to food**. Given that, "at ground level sunlight is 44% visible light, 3% ultraviolet (with the Sun at its zenith), and the remainder infrared.", the UV contribution is even more negligible.

Sunlight (and especially UV light) is purely a signal to give information to the body. The intensity and duration of that sunlight obviously conveys different signals, which then has to be interpreted by the body. Every body will react differently, and I do not know how to gauge that difference ahead of time other than some rough guess based on ancestry.

eg: I come from ancestors living at around 23 deg North, Cairns at 17 deg South is "more similar" than the big fluctuation you see in Melbourne (graph given below).

@[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) on the other hand, has reported before that time closer to the Equator did not suit him well. I can especially believe his account given both the objective and subjective metric tracking that he does.

\----

Dopamine and UV light may be loosely coupled, but it isn't a straightforward relationship.

For starters, just because a protein absorbs at a specific frequency under lab conditions, that fact says NOTHING about how they work under physiologic conditions. So what if Tyrosine absorbs UV light squint emoticon DNA does it too, and so does every single thing with a peptide bond.

What about reaction intermediates? What about cofactors that seem to be supplying some addition of charge to the reaction? .... It's not a simple "add this amount of energy and things work out" formula.

How can you just apply UV light to things and expect magic to happen?  Obviously the body doesn't work this way .... It needs CONTROL over all endogenous compounds, and it does that through enzyme mediated feedback loops with many intermediate products.

UV light in vitro will destroy most enzymes. "Transferred a UV quanta of energy" between molecules is NOT the same as exposure to direct UV light (Unless we are dealing with direct photopigments like the opsins, but those are specifically designed for this direct absorption task -- we cannot apply the same logic to other compounds in the tight spaces of the body)

Those control mechanisms may be loosely coupled to incoming solar radiation insofar as that solar radiation affects everything (many still unknown) encompassed by the term "circadian cycles" and "cell signalling".

Sidenote: the reason I hate the "Quantum Tunnelling" mechanism is because all it says is "put these things together and magic happens". Why does Tyrosine Hydroxylase draw energy specifically from O2? Why does it need BH4 to be around? Why Fe2+, what about this specific metal does forces this specific reaction?

The answer is "I dun kno , but that's the observation, and the observation is reliable".

Whatever the case, this is under heavy regulation by things that we do not understand, and I will not claim to understand any of it. I just report observations​

To be clear, there is a definite strong observable link between circadian cycles and dopamine production. That's like 3 layers up, and has little to do with the mechanics of UV absorption by that point.

Or more specifically, any number of failures can screw the circadian cycle => dopamine chain. Everything from having a screwed up liver, to getting hit in the head with a baseball bat. Similarly, shoot some cocaine or chew some nicotine for a quick dopamine hit  (not advocating either BTW).
**It's the endogenous feedback cycles that regulate the levels of dopamine that matter**, just like every other one of the millions of compounds in the body

Can a person with "good metabolic function" and "great mitochondria" modulate their dopamine levels favourably? Yes, as seen in the observed edge cases of people who just do not break despite horrendous Circadian habits.

Finally, PUFA depletion will probably HELP deal with a variety of conditions, including dealing with the modern version of low light and high stress situations.

At the very least, getting rid of all the damn PUFAs in an unhealthy liver will help you regulate dopamine => catecholamine metabolism properly, even under times of stress, in order to better regulate overall dopamine levels. This is of course, just one input in a long chain of dopamine regulatory processes. (and we haven't talked about the other thousand important molecules )

Yes yes, "natural is best", and living a boring life, especially during the winter months, is what I would call ideal. That's not the reality of modern day humans, and I will not go about pushing for ideal and missing out on practical interventions for modern life.​

....

### Jonathin — 2016-01-11T03:39:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-185942>

**"the energy received from the sun is negligible compared to food"**

I would contend that you are trying to compare two things that are not comparable in a meaningful way. Photons interact directly with electrons in atoms and molecules. There is no overhead such as energy loss. Digestion of food and ox phos in the mitochondria has substantial overhead and inefficiencies. IR from food has a limited ability to spin up the energy of electrons hence the DC electric current advantage with UV for critical systems. Obviously, though, there are many circuits and subsystems that keep the body functional. I don't think anybody is saying that you can "live" off of only sunlight (at least on this forum).

### Jack Kruse — 2016-01-11T04:28:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-185944>

Yew food is sunlight in another format. Jonathin is correct. And with that I am out of this thread.

Life is not a thing as most perceive it to be; it's an [iterative process.](http://aeon.co/essays/why-life-is-not-a-thing-but-a-restless-manner-of-being) While most origin of life theories have focused on the molecules of information transfer, evidence is accumulating that life is primarily rooted in mechanisms for energy capture and storage. This was hinted at in Nick Lane's latest book, The Vital Question and it has been a running theme in Stuart Kaufman's work too. This radical re-framing focusses on a foundational paradox; the global increase of entropy requires the emergence of local instances of order. As such, scientific research, much like life is always conducted along the ragged, shifting line between what we know, and what we don’t. Many enticing clues emerge in this casino too, but ultimate discovery frequently remains elusive because we don't pay attention to correlated novelties of order and its sequence of events. DHA preceded photosynthesis and the Cambrian explosion appeared before the food webs were developed so the sun had to order life before it passed this job off to food. Novelties and innovations in science share the same dynamics, where one novelty fosters another innovation to build the complexity that life needs to harness energy to limit entropy.

There is no overhead such as energy loss. Digestion of food and ox phos in the mitochondria has substantial overhead and inefficiencies. IR from food has a limited ability to spin up the energy of electrons hence the DC electric current advantage with UV for critical systems. Obviously, though, there are many circuits and subsystems that keep the body functional. I don't think anybody is saying that you can "live" off of only sunlight (at least on this forum).

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=185944)
>
> Yew food is sunlight in another format. Jonathin is correct. And with that I am out of this thread.

And Mitochondria require NADH or FAD in order to generate ATP . If someone can show me a direct path from sunlight to significant amounts of **raw energy** for ATP generation, then I will say that "food is light".

ie: Show me how **15g of sucrose**, which equals **0.0438216601000395 moles,** which equals **2.6390023788734204 \* 10^22** molecules of sucrose, where each molecule provides 12 units of carbon, and has been empirically observed to provide 30 ATP for every 6 carbons (after digestion and what not), and therefore 2.6390023788734204 \* 10^22 \* 2 \* 30 = **1.5834014273240522 \* 10^24** units of ATP.

Sidenote: BTW, so what if "food is light". X-rays are "just EMF" like UV light. And if Mathis is correct, an electron is "just" a spun-up photon, and a proton is "just" a spun-up electron. Everything be light!!!!

We make a functional distinction between various forms of light based upon their observed functional effects. There is no point in asking "Why" UV light works the way it does. It just does . There exist UV light on this earth, and organisms happen to use it for signalling purposes. There exist high energy substrate like NADH, and organisms use it to push charge through mitochondrial complexes.​

Incident Sunlight of any form, in the amounts that are naturally present and are naturally designed to be dealt with are a **signalling mechanism,** not an energy additive.

And regarding:

Photons interact directly with electrons in atoms and molecules. There is no overhead such as energy loss. Digestion of food and ox phos in the mitochondria has substantial overhead and inefficiencies​

Show me that:
**
(a)** there is substantial energy loss from the digestive process outside of the normal observations of the thermic effect of food, and the observed ATP per unit molecule yield from ingested food substances.

**(b)** that the energetic barriers and overhead are so significantly lower from direct light incidence on the body, that they can make up for the massive raw energetic potential of food

And yes there is lots of lost efficiency from incoming light . Biology requires specialised molecules to absorb various forms of incoming light.

\- Melanin can absorb 99.9% of incoming UV light (and BTW, turn it to IR light ). Do you have melanin in the places that are subject to such light frequencies? In some people, yes, in others, nope. Excessive UV light passing into the body causes interactions with proteins that are completely counter-productive to optimal health.

**This is a signal, and one that forces a response from the body**. Can you handle an extra UV signal? This is going to depend on so many things .....

But we have to be clear. Adding UV light into the body is a **signalling mechanism,** to which the body must respond appropriately. This is not "energy addition", and requires specific hardware to be used effectively. What happens when the body can't meet the demands of the signal? What happens when you have a general who asks his fleet of 100 wounded and weary soldiers to do battle for the 15th day in a row?  That's what a shitty liver, or maxed-out adrenals, or crapped out thyroid gland is facing.

\- Even far-infrared light can't be used efficiently. This is "heat" in the common-use sense, which obviously is rejected when too much is supplied so that optimal operating conditions are not disrupted. Over-heating of any component is not a good thing at all.

\- Even the "special" 600-1200nm range (red and IR) can't be used completely efficiently. Anyone who practices LLLT knows that there are specific ranges of exposure which are useful, and excess is always harmful (eg: lots of ROS production).

I can cite many uses for this frequency range of light, and Guenter-Albretch Bueller's work (previous cited in posts in this thread) has shown just how important this range of light is to signalling between cells (and thus "cell intelligence"). **This is still a signal**, and too much of a signal at the wrong time is still a bad thing.

Cells regulate this signal very tightly during their normal metabolic processes, in order to determine when to do what. Adding in an exogenous signal is messing around with the balance of things. Sometimes, this "messing around" leads to very positive clinical outcomes -- eg: dislodging a bunch of Nitric Oxide from Cytochrome C Oxidase, and allowing for normal metabolism to resume. In excess, this leads to negative clinical outcomes -- eg: over-active metabolism without uncoupling, high ROS load, and "cellular overwork".

\----

Quote:

IR from food has a limited ability to spin up the energy of electrons hence the DC electric current advantage with UV for critical systems​

Show me that "food contains IR energy", or that "UV is used for critical systems".

Fats and Carbohydrate are broken down to NADH and FADH, which BTW encode and transfer "a UV quanta of energy" during Electron Chain Transport. Show me instances **in vivo**, whereby you can find free UV light doing the same thing, whereby the use of "molecular encoders" like NADH are not required.

\-----

Light is a bloody important signal to the body. Certain frequencies of light (specifically the red and IR bands) can even interact directly with the energy producing equipment of the body.

But to think that one can ask the body to do something by providing it a certain set of signals, and expect a uniform and positive response, is not correct in the slightest.

Light is a signal. Some frequencies are known to be harmful at specific doses. The only safe route is to experiment with providing the body with those different signals, then watching and measuring the response of the body, while giving it the appropriate energetic substrate to response to those signals, while cutting down on as many known and controllable stressors as possible.

@[bio-fractal-soul-self](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/12496/) you just described me living in Guam. I will say that IME the above bolded did more for my health than all the efforts I've done over the past 15 years. But different times different life.

Interesting thing - yesterday I went to my PCP and we looked at not only the last set of labs I've drawn and she asked me what I was doing before my labs were drawn in April. Which was 2-3 sweet potatoes/day plus fresh fruit/veggies of epic proportions. We then looked at my labs pre-paleo and pre-pregnancy where my HDL was in the 90s, my LDL was in the 220s and my trigs were in the 30s. at that time I was working in the pentagon, and eating gluten free (corn, rice, buckwheat), dairy free and lots of veggies with minimal protein - that was either seafood, and organic/grassfed beef, lamb, chicken and pork and a SHIT TON of sugar (2-3tlbs in my two cups of coffee every morning) and my fat was either coconut oil, or bacon fat, or olive oil on salads. She looked at me and said "well, HFLC and paleo isn't working I want you to do a 4 month hack. I want you to eat rice several times/week, big ass salads every day, sweet potatoes several times/week, seasonal fruit and if you need/want sugar in your coffee - have stevia. I want to see what your labs look like if you go back to eating the way you did before you went paleo. "

Back in November I switched to eating the following ratios 40% carbs/ 30%protein/30% fat - after pushing keto in October gaining 10lbs. In November I had two bouts of cellulitis - which my redox handled w/o a blip - but those two bouts, plus having to step up and take on the PM role for my project at work (basically I 'm now performing 3 jobs - instead of my "usual" 2) along with two classes - depleted my redox quickly and I ended up with pneumonia -which required abx and 6 weeks worth of prednisone to recover. The interesting thing - is that even though I was sick as a dog - I had massive energy eating 40/30/30. As soon as I felt recovered enough I couldn't sit still I had to move. and BAM I'm 3 weeks into working out every morning for 15-30mins either HIITs or a short Jog (remember the J is silent) between morning telecons getting my AM UV. So I'm rolling with this higher carb/moderate protein/fat combo. During this hack I'm still going to maintain my strict light management.

I realize I'm straying from Jack's protocols but I'm trying to find what works for me in the long run. I fully understand that light plays a roll, but I have to question the food, as the paleo/epi-paleo way isn't dialing me in 100%. Yes I had success with Jack's protocols initially - but the longer I've used them the worse my labs have gotten. I've moved to an amazing environment - EMF readings are awesome. I've changed my work routine - such that I can get AM UV Light every morning. I don't sit in traffic commuting any more. I spend more time outside than before - hikes in nature and working in the yard have expanded. So if its my environment - have to ask - where isn't the environment fucked up courtesy of nnEMF on planet earth? I'm coming to the realization that regardless of where I could move - there will be nnEMF, so how do I make my environment work to my advantage? Part of my environment includes a source of income that ensures I can take care of my family as well as access to good medical care.

Correct. Light is a Signal for the "summer metabolism" which supports higher Free T3. Light alone is not going to do that. The body needs energetic substrate in the form of food (and most directly, carbohydrate) to cause that increase in Free T3.

Can food do this independently? To a large extent, yes, and independent feeding of carbohydrate can also boost Free T3 without light, especially if it is suboptimal to begin with.

Another key point is that the body can and will adapt to conditions of existence. Example: Even in conditions of complete darkness (Antarctic winter), we see a normal T3 profile, with the associated (though not directly causative) normal metabolic rate (even with some increases in study 2). Study (1) was with Indian Men with presumably darker skin (based on my baises of having been there before). The "cost" is seen in a higher TSH -- the thyroid seems to be "working harder" to maintain equilibrium.

(1) 'Thyroid function during a prolonged stay in Antarctica' (Sawhney et. al., 1995) -- [http://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF00964127](http://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF00964127)
(2) 'Changes in Serum Triiodothyronine (T3) Kinetics after Prolonged Antarctic Residence: The Polar T3 Syndrome' (Leste Reed et. al., 1989)-- [http://press.endocrine.org/doi/abs/10.1210/jcem-70-4-965](http://press.endocrine.org/doi/abs/10.1210/jcem-70-4-965)

All of this won't work well if the liver is thrashed and thus T4 -> T3 conversion is gone. A really sick person will add light, supplement Natural Thyroid Hormone, and see no positive change to Thyroid hormones (just ask some people on this site). Supplementing directly with T3 may help, but keep in mind that this is artificially altering hormone balance, and must be treated in a holistic fashion.

Personally, I am of the opinion that more light is good (hence why I live in a sub-tropical region).

I will still say that light induced changes in bio-chemistry requires adequate food-derived energetic substrate to take advantage of the changes. All a hormone like T3 does is to allow for the biologic conditions by which energy is used.

Sidenote: and yes, I know there are arguments for saying why more T3 isn't necessarily better. That's a separate discussion altogether.​

\----

>
> N=1 and redox. When I was 21, I could eat whatever I pleased without putting on a single pound....food/environment really didn't matter. Fast forward 30+ years and poor redox --seafood has made a big difference for me when I'm in the sun...for years I used to turn beat red in the face within 5 minutes outside...no more. Just got back from FL with my family and we all tanned beautifully...no sunscreen and spent a lot of time outside. Also, lost a couple pounds down there and felt great and not hungry. Last summer while on the cruise, lost 5 pounds came back to NE then went directly to Denver for a week and gain 5 pounds back plus another 3. My weight hardly ever fluctuates at home. My DO has had me take supplements for years using the testing Yew describes. Almost every time I have seen him over the years, I always have a blockage in my liver, so I do liver cleansing supplements--- N=1. I keep having positive benefits on this site ...that's why I'm here.

Go to cruise => lower stress => better health.
Go to Denver => higher stress => worse health.

This has less (if anything) to do with seafood, and more to do with ambient stress conditions. Figure out what is stressful, and try best to eliminate it.

We must not attribute the gains from stress reduction to the addition of something else.

If the protocols laid out on this site are not conducive for a particular person's stress-resilience, then I see a problem which needs to be fixed. What Gretchen is doing is simply responding to her current state and trying to figure out what is best to get her through whatever challenges she is facing. I'd support that

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2016-01-13T05:24:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-186137>

I know a multitude of people, as well, who live in conditions with great incidences of light, and they make use of it (are out in the sun all the time, aren't using tons of sun screen, etc), and they ONLY utilize incandescent bulbs, candles, and the like while indoors, and yet find the need to make use of "Peaty" methods for their thyroid/metabolism. I suspect this is because, even if we were to follow the line of thought of Janz and/or Jack, if light is increasing T3 and thus the metabolic rate, it is, as Yew stated, merely a signal for that increase, and there will need to be a concomitant increase in biochemical material to continue the support of said increase in T3/metabolic rate. Thus, in a way, there could be a potential undesired and unexpected effect where symptoms are **exacerbated** when light is increased _if_ there is not a concomitant increase in calories, carbs, minerals, b-vitamins, and so on and so forth. Light itself will not fix hypothyroidism nor lead to some massive increase in performance or any metric, really. Hence, the holistic approach Yew was advocating above.

I'm also not convinced of Jack's argument in his recent podcasts that CRON will work if people are gaining "free electrons" from the sun and earth. If there is going to be a rather significant increase in the metabolic rate and thyroid hormone levels, if anything, there would be a need for MORE food. Anyhow, mitochondrial uncoupling, and the use of uncouplers such as DNP, aspirin (and its derivatives), and caffeine, have been shown in many models to increase longevity irrespective of caloric intake (such as shown [here](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1474-9726.2008.00407.x/abstract;jsessionid=90672646FD7F6DC4319A29BA36B08488.f01t03), . This all makes much more sense when coming from the perspective of the researchers I have mentioned before, Nathaniel J. Dominy and Katharine Milton, PhD, and the research on spider monkeys and bonobos.

[http://www.scienceworldreport.com/a...nger-longer-thyroid-hormones-trump-chimps.htm](http://www.scienceworldreport.com/articles/11657/20131218/bonobos-stay-younger-longer-thyroid-hormones-trump-chimps.htm)
**Bonobos Stay Younger for Longer: Thyroid Hormones Trump Chimps**

Here is an interesting observation. The Spider monkeys often live in a similar/the same environment, yet eat many more calories, much more fruit, and thus, more carbs, and in particular, fructose.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2016-01-13T05:34:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-186139>

agree with all of what yew is saying.
it is the natural condition in polar regions to suppress T3 and slow metabolism so as not to expend as much energy in times of stress (scarcity)
this is completely normal for a naturally living mammal, there is not something wrong with you if you live in canada or the south of australia and you feel flatter in winter. Thr problem is still exposing oneself to the modern day stressors that were faced in higher light/more abundant times (ie work/university/monetary issues/tax/etc) and expecting the same resiliency with fewer resources. In this regard i can see it as 100% appropriate to use carbohydrate in winter, just as one would use exogenous (fake) light in order to try and fool the brain, in order to maintain a level of signallinga nd energy production to cope with the persisting stressors.
