What about CT (I'm thinking particularly water immersion) increasing Free T3?

Hibernation in the Burrow: Perhaps suppressing T3 and slowing metabolism in higher latitude regions is one pathway - along with increased desire for carbs splurge into late fall creating insulin resistance for that metabolism slow down ('traditional' northern region holiday foods such as several week consumption of fruit preserve pies, Pork and Omega 6 Turkey) ... a sort of 'hibernation in the burrow' inducing state of fat and omega 6 packing that is meant to last until spring

Active Ice Water Hunter: while conversely if there is consistent exposure to increasing gradient of cold and especially Cold Water immersion, 'burrowing' is foregone and another pathway is being signaled that increases T3 even sans carbs for greater energy to maintain and even increase Fishing (more so than hunting) activities throughout a carb deficient season

### yewwei.tan — 2016-01-13T06:37:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-186143>

> [lilreddgirl said:](/goto/post?id=186142)
>
> What about CT (I'm thinking particularly water immersion) increasing Free T3?
>
> Hibernation in the Burrow: Perhaps suppressing T3 and slowing metabolism in higher latitude regions is one pathway - along with increased desire for carbs splurge into late fall creating insulin resistance for that metabolism slow down ('traditional' northern region holiday foods such as several week consumption of fruit preserve pies, Pork and Omega 6 Turkey) ... a sort of 'hibernation in the burrow' inducing state of fat and omega 6 packing that is meant to last until spring
>
> Active Ice Water Hunter: while conversely if there is consistent exposure to increasing gradient of cold and especially Cold Water immersion, 'burrowing' is foregone and another pathway is being signaled that increases T3 even sans carbs for greater energy to maintain and even increase Fishing (more so than hunting) activities throughout a carb deficient season

Thyroid was addressed in this QH group thread -- [https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/1099389330073960/](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/1099389330073960/)

I will quote only my own posts since it is a closed group:

\----

**Study reference**: 'Drosophila Ionotropic Receptor 25a mediates circadian clock resetting by temperature' (Chen et. al., 2015)

\----

This study in no way shows that "cold is good". It shows that flies have a particular gene that is absolutely necessary for sensitivity to ARTIFICIAL Light/Dark cycles, when (and only when) temperature variations are low. (2-4C difference between light and dark)

Be clear about the experimental conditions:

\- 555nm LEDs used to artificially simulate "daylight"

\- Maximum temperature difference used is 16C to 25C (61F to 77F). Most of the "low amplitude temperature difference" scenarios were done from 25-27C or 25-29C

\- 12h Light, 12h Dark

I see no reason to doubt the measurements of Zeitgeiber time, so we will assume that the Time Indices are accurate.

The study then showed that:

\- If the flies DID NOT have the Ionotropic Receptor 25a (IR25a), they COULD NOT ENTRAIN to a 2-4C variation in daily temperature. Circadian signalling is gone.

\- If the flies DID NOT have IR25a, they COULD ENTRAIN to a large variation in daily temperature (>4C). Circadian signalling is intact. IR25a is ONLY needed for days with low temperature variation

\- If the flies DID HAVE IR25a, then they could entrain to the light source all the time. (which again, is an artificial 555nm LED)

All this study says is that flies have the ability to use temperature cues to entrain themselves to whatever light signals out there.

This does not imply that:

\- Temperature is the dominant factor
\- That a different type of light source would have the same effects

I'm concerned about practical applications to myself (humans). This experiment was explicitly designed to take cryptochrome signalling out of the picture (the researchers state this themselves). Realistically, this is impossible, since cryptochrome-activated light is going to be availble even during winter (more so in today's artificially light world).

Moreover, humans (and mammals) have arguably more sensitive photopigments like melanopsin, which are involved in circadian signalling.

\---

In the above critique, I do not say that "temperature does not matter". All I say is that this study only proves that there are receptors on flies which are absolutely required for circadian entrainment under low-temperature variation conditions.

Realistically, this means nothing for human practical application.

The statement about "Cold being a metabolic trap door" is unsupported by this study, which used balmy temperatures to begin with. Again, this is a study about daily temperature variations, not absolute temperatures.

To be clear, I am not saying that "cold is not a metabolic trap door", just that this study tells us nothing about it.

\----

If one wants to use a study to give context for some other claims (about CT), then they must show exactly what aspect of the study logically supports some part of the claim. I see no such support here.

All the fly study showed was that flies could sense small variations in temperature across a day, and use those small variations to set their circadian clocks.

Can humans do this? Likely yes, but how does that tie back to any of the claims made?

How about going through them one by one

\----

\> Endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) is induced by cold.​

True. People can go look up studies showing all of increased eNOS activity in cold, increased serum NO in cold, inhibition of eNOS through arginine depletion, etc ....

Mechanistically speaking, one very good reason to not favour an NO excess, is because it binds to Cytochrome C Oxidase (Complex 4) in Mitochondria. Once NO is bound to this complex, it cannot accept electrons. Electron Chain Transport falls apart, and this is a feedback signal, which if is triggered enough, will activate caspases that lead to cell apoptosis.

This could likely explain why controlled amounts of NO can seemingly enhance mitochondrial biogenesis. That is, you muck up some mitochondria’s Complex 4 activity with NO, but not enough to send the apoptotic signal. Instead, you get recycling of the mitochondria which NO screwed up

Paper here: ‘**Mitochondrial Biogenesis in Mammals: The Role of Endogenous Nitric Oxide**’ (Nisoli et. al., 2003) -- [http://www.sciencemag.org/content/299/5608/896.short](http://www.sciencemag.org/content/299/5608/896.short)

Obviously, excess NO is still bad.

\----

\> Cold functions as a metabolic trap door​

One of the vaguely possible arguments that can be made is that the sensitivity to temperature variation in the fly study shows that cold is some sort of a switch.

There is no doubt that cold has effects on metabolism. But what does "trap door" mean?

\- Does it mean that there is a threshold value where there is a sudden metabolic shift? (this does happen in certain biologic contexts)

\- Does is mean that there are "inaccessible attributes" of humans that the cold brings out? (not true, but I don't want to enumerate the details now)

Good science demands that Terminology be distinct and have a 1-to-1 mapping from identity to concept; 1 word means 1 thing. That is the only way to prevent confusion, and allow for proper discussion of concepts.

In any case I do not believe the proceeding statement to be true.

\----

Dealing with the sentence in full:

\> Cold functions as a metabolic trap door, shutting down neuropeptide Y in the hypothalamus.​

This claim does not hold up to the observations of many other studies:

**(1)** '**Widespread increases in regional hypothalamic neuropeptide Y levels in acute cold-exposed rats**' (McCarthy et. al., 1993) -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/030645229390388V](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/030645229390388V)

\> To clarify the involvement of hypothalamic neuropeptide Y in cold-induced thermogenesis, groups of rats exposed to 4C for 2.5 or 18 h were compared with warm-adapted rats (22C).
\>
\> Neuropeptide Y was measured in eight selected hypothalamic regions, including those known to be involved in the regulation of energy expenditure.
\>
\> Compared with warm-adapted controls, neuropeptide Y levels were significantly raised by 80-170% in several hypothalamic regions of rats exposed to cold for 2.5 h, namely the medial preoptic area, paraventricular nucleus, ventromedial nucleus, dorsomedial nucleus and lateral hypothalamic area.
\>
\> Neuropeptide Y levels in 18-h cold-exposed rats were similarly elevated in these regions and were also significantly increased in the anterior hypothalamic area (75%).
\>
\> By contrast, neuropeptide Y levels in the arcuate nucleus, the main hypothalamic site of synthesis, were not increased by cold exposure, being significantly reduced by 21% after 2.5 h exposure and comparable with controls after 18 h.​

**(2)** '**Regulation of leptin receptor and NPY gene expression in hypothalamus of leptin-treated obese (ob/ob) and cold-exposed lean mice**' (Mercer et. al., 1997) -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0014579396015256](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0014579396015256)

\> Exposure of lean mice to cold for 24 h caused an induction of leptin receptor and NPY mRNA which was normalized when animals were returned to the warm.​

**(3)** '**Hyperphagia in cold-exposed rats is accompanied by decreased plasma leptin but unchanged hypothalamic NPY**' (Bing et. al., 1998) -- [http://ajpregu.physiology.org/content/274/1/R62.short](http://ajpregu.physiology.org/content/274/1/R62.short)

Title is self explanatory. We should mention that this involved rats exposed to 4C for 21 days. That is chronic cold exposure :\\

Hypothalamic microdissection and assessment of NPY in 8 different regions was again used. So we can say that the observation is likely correct.

**(4)** '**Role of Neuropeptide Y in Cold Stress-Induced Hypertension**' (Han et. al., 1998) -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196978197002970](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0196978197002970)

\> Chronic cold stress (4C) produced a sustained increase in mean arterial pressure in both normotensive and borderline hypertensive rats (BHR). The high blood pressure in BHRs was significantly reversed by a neuropeptide Y (NPY) Y1 receptor antagonist suggesting that NPY is involved in mediating stress-induced hypertension​

That is, blocking NPY activity prevented stress symptoms, so likely elevated NPY during cold stress.

**(5)** '**Neuropeptide Y acts directly in the periphery on fat tissue and mediates stress-induced obesity and metabolic syndrome**' (Kuo et. al., 2007) -- [http://www.nature.com/nm/journal/v13/n7/abs/nm1611.html](http://www.nature.com/nm/journal/v13/n7/abs/nm1611.html)

This time we tie NPY to peripheral adipose tissue signalling (not in a good way) ....

**(6)** '**Role of hypothalamic neuropeptide Y neurons in the defective thermogenic response to acute cold exposure in fatty Zucker rats**' (Bing et. al., 1997) -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306452297001218](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306452297001218)

\> We suggest that increased brown fat thermogenic capacity induced by cold in lean rats may be mediated, at least in part, by decreased neuropeptide Y release in the paraventricular nucleus, resulting in its accumulation in this site
\>
\> Defective thermogenic responses in fatty rats may result from central dysregulation of brown adipose tissue due to sustained and non-suppressible overactivity of hypothalamic neuropeptide Y neurons.​

This is in line with the mechanics discussed prior. There are many papers showing a link between how NPY signalling to adipose tissues prevents the formation of BAT. One proposed mechanism is that since BAT formation is a survival advantage, what we have is accumulation of NPY in the hypothalamus.

Regardless of what the mechanism is, we clearly see higher hypothalamic NPY in the hypothalamus upon chronic cold exposure. (Most experiments used 2.5hrs or longer)

Practically speaking, does this mean anything? Well, NPY is sometimes associated with hunger, though I think there must be other factors at play.

Regardless, there are some people who report feeling absolutely ravenous after Cold Exposure, including indiscriminate eating behaviour (carbs included). I shall not name names

\----

\> This means there are no "safe starches" in cold environments​

This claim is unsupported by the NPY argument.

I have also seen some arguments made before that "carbs encode high energy blue light", supposedly at the mitochondrial level.

First, glucose metabolism produces NADH. Beta oxidation of fatty acids produces NADH and FADH2. Both would generate acetyl-CoA for the TCA cycle, and it being a cycle, would genreate all products during oxidative metabolism anyway. (altenating between Complex 1 and Complex 2 ECT entry, but possibly having one entry point be dominant depending on substate use).

NADH is NADH, whether it comes from fat or carbs. It's still going to donate a quanta of energy equivalent to a 380nm UV photon regardless. Same with FADH2, except that is a 450nm blue photon equivalent of energy.

Mitochondrial ECT only sees such molecules. As far as normal metabolism is concerned, the only exogenous light that can reach a mitochondria is red light in the range of about 600-1200nm (because all other wavelengths are either absorbed or scattered very quickly by water and other proteins).

So no, carbs do not "encode blue light" on the mitochondrial level.

If we were to argue for carbs being "unsafe" during winter time, we would have to look at higher level physiologic cues incuded by carb consumption, that make one unsuitable for survival during winter.

\-----

This is where all the "it depends" arguments will come in.

Does eating carbs lead to more insulin in the blood? It depends. Compared to what? A eucaloric ketogenic diet? A mixed diet? Clearly we can have the same levels of insulin on a variety of different diets. It's not a clear relationship, and will vary on a daily basis and will vary according to the individual.

Sidenote: The only reliable way to reduce absolute insulin levels is to restrict food intake, which some would argue is the "Natural Winter State"

What kind of fat are we talking about? PUFAs?  I can make an argument that PUFAs suppress the metabolism, prevent immune response, act as an anti-freeze of somewhat, and help induce the hibernation state to survive times when food is scarce. Does this sound like modern living to you?

No matter how much "Circadian Optimisation" you do today, the real circadian mismatch is getting up and going to a job for 8 hours a day during the winter, when you were supposed to be sleeping or not doing much. You're not going to live by natural standards anymore, and the practical focus should be on achieving whatever Metabolic state is needed to function under your current conditions.

(And BTW, all the above still assumes that "Winter" as we see today is natural -- [http://tanyewwei.com/blog/ancient-civ-adv-af/](http://tanyewwei.com/blog/ancient-civ-adv-af/))

\----

As usual, I am not prescriptive. I just talk about what I think is wrong, and leave whatever is unlikely harmful to free experimentation

....

### yewwei.tan — 2016-01-13T06:38:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-186144>

**(CONTINUED)
**

**(1)** NO is used when CO2 is low (which usually also means that O2 is low, since CO2 is needed to dissociate O2 from hemoglobin). You'd rather have CO2 over NO, but NO is a great acute backup solution when CO2 is low.

**(2)** UV light is a stressor if appropriate biological transducers or capturing agents are not present. Primary compounds are Melanin (which absorbs UV-A) and 7-dehydrocholesterol (which absorbs UV-B to form Vitamin D).

**(3)** Sunlight contains a big mix of frequencies. The red light and IR frequencies are likely good even with high levels of exposure because of the effect they have on cell water, mitochondria, and cell signalling. Frequencies of light more energetic than red usually require pigments for capture (again, melanin can absorb all of this, step down the energies, and then re-emit IR light)

Nitric Oxide still bad in excess (-\_-)

UV light still bad in excess (-\_-)

\----

**Sidenote**: typically, Melanin absorbs up to photons with energenies corresponding to about a 300nm wavelength; more energetic photons are not dealt with well.​

Interesting paper though, '**Photoprotective Role of Epidermal Melanin Granules against Ultraviolet Damage and DNA Repair in Guinea Pig Skin**' (Ishikawa et. al., 1984) -- [http://cancerres.aacrjournals.org/content/44/11/5195.short](http://cancerres.aacrjournals.org/content/44/11/5195.short)

Total Irradiation energies were: 330, 660, 1300, 2600, 3900, and 8000 J/m^2

Dose rates were either 1.2 W/m^2 or 2.2 W/m^2

\> The skin was exposed to short-wave UV from a 15-watt germicidal lamp (Toshiba GL 15 UV lamp) with predominant emission at 254 nm (dose rate, 1.2 J/sq m/sec) at a distance of 50 cm, or to UV-AB from three 20-watt sunlamp fluorescent tubes (Toshiba FL 20 S. E. sunlamp) with emission at 270 to 440 nm and a peak at 312 nm (dose rate, 2.2 J/sq m/sec) at a distance of 20 cm.​

These are realistic exposure irradiance values.

Look at Figure 4 in the following paper. Even if you adjust for difference in solar incidence angle, most values will get you about 1 W/m^2 UV-A or UV-B exposure (a lot more for UV-A of course). '**The effect of altitude upon the solar UV-B and UV-A irradiance in the tropical Chilean Andes**' (H Piazena, 1996) -- [http://www.researchgate.net/profile...lean\_Andes/links/5548a46d0cf26a7bf4daf7e4.pdf](http://www.researchgate.net/profile/Helmut_Piazena/publication/245169588_The_effect_of_altitude_upon_the_solar_UV-B_and_UVA_irradiance_in_the_tropical_Chilean_Andes/links/5548a46d0cf26a7bf4daf7e4.pdf)

In any case, back to the previous study, our guinea pigs had Melanin granule counts of 15 per nuclues at around 1000 J/m^2 exposure, and only increased slightly to 20 at 8000 J/m^2

If Melanin protection increases linearly to the grain count, then we can say that the protective effect of melanin caps off very quickly, and doesn't offer a lot of protection at extreme UV exposures.

In any case, there was no redness (Erythema) in the guinea pig skin, and yet we see unschedule DNA synthesis (UDS) increase linearly with UV light exposure.

Now, UDS isn't necessarily "bad", but it does tell us that UV light on the skin is a "work stimulator" of some sort.

IMO, any time there is work to be done, you better provide the resources to do that work. That means enough substate like NAD+, oxygen, CO2, etc ...

We can speculate on why Nitric Oxide forms in the skin, but in the acute absence of enough substate, you would get a relative hypoxic state, and eNOS activity being upregulated. I find this (metabolic) explanation plausible.

The guinea pig paper also speculates on other aspects of UV protection by Melanin, included the way melanin granules are packed for maximal UV diffusion.

In any case, too much UV light clearly induces a defence mechanism by the body. Like anything, UV light needs to be dose properly, and the consequences of over-dosing are clearly detrimental. (and definitely more so than over-dosing on Red Light)

\----

I will not discuss why white skin evolved for now, mainly because I do not have enough evidence to argue either way.

Just know that there is some DNA evidence to suggest that white skin did not exist until 7,000 years ago:

\- One study about evolution of light skin -- [http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v507/n7491/abs/nature12960.html](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v507/n7491/abs/nature12960.html)

This was the article where I found that study -- [http://www.livescience.com/42838-european-hunter-gatherer-genome-sequenced.html](http://www.livescience.com/42838-european-hunter-gatherer-genome-sequenced.html)

\- Need to wait for full study -- [http://meeting.physanth.org/program...15-phenotypic-inference-from-ancient-dna.html](http://meeting.physanth.org/program/2015/session05/mathieson-2015-phenotypic-inference-from-ancient-dna.html)

Article discussing this study here -- [http://news.sciencemag.org/archaeology/2015/04/how-europeans-evolved-white-skin](http://news.sciencemag.org/archaeology/2015/04/how-europeans-evolved-white-skin)

I have speculated that humans under Proto-Saturn had uniform UV and IR light exposure, which was likely less intense than today's Sun -- [http://tanyewwei.com/blog/ancient-civ-adv-af/](http://tanyewwei.com/blog/ancient-civ-adv-af/)

\----

\[Study\] '**Structure and dynamics of calmodulin (CaM) bound to nitric oxide synthase peptides: effects of a phosphomimetic CaM mutation**.' (Piazza et. al., 2012) -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22486744](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22486744)

This gets into details, but just know that:

\> The small acidic calcium binding protein, calmodulin (CaM), is required to fully activate the enzyme. \[Nitric oxide synthase (NOS)\]​

\[Study\] '**Nitric oxide, peroxynitrite and nitroso-compounds formation by ultraviolet A (UVA) irradiated human squamous cell carcinoma: potential role of nitric oxide in cancer prognosis**.' (Villiotou V and Deliconstantinos G, 1995) -- [http://europepmc.org/abstract/med/7544092](http://europepmc.org/abstract/med/7544092)

\> Ultraviolet A (UVA) irradiated human squamous cell carcinoma (SCC-13) releases nitrogen oxides, i.e. nitric oxide (NO), peroxynitrite (ONOO-), nitrosocompounds, ammonia (NH3) and hydroxylamine (H2NOH) formed from L-arginine
\>
\> Squamous cell carcinoma possesses higher NO synthase and sGC activity as compared to normal keratinocytes.​

\----

**Prefer CO2 to NO**

\- '**Effect of CO2 on the systemic and coronary circulations and on coronary sinus blood gas tensions**.' (Foëx P and Ryder WA, 1979) -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/497491](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/497491)

\- '**Changes in Microvascular Diameter and Oxygen Tension Induced by Carbon Dioxide**' (Brian R. Duling, 1973) -- [http://circres.ahajournals.org/content/32/3/370.short](http://circres.ahajournals.org/content/32/3/370.short)

Same stuff that we already know, that CO2 allowed for both more blood flow through any blood carrying tubule, as well as increased O2 delivery (via the Bohr effect).

\----

A more detailed discussion of NO, O2, and CO2: '**Role of NO in the O2 and CO2 responsiveness of cerebral and ocular circulation in humans**' (Schmetterer et. al., 1997) -- [http://ajpregu.physiology.org/content/273/6/R2005.short](http://ajpregu.physiology.org/content/273/6/R2005.short)

You can go read all the details, but it basically showed the various effects of NO on CO2 vasolidatory effects, using a non-specific NO inhibitor (and mild ATP inhibitor) to show a slight coupling and overlap of NO and CO2 activity.

Some people will read this as "some NO is always needed to get vasodilatory effects", some would read this as "if enough CO2 is produced, NO is not required".

Whatever the case, clearly some level of acute NO production is great, but that must be able to be quenched very quickly.

**Chronic NO excess is obviously bad.** Anyone interested can search for studies on the compound "L-NAME", which is an arginine inhibitor, and hence NO inhibitor, and all the positive non-specific benefitical effects it has on everything from Alzheimer's disease to terminal stage cancers.​

This then indicates that all disease states are basically chronic hypoxia states, usually marked by an increase in NO, and an increase in Lactate Dehydrogenase activity.

While NO is directly harmful in high doses, getting rid of NO isn't the be-all-end-all solution. You still need to restore metabolic capacity, and that is too long a discussion for this thread

......

\> As expected, breathing 5% CO2 with 95% air produced a significant increase in PCO2 and a significant decrease in pH independent of the pretreatment (Tables 3 and6). Hypercapnia did not affect systemic hemodynamics.
\>
\> CO2 breathing increased FPA during all inhalation period​

Fundus Pulsation Amplitude (FPA) is a method used to measure choroid (in the eye) blood flow. So the same stuff we already knew -- CO2 increases blood flow.

\> Pretreatment with L-NMMA significantly reduced the change in PCO2 in the MCA and in the choroid (Tables 4 and 5).
\>
\> However, this L-NMMA-induced effect was almost completely reversed by coadministration of L-arginine​

That is to say, inhibiting Nitric Oxide Synthase activity (no NO present) also decreased CO2 levels in particular tissues.

Note: L-NMMA (L-NG-monomethyl Arginine citrate) is a non-specific NOS inhibitor

**Caveat**: p-values were horrible, and error margins were like +- 27.5% -\_- -- [http://ajpregu.physiology.org/highwire/markup/20235/expansion?width=1000&height=500&iframe=true&postprocessors=highwire\_figures,highwire\_math,highwire\_inline\_linked\_media](http://ajpregu.physiology.org/highwire/markup/20235/expansion?width=1000&height=500&iframe=true&postprocessors=highwire_figures%2Chighwire_math%2Chighwire_inline_linked_media)​

All this is saying is that CO2 and NO act co-operatively to exstablish vasodilatory effects.

It's probably easier to understand this by viewing the effects of L-NMMA under normal breathing conditions.

\> FPA was reduced by −19% during administration of the NO synthase inhibitor (P < 0.005 vs. placebo and vs. baseline, Fig. 3).
\>
\> This decrease in FPA (Fig. 4) was almost abolished by coadministration ofL-arginine (−3% vs. baseline, P < 0.001).
\>
\> As expected, exhaled NO was reduced by -41% during L-NMMA administration (P < 0.005 vs. baseline and placebo, Fig. 3). Coadministration of L-arginine increased exhaled NO to 82% of baseline (P < 0.001, Fig.4).​

\----

Collagen gets damaged by UV light, '**UV-vis and FT-IR spectra of ultraviolet irradiated collagen in the presence of antioxidant ascorbic acid**.' (Metreveli et. al., 2010) -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20036425](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20036425)

This should be obvious enough. since all the amino acids basically absorb UV light well. ie: UV light is excitatory to proteins, and over-excitation leads to changes in protein shape.

**Sidenote**: UV light as an action potential agenst may be a generic mechanism to mitochondrial energetics. This is based of Guenter Albrecht-Buehler's work​

Collagen and water superconducts charge (Infrared photons), not UV light -- [http://www.i-sis.org.uk/SuperconductingQuantumCoherentWaterinNanospace.php](http://www.i-sis.org.uk/SuperconductingQuantumCoherentWaterinNanospace.php)

\----

And regarding UV transmission in the body, this is not possible to any large degree.

How can UV light transmission be good if:

**(a) Proteins absorb UV so well (without emitting it)**

There are lots of proteins bloacking the path of UV, and they do nothing to help with "bouncing" UV light from protein to protein. The way it works is that proteins absorb UV light and do stuff with it (without re-emission).

**(b) Water itself doesn't emit UV**

And if you want to talk about water's absorption specturm, it doesn't even absorb UV light well in the bulk state -- [http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/water\_vibrational\_spectrum.html](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/water_vibrational_spectrum.html)

If you look at image -- [http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/images/water\_spectrum\_2.gif](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/images/water_spectrum_2.gif) , you will see that absorption coefficient drops exponentially from 10^5 at just over 100nm light, to below 0.01 once you hit 200nm light, and doesn't even go above

\----

Same with hair, '**Hair color changes and protein damage caused by ultraviolet radiation**' (Ana Carolina Santos Nogueira and Ines Joekes, 2004) -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1011134404000375](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1011134404000375)

UV-B more damaging, but UV-A still damaging. Melanin depletion and color bleaching observed.

\----

First off, a couple of important philosophical points:

**(1) Practicality first and foremost**

Practicality of application is achieved not by preaching "this is the way". It is achieved by saying:

\- These are some observations which come up in studies, and these are the mechanisms to which we think these observations are explained by

\- Do your own enlightened risk-taking. You are the captain of your ship

\=> I think A, B, C are not harmful. You can experiment with those methodologies with little risk.

\=> I think X, Y, Z and definitely harmful. Stay away from those.

\=> I think L, M, N are uncertain. There may be some benefit given your context, but there are also known risks. You should likely back off if you experience Ls, Ms, and Ns side effects

\----

**(2) One Disproof is all you need for a theory to be invalid.**

I am not "missing the forests for the trees". I am saying, "hey look there, that brick isn't resting on a stable foundation"

I am claiming that many tenets in the CT series are false.

This doesn't mean that "CT is bad". All it means that you cannot universalise the concepts like the CT series claims to do.

"Disproof" just means disproving in the generic sense. You can qualify a person's context further to allow them to benefit from CT.

eg: your Liver is shot, and your cortisol is high. No CT 4 u pl0x

\-----

**(3) Observations DO NOT compose. Mechanisms do**

I can mechanistically compose the 3 statemetns together:

\- Binding of Nitric Oxide (NO) to Complex 4 (Cytochrome C Oxidase) prevents the complex from accepting electrons coming down Electron Chain Transport (ECT). This leads to many bad side effects. \[insert $DESCRIPTION\_OF\_SIDE\_EFFECTS\]. Therefore ensuring that Complex 4 is not blocked is important

\- Red Light has been empirically shown to dissociate NO from Complex 4, with little side effects if doesd properly. \[insert $RED\_LIGHT\_DOSING\_CONSIDERATIONS\]

\- Methylene Blue has been empirically shown to dissociate NO from Complex 4, with little sidde effects if dosed properly, \[insert $METHYLENE\_BLUE\_DOSING\_CONSIDERATIONS\]

\- Therefore Red Light + Methylene Blue is likely an additive effect (when used properly).

\- Are there alternatives to this? Yes, you can try and recyle the mitochondria. Else, NO binding to Complex 4 is only safely reversible through the 2 methods described (given our current state of knowledge).

\- This is likely a safe experiment to run. Your Mileage will Vary.

I have identified a mechanism, and then shown complentary forces that independently affect that mechanism, and those forces likely do not interfere with each other. I can therefore compose the 2 and recommend an experiment.

Standalone high level observations, to which there is no known mechanism, cannot be composed in this fashion.

One cannot say, "T3 is raised by cold" (in the short term), and "Brown Adipose Tissue increases Energy Expenditure", and then attribute both of them to "cold is good" or "cold is a metabolic trapdoor" (assuming that is a desirable side effect).

Those are just 2 observations, but there are more assumptions:

**(1) Is raising T3 independently a good thing?** Likly yes, but we need to weigh it against the other side effects of cold exposure (increased TSH, increased glucocorticoids).

Also, is raised level of T3 temporary? Likely yes (at least that's what the study you quoted claimed to show, I will give a rebuttal below)

What other ways raise T3 as much as sitting in the cold would? Do they give less side effects? (and therefore, what is the most practical route for a particular person)

**(2) Is raising energy expenditure via BAT a good thing?** Probably yes, but again we need to ask:

\[2a\] What are the "costs" of getting BAT?
\[2b\] Can we assume that I will get the same effect (studies often done on healthy young men)
\[2c\] How do I test that I am benefitting from a higher metabolic rate?
\[2d\] What other ways are there to get the benefits of BAT (mainly heat and cold tolerance, as well as increased energy expenditure)? (I personally other factors trump BAT, but that's a topic for another day)

**(3) What are the mechanics of T3, specificially in the context of cold?** Do these effects generalise to chronically "better T3 levels"? Are the effects beneficial outside of a cold context? etc ....

Here's another OBSERVATION that T3 is required for BAT formation and thermoregulation in the cold -- [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC209445/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC209445/)

Again, it is up to the proponents of cold exposure to prove (under specific contexts and observations) that the mechanisms work reliably and safely.

If it is not reliable and safe, then appropriate qualifications need to be made, so that enlightened experiment may occur.

Instead, what I see is the consant "Cold is Good" message being sent, while trying to use faulty mechanics to support that claim.

\-----

**Motivation: Practicality first and foremost**

I have seen some people benefit tremendously from CHRONIC cold exposure. 1 hour in a cold tub is still chronic exposure.

The CT series claims that cold is good, regardless of the context. (because physics  , which I have said I think to be falsified as well -- [http://tanyewwei.com/blog/ct-hormetic/](http://tanyewwei.com/blog/ct-hormetic/))

But I have seen many more people to whom CT has been not only a non-solution, but an actual stressor that caused them negative side effects. Everything from increased hunger, to extreme shivering, to an inability to adapt to a high fat diet, etc ....

The correct way forward is to state all the risk factors, how to spot those in yourself, and then run the appropriate experiment with the appropriate precautions.

\----

### yewwei.tan — 2016-01-13T06:38:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-186145>

**(CONTINUED)**

Sidenote: "Cold Adapted" animals of all sort increase the Poly-unsaturated fatty acids (PUFA) in their system when faced with cold stress.

Hibernating mammals reduce their metabolic rate and basically become diabetic during winter. This is a survival response, and the PUFAs probably have a great deal to do with it.

\- 'The role of polyunsaturated fatty acids in the expression of torpor by mammals: a review' (Daniel Munro and Donald W. Thomas, 2004) -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944200604000078](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0944200604000078)

\- '**Fatty acid elongases in mammals: Their regulation and roles in metabolism**' (Jakobsson et. al., 2006) -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0163782706000051](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0163782706000051)

Now, for anyone interested, go research the effects of PUFAs in generating metabolic illnesses in humans  (especially Diabetes and Cancer)

If you want my biased opinion. Excess PUFAs are incredibly stressful. PUFA restriction on a diet is almost always helpful. (DHA is a PUFA BTW. Again, something to experiment with)

\----

I then went on to show that excess NO is a bad thing. If cold exposure leads to excess NO, then cold exposure is a bad thing.

The more interesting question is "**How do I test for Excess NO when performing a stressful activity like CT?**"

A couple of possibilities:

\- Nitric Oxide saliva test strips

\- Put a drop of Methylene Blue onto the cold-exposed tissue after CT. If the methylene blue disappears before 6 hours, the tissue is likely hypoxic, and not up to the task of cold exposure

\- Best case -- do Capnography during CT, and monitor CO2 production levels. CO2 should ideally rise during cold exposure, or at the very least, not drop. The moment CO2 levels start to fall below your normal baseline, that is when you terminate CT.

\-----

**NPY increased upon 18-hours cold exposure.** \*\*(IMPORTANT!!! 18 hours of exposure to 4C temperatures)\*\*

Again, it seems that high NPY in the hypothalamus inhibits BAT formation.

A quote straight from the study, '**Mol. Cells, Vol. 23, No. 1, pp. 88-93 Short-Term Cold Exposure May Cause a Local Decrease of Neuropeptide Y in the Rat Hypothalamus**' (Park et. al., 2007)

\> NPY injected directly into the PVN suppresses the activity of the sympathetic nerve fibers innervating IBAT, and IBAT thermogenesis, by reducing UCP-1 mRNA levels​

IMPORTANT: The Park study did find that \*\*2.5 hours of exposure to cold\*\* decreased NPY.

This is very different from the 18-hour cold exposure from the previous study. This is more "acute cold" than it is chronic cold.

Either way, it's still bloody cold

Note that Park et. al. state that hypothalamic NPY levels are fickle:

\> Long-term (weeks) cold exposure results in enhanced UCP-1 mRNA and hyperphagia, but hypothalamic NPY remains unchanged (Bing et al., 1998). By contrast, short-term cold exposure (hours) increases NPY in most hypo-thalamic nuclei, but decrease them in the ARC (McCarthyet al., 1993).​

\----

Another interesting observation (see Figure 1)

\> The cold stress decreased internal cT (ΔT, difference from the mean basal temperatureby) approximately 1°C within 0.5 h, and increased heart rate (bpm, beats per minute)​

Rats were cold, and slowly getting hypothermic throughout the entirety of cold exposure.

This is exactly what you DO NOT want in practical CT. Proper physiological response (at least if you count someone like Wim Hof as an example) is maintenance of core temperature.

Is this a sign of an undesirable stressor? What happens if we let the rats acclimatise to mildly cold conditions (say 15C) for a week before running the 4C experiment? Is this decrease in core temperature coupled to the observed decrease in NPY? No clue, more research is needed, but just bear in mind the context of this study.

\----

BTW, if you look at Fig 3, after cold exposure, NPY-IR products in:

\- median preoptic nucleus (MnPO) barely changed
\- septohypothalamic nucleus (SHy) DECREASED by what looks to be 10%
\- medial preoptic nucleus (MPO) INCREASED by about 20%
\- lateral preoptic area (LPO) INCREASED by about 15-20%
\- anterior hypothalamic area (AHA) INCREASED by about 15%
\- paraventricular nucleus (PVN) DECREASED by >20%
\- dorsal hypothalamic area (DA) INCREASED slightly
\- dorsomedial hypothalamic nucleus (DMH) DECREASED by probably 15%
\- lateral hypothalamic area (LHA) INCREASED by maybe 10-15%
\- ventromedial hypothalamic nucleus (VMH) DECREASED by about 20%
\- arcuate nucleus (ARC) DECREASED by about 20%

First off, the PVN value is likely the most important. A 20% decrease in NYP activity in that region is significant.

We must still be wary to see that this "decrease in NPY" isn't universal across all physiologically relevant regions of the Hypothamalus.

This decrease is also certain nowhere compared to the magnitude of increase seen in all the other studies which I cited (usually about 70% increase)

\----

Quoting the Thyroid study.

\> 1. Four men, of ages varying from 23 to 28 years, living at Halley Bay, Antarctica (750 31' ,S 260 39' W), were exposed to a mean air temperature of 6.6°C​

Young men in 1970. Presumably healthy enough.

\> 2. The concentration of serum triiodothyronine (T3) rose significantly by the second day, remained raised, and returned to pre-exposure levels within 2 days of return to a normal environment​

Table a look at Table 1. You you indeed see significant rise in T3. But again, this is decreased immediately to baseline levels after cold exposure.

The researchers are being honest. T3 increase is not likely a sustained thing. We cannot say that acute cold exposure leads to chronic increases in T3 (which is usually what is beneficial for curing disease)

\> 3. The concentration of serum thyroxine (T4) rose more slowly than did the T3, reaching a maximum in 3-4 days and also returning to normal within 2 days of return to a normal environment.​

This likely correlates with the actual increased production of Thyroid hormones, and likely is matched to the increased TSH which the researchers cite from other longer term cold exposure studies.

Again, increasing TSH is usually seen as "not good", but that needs to be qualified during cold exposure. One can speculate that the body needs more TSH and thyroid production in response to cold stress.

\> 4. There was a wide individual variation in the change of concentration of serum cortisol.​

Yup, and Cortisol in 3 of the 4 subjects followed the same pattern of initially dropping in the first few days of cold exposure, followed by a rise (again, see Table 1)

Subject I.L had the highest Cortisol to begin with, and his cortisol immediately rose upon cold exposure, along with his T3 levels.

The most interesting thing IMO, is that in every single person, the higher the Cortisol, the higher the T3. Are these linked somehow? If so, does this mean that the "stress response" is directly linked to the "amount of T3 needed"?

\----

I think people know my opinion by now. I think Cold is a stressor, and the rise in T3 is meant to meet that stressor.

In any case, this was chronic cold exposure to 6C air. Very different from acute cold exposure to 10-14C water (which is the recommendation of the CT protocol)

People should take this information and experiment with the CT protocol, but do not be pulled into thinking that "more cold is better".

....

(**DONE**)

Results: Participants lost 3.7% and 2.2% total fat following the reduced-CHO diet and STD diet, resp. (_p_<0.05 for difference between diets). The reduced-CHO diet induced a decrease in subcutaneous-abdominal, intra-abdominal, and thigh-intermuscular adipose tissue (−7.1%, −4.6%, and −11.5%, resp.), and the STD diet induced a decrease in total lean mass. Loss of fat mass following the reduced CHO diet arm was associated with lower insulin AUC (_p_<0.05) during the SMT.

Note that this reduce carbs is not necessarily ketotic, nor is it zero carbs, so for any ladies having issues out there - this is food for thought....

### yewwei.tan — 2016-01-14T03:24:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-186242>

> [lilreddgirl said:](/goto/post?id=186146)
>
> Also thoughts on a 'Pufa depletion diet'
>
> Interested in consideration of the Dr. Sebi diet... not all explanations of diet are scientific yet does have proof of having cured conditions including several previous documented AIDS cases
>
> basis of diet involves exclusive eating so called 'electric' live foods only which are plant (sea and land) based... diet is meant for people with higher amount of skin melanin (and therefore will perhaps not be beneficial or as beneficial long term to those with less amounts of skin melanin) ... foods that are 'mucous producing' such as starch containing plants are excluded... foods considered having been 'hybridized' by man through even some traditional hybridization practices are excluded for no longer having the natural balance of vitamins and minerals...I understand plants are tested for starch to exclude from list of recommendation... Wild herbs are promoted as well as sea vegetables especially Sea Moss (Irish Moss). The supplements used in therapy in addition to the diet often contain Sea Moss - (my thoughts perhaps bringing in a form of DHA into the diet in addition to that endogenously created by the body).
>
> while maintaining an energetic 'PUFA depletion state' other than of algal DHA imputs. In the past Dr. Kruse has mentioned on a Face Book comment equatorial vegans being able to access a biochemical pathway whereas they would produce DC current through consumption of large amount of green plant material driving NO. This made me think of this diet and I asked if those tapping this pathway would achieve adequate levels of DHA if significant amount of DHA was also consumed. He answered yes. (he said most people were not consuming it). The significant amount of algae consumed on this diet would provide that.

I've stated in my threads above that I believe NO to be required as a critical stress-response molecule. Having more Nitric Oxide Synthase activity for the sake of it being higher is a bad thing.

Green plant material is a complicated issue. I do not believe at all in the argument, "equatorial vegans being able to access a biochemical pathway whereas they would produce DC current through consumption of large amount of green plant material driving NO". There is no plausible mechanism being described there, and if the mechanism is increase in NO, well, I've already explained the mechanics in my long posts above.

If you want to take a biological stance, leaves and seeds are the most toxic plant material to be eating, since those are life-critical components of plants, which is in the interest of plants to protect. Leaves being the energy producing component of the plant, and seeds being the reproductive component.

As such, these are usually where you find the most about of "life force" , ie: stuff to help protect the life of the plant (at the expense of the life of the animal trying to eat the plant). We can go into all the potentially harmful components found in leaves and seeds, but I'll leave that to anyone whom is interested in that topic to do their own research.

Note that just because these are "toxins", that fact doesn't imply that they will be harmful to a particular person. Too many unknown variables to account for, especially the unknown role of the gut flora in mediating body-wide signalling. Observationally, some people react to this very well from a hormetic stressor perspective, and have their stress tolerance improved as a result. Others fall flat on their face (like me )

The components of a plant that are the least toxic are then the fruits (which are designed to be eaten), and the roots (which are usually dormant storage components of the plant). Again, this is not a universal rule, and we need to take into consideration specific foods, and not just plants in general -- an apple (high in pectin) is very different to a mango (potential allergies if you are allergic to poison ivy) which is different to a banana (potential serotonin imbalances if you are sensitive to tryptophan).

Tolerance to sea vegetables will also vary. Some people cannot deal with the proteins found in these things. Histamine intolerances can be very real and very harmful. Polysaccharides like Carrageenan are known carcinogens. etc .... Again, self experiment is needed.

Starch is another ball of wax. First off, the concept of "starch" is useless, because nobody just eats the polysaccharide amylose or amylopectin. They eat it in real food, which comes with a whole set of other things which can be potentially good or bad. Potatoes have saponins, Corn have prolamines, Wheat has gluten, and even Rice has shown to be inflammatory in some people.

Then there is the issue of amylopectin vs amylose ratio, which some in vivo imaging studies showing potato amylose lodging itself in intestinal tight junctions and causing an inflammatory response. In some people this never happens, and in some people, this happens but causes no issues. etc ......

Personally, I'm fine with fruit, fine with sugars of all forms, fine with rice, OK with potatoes, OK with wheat, horrible with any greens or fibrous vegetables, etc ..... I've got my own (thankfully large enough) list of acceptable foods to be consumed safely, and that's what matters. I do not buy into any one diet as a result. Figure out what works for you.

>
> Very interesting, for the first 7-8 months of pretty much daily CT, I experienced extreme shivering and minimal adaption, however when I added light (and gastroparesis reduced, thus calories increased) I adapted much faster and now I only shiver very slightly and it is 10 times easier.

Yup, red light is usually very helpful. There is still a problem with excess red light, but I assume that you've got that issue sorted out.

You know my opinion on CT being stressor by now , so I'd agree with the "more light => better stress tolerance" line of logic.

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=186233)
>
> I don't think ATP is that important, since good ATP production isn't enough to supply energy to power athletes. By a factor of five, iirc?

I have never heard of this claim, nor do I think it is accurate in any possible way.

Muscle contraction is fundamentally dependent on ATP. Whether or not the mechanisms here are fully accurate, there is undoubtedly the use of an ATP molecule every time a myosin head moves.

As for the other aspects of exercise, like delivery of blood-borne substrate like oxygen, sure, we can make some case for electrical potentials helping to get things to where they need to be (which is also heavily dependent on ATP to achieve the needed redox cycling and electric potentials ). But to even move a millimeter, ATP must be used. No ATP => no movement of anything.

Regardless, outside the exercise scenario, its pretty clear that the more ATP there is the better. It is clearly THE MOST important Electron Withdrawing Cardinal Adsorbent across all eukaryotic organisms (and almost all organisms, with the exception of some which can use GTP).

Sidenote: ATP is actually stable in solution, meaning that it can float around in excess completely safety. Realistically, the body is always trying to produce enough ATP, and ATP excess is never a phenomena.

>
> Regardless, outside the exercise scenario, its pretty clear that the more ATP there is the better. It is clearly THE MOST important Electron Withdrawing Cardinal Adsorbent across all eukaryotic organisms (and almost all organisms, with the exception of some which can use GTP).
>

My experience (N=1) I have both a 660 and 630nm UFO Red LED. I do not use it daily. I also only use it btwn sunrise and 4pm. On grey cloudy days I goes on first thing in the am. I sit in front of it for no more than 15mins. I've also done short 12-15min HIITS in front of it. when I had pneumonia back in Nov/Dec - I sat in front of it with it shining on my back to activate healing in my lungs, while doing the nebulizer. This is my N=1.

### Linz — 2016-01-15T16:12:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-186354>

And my experience (Hope this os OK in your diary Yew). I use 660/880nm therapy pad on my horses and some love it for the full 20mins it allows before switching off. Others very definitely let me know when they have had enough (fidgeting, pushing). I use it myself (at a distance ) usually on dull mornings and nowhere near daily but love the feeling it gives. Last night I burned my hand lifting a very hot and heavy pan. It was very sore and I kept it in icy water until bedtime. Still really sore when it came out of the water I tried shining my bionase (630nm ) into the water with almost instant relief. I was still in some pain trying to sleep but woke up this morning with only the smallest discomfort and much less visible damage than I expected.
Lilred, I suggest you try for yourself and just don't overdo it. Therapy devices will give instructions/automatic cutoffs etc if you want them

This comment is over-simplifying the the actual comments.

Yes, Jack has been flippant about the comment to "move to a better environment" before, but even his point is much more nuanced, and really is about adapting your behaviours to your environment. (I disagree with this of course )

I'll repeat part of Josh's post:

The problem is still exposing oneself to the modern day stressors that were faced in higher light/more abundant times (ie work/university/monetary issues/tax/etc) **and expecting the same resiliency with fewer resources**. **In this regard \[HIGH STRESS\]** i can see it as 100% appropriate to use carbohydrate in winter, just as one would use exogenous (fake) light in order to try and fool the brain, in order to maintain a level of signallinga nd energy production to cope with the persisting stressors.​

Simple point: Dealing with stress requires resources.

This is NOT a recommendation for everyone to eat carbs, and this is DEFINITELY NOT a recommendation to start pounding the carbohydrate.

How about 100g of carbs a day . Enough to keep baseline metabolic stores in check (like Liver glycogen), while trying your best to keep stress low and avoiding PUFAs.

Sidenote: It's more likely the restriction of energetic substrate while under high stress that causes neolithic disease. Exactly what @[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/) experienced last year in (southern hemisphere) winter when he tried to go Keto while dealing with many stress-inducing clients​

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=186372)
>
> \-----------------
> Please give list of examples for actions that improve T4-->T3 conversion,
> on top of:
> iodine/potassium iodide and selenium, light
> and all 34 micronutrients as analyzed by Spectracel.com Micronutrient test.
> [http://www.spectracell.com/clinicians/products/mnt/](http://www.spectracell.com/clinicians/products/mnt/)
> \======================================
>
> .

I am not here to recommend a fix. I just used it to highlight a possible fault in the chain.

Oh, and PUFAs can likely inhibit the action of Thyroid hormone on the cell , meaning that one can be making enough Thyroid hormone, and lab tests come up with nice values of T3 (and low rT3, and what not), but yet the body is still functionally hypo-thyroid (cells do not see the thyroid hormone floating around)

\-----

And anyone saying "Just Go into Nature", **anyone experienced on this forum knows that already**.

Some like @[Shijin13](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/969/) , like many others on this forum, already know that modernity imposes major stresses to the body, and already try their darnest best when try to deal with the situation.

I am a young single guy, with enough funds and enough baseless gall to do crazy shit like uproot myself and move 2,000km North from Melbourne to Cairns, all the while it losing 1 month of productive employment and costing me $7,000, and not to mention all the other factors like family and social obligations which I just said "FUCK YOU" to.

Now add a bunch of Chaos!!??! and Havoc!! (codename for Shijin's kids ), a house, a job, and god knows what else, and it's already fucking amazing that Shinjin managed to make a move to a low nn-EMF location in the first place.

Oh, and moving to Cairns didn't automatically fix me  ....... being in the sun and ocean every damn day for a long time didn't fix me. Eating seafood did not. CT did not. None of the tactics here did, and none of the tactics from almost any Internet health resource would.

I had a specific condition that required specific targeting to fix. Look up my very long commentary about Mudpacking, liver support, and dietary switches to avoid liver stress (low protein and fat), that I've gone into more than enough detail about. Only after 7 months of that do I feel that I'm in a better position to meet the challenges of life.

Over-simplifications like "Head out into Nature" is going to do nothing to help the problems that so many people face.

\----

OK, now I need to move house ..... while doing a ton of work for me new job

....

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2016-01-16T01:27:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-186427>

ATP, ADP, phosphate ions, and kinesin walking on microtubules. Here may be a better animation of it (22 seconds):

The book, "Life's Ratchet: How Molecular Machines Extract Order from Chaos" by physicist and engineer Peter Hoffman (who began studying biology, or more so, biophysics and medical physics) has a great deal of information on molecular motors and how ATP plays a pivotal role in powering them in their movement in and on cells to allow for everything to happen. He is definitely not aware of the work of Ling, Pollack, Miles Mathis, etc. (at least as far as I'm aware), but he has done a great job of applying physics and engineering to biology and biochemistry, evolutionary development, etc.

Point being, there is, as Yew pointed out, no evidence that ATP is not important, unneeded, etc. It merely may serve different functions or participate in biochemical reactions and life processes in different manners than typically thought by those only buying into Peter Mitchell's ideas.

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2016-01-16T05:36:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-186453>

I don't mean to be blunt, but the above has NOTHING to do with anything that Yew is experimenting with and speaking about, educating various individuals here. Read his DHA article, or as I have said many times already, read Chris Masterjohn, PhD, all his articles on lipids; then, get to reading Dr. Peat on dietary lipids; Andrew Kim, Peter @ Hyperlipid, and others have good articles as well.

What is the evidence that one should have a mid range value for any of the fatty acids above, let alone all of them? If I'm not mistaken, you largely come from the perspective of Kane et al and her lipid replacement ideas. Her ideas are irrelevant to both Jack's approach, and what people such as Dr. Peat advocate. IIRC, she completely buys into the fluid mosaic model of the "lipid bilayer", and she has said NOTHING about cholesterol sulfate moities, sulfated neurosteroids (PregS, DHEA-S, etc), MINOS, etc. and their impact on everything typically regarded to as the "lipid bilayer". Additionally, I haven't really seen any quality research from her or her husband, just a bunch of really simplistic articles making unfounded claims on o36 ratios, and the ratios of specific o3 and o6 lipids, and then attempting to explain how lipid replacement with her protocol can play a pivotal role in shaping one's recovery, enhancing cognitive function, etc. There is one Asian researcher who I believe you and her have cited, and he really only has one "good" study to his name about his "4:1" ratio idea, and even that is very easy to debunk.

As Yew, Kim, and Masterjohn have explained in immense detail many times, there is very little need for ANY dietary PUFA, whether "shorter chain" (ALA, LA) or long chain (DHA, AA, etc), and DHA is likely the only "essential" PUFA, and even then, in milligram amounts in unhealthy, lactating, and/or growing individuals, let alone a grown adult with no documented health problems. Or, perhaps even Dr. Peat is correct with his more nuanced idea, in that mead acid alone, in addition to the other n7 and n9 fats, are optimal, and there isn't even a necessity for DHA.

Also, I'm not sure how 100 grams of carbs on a 2500 calorie diet is "possibly acceptable". How would even getting the majority of one's 2500 calories from carbs be harmful? A dysregulation of glucose/fructose metabolism is likely a/the problem in "carbohydrate intolerance", not glucose/fructose itself. Hence the well documented history of extremely hig carb, rather high calorie diets being used to completely reverse/cure diabetic and obesogenic conditions for decades (in addition to Roy Swank's research on extremely low fat diets for the treatment of MS). As Dr. Peat has written about, in the late 1800's there were even two researchers who started treating diabetic patients with pure sucrose.

If there is some extreme problem oxidizing glucose, fructose, lactose, maybe galactose, and other whole-foods sugars can be experimented with, with supplementation of niacinamide (if one doesn't have extremely low serotonin/messed up neurotransmitters), aspirin and/or aspirin derivatives, methylene blue, quinones, and other compounds, will likely reverse the problem, especially when dietary PUFAs and xenoestrogens are removed.

> This is NOT a recommendation for everyone to eat carbs, and this is DEFINITELY NOT a recommendation to start pounding the carbohydrate.
>
> How about 100g of carbs a day . Enough to keep baseline metabolic stores in check (like Liver glycogen), while trying your best to keep stress low and avoiding PUFAs.
>
>
> Sidenote: It's more likely the restriction of energetic substrate while under high stress that causes neolithic disease. Exactly what @[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/) experienced last year in (southern hemisphere) winter when he tried to go Keto while dealing with many stress-inducing clients​
>
>
>
>
>
> I am not here to recommend a fix. I just used it to highlight a possible fault in the chain.
>
> Oh, and PUFAs can likely inhibit the action of Thyroid hormone on the cell , meaning that one can be making enough Thyroid hormone, and lab tests come up with nice values of T3 (and low rT3, and what not), but yet the body is still functionally hypo-thyroid (cells do not see the thyroid hormone floating around)
>
> \-----
>
> And anyone saying "Just Go into Nature", **anyone experienced on this forum knows that already**.
>
> Some like @[Shijin13](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/969/) , like many others on this forum, already know that modernity imposes major stresses to the body, and already try their darnest best when try to deal with the situation.
>
> I am a young single guy, with enough funds and enough baseless gall to do crazy shit like uproot myself and move 2,000km North from Melbourne to Cairns, all the while it losing 1 month of productive employment and costing me $7,000, and not to mention all the other factors like family and social obligations which I just said "FUCK YOU" to.
>
> Now add a bunch of Chaos!!??! and Havoc!! (codename for Shijin's kids ), a house, a job, and god knows what else, and it's already fucking amazing that Shinjin managed to make a move to a low nn-EMF location in the first place.
>
> Oh, and moving to Cairns didn't automatically fix me  ....... being in the sun and ocean every damn day for a long time didn't fix me. Eating seafood did not. CT did not. None of the tactics here did, and none of the tactics from almost any Internet health resource would.
>
> I had a specific condition that required specific targeting to fix. Look up my very long commentary about Mudpacking, liver support, and dietary switches to avoid liver stress (low protein and fat), that I've gone into more than enough detail about. Only after 7 months of that do I feel that I'm in a better position to meet the challenges of life.
>
> Over-simplifications like "Head out into Nature" is going to do nothing to help the problems that so many people face.
>
> \----
>
> OK, now I need to move house ..... while doing a ton of work for me new job
>
> ....

Thanks for clarifying what I meant when I was talking about resources

### Shijin13 — 2016-01-16T17:21:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/take-it-slow.9428/post-186481>

> [bio-fractal-soul-self said:](/goto/post?id=186427)
>
>
> ATP, ADP, phosphate ions, and kinesin walking on microtubules. Here may be a better animation of it (22 seconds):
>
>
> The book, "Life's Ratchet: How Molecular Machines Extract Order from Chaos" by physicist and engineer Peter Hoffman (who began studying biology, or more so, biophysics and medical physics) has a great deal of information on molecular motors and how ATP plays a pivotal role in powering them in their movement in and on cells to allow for everything to happen. He is definitely not aware of the work of Ling, Pollack, Miles Mathis, etc. (at least as far as I'm aware), but he has done a great job of applying physics and engineering to biology and biochemistry, evolutionary development, etc.
>
> Point being, there is, as Yew pointed out, no evidence that ATP is not important, unneeded, etc. It merely may serve different functions or participate in biochemical reactions and life processes in different manners than typically thought by those only buying into Peter Mitchell's ideas.

I found this mechanism fascinating when learning about it in A&P this fall. And it really made me stop at think, especially with regards to muscle activation and soreness. How is the muscle soreness linked to troponin & myosin activation and crossbridge formation and the CA+ efflux in to the muscle filaments and the generation of ADP and P+? is the soreness the result of the CA+ efflux itself to activate the myosin? or the result of the depletion of ATP as a result of the crossbridge?

I'm not sure soreness after movement is a negative. I do think that there maybe issues when the layers facia becomes stuck, though given my own experiences of facia issues, that are not related to exercise but stress. Which tie into @[yewwei.tan](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6824/) comments below.

Stress does not always manifest in a fight or flight response. IME it is internalized until it reaches a level the body can no longer absorb and in the past resulted in migraines, explosive anger, short temper or general apathy and depression. Stress requires a whole host of thing to deal with. One for me has always been exercise. Over the past 4 &1/2 years I've avoided this, which for me wasn't a smart choice. I've ignored the needed to exercise daily and it ended up being a bad thing for me, and I substituted CT - which triggered some dopamine as well as other stress responses which counter balanced my other environmental stresses but not enough to mitigated the internalization of the stress I'm exposed to. Since I've been working out again - I feel better. more connected to myself, along with less impact of stress...

As for moving... in my short life I've moved a total number of 21 times. with the longest period of living in one region being my move to the DMV, yet even when living in the DMV I've moved a total of 5 times in 14 years. I think I'm a nomad at heart. DH on the other hand is more of a tree (LOL). As for Chaos and Havoc they rolled with the move... I think we should be more nomadic, hence why we desire vacations to get away from our daily lives. but being nomadic doesn't work in our modern world... so we're back to figuring out how to mitigate the stresses of a modern world

Which leads me to end with this:

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=186401)
>
> This comment is over-simplifying the the actual comments.
>
> Yes, Jack has been flippant about the comment to "move to a better environment" before, but even his point is much more nuanced, and really is about adapting your behaviours to your environment. (I disagree with this of course )
>
> I'll repeat part of Josh's post:
>
>
> The problem is still exposing oneself to the modern day stressors that were faced in higher light/more abundant times (ie work/university/monetary issues/tax/etc) **and expecting the same resiliency with fewer resources**. **In this regard \[HIGH STRESS\]** i can see it as 100% appropriate to use carbohydrate in winter, just as one would use exogenous (fake) light in order to try and fool the brain, in order to maintain a level of signallinga nd energy production to cope with the persisting stressors.​
>
>
> Simple point: Dealing with stress requires resources.
>
> This is NOT a recommendation for everyone to eat carbs, and this is DEFINITELY NOT a recommendation to start pounding the carbohydrate.
>
> How about 100g of carbs a day . Enough to keep baseline metabolic stores in check (like Liver glycogen), while trying your best to keep stress low and avoiding PUFAs.
>

Exactly what @[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/) experienced last year in (southern hemisphere) winter when he tried to go Keto while dealing with many stress-inducing clients​
>
>
>
>
>
> I am not here to recommend a fix. I just used it to highlight a possible fault in the chain.
>
> Oh, and PUFAs can likely inhibit the action of Thyroid hormone on the cell , meaning that one can be making enough Thyroid hormone, and lab tests come up with nice values of T3 (and low rT3, and what not), but yet the body is still functionally hypo-thyroid (cells do not see the thyroid hormone floating around)

> I don't mean to be blunt, but the above has NOTHING to do with anything that Yew is experimenting with and speaking about, educating various individuals here. Read his DHA article, or as I have said many times already, read Chris Masterjohn, PhD, all his articles on lipids; then, get to reading Dr. Peat on dietary lipids; Andrew Kim, Peter @ Hyperlipid, and others have good articles as well.
>
> What is the evidence that one should have a mid range value for any of the fatty acids above, let alone all of them? If I'm not mistaken, you largely come from the perspective of Kane et al and her lipid replacement ideas. Her ideas are irrelevant to both Jack's approach, and what people such as Dr. Peat advocate. IIRC, she completely buys into the fluid mosaic model of the "lipid bilayer", and she has said NOTHING about cholesterol sulfate moities, sulfated neurosteroids (PregS, DHEA-S, etc), MINOS, etc. and their impact on everything typically regarded to as the "lipid bilayer". Additionally, I haven't really seen any quality research from her or her husband, just a bunch of really simplistic articles making unfounded claims on o36 ratios, and the ratios of specific o3 and o6 lipids, and then attempting to explain how lipid replacement with her protocol can play a pivotal role in shaping one's recovery, enhancing cognitive function, etc. There is one Asian researcher who I believe you and her have cited, and he really only has one "good" study to his name about his "4:1" ratio idea, and even that is very easy to debunk.
>
> As Yew, Kim, and Masterjohn have explained in immense detail many times, there is very little need for ANY dietary PUFA, whether "shorter chain" (ALA, LA) or long chain (DHA, AA, etc), and DHA is likely the only "essential" PUFA, and even then, in milligram amounts in unhealthy, lactating, and/or growing individuals, let alone a grown adult with no documented health problems. Or, perhaps even Dr. Peat is correct with his more nuanced idea, in that mead acid alone, in addition to the other n7 and n9 fats, are optimal, and there isn't even a necessity for DHA.
>
> Also, I'm not sure how 100 grams of carbs on a 2500 calorie diet is "possibly acceptable". How would even getting the majority of one's 2500 calories from carbs be harmful? A dysregulation of glucose/fructose metabolism is likely a/the problem in "carbohydrate intolerance", not glucose/fructose itself. Hence the well documented history of extremely hig carb, rather high calorie diets being used to completely reverse/cure diabetic and obesogenic conditions for decades (in addition to Roy Swank's research on extremely low fat diets for the treatment of MS). As Dr. Peat has written about, in the late 1800's there were even two researchers who started treating diabetic patients with pure sucrose.
>
> If there is some extreme problem oxidizing glucose, fructose, lactose, maybe galactose, and other whole-foods sugars can be experimented with, with supplementation of niacinamide (if one doesn't have extremely low serotonin/messed up neurotransmitters), aspirin and/or aspirin derivatives, methylene blue, quinones, and other compounds, will likely reverse the problem, especially when dietary PUFAs and xenoestrogens are removed.

@[bio-fractal-soul-self](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/12496/)
Thank you for kind comments. Very informative.

I am not interested in method of curing diabetes by eating sugar.
I am rather satisfied with results of my hack few years back.
I assume that sugar and rice diet is short term hack, do not know that for sure but hope for success.

But
I am all ears if you have better information on desirable levels of fatty acids (in serum).
Mid range is just first approximation due to the lack of better information.

You may be right that there is no need for dietary DHA but I would like to be able to test that.
Current drawback in supplementing DHA by eating seafood is that many people end up messing their fatty acids, specially o6, because of excess of EPA. Many but not all, so there is a need for testing.

The other question would be, those who do ok on seafood, what do they have that the others do not?

Do not worry about 4:1 ratio.
But be happy that at least it was defined what it is. Others don't even do that.
\-----------------------------------
As far as I understand, lipid replacement with PK protocol have not much to do with o3 or o6 directly, it is about getting rid of renegades, odd saturated and trans isomers, while preserving good levels of o3 & o6.
\-----------------------------------
You have mentioned DHEAs.
I have a question:
How to increase DHEA,serum when it is within low level but still within laboratory range,
while DHEAs is high, on top of laboratory range for 20yo. Something is blocking conversion.
Multiple blood tests, so discount variability of DHEA.

When you are clueless of how the photoelctric effect really works you believe as Peat does. This changes what DHA requirements are needed by eukaryotes. 600 million years of exclusive use in LIFE's experiments are daunting. Bio fractal continue your search for your Rx is all I can say. But I am done discussing where he falls short. This site is not about Peat and never will be. If you want it to be please go to his site or Yew's site and have a fest and do your thing there. Here we understand that the light environment has a massive role on DHA biochemistry of eukaryotes. We know you and Yew like Peat's ideas.........but a guy like Feynman made the best point ever about "good theories"and reality when you examine them. In Nature we have the best result of experiment on DHA. **DHA has been present for 600 million years in the eukaryotic tree and NEVER REPLACED EVEN ONCE. If this lipid is bad in anyway, as Peat advocates, and you two believe, just by chance alone you'd expect something else to have been naturally selected for to replace it.**
Even though DHA has other competing PUFA's like DPA, that are easier to acquire and create on an energy basis, it remains unchallenged in eukaryotic cell membranes. This raises the deep question for Peat to answer, why has nature specficially continued to use it exclusively even when experiments show it has an ugly side? He cannot. Nature is absurd in what she does for a deep reason. That reason is the duality of light. The duality of light is basis of QED laid out by Feynman.

Feynman's insight here bears repitition. When you realize that is nature's choice for this long, any theory that bastardizes DHA like Peat's has, has to explain this result. Nothing Peat has proposed can explain it. No matter how many bad things he finds in lab experiment matters under the crucible of nature's experiment. If nature uses it exclusively over 600 million years, we should not question its use, regardless if we have contrary proof from a lab experiment, that it might be a problem. That is why I cannot in ANY way support Peat theories on DHA. Why do I echo Feynman's view point here? Life is lived in nature and not in a lab.

Are you a bit lost because everything in life appears to be a contradiction? I think this thread is now.......... In QED all possibilities exist, therefore there is no contradiction here. You beliefs are contrary to nature's clearly posted result of 600 million years. Realize that this date pre-dates the Cambrian explosion. That is what makes nature's choice so powerful. There is a lapse in your understanding of the implication of the QED mechanisms at play. I've told you this all before.............and you persist in that blind alley.
