I have grass to put my feet in every day, and the most amazing sunrises and sunsets. Have been going full out to write a book and build my business, but feel more alive than ever. Now that I'm out of the EMF swamp, I can finally hit some biohacks in a big way and will stand a chance of actually seeing some results. If your environment sucks big time, then the best biohack in the world is like spitting in the ocean and hoping to see the water level rise!

# Thread: TV Show "Freaks of Nature" had Wim Hoff and a human magnet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tv-show-freaks-of-nature-had-wim-hoff-and-a-human-magnet.9443/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Curves — 2014-01-23T18:30:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tv-show-freaks-of-nature-had-wim-hoff-and-a-human-magnet.9443/post-106775>

I'm curious. Has anyone else seen the Weather Channel TV show Freaks of Nature? They featured Wim Hoff (the Ice Man) in a tank of ice for an hour and the episode I saw last night was about human magnets.

The human magnet could stick metal objects on his body and they would stay. The last thing he held with his body was a 178 pound piece of marble. Anybody have any thoughts on this? I don't know what to think but I'm fascinated.

[http://www.weather.com/tv/tvshows/freaks/main](http://www.weather.com/tv/tvshows/freaks/main)

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-23T21:39:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stimulation-of-the-vagus-nerve.9444/post-106787>

The vagus nerve is something Dr. Art Ayers over at the "Cooling Inflammation" site often mentions. I see it comes up in Jack's book, too.

Now here's something I heard on a podcast that interested me. It was Datis Kharrazian (sp?) and he was saying you can help gut health by stimulating the vagus nerve, which may not be as active as it should be.

He says they use (a) singing loudly, (b) gargling very strongly (with up to 6 glasses of water a day), and (c) pushing the tongue down with a little wooden paddle until the gag reflex starts to activate.

Makes you think. I wonder if these practices would be worth trying.

It also occurred to me that many people have a much too easily tripped gag reflex and find it difficult to swallow pills etc. I wonder if this is connected with the dental-arch deformities Weston Price noted in modern populations. IIRC, the mouth ends up undersized, particularly if teeth are drawn and the upper set pulled in with a dental brace. Yet the soft tissue -- like the tongue -- grows to the full size dictacted by your genes. Would this muck up the gag reflex?

Mine isn't bad, but I don't think it's good. I noticed just today, in fact. I was getting a mug and some tea out a cupboard and while doing all this to free my hands I put a teaspoon crossways between my lips. After a while that started to activate the reflex. I could just stay relaxed and let the reaction recede again but it was activating. Interesting.

Good job I'm not a pirate in the age of sail. No way I could put a dagger between my teeth while swarming up the ratlines.

### nonchalant — 2014-01-23T22:02:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stimulation-of-the-vagus-nerve.9444/post-106788>

LOL, Gagnrad. Put away your eye patch.

I experienced a heightened gag reflex while pregnant. I didn't have morning sickness, but no way hold a spoon in my lips. I couldn't even hold my mouth slightly open for more than a few seconds. I had a very difficult time brushing my teeth. In fact the only "morning sickness" I had was once, when I said "this is silly, I'm going to brush my teeth properly."

This gag reflex stayed on, in a much reduced manner, for about a decade afterwards. I think my dental hygienist thought I was a bulimic. ;-)

I'm sure my gag reflex is a bit whacked too, I get unexpected throat spasms that close my throat stopping me from breathing. It's very frightening, odd things do it, once when I had a cold I coughed and bang, my throat shut. It resolves probably in under a minute but it's very frightening. Sometimes swallowing awkwardly or biting into a grape causing the juice to hit the back of my throat triggers it. I can't find anything on Google that gives any reason for it.

So you think Cold thermogenesis is just hormetic huh? Journal of Cell is now getting the message I shared 3 years ago with you. [http://download.cell.com/images/edimages/Trends/EndoMetabolism/tem\_932.pdf](http://download.cell.com/images/edimages/Trends/EndoMetabolism/tem_932.pdf)

He Didnâ€™t Have a Cellphone. He Couldnâ€™t Walk. So He Did the One Thing He Could After His...
[www.theblaze.com](http://www.theblaze.com)
Six hours. That's how long it took for a 57-year-old man to crawl 2.5 miles for

You all must realize the effect of cold temperatures on the brain and HPA axis, leptin and adiponectin. Cold empties fat cells to provide mitochondria protons when electrons from the diet are poor. The missing piece to the story is water. A proper ketogenic diet requires substantial intake of water. When water is used in the proper circadian seasonal temperature driven something unique happens in water chemistry. Cold water creates a higher exclusion zone. The larger the exclusion zone the more charge separation occurs in water to transfer potential energies to things like the mitochondria. Moreover, a larger exclusion zone creates a boatload of protons. These protons create huge flows of hydronium ions in the face of a low light cycle. This is precisely how things are designed to work in winter. You really need to add Ling and Pollack's work to your routine and you will begin to see the magic in my CT protocol and why Zeltiq cool sculpting works physiologically.

Protons carry a positive charge.......if you can not move them thru your mitochondria positive charges increase and inflammation takes hold. all inflammation is positively charged. Think acids, ROS, and LR. You have to move the separated charges into mitochondria........who should move them well.......and if they dont you develop molecular chaos and swelling and lose water inside the cell because acids decrease water's exclusion zone which decreases their redox potential. This destroys mitochondria.

### Hope — 2014-01-23T23:43:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-now-makes-it-into-the-journal-of-cell.9445/post-106826>

This is everything in a nutshell. Right? This is what it all seems to....boil down to. No pun intended.

I can't believe I am reading this and I'm actually understanding this, after Monday night. I don't know what hydronium ions are. Or Ling or Pollack. Yet. But I love that I 'get' everything else. At least I feel like I do.

Mitochondria use electrons from food and protons from water. Actually, the mitochondria move the protons along if you are well....bc of the protons' positive charge that they need to get rid of. So being unwell may not just mean a lack of electrons, but an abundance of protons?

I have wondered if this is also why we, when we are lost on a desert island, survive longer if we have no food but water, than if we have no water. Is there a connection?

I'm just thinking out loud. I know I'm not adding anything to this thread. Except the desire to have someone confirm or correct me.

### Hope — 2014-01-23T23:47:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-now-makes-it-into-the-journal-of-cell.9445/post-106829>

PS From the article you linked, I love this.....

Maximal thermal comfort in the built environment may increase our susceptibility to obesity and related disorders, and in parallel requires high energy use in buildings. Mild cold exposure increases body energy expenditure without shivering and without compromising our precious comfort. Hence, rethinking our indoor climate by allowing ambient temperatures to drift may protect both health and bank account.

I wish I could get my DH to buy into this....but in his case, he doesn't need to lose a lb. We spent a few years helping him gain 20lbs. But I on the hand...

### nonchalant — 2014-01-24T18:30:25+0200

I had been outside earlier at dawn in below freezing temps with just shorts and tank top, but after eating the chili I was very cold in much warmer indoor temps. While miserable and huddled in bed, I remembered D-Ribose. A couple scoops later, I seem to be recovering. Just my hands and feet are cold. Don't you just love it when science bites you in the butt?

# Thread: Kids and Concussions:  Know the risks.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-concussions-know-the-risks.9446/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-01-24T00:52:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-concussions-know-the-risks.9446/post-106838>

KEY POINTS
\* Teens with a history of concussions are more than three times as likely to suffer from depression as teens that have never had a concussion.
\* Researchers suggest the association between depression and concussion could stem from confusion about similar symptoms between the two conditions or social isolation during recovery among other possibilities.
Newswise â€” Teens with a history of concussions are more than three times as likely to suffer from depression as teens who have never had a concussion, finds a new study in the Journal of Adolescent Health.
â€œWhat this study suggests is that teens who have had a concussion should be screened for depression,â€ said lead study author Sara Chrisman, M.D., a pediatrician at Seattle Childrenâ€™s Hospital.
Concussion, considered a mild traumatic brain injury, can also have serious psychological effects. Most prior research on these psychological effects has focused on adults. However, many teens experience concussions through sports injuries or accidents, and less is known about long-term complications in adolescents.
The study used data from the 2007-2008 National Survey of Childrenâ€™s Health and included health information from over 36,000 adolescents ages 12 to 17. 2.7 percent of the sample had had a concussion and 3.4 percent had a current depression diagnosis.
Teens who were 15 years or older, lived in poverty or who had a parent with mental health problems were more likely to be depressed than other teens, said Chrisman, â€œbut what was surprising was when we took those factors into consideration, it didnâ€™t take away from the association between depression and a history of concussion.â€
Chrisman also cautioned that itâ€™s not known what exactly might account for higher rates of depression in teens with a history of concussion. It could be the brain injury itself, diagnostic bias due to repeated medical visits for concussion, doctors mistaking symptoms of a concussion for depression, or from the social isolation that they may experience while recovering.
Jeffrey Max, M.D., a psychiatrist who specializes in psychiatric outcomes of traumatic brain injury in children and adolescents at the University of California, San Diego noted, â€œIn our research, weâ€™ve found that about 10 percent of the kids had a full depressive disorder or subclinical depressive disorder 6 months after a concussion.â€ Children who have a history of concussion are more likely to develop attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and have difficulties controlling their moods, especially anger, rather than experience depression, Max added.
Unlike Chrisman, however, Max observed that the actual brain injury associated with concussions is probably a major cause of depression in the first few months after injury. â€œIn the clinic, weâ€™ve certainly seen cases where within hours \[of sustaining a concussion\], a kid whoâ€™s never had depression before is suddenly depressed and suicidal. One of our studies found that the brain images in children with traumatic brain injury and depression were actually quite similar to those seen in adults who develop depression as a result of traumatic brain injury.â€
TERMS OF USE: This story is protected by copyright. When reproducing any material, including interview excerpts, attribution to the Health Behavior News Service, part of the Center for Advancing Health, is required. While the information provided in this news story is from the latest peer-reviewed research, it is not intended to provide medical advice or treatment recommendations. For medical questions or concerns, please consult a health care provider.
Journal of Adolescent Health: Contact Tor Berg at (415) 502-1373 or [tor.berg@ucsf.edu](mailto:tor.berg@ucsf.edu) or visit [www.jahonline.org](http://www.jahonline.org)
Chrisman SP and Richardson LP. Prevalence of diagnosed depression in adolescents with history of concussion. 2013. J Adolesc Health.

### Scompy — 2018-05-14T04:29:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-concussions-know-the-risks.9446/post-241149>

This is good to see... I saw this coming years ago, but I think it's a great thing to talk about. If helmet-wearing is not mandatory, the problem arises when some players will have them and others will not. It needs to be an all or nothing rule, just like the shin-guards. Ironically yes, shins are more important than brains. Some aggressive players with helmets often feel like they can take more chances with their heads, putting other players more at risk if they choose not to wear helmets.
[https://www.blackandredunited.com/o...occer-concussion-risk-reduction-virginia-tech](https://www.blackandredunited.com/opinion/2018/5/3/17310578/soccer-concussion-risk-reduction-virginia-tech)

### drezy — 2018-05-14T15:30:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-concussions-know-the-risks.9446/post-241161>

When you think about the added implications of a bruising up a young brain that needs to be highly myelinated AND subjecting it to crap light and nnemf that's pretty horrific. I can't even imagine the potential that society loses over a longer timeframe. The immediate effect is bad, but the lingering effects across a lifetime are sad.

> [Scompy said:](/goto/post?id=241149)
>
> Some aggressive players with helmets often feel like they can take more chances with their heads, putting other players more at risk if they choose not to wear helmets.

That's sadly predictable

Moral hazard is built into humans. Give a person something that supposedly makes them safer and you ultimately elicit riskier behavior. One drive on a highway, with the safest cars ever, demonstrates this behavior perfectly. Linear thinking goes out with the trash again.

# Thread: K2 causing delayed wound healing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/k2-causing-delayed-wound-healing.9450/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### K2D2 — 2014-01-24T14:21:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/k2-causing-delayed-wound-healing.9450/post-106904>

Hi. I stumbled across the forum looking for people discussing high dose K2 supplementation. I hope it is okay for me to ask this here.

I started taking K2 to help with my teeth, then I tried megadosing 45mg MK-4 to help with my CFS/ME. I didn't notice much, but the combination of MK-4, Ca+Mg+B, riboflavin and methyl-B12 gave me a significant boost in mental clarity, wellbeing and hormonal symptoms (Not confirmed by labs).

However, just one 15mg MK-4 capsule seems to greatly reduce my wound healing. My acne seems to last forever, then crisp up without healing properly. In some of the Japanese studies skin lesions were reported as a side effect; could this be relevant?

**Are there any recommended co-factors for megadosing K2?** I am wondering if high doses of vitamin D could be required. I am taking lots of retinol, but only 600iu of D currently.

I am going to get a vitamin D blood test if my doctor will let me, but funds are too limited to do any extensive testing. I really don't want to lose the benefits of MK-4, but currently the scarring is a dealbreaker.

Cheers!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-01-24T14:36:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/k2-causing-delayed-wound-healing.9450/post-106905>

K2 co factors are D3, Vitamin E and C and water. Reverse osmosis water is best mineral water is second best and and well water follows. If you can collect rain water this is also pretty solid. If you are eating a high fat low carb diet there is no need for extra retinol. You need to read the blog I wrote in pseudotumor cerebri to see why Vitamin is not a vitamin to supplement with. Vitamin D 3 is a proxy for your redox potential. If you can not make your D rise with change of life style, then supplementation is fine as long as you realize it means you need to also make sure your redox potential is also being strengthened at the same time.

# Thread: Evolution of lactose tolerance likely has nothing to do with calcium needs

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution-of-lactose-tolerance-likely-has-nothing-to-do-with-calcium-needs.9451/post-106906>

DNA extracted from ancient bones in "Sunny Spain" appears to knock that hypothesis on the head:

> "But here's the thing", says SverrisdÃ³ttir, "if natural selection is driving lactase persistence evolution in a place where people have no problems making vitamin D in their skin, then clearly the vitamin D and calcium explanation (known as the calcium assimilation hypothesis) isn't cutting it. So while the calcium assimilation hypothesis may have some relevance in Northern Europe it's clearly not the whole story".

[http://mbe.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2014/01/20/molbev.msu049.full.pdf+html](http://mbe.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2014/01/20/molbev.msu049.full.pdf+html)

### nonchalant — 2014-01-24T17:52:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-106917>

I first became interested in Jack Kruse because of his scientific blogposts. He was writing about something I was interested in, and he was approaching the subject from a different angle than what I was used to reading. Some of his ideas were familiar, but some were not. He made some suggestions, and I tried some of them, and was pleased with the results. I kept reading and I started participating in the thought process (blogs & forum). As I learned more, and experienced more health benefits, I became comfortable with discarding previous ideas about health. I decided to "hire" Dr. Kruse. I decided to "fire" other people/corporations/ideas I used to rely on. And I found a new local doctor who was more in line with Dr. Kruse's thinking.

I need to learn more about quantum biology if I want my family to live in this new, hostile environment. Other institutions have proven themselves to be unreliable, so I have to learn for myself. I will be moving to a place where my internet access is uncertain, so I have been printing blogs and buying books. Lots of us like webinars and podcasts, but I am becoming more reliant on the printed information.

### Ricardo — 2014-01-24T18:54:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-106920>

Well, like a lot of people these days, I am looking for answers as to why people are getting sick and, importantly, what can be done about it.

When people who are athletic and who have good eating and sleeping habits become diseased... the question becomes.. why? How did it happen?

I believe that, more-so than any other web source, Dr Kruse has provided potential answers or, in some cases, avenues of reflection. Understanding these answers may help us to avoid disease and become optimally healthy.

Dr Kruse's vision, seeing things from a perspective of quantum physics, may be the most efficacious approach.

As a suggestion, I think the material would be easier to assimilate for most people if it were accompanied by graphics such as:

an Epi-PaleoRx diet pyramid
Mind maps ( especially useful to connect the dots and demonstrate the relationships between various bits of information )
More big picture overviews and resumed information
Separate "how to" instructions that are about how to do it and are restricted to practical application of the information.
Charts, when possible

I recognize there is a lot of research behind the data being presented.

Please keep up the good work !

Ricardo

### Jonathin — 2014-01-24T19:25:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-106921>

My big attraction to Jack is that he integrates scientific and other disciplines into a big picture. Whether that picture is flawless is not a deal breaker. His approach is resonant to me. What I find troubling and frustrating about social thought, science, etc. is the disjointedness of facts and ideas and their isolated applications. I am seeking understanding and connection for my empowerment on all levels. I am not wanting to be a devotee. Jack is helping me to connect the dots with a number of things that I already know and adds information and connection that I was not aware of.

### caroline — 2014-01-24T23:49:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-106931>

Many years ago I heard a psychologist speak. She asked us to think of our eyes as lenses and that is how we view the world - and everyone's lenses are different and so everyone views the world differently. That was probably 30 years ago and I have never forgotten that evening. She also told us that what we think we do best is probably not what someone else thinks we do best. That was a revelation to me and I have always tried to be mindful of that.

I like that Dr. K. is shining his flashlight and l need to do the work and figure it out for myself. Would it be easier if he told me what to do? heck ya!
Is he right about all of this? I don't know ....I am not smart enough to figure that out. My gut tells me he has a lot right and the most important thing to me ... he has integrity and passion and he is on this learning curve with us ...albeit many miles ahead! and I love hanging out with people that have those qualities.
I have always asked a lot of questions and I have also tried never to settle and I have to say that we always had good Doctors who listened and tried their best - to the best of their capabilities. Dr. K. has made me appreciate how limited they are but it isn't their fault - as long as they are on this learning curve with me.

I do love the podcasts because I think Dr. K. communicates really well on a very earthy level. He knows how to break it down for us when he is asked the questions ...and he never seems to get tired of answering the same ones over and over!

I also think that the webinars and live Q&A are invaluable. It really helps me to understand some of the tougher stuff and we all know that Jack can be brutally direct and honest. That's good! I do start to worry when the webinars are tough - but I just have to work harder!

He must think to himself sometimes ... are they ever gonna get it??? but then the penny drops for some one and I think I can see him smile and say finally! JK seems to have infinite patience when it comes to getting his message out ...but I suppose that comes from being a clinician and having to explain tough messages in a concise, yet understandable and thoughtful manner.

I guess I wish there was a better way to organise the sheer volume of stuff here now ... but I think that will come.

This is a new frontier.

Just one more thought .... I do love a man that adores his family and has a passion for life ...that was one of the things that drew me here - that is hugely important to me. Will Jack get it all right? I doubt it ...but as long as he keeps trying - so will I. Will he be able to please everyone? impossible. Will everyone like him? not likely.

This is an adventure that is too good to miss....my life has changed dramatically because Dr. K. has empowered me to do better and be better .... and I can do that. Maybe that is his strength - and when times got tough he didn't fold, he re focused and shook it off.

I guess sometimes I wish he wouldn't speed read thru things that we write....

But then again - he isn't perfect and neither am I ...I have said this before - That is when I truly knew that I was a fully functioning adult... I can appreciate a person in spite of and maybe because of their imperfections....

This is way to long - sorry

Dr. K. I like how you communicate in a consult. I once asked on the forum about how we could maximise our time with you and you answered that it was about it being a conversation between the two of us. You have a gift for listening and appreciating what we are trying to say and you always listen to our point of view and take it on board. That is pretty unique I think.

All I know for sure .... Three years down the track I am in a way better place.... and that is because you made me think about a lot of stuff ... and sometimes you ticked me off - but that is good too because I learned to suck it up and appreciate what is important to me.

### Josh — 2014-01-25T00:49:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-106940>

1\. For the webinars, a powerpoint or slides would help. Pollack's use of his images and slides in the various presentations he made around the book and the 4th Phase ar good illustrations of the value of simple clear illustrations and well coordinated slides. It is what makes the book and his work so accessible.

2\. Collate the blogs better so that they are all together by topic in a prominent easy to find place on the website. In addition, index them more thoroughly and or put them in a collection that can be searched in depth as a group. For example, if each set were one pdf that could be searched as a single document. I had to look around the site to collect them all and put them in collections myself to read them in order and get the full effect so to speak. This has helped me greatly in understanding what you are presenting and pointing at. It might help to edit some of them for typos, etc.. Finally the earlier ones that are not collected by topic might be collated into groups by topic and or grouped so that they can be searched comprehensively as a group. There are important themes running through the blog series and the older ones that may be missed in the large by topic groupings currently featured on the blog. There is really an amazing amount of diligently developed content that deserves to be read.

3\. You might add a "for Geeks" section on the forum with things broken down for both "Geeks" and "Non-Geeks" as you do many time sin the blogs.

4\. Key concepts are beginning to emerge in the development of the message and you might take a little time to review the essentail goals and purposes of the message and how to communicate it in a relevant and useful way to the lay person and most of the practitioners who have little or no background in advanced sciences. Quantum Physics is used to sort of explain so many things these days that bringing into specific applications and considerations with regard to health and the body is the remedy for too much vague marketing hype, etc.. Maybe a key concepts we are working on now list, possibly with references to blogs where you explained these things might help people enter in the middle of the class so to speak. You do this many times and a standard mini-outline of relevant considerations and references at the beginning of a blog or even in advance of a webinar to give us a chance to review key concepts necessary to encounter the new material would be great.

Needless to say, kudos for having the courage to put yourself out and create this.

### caroline — 2014-01-25T01:11:58+0200

### Martin — 2014-01-27T17:37:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-107043>

I could begin by saying that the fact you asked us our opinions is perhaps what is missing from many sources out there.

The recent additions like the Bio-Hacker's and Optimal Reset emails are really timely. Both old and new members/lookers/lurkers can hone in on some guidelines that they can apply quickly to get some results. As we change for the better, we can dive deeper into the blogs for the reasons "why" because we are just simply curious humans trying to make sense of everything. It's like good writing structure - the first sentence or two sets up the entire paragraph, then one can get pulled along through the details armed with a basic understanding of very complex subject - us.

As always, the webinars where you speak from your knowledge base and let fly the take-aways in the concepts that, in the written form, can be daunting for neophytes . You're a good writer, but a damn good orator. Reading it, then hearing it, then going back and re-reading it is how I get it best.

We out here appreciate how you run up to the line of "medicine on the internet", let fly an observation, and then perhaps push the boundary a bit, all with the intention of helping people out here in the hinterland who are foundering on the shoals of Conventional Medicine and need an intelligent (able to judge relative importances) voice to separate the gluten-laden wheat from the chaff.

On this site, we now have a perspective that is Nature-based, not some skewed, political, money grubbing, career-saving, kow-towing horseshit from some biased source.

"…daunting for neophytes… " "…foundering on the shoals…" "…gluten-laden wheat…" Talk about horses hit!

Big Opinionated Head

### caroline — 2014-01-27T23:04:50+0200

Martin .... I am patiently waiting for the book. I have always said that you are a silver tongue devil ......I like it a lot..

You are a renaissance man of the highest order ... I think you are just coming into "your own"....

I know this is supposed to be about Jack - but it really is about all of us ...... what we have accomplished the last three years thanks to the prodding and pushing and cajoling and caring and all that darn, pesky science \[that I have grown to love!\] I feel like I don't say it enough - Thanks Dr. K.

I just have a request from my book club to submit titles for this coming year - so I have added "going somewhere" and "The body Electric" and "Cells, Gels and the Engines of Life" Everyone thinks I am mad ... and I love that

### tellmisty — 2014-01-29T04:16:58+0200

It makes me so happy to hear you say this!!!  Wrangling this beast of a platform and all this content has been a monumental task. The last few months, I've focused with the team really hard on setting up eCourses and seeing what we can do to lay a track down next to all this content that makes things really actionable. We've got more blog posts coming like [Simple Steps to Reduce EMF Exposure in Your Home or Office](http://www.jackkruse.com/reduce-emf-exposure-in-your-home-or-office/) (we're working on one on water next!) and by the end of the week, we will have a full Table of Contents for the entire blog under "Blog Index" to make it easier for you guys to review past series.

AND ... we're updating the site design so it works better on mobile devices and includes a member area to make it easier for you to have a place to login to get monthly webinars and Q&As that are part of your membership, and to get reduced pricing on bootcamps.

Dr. Kruse is also going to start doing more videos for his blogs, which will be the bomb!

It has been a big challenge taking all this content and organizing it so that what we're building together is scalable ... but dammit, we've climbed the mountain and 2014 is gonna rock our faces off!!!

### Josh — 2014-01-29T07:18:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-107255>

Eh, the gradient is often too steep. You cannot go from a rough undigested or partially digested fiber to the redox potential of my mitochondria, gross macro stuff I can see and feel to subatomic particles I have only imagined without any intermediate steps please. Eventhough I have actually studied this, some reminders of the steps involved and where it gets relevant to the physics part would be great. This is an example from the Q and A the other night and I feel it is relevant to the site. In my case, I spent a bit of time organizing the material chronologically and then trying to figure out how it unfolded for you in your life and case. Then I moved to how you tried to organize and present what you had discovered and where you wanted to go with it. Your story and personal examples spread through the blog and the book with more in the webinars paints a pretty good picture, but I had work hard to figure out how you think. In the East, it is said that a good student/disciple must steal the jewels of wisdom from the teacher/master. With you, it is somewhere in between. You are trying to give it to us, but I feel I end up having to try to steal it anyway because you do not realize where you have dropped the breadcrumbs. You have put enough material out, and at this point "curriculum tracks" or something like that might be a good amusement. Maybe XXX101, 202, etc.. You started a little with the Hormones 101 and 102, and sometimes the series follow on each other more or less logically and sometimes they do not. It is perhaps the in between world of the "Blog" where things are a bit more casual, but then it gets challenging when one is trying to get the basics clear. Graduate programs tend to be more casual than undergraduate.....

You are inconsistent in supplying appropriate metaphors and or maps to create a path/gradient that can be followed. If someone misunderstands or does not understand a word or symbol, their mind goes blank and they will not remember what comes after it. So, as I listed earlier, perhaps some tools to connect A to Z would bless us all and save you some air. I used Pollock's example because he has done an amazing job with his son of creating images that can take right brained people like my wife into the heart of physics as if they are reading Dr. Seuss....We understand your son is already a nuclear physicist and unlikely to revert to being an illustrator, still a little more attention to gradient would be appreciated with whatever props are appropriate and easily available. In one group I know of, they force people to present concepts in clay without speaking so that the observers understand the concept without words. Extreme, yet a challenge to us hyperverbal types. I just went through Oschman's presentation at the water conference and his powerpoint which for me stands alone as a preview and review of the presentation, a map of sorts. I learn a lot faster this way. I also am better at just writing a lot to explain things and I realize powerpoint, visio and excel are powerful tools that I am just starting to use at the level 12 year-olds probably do. I am finding that once I can think like a Microsoft software engineer, the basics of all of these programs seem pretty simple, just an idea....Video is great, but not without visuals to refer to. That's probably enough from me for tonight.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-01-29T09:00:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-107259>

Josh I would suggest forgetting how I think........you'll never get it because it comes from how I learned all the parts of science. The most important part for you to realize is that the reason many don't understand or follow what I am saying is because we are in different rooms of the same house. You see the house thru the room you're in now not the room I am currently in. I am aware of it and most others do not appear to realize this. You do not need to know all of this.....you just need to have an appreciation of the info and then know how to apply it. Most of what is in my Rx blogs do just that. Where people get confused is they think because we are all human we all just have the same Rx pathways. Not true at all.

Clinical medicine today is also on this very superficial level of biologic understanding. What I have learned is that to be a good clinician you first have to realize that all life scales from macroscopic biology to quantum levels. The scale or levels all have different jargon that go with them, but there are ciphers, quotes, and metaphors that help one understand a jump or a fall back in the scale.

For example.......an everyday neurosurgeon sees the brain as an organ that controls many vegetative and autonomic functions because they see the world through the human reality of how we respond to the environment through our behaviors. A neurosurgeon, like me, sees how the level of tissue organization is immaterial and confounding when one considers how water alters its nuclear spin to the environmental electromagnetic force actually is what determines who is mentally ill and who is not. Who suffers from adrenal fatigue and who will get MS.

The difference in the understanding the scale is the educational barrier that most people struggle with because of how the believe biology is organized and categorized by human historical standards based in medical education. The scale I am showing people is the smallest scale........and in my blogs I show you how the atomic and 3D molecular arrays actually dictate and explain anatomic findings published in books that have no current explanation;

These unknown quantum scales however contain solutions to these paradox and explain unknown physiologic mechanisms of behaviors that all humans have, such as sleep. The reason why scale is hard to convey to any person is because no one is trained to move from atomic to macroscopic biology from an educational standpoint.

My diverse interests and curiosity have built that framework in my mind. You can not re create it. I just have to get you to see the patterns in how the scales are translated into things you observe to be factual.........each scale has a Rosetta stone that allows one to go up or down the ladder of scale.

I think when you read EE 12 you will see how we go from Larmor frequency to water, to redox potential, to amino acids, to complex proteins, to DNA and RNA, the organelles, and then back to water, light, and magnetism with the easy of a child sliding down a sheet of ice on ice skates........Each one of those examples is a step up or down in scale of how biology acts in different complexities or layers.

Right now most of you and clinical medicine is stuck on certain basic labs to draw inference. Guys like me understand the labs nuanace and how one persons high E2 maybe identical in two people but mean two different things when one considers the context in which both those numbers were built upon. Most of you and your docs think a testosterone of 248 in two people mean the same thing and nothing could be further from the truth.

Most clinicians do not even understand how hormones tie into life's equation, the redox potential. We need to stop thinking they way we do.......and begin to think in layers, scales, or peeling the onion skin back to get to the core issues........the quantum issues.

These are the fundamental building blocks of life. Light, water, and magnetism.............The application and assimilation of those 3 simple things.......is complex because their interactions create the many different scales.........and what controls the scales or layers of action? The myriad of frequencies buried in the electromagnetic force. No one.......seems to grasp the power of this force. This force dictates how every scale actually works microscopically and leads to different macroscopic results.

This is why people even on this site can not fathom that food is not that important.........but that is because they do not understand the scale of the atomic world. For example, the macroscopic understanding today of neurodegeneration encompasses the misfolding of proteins and a lack of energy as key issues. A quantum scale shows that an important step to the solution of the protein folding problem is the detailed understanding of the balance of forces that stabilize the native protein structure. The 3 D molecular structure of water and its interaction with the photoelectric effect and the electromagnetic force is indispensable in this understanding.

Water acts as a chameleon under the direct influence of the electromagnetic spectrum and the electromagnetic force it is in and therefore can be a true demon or a true god to life to cause protein to mis-fold or to make it happen effortlessly.

Medicine and modern researchers have no idea of these quantum scale problems because their disciplines were never taught to co mingle. One can not know something you are ignorant of. This blog brings you the solutions to the scale problem way before medicine will see it........because when you see it.......you'll begin to think differently and act differently to offset your own risks of getting what most other people get when you only have the macroscopic myopic picture of how things unfold. This is a process of learning slowly and steadily..........You wont pick it up like a foreign language........you have to bathe in the water and live the science for yourself a bit to see the magic nature has for us all.

### Josh — 2014-01-29T19:38:40+0200

I look forward to EE12 and it sounds as though Oschman's presentation was a good introduction for me. It was the first time I had heard of and saw a model of Larmour frequency and its relation to water, etc.. Between his and Pollack's images, I have a good set of pictures to augment your work. This is still all new for me and the herculean task for me would/will be to go back and revisit all of the Oriental Medical Theory where most of this is modelled in a right-brained manner that I have always been challenged to work with past a certain point because it was hard to make it "real" enough for people. I will see if that is part of my destiny....

P.S. What were you doing up at 1 AM responding to a post??? You need to sleep!!! Perhaps you were on at the hospital?

It was the first time I had heard of and saw a model of Larmour frequency and its relation to water, etc.. Between his and Pollack's images, I have a good set of pictures to augment your work. This is still all new for me and the herculean task for me would/will be to go back and revisit all of the Oriental Medical Theory where most of this is modelled in a right-brained manner that I have always been challenged to work with past a certain point because it was hard to make it "real" enough for people. I will see if that is part of my destiny....
>
> P.S. What were you doing up at 1 AM responding to a post??? You need to sleep!!! Perhaps you were on at the hospital?

The Gulf coast of the USA is in the midst of a historic winter storm. Down here we got 5 inches of snow and 2-3 inches of ice under the snow. It has been sub freezing for three days and we are effectively shut in because the deep south does not have salt or snow equipment. We have to wait until the sun melts the snow. That will happen 12 noon Thursday. Friday will back to normal at 71 degrees. Its been good for me......CT and all but the region is completely closed down ghost town. I have never seen anything like it in all my life.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-01-29T23:37:19+0200

I understand you all want it more simple.......here is a problem.......quantum principles are not inherently simple no matter how you cut it. So how would one do it? Take something difficult and make it into digestible small bites. And then give people time to ask questions........I personally think if you are not reading and asking questions then I assume you got it. If you dont is this one me or you?

When I get to the easier stuff.......I use clinical metaphors and examples to get principles across. When it comes back to QED.......it all goes back to 3 simple things I wrote about in EE4. I think going back and remembering that 3 legged stool in a deep QED blog helps. I say this a day before I am releasing a very complex EE 12........but this blog is all about how to make optimal work. It is the critical clinical piece. The video I begin with is for the non geeks. We'll see if you all give feedback on the new delivery of the info or not.

I think we all \[non geeks\] get that you have to write the scientific stuff .....and we aren't going to get it the first time around or probably the second or even the third...but that pushes us and encourages us to do better. If someone isn't interested enough to keep at it - that isn't your failing - that is ours.

A friend just asked me why I am reading all this stuff and I was beaming as I said "I love it!" I will never be able to have an in depth discussion with you - but I do love trying to figure it out and relate it to me and keep moving forward.

I think you are exactly right - if we aren't reading and asking questions .... you have to assume that we get it. I think sometimes it is you - and if that is the case we need to let you know. As we all say - "take one for the team!"

You are trying to do something hugely difficult ..... How is it possible to get so many diverse people to understand such complicated stuff. You always seem to get it when we are struggling in a thread - and you give us tons more information.

I think we have figured that out lately ... so that is a good vehicle for us .....you can see where we are at in the thought process and tell us where we are going wrong or getting off track. It is also great for us non geeks because other more knowledgeable members can also lend a hand - as Randy has done for me.

The comment section after your blogs works really well too ..... you give a lot to us there.

You can be pretty intimidating ... but that is okay too - because we need to suck it up and learn how to ask better questions
\- and if you shoot us down we need to learn to suck that up too and maybe let you know you can sometimes be a little severe.

### Josh — 2014-01-30T19:36:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-107471>

Before I launch into the video and EE12, these came to me in my morning sitting:

2 stories I have enjoyed that I am sure are unviversal:

Three novices joined a Trappist monastery. After a year they were seated at breakfast and the Abbot
turned to a novice:
”Brother Paul, you have been with us a whole year and you have kept your vow of silence. If you
wish you may now speak. Do you have anything to say?”
”Yes, Abbot. I don’t like the breakfast food.”
”Very well, perhaps we shall do something about it.”
Nothing was done, and another year passed, a year of silence. Once again they were seated about
the breakfast table and the Abbot turned to another young monk:
”Brother Peter, I commend you for having kept your peace for two years. If you wish you may now
speak. Do you have anything to say?”
”Yes, Abbot. I don’t see anything wrong with the breakfast food.”
A third year passed, a year of silence. Again the Abbot turned to the third novice:
”Brother Stephen, I commend you highly for your vow of silence which you have kept for three whole
years. If you wish you may now speak. Do you have anything to say?”
”Yes, Abbot, I do. I can’t stand this constant bickering.”

LIEH TZU WAS GOING TO CH’I BUT TURNED BACK HALF WAY!. ON THE ROAD HE MET
PO-HUN WU-JEN WHO ASKED HIM WHY HE HAD TURNED BACK.
LIEH TZU SAID ’I WAS ALARMED BY SOMETHING.’
’WHAT WAS IT?’ ASKED THE OLD MAN. ’I ATE AT TEN INNS AND AT FIVE THEY SERVED ME
FIRST.’
’WHEN A MAN’S INNER INTEGRITY IS NOT FIRM, SOMETIMES, SOMETHING OOZES FROM
HIS BODY AND BECOMES AN AURA, WHICH, OUTSIDE HIM, PRESSES ON THE HEARTS OF
OTHERS; IT MAKES OTHER MEN HONOUR HIM MORE THAN HIS ELDERS AND BETTERS,
AND GETS HIM INTO DIFFICULTIES.’
THE ONLY MOTIVE OF AN INNKEEPER IS TO SELL HIS RICE AND SOUP, AND INCREASE
HIS EARNINGS. HIS PROFITS ARE MEAGRE, AND THE CONSIDERATIONS WHICH SWAY HIM
HAVE LITTLE WEIGHT. IF MEN WITH SO LITTLE TO GAIN FROM ME VALUE ME SO HIGHLY
AS A CUSTOMER, WILL IT NOT BE EVEN WORSE WITH THE LORD OF TEN THOUSAND
CHARIOTS WHO HAS WORN OUT HIS BODY AND DRAINED HIS KNOWLEDGE IN STATE
AFFAIRS? THE PRINCE OF CH’I WILL APPOINT ME TO SOME OFFICE AND INSIST THAT I
FILL IT EFFICIENTLY. THIS IS WHAT ALARMED ME.’
’AN EXCELLENT WAY TO LOOK AT IT! BUT EVEN IF YOU STAY, OTHER MEN WILL LAY
RESPONSIBILITIES ON YOU’ SAID THE OLD MAN.
NOT LONG AFTERWARDS, WHEN PO-HUN WU-JEN WENT TO CALL ON HIM, LIEH TZU’S
PORCH WAS FULL OF THE SHOES OF VISITORS. PO-HUN WU-JEN STOOD FACING NORTH;
HE LEANED ON HIS UPRIGHT STAFF AND WRINKLED HIS CHEEK AGAINST IT. AFTER
STANDING THERE FOR A WHILE, HE LEFT WITHOUT SPEAKING. THE DOORKEEPER TOLD
LIEH TZU. LIEH TZU RAN OUT BAREFOOT HOLDING HIS SHOES IN HIS HANDS AND CAUGHT
UP WITH HIM AT THE GATE.
’NOW THAT YOU HAVE COME, MASTER’ HE SAID ’AREN’T YOU EVEN GOING TO GIVE ME MY
MEDICINE?’
’ENOUGH! I TOLD YOU CONFIDENTLY THAT OTHERS WOULD LAY RESPONSIBILITIES ON
YOU, AND IT TURNS OUT THAT THEY HAVE. IT IS NOT THAT YOU ARE CAPABLE OF
ALLOWING THEM TO DO SO, YOU ARE INCAPABLE OF PREVENTING THEM. WHAT USE IS
IT TO YOU TO HAVE THIS EFFECT ON PEOPLE WHICH IS INCOMPATIBLE WITH YOUR OWN
BASIC PEACE? IF YOU INSIST ON MAKING AN EFFECT, IT WILL UNSTEADY YOUR BASIC
SELF, AND TO NO PURPOSE.’

And, the other night after I watched James Oschman's presentation from the water conference, Doug McGuff's 2010 "21" Primal or something presentation he talked about fitness versus health. I watched for about 30 minutes and he came to the point where he modeled fitness versus health with his hands and how it related to training versus death. IT was so simple and this morning I got it, only 36 hours later. I am sure the gap he made with his hands could have been the EZ of water in the body, especially the mitochondria or the redox potential. Regardless, it explained something fundamental about my life and the experience I have had with this body. My sleep is so deep and my skull is so thick-as I was once told "teaching you is like teaching a brick wall..." I am now excited to see how Dr. Kruse fills in the rest of the story....

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2PdJFbjWHEU](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2PdJFbjWHEU)

I am one of those lucky people who can now really see the light at the end of the tunnel and it all thanks to your incredible vision and generosity, Jack. I canâ€™t help thinking though, how much faster I might be getting there if only ....**there could be just a bit more information pertaining exclusively to people with AI conditions.** I know that you have been mentioning AI quite a bit here and there, but it would be so very helpful to have a separate webinar and a forum dedicated just for that purpose only. So, perhaps you might want to consider that.
>
> Many thanks again for all the wonderful work that you do.

I read the following in EE 12 and it's about my favorite subject - me! Tanya, this could help you understand where we're going on the blog with respect to Auto-Immune issues. Having said this, I'm with you on a dedicated blog post on how to use what we learn here to identify, measure, and some treatment guidelines on AI, biotoxin illness, CF, Fibro, etc., but I gotta tell ya, it's already here in the blogs. I just like it when Doc talks about me in particular! While some people think Doc is all about fatties and carbs, there's a ton he's showing me about a skinny guy with chronic fatigue, Fibro, et al. It's here on these pages, but it's like human body processes - complicated as hell!

From EE12:
"Glutathione is critical in maintaining optimal redox reactions, like the Fenton reactions you learned about in Energy and Epigenetics 8. Optimizing your intracellular glutathione levels are how you combat aging and neolithic disease generation best, in my opinion. Glutathione cannot get rid of the hydroxyl free radicals from the Fenton reactions, but the hydroxyl free radicals causes other proteins and metabolic pathways to become oxidized, and glutathione can help limit that collateral damage. When the process is excessive, glutathione levels inside a cell will be run way down, and your immune function will also seriously decline and open you up to many other disease processes. This is precisely what happens with T2D, **chronic fatigue, mold illnesses, lyme disease, autoimmune conditions and fibromyalgia.** Glutathione recycling becomes the battle line of how the quantum cell maintains its ability to stay chemically reduced for as long as possible."

Big CF Head

Martin, I really appreciate everything that you said in your post. And you said it all so very well, by the way.

I admit that the idea of a dedicated AI blog post has been my wishful thinking ever since I joined this community, but it would have never even occurred to me to transform it into an actual suggestion, if Jack would not have asked for our feedback...

Martin , I can so identify with most of the health problem that you mentioned. They are also on my long list of what I am struggling with. I also completely agree with you saying that the information that we need to combat them is already here. We just need to work a bit harder then the others - trying to sift through this huge amount of data that Jack is sending our way and extracting what we specifically need to know.

There are a few obstacles to that strategy that I personally need to find the way to overcome. One of them being that, like so many others here, I try to limit my non-native EMF exposure and avoid spending too much time online. That definitely limits my research time here. The other one is even more significant for me is a huge brain fog that is still with me, and makes it even more difficult to comprehend all that important information. I try to translate some of the key terminology into Russian so that I could better retain it, but it is still a very slow process for me. My goal is to get it all right - and to get it fast. My margin for any setbacks is very narrowâ€¦.

> I also don't understand why using Twitter during a webinar is a good thing (Tim Jackson's)...this all seems like caving to a system that puts "multi-tasking" in front of deep understanding and the ability to remain present.

The conversations we are having around this content are important to have in whatever channels our members want to have them. We are supporting the conversations. In the Connected Age, you do not control where people have conversations ... you can only support people in having conversations where they want to have them. The field of play is very dynamic and I can definitely see why some people would perceive it as "scattered." It is what it is. You can't "force" people to have any and all conversations here. Either many convos just won't be had, or people will set up their own "conversation space" in these channels to talk about whatever-floats-their-boat without us being able to moderate and chime in and lend support. By providing the groups, we at least are giving you all the option to engage in places that are most meaningful--and convenient--for you. We are also able to give you guys more real-time communication, which means we're alleviating some frustrations as they happen.

No one needs to set up a facebook page if they don't have one. Dr. Kruse has been actively engaging on Facebook since the beginning ... there have always been a lot of conversations happening in social channels that people who did not have a Facebook page or Twitter account were not participating in. There is no way that social channels can even touch the amount of content and conversations we're having over here, so don't worry if you're not in those channels!

### Gracelind — 2014-02-05T05:08:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-108397>

I find the redundancy very helpful. When I read a concept in one or more blogs, hear it multiple times in the webinar, and again in the Q&A, after several repetitions it becomes part of my repertoire. The video added a good foundation for building my comprehension.

When I read a new concept for the first time I rarely get it right away. Using multiple senses--visual and audio--makes the reading easier to "get."

A few videos ago I had to smile when the cat jumped up on the back of the couch. The feline body language and facial expression made it clear the cat was going to assess the situation and then plunk down on your shoulder. To my surprise the cat also left just before you finished. I wonder if you gave it subconscious clues with your own body language? Otherwise, that cat is psychic!

I can't imagine how overwhelming it must be for someone new to begin trying to read from the beginning!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-23T16:42:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-111808>

I think if we do it........it will be for members and not the general public. The people complaining the loudest were not members. I'd rather support the people who understand hard is hard. I bit my tongue on this issue........because I wanted to explode. Misty told me to shut my trap about it. Health in this modern world requires tough choices and arduous tasks because of what we are facing. Most people do not have gumption to accept this.

The road to success is a fairly long journey. We all live in microwave society, so people generally expect to see success pretty quickly. When they do not see the expected results, they quit pretty soon and bitch and moan about what they "had or have to do" to obtain it. However, successful people know that great success is achieved by working diligently, day in and day out irrespective of the results, because they understand that long term success requires hard work, believe in yourself, have faith in God, total commitment towards your dreams and goals. It is a test of one's character, how strong you are internally to keep doing things, even though you do not see the results straight away.

### tellmisty — 2014-02-23T17:00:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-111810>

Everyone will get a login, and in this area, they will be able to see the webinars that are included in their membership, they'll be able to access their downloads (like the cookbook), subscribe to eCourses, etc. We've got some extra special opportunities with Dr. Kruse for Gold & Platinum members that will be hosted in this space, and we'll be able to do some communicating with these members about things like a Get Optimal weekend.

You all may remember that we had a member area at one time, but we killed it. The developer just didn't build it well and it was buggy. We have rebuilt it and hopefully, it will present fewer issues this time around!

### caroline — 2014-02-23T23:49:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-111863>

I wonder how we convey properly to folks that this isn't a quit fix ......But - Dr. K. did tell me once that not everyone makes it......

I was speaking to a friend yesterday who has had thyroid cancer and is very overweight and very bad knees etc. I tried to gently suggest that she think about going paleo and doing C/T.
I could not believe how fast she tuned me out and wasn't the least bit interested...
Talk about no gumption .....and putting everything in the "too hard" basket - and yet she is a very successful woman
BTW - she does swim a few times a week .... but it has to be a heated pool. Her DH is considered a "know it all" by some - but he understood immediately the significance of C/T - he got it hook line and sinker and tried to tell her ....but she tuned him out faster than she did me!

### caroline — 2014-02-24T04:11:49+0200

### caroline — 2014-02-24T04:26:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-111886>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=111871)
>
> If you want to see the people circling the drain you should sit in an ER........I see them all the time. They are unaware of what they do not know and their providers are unaware of what to do............because no one has taught them that the smallest things really matter. I would hope RobH would chime in here. He is an ER doc.

When my DH was so very ill ...... We listened to all the Docs etc. who had been caring for him for years. They were all extraordinary professionals and really cared for both if us in may ways - but - I don't think I would have been too open to any of this even tho I am a very open minded person. We have been programmed to listen to the professionals that care for us and I would have been too scared to go against what they were telling us. There was a surgeon tho who I hated - he was so arrogant and dismissive and I truly did have smoke coming out of my ears one day and I had done my homework. Actually there was another surgeon who was a real piece of work and my DH made short work of him.
Now I know so much more .....but I didn't then...
In a hospital setting you get backed into a corner by everyone ......it is very hard to go against all these people that supposedly have your health and best interests at heart......

### RobH — 2014-02-24T20:32:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-111961>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=111871)
>
> If you want to see the people circling the drain you should sit in an ER........I see them all the time. They are unaware of what they do not know and their providers are unaware of what to do............because no one has taught them that the smallest things really matter. I would hope RobH would chime in here. He is an ER doc.

All true. So tragically true.

The ER is the final common pathway for the failures in our medical system and a ringside seat to watching the de-evolution of human beings. Many of those who have no real discernible quantifiable diagnosis (per modern medicine and their primary care doctors... when they are able to find one) but have issues that are tremendously real to them (chronic pain, fatigue, hormonal issues, brain fog, etc) eventually show up in the ER for some reason or another, and frustrate us all because we really don't know what it is that we don't know in order to help. We see the devastating results of real diseases every day (heart disease, strokes, dementia, DM, osteoporosis, etc.) that seems to happen to younger and younger people now than they did when I started (a mere 15 years ago). Every single shift I see the human tragedy of the pathway we are now walking.

Many people think of the ER and think of the over dramatized trauma center TV shows (where the doctors never do paperwork or enter stuff in to the computer) but the reality is it's much more dealing with acute exacerbations of chronic neolithic disease than it is heroically saving gunshot wound victims or whatever TV shows it to be.

RobH

### Josh — 2014-02-24T20:38:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-111962>

A friend ran the psych ER at a regional VA center one year. It was in the middle of the "our vets are getting the best care...." He became fascinated with PTSD and how little he was empowered to do about it. 20 minutes every 6 months for severe cases is what I remember.

### NeilBB — 2014-02-24T21:07:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-111965>

> The ER is the final common pathway for the failures in our medical system and a ringside seat to watching the de-evolution of human beings. Many of those who have no real discernible quantifiable diagnosis (per modern medicine and their primary care doctors... when they are able to find one) but have issues that are tremendously real to them (chronic pain, fatigue, hormonal issues, brain fog, etc) eventually show up in the ER for some reason or another, and frustrate us all because we really don't know what it is that we don't know in order to help.

There is another "final common pathway" for these same folks, Rob and Jack, and its the one I preside over. It's a large academic center "seizure monitoring unit." Approximately 50% of our admissions (7 beds, 365 days/yr) do not end up having epileptic seizures, but instead various types of "bizarre spells" that modern medicine usually has no answer for. Some have a clear psychogenic (PTSD-type etiology). Some do not. Most are "poster-children" of neolithic disease, obesity, insomnia, and inflammation, exacerbated by the over-medicating tendencies of the modern system. I do what I can to point them in a better direction...

### Arcole — 2014-02-24T23:45:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-111981>

In the past year, my health has become a freaking money pit. I was so delighted to see the new email classes coming out that provide such fabulous information and for FREE! I can't thank you all enough for these.

Nobody wants to go cheap on their health, but even if you are lucky enough to get insurance to pay for some testing, this journey can get expensive---prohibitively expensive for so many. I would love to hear even more about setting priorities. For instance, what order should tests come to help spread investment costs out? If you've got to choose between which supplement to pay for and which to skip in order to buy groceries, which should it be?

The fact that everyone's journey is different with individual needs and situations makes this very difficult I know. But a "fix in this order" flow chart with "ideal/less than ideal/better than nothing" choices might make things easier for people. I would hate for people to get overywhelmed by the information and start wasting money in areas that are much lower down the priority list just because that money was easy to spend instead of holding out for something much more necessary (yep, feel free to look here. I've done it.)

The forums contain a lot of good advice, but it gets really difficult to find reviews of products or people's experiences with supplements when one review post is buried in a pile of hundreds in a thread.

I'm constantly searching for the best bang for my health buck because without results, I'm throwing money up a wild hog's rear end as the system blows sunshine up mine!!

### caroline — 2014-02-24T23:51:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-111982>

Dr. K. and Neil and Rob ......how much of a difference can you actually make in a hospital setting?
You have hospital policy and colleagues - and all the rules and regulations and CW.... and all the patients who don't seem to want to help themself.

I would think it would be so much more frustrating/heartbreaking for you - to make a difference ... especially since you know better.

It must seem like lambs being led to the slaughter some days ......

What influence do you have with your colleages?

I've done it.)

I keep trying to find ways to make things easier and more accessible, and honestly... It's just a lot of work. I think our eCourses can help you get traction faster, but that doesn't mean we make it easy. It's just flat hard work.

I have spent THOUSANDS of dollars, and... I think you will save a lot of money and get results faster in the long run by biting the bullet and getting all your labs done at once and working with someone kickass like Dr Tim. The Heal Your Hormones Bootcamp walks you through the "right order" for approaching your own n=1.

The fundamentals of Dr Kruse's protocols are not pricey, really. Good water, amber glasses, cutting wifi, grounding, sun, CT. You can definitely get started affordably, and as you get into things more, learn more about your n=1, you'll see where to prioritize additional investment.

This is just my opinion... Hopefully others will chime in with their thoughts too!

### NeilBB — 2014-02-25T03:36:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-112032>

> Dr. K. and Neil and Rob ......how much of a difference can you actually make in a hospital setting?
> You have hospital policy and colleagues - and all the rules and regulations and CW.... and all the patients who don't seem to want to help themself.
>
> I would think it would be so much more frustrating/heartbreaking for you - to make a difference ... especially since you know better.
>
> It must seem like lambs being led to the slaughter some days ......
>
> What influence do you have with your colleages?

Caroline, I just wrote a long post to thoroughly answer your question, but erased most of it cause I thought it was just too negative to publish in the "non-healthcare provider section." Yeah, it's frustrating.  But I will say this...

Jack's depth of thinking and scope of integration is unprecedented in my experience. And then, his willingness to then apply and implement his synthesis without waiting for a consensus is exceedingly rare these days. An amazing amount of mental focus, confidence, and courage is needed for all that. It will be very hard to enlist supporters from within medicine for those reasons and more. There's only risk, not much reward for an individual in opposing all the powers of society. The "art of medicine" has mostly already given way to the so-called "evidence-based" approach, which is really a synonym for bureaucratic groupthink based on a flawed biological literature. That is the norm.

There are still some rewarding moments for me though. You just try to chip away patient by patient and try to get them to move in the right direction within their own capabilities. And since I am an educator at a medical school, I do have the opportunity to do the same with medical students, neurology and neurosurgery residents, and epilepsy and sleep medicine fellows. I plant seeds where I can. You never know what might eventually grow. So there is at least some satisfaction in that.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-25T04:47:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-112039>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=111982)
>
> Dr. K. and Neil and Rob ......how much of a difference can you actually make in a hospital setting?
> You have hospital policy and colleagues - and all the rules and regulations and CW.... and all the patients who don't seem to want to help themself.
>
> I would think it would be so much more frustrating/heartbreaking for you - to make a difference ... especially since you know better.
>
> It must seem like lambs being led to the slaughter some days ......
>
> What influence do you have with your colleages?

I look at the hospital as a tar pit where elephants go to die. They are ghost towns, and we are ghosts within that town. In that town something interesting happens. We begin to be haunted by the memory of those we love, those with whom we feel we have unfinished business. Some patients, family and some lovers......while they may no longer be with us, a faint aroma of their presence remains in the air, a presence that haunts us until we make our peace with them and let them go. The problem, however, is that we tend to spend a great deal of energy in attempting to avoid this truth. We construct an image of ourselves that seeks to shield us from a confrontation with our ghosts. That image has a white coat or scrubs on. Hence we often encounter them only late at night, in the corridors of our dreams. floating in our own tar pits............

### Josh — 2014-02-25T04:56:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-112040>

Very poetic...I occasionally pull out the extra drain spike that a nurse gave me when she casually walked into the NYU cancer ward room where my sister lay with her breast freshly removed. My mother was down the hall having been pronounced "terminal" in the ER the previous week. The attending had walked out where she was sitting with my father who had returned after 17 years of estrangement to care for his daughter and help with my mother and told them that he was sorry, she had metastasis all over. He was surprised that they did not know yet. So the nurse walks in and says "they sent an extra one, you can have it if you want it". So I took it. As you know, it is a 3/8" razor sharp spike with threads on the back for the tubing. I pull it out to see if people are motivated to avoid having one threaded into their body like a sewing needle. I get mixed results and it is a reminder to me of what I would rather avoid...In an earlier incarnation, I spent a year as the monitor tech in CCU watching 36 hearts simultaneously all night-age 19. The EMF probably damaged me for life, but I liked the nurses and the camaraderie...

### NeilBB — 2014-02-25T05:53:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-112053>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=112048)
>
> but since you are an educator ..... There must be all kinds of parramaters .... Rules and regs that you have to abide by.
> Could you tell students that C/T Is an awesome idea to reduce inflammation for example?

It's complicated. There are many different settings. What I would teach in a lecture to a group of 2nd or 3rd year med students that I see once would be very different from say a small group of neurology residents that I get to know for 3 years, or a one on one situation with an epilepsy fellow that I spend a whole year working very closely with day after day. The ones who spend the most time with me and observe more patient encounters get to absorb more outside the box situations.

There is plenty of scientific literature and athletic use supporting CT type practices so yes, I tell some about it. But most students are unfortunately not too interested in straying from the well worn path.

Since I am board certified in sleep medicine, I focus on blue light and circadian rhythms a lot also. I try to get them to get electronics out of bedroom. I rarely meet patients who can't benefit from improving sleep.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-25T05:58:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-112055>

There are no rules...........It is here that biology, the science of living things, becomes one with physics, the science of matter and motion. It is in the living of our lives that we begin to experience oneness........we become experts on the real laws of nature.

Yesterday's rule lead to today's paradox. Today's anomalies become tomorrow's theories and laws. There are no rules. The real journey of discovery consists not in seeking new landscapes, but in having new eyes to see them.

### RobH — 2014-02-25T17:28:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-112114>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=111965)
>
> There is another "final common pathway" for these same folks, Rob and Jack, and its the one I preside over. It's a large academic center "seizure monitoring unit." Approximately 50% of our admissions (7 beds, 365 days/yr) do not end up having epileptic seizures, but instead various types of "bizarre spells" that modern medicine usually has no answer for. Some have a clear psychogenic (PTSD-type etiology). Some do not. Most are "poster-children" of neolithic disease, obesity, insomnia, and inflammation, exacerbated by the over-medicating tendencies of the modern system. I do what I can to point them in a better direction...

Fascinating Neil.

Where I am there is no such thing. So we see people with these "bizarre spells" do the usual workup, all the labs (that we do) are usually fine (although BUN/Cr ratio I'm now noticing is NEVER < 8 (in my world < 20 is considered "normal"), and usually send them away, lost to follow up, recommend seeing a neurologist (knowing that is unlikely) for an EEG. Once in a while a brain tumor (too often) turns up on the CT scan and then off they go to a guy like Jack. I usually try to talk to them about blue-light / laptop / iPad avoidance but mostly they look at me like I'm crazy at that point...

I'm interested that some centers actually have units to further observe such patients and try to elucidate the etiology of these episodes.

RobH

### RobH — 2014-02-25T17:37:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-112118>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=111982)
>
> Dr. K. and Neil and Rob ......how much of a difference can you actually make in a hospital setting?
> You have hospital policy and colleagues - and all the rules and regulations and CW.... and all the patients who don't seem to want to help themself.
>
> I would think it would be so much more frustrating/heartbreaking for you - to make a difference ... especially since you know better.
>
> It must seem like lambs being led to the slaughter some days ......
>
> What influence do you have with your colleages?

Caroline, good question.

Not much.

Most doctors I know are tremendously well meaning. They work very hard and long hours (60+ hours / week). In the US our healthcare system is undergoing a lot of turmoil right now and so many are just trying to maintain their incomes and keep their heads above the water economically. In the hospital environment there is a tremendous regulatory and paperwork burden, and when you throw in the transition to the Electronic Medical Record, most are working more hours (with computers) than they are spending time with patients. When you get home at the end of such a day (or night) there is little energy left for the curiosity that Jack seems to so amply be endowed with, unless it is really personal to you (as it is to all of us). Otherwise these folks believe (rightly or wrongly) they are already the masters of their craft and so don't particularly want to retool their practices after spending years learning how to do what they do.

My job has me spending a tremendous amount of time with the computer and the phone (in addition to doing ER shifts) and the last thing I want to do is spend a bunch more computer time at the end of a day... thus my absence from here for a while. However, I've also come to realize that this is what is necessary for me to heal me.

I've passed around a printouts of blog posts to various physician friends -- The Quantum Brain (EE5)to our local neurologist, some of the stuff on Concussion (early blogs) to a neurosurgeon, The Gallbladder CPC to a surgeon. Talked to a plastic surgeon about CT (and Zeltique). Mostly when I followed up with them they hadn't taken the time to read it and / or didn't see the relevance. Just too busy working their way through the weeds and trees to take a look at the forest.

That forest view would cause them to see the trees and the weeds very differently though.

RobH

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-25T17:43:02+0200
