### NeilBB — 2014-02-25T20:05:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-112142>

> Where I am there is no such thing. So we see people with these "bizarre spells" do the usual workup, all the labs (that we do) are usually fine (although BUN/Cr ratio I'm now noticing is NEVER < 8 (in my world < 20 is considered "normal"), and usually send them away, lost to follow up, recommend seeing a neurologist (knowing that is unlikely) for an EEG. Once in a while a brain tumor (too often) turns up on the CT scan and then off they go to a guy like Jack. I usually try to talk to them about blue-light / laptop / iPad avoidance but mostly they look at me like I'm crazy at that point...
>
> I'm interested that some centers actually have units to further observe such patients and try to elucidate the etiology of these episodes.

Rob, I am at a pretty big center. We have over 50 neurologists in our department, with many different sub-specialty backgrounds. My work here is purely as an epileptologist. There are 5 of us, and we are looking to hire two more epileptologists because we are so busy. We do seizures and seizure-like events--that's it. We got so busy, I had to give up my sleep medicine clinics and studies last year, because there wasn't time. That's how much seizure work there is now.

Video EEG monitoring is the centerpiece of a major epilepsy program. The purpose of a vEEG monitoring unit is basically twofold. (1) EEG localization of seizure focus using both scalp and intracranial electrodes in order to offer surgical resections to patients with intractable epilepsy, and (2) Definitive diagnosis of "spells" into one of two categories, epileptic seizures or "non-epileptic spells." Since more and more people are physically and mentally broken these days, our admissions for the non-epileptic events have risen dramatically in recent years. It is a very heterogeneous population that the system and modern life has utterly failed. They are often misdiagnosed with epilepsy and treated with anticonvulsants, oftentimes for years before they come to us.

Oh, and Rob. You may find this both disheartening and hard to believe, but its a well-known fact among epileptologists, that about 80% of the general neurologists performing them have absolutely no clue how to properly interpret an EEG, and routinely overcall various types of common artifacts as epileptiform discharges, doing a tremendous amount of harm to patients in the process by causing them to be overdiagnosed and improperly treated.

### NeilBB — 2014-02-25T21:30:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-112149>

> This is hard to wrap my head around ....so even some or most of your students aren't Interested in straying from the well worn path.
>
> So, In the end, We can only live our life .....

Caroline, It's not like we as teaching physicians are constantly censored (not yet anyway). In clinical settings we can still teach based on our own experiences. Clinical settings are different from didactic lectures and are an integral part of advanced medical education. The bigger problem is more this... You can teach a person an innovative idea, about CT or light or diet, etc, etc. And he/she may be interested and think its cool. And that's great as far as it goes! BUT...

But then he goes home and grabs a burger and fries at a fast food joint cause he's in a hurry, and stays up late on computer cause he's busy, and looks to the left and to the right to see what other people are doing. And he notices that they are all on I-Pads and they aren't really getting in ice baths or wearing funny glasses at night. And then he comes to work and notices that the neuro-oncologist down the hall routinely puts his cellphone right up against his ear. (Yes, they do.) And he ultimately concludes that if all this stuff were harmful, everybody would already know about it. And since they obviously don't, it must be OK.

There seems to be a tremendous human need to look to the left and to the right for validation. Society counts on it for stability. (That is why religion has dominated science throughout history. Religion is stabilizing and based on conformity. Science is evolving and destabilizing and based on discovery and revision.) To stand out from society seems to trigger some sort of deep-seated fear mechanism that most people would rather not confront. Doctors and other educated people and people that are financially or intellectually highly-invested in the status-quo are subject to this even more so than others.

People on this website are committed and invested and looking for real answers and willing to do what it takes. That is not generally the case with patients (or trainees) in the real world that I see every day. You can't give that spark to another person. They have to find it for themselves. When and if they find it, THEN they can be educated and helped. Prior to that, there is really not much you can do. All you can ultimately do is search through the masses and look for the few that have that spark in their eyes. The rest are basically already dead...

### Josh — 2014-02-28T18:21:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-112672>

Something to consider:

[www.keas.com](http://www.keas.com)

They are the child of Google Health. Even their free stuff has a lot of good info on how people respond to health and wellness stuff. One thing I took away is that if you just give people information, you get about 10% compliance/interest. If it is made into a social media game, one gets 70-75% participation. It seems we are somewhere in between at this point, still weighted towards the information model.

Also:

[www.samuelliinstitute.com](http://www.samuelliinstitute.com)

They are working on a lot of this stuff in the integrative context with models of analysis and implementation.

### NeilBB — 2014-02-28T21:13:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-112700>

> but since you are an educator ..... There must be all kinds of parramaters .... Rules and regs that you have to abide by.

Interestingly, most of the rules and regs that we are hassled about on a daily basis have little to do with either patient care or medical education. They are primarily issues of corporate compliance and electronic documentation as well as taking courses on things like "diversity training." We are also graded on on our "consistency in hand washing" prior to patient encounters. (Patients are routinely given a form to fill out and turn in on whether the doctor washes his hands in sight of the patient which is considered mandatory protocol.)

The good part is that we are still free to do as we think best in patient care and education in many respects. The bad part is that much of our time is spent dealing with the corporate bureaucratic policies that are never ending.

### NeilBB — 2014-02-28T22:31:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-112718>

**

> I am fascinated by the need for epileptologists?

**
**What is an Epileptologist?**
The coining of the word **EPILEPTOLOGIST** most certainly dates to at least the turn of the century. Spratling was North America's first Epileptologist, \[see Dasheiff, RM, The First American Epileptologists: William P. Spratling, MD, and Roswell Park, MD Neurology 1994;44:171-174.\] Spratling used the term _epileptologist_ to describe himself and others who specialized in epilepsy in his book: Epilepsy and its treatment. Phila. WB Saunders, 1904. Others picked up on this term as evidenced by L. Pierce Clark's chapter "A Suggestion to Epileptologists" found in Transactions of the National Association for the Study of Epilepsy and the Care and Treatment of Epileptics. Vol. IV. Ed. W.P. Spratling, Sixth annual meeting, New Haven, Conn. November 8, 1906l pp.204-212

Nevertheless, epilepsy as a specialty did not enjoy widespread visibility until after World War II. The invention of the EEG, modern Neurosurgical practices, and substantive clinical and basic research helped the field develop. The Veterans Administration spearheaded the movement by establishing Epilepsy Centers in the 1970's launching a new breed of Neurologist's who began to specialize in the treatment and research of epilepsy.
The Epileptologist practices in four areas:

-   Patient Care
-   Research
-   Teaching
-   Public Service

The quintessential EPILEPTOLOGIST is a physician who is Board Certified in Neurology (or Pediatric Neurology, or Neurosurgery), Board Certified in EEG (Clinical Neurophysiology), had Fellowship training at an Academic Epilepsy Center, had Postdoctoral training in Basic Research. This physician may or may not additionally have a higher degree (e.g. Masters, Ph.D.). S/He usually belongs to a number of Academic Societies: [American Epilepsy Society](http://www.aesnet.org/)(AES), American Clinical Neurophysiology Society (ACNS) \[formally Am. EEG Soc.\], [American Academy of Neurology](http://www.aan.com/) (AAN), Child Neurology Society (CNS), [Epilepsy Foundation of America](http://www.efa.org/)(EFA), [Society for Neuroscienc](http://www.nas.edu/cwse/SFN.html)e (SN), [American Association for the Advancement of Science](http://www.aaas.org/)(AAAS).

The Epileptologist predominantly sees patients with epilepsy, seizures, and spells (the three are different) at a program which has multidisciplinary support (outpatient, inpatient; medical, surgical, experimental treatments; psychology, psychiatry; social; nursing; pharmacy).

In addition to patient care, an Epileptologist must concurrently be engaged in Research. \[ [NIH Homepage](http://www.nih.gov/home.html), [Nat'l Inst. Neurological Disease and Stroke](http://www.ninds.nih.gov/) \] The research may be either clinical e.g. antiepileptic drug studies, epidemiology, surgical, etc.; or basic e.g. anatomic, biochemical, computer modeling, EEG, genetic, etc.

The Epileptologist teaches others in the Health field (medical students, Neurology residents, Epilepsy Fellows, nurses, pharmacists, etc.) as well as researchers in training (predoctoral, postdoctoral).

Finally, an Epileptologist is concerned about the non-medical issues of Epilepsy (social injustice, driving regulations, access to employment and healthcare, quality of life). He may pursue this through local and/or national organizations such as [EFA](http://www.efa.org/).

As the field of Epilepsy grew, Basic Research scientists became involved not only with epilepsy research, but with the broader aspects of teaching and public service. Although excluded from direct patient care as non-physicians, they nevertheless embodied the full ideal of an Epileptologist.

In the 1980's, the concept of EPILEPTOLOGIST was embraced by clinical researchers and nurses, the later forming their own Neuroscience Nursing Society.

More and more medical and non-medical professions began to use the term in association with persons who wished to emphasize their subspecialty of expertise. However, just as there are seizures and pseudoseizures, there are pseudo-epileptologists. These are people in both the medical and non-medical professions who lack much of the training and expertise described above. Since imitation is a form of flattery, the field of Epilepsy is justified in saying it has now matured and flourished into a major force within the medical community.

Despite the fact that Epilepsy affects just as many people as Diabetes, it is an illness which is_in the closet_. An unpopular illness with great social stigma. Neurology as a whole is a small specialty, and the trends in healthcare further decrease referrals to Neurologist from Primary Care Providers.

**HOW DO EPILEPSY PATIENTS GET TO AN EPILEPTOLOGIST?** Most don't!

Most are treated by Family Practitioners, General Practitioners, Internists, Pediatricians. Most benefit from community standard medicine. For patients which appear to be more difficult to manage, referrals are made to Neurologists or Neurosurgeons for another level of care. Finally, the highest level of expertise is the Epileptologist. These 100 individuals spread throughout the United States couldn't possible handle all 4 million people with epilepsy. They are usually very busy seeing medically intractable patients, patients with difficult to diagnose spells, pseudoseizures, and epilepsy surgical candidates. However, if a patient doesn't think he or she is getting what is listed in the epilepsy [BILL OF RIGHTS](http://www.oumedicine.com/neurology/department-divisions/epilepsy/epilepsy-bill-of-rights), they can ask for a referral.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-07T01:19:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-190300>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=107259)
>
> Josh I would suggest forgetting how I think........you'll never get it because it comes from how I learned all the parts of science. The most important part for you to realize is that the reason many don't understand or follow what I am saying is because we are in different rooms of the same house. You see the house thru the room you're in now not the room I am currently in. I am aware of it and most others do not appear to realize this. You do not need to know all of this.....you just need to have an appreciation of the info and then know how to apply it. Most of what is in my Rx blogs do just that. Where people get confused is they think because we are all human we all just have the same Rx pathways. Not true at all.
>
> Clinical medicine today is also on this very superficial level of biologic understanding. What I have learned is that to be a good clinician you first have to realize that all life scales from macroscopic biology to quantum levels. The scale or levels all have different jargon that go with them, but there are ciphers, quotes, and metaphors that help one understand a jump or a fall back in the scale.
>
> For example.......an everyday neurosurgeon sees the brain as an organ that controls many vegetative and autonomic functions because they see the world through the human reality of how we respond to the environment through our behaviors. A neurosurgeon, like me, sees how the level of tissue organization is immaterial and confounding when one considers how water alters its nuclear spin to the environmental electromagnetic force actually is what determines who is mentally ill and who is not. Who suffers from adrenal fatigue and who will get MS.
>
> The difference in the understanding the scale is the educational barrier that most people struggle with because of how the believe biology is organized and categorized by human historical standards based in medical education. The scale I am showing people is the smallest scale........and in my blogs I show you how the atomic and 3D molecular arrays actually dictate and explain anatomic findings published in books that have no current explanation;
>
> These unknown quantum scales however contain solutions to these paradox and explain unknown physiologic mechanisms of behaviors that all humans have, such as sleep. The reason why scale is hard to convey to any person is because no one is trained to move from atomic to macroscopic biology from an educational standpoint.
>
> My diverse interests and curiosity have built that framework in my mind. You can not re create it. I just have to get you to see the patterns in how the scales are translated into things you observe to be factual.........each scale has a Rosetta stone that allows one to go up or down the ladder of scale.
>
> I think when you read EE 12 you will see how we go from Larmor frequency to water, to redox potential, to amino acids, to complex proteins, to DNA and RNA, the organelles, and then back to water, light, and magnetism with the easy of a child sliding down a sheet of ice on ice skates........Each one of those examples is a step up or down in scale of how biology acts in different complexities or layers.
>
> Right now most of you and clinical medicine is stuck on certain basic labs to draw inference. Guys like me understand the labs nuanace and how one persons high E2 maybe identical in two people but mean two different things when one considers the context in which both those numbers were built upon. Most of you and your docs think a testosterone of 248 in two people mean the same thing and nothing could be further from the truth.
>
> Most clinicians do not even understand how hormones tie into life's equation, the redox potential. We need to stop thinking they way we do.......and begin to think in layers, scales, or peeling the onion skin back to get to the core issues........the quantum issues.
>
> These are the fundamental building blocks of life. Light, water, and magnetism.............The application and assimilation of those 3 simple things.......is complex because their interactions create the many different scales.........and what controls the scales or layers of action? The myriad of frequencies buried in the electromagnetic force. No one.......seems to grasp the power of this force. This force dictates how every scale actually works microscopically and leads to different macroscopic results.
>
> This is why people even on this site can not fathom that food is not that important.........but that is because they do not understand the scale of the atomic world. For example, the macroscopic understanding today of neurodegeneration encompasses the misfolding of proteins and a lack of energy as key issues. A quantum scale shows that an important step to the solution of the protein folding problem is the detailed understanding of the balance of forces that stabilize the native protein structure. The 3 D molecular structure of water and its interaction with the photoelectric effect and the electromagnetic force is indispensable in this understanding.
>
> Water acts as a chameleon under the direct influence of the electromagnetic spectrum and the electromagnetic force it is in and therefore can be a true demon or a true god to life to cause protein to mis-fold or to make it happen effortlessly.
>
> Medicine and modern researchers have no idea of these quantum scale problems because their disciplines were never taught to co mingle. One can not know something you are ignorant of. This blog brings you the solutions to the scale problem way before medicine will see it........because when you see it.......you'll begin to think differently and act differently to offset your own risks of getting what most other people get when you only have the macroscopic myopic picture of how things unfold. This is a process of learning slowly and steadily..........You wont pick it up like a foreign language........you have to bathe in the water and live the science for yourself a bit to see the magic nature has for us all.

### Sheddie — 2017-07-24T17:54:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-219942>

I come at this thread from a more 'neuro-social' than neuro-science study framework. A functioning 'wholistic' mind-brain is too rare out there, but will eventually be the most highly valued modern 'creative' person's legacy in this runaway Information Society era. If, that is, the fount of the 'scientific hermaneutics' we have herein isn't suppressed or removed from view... I remind myself frequently that the history of the 'production & distribution of knowledge' has favored deductive reasoning. The design and development of current medical science went to a more 'fill in the blanks' style of research (higher number of publications get in the databases) completely caged in academia and corporate labs. IMHO, the abyss between data and results in the last 50+ years of medical 'knowledge' seems to have seen a lot of poor interpretation of the data, and, poor statistical methods. The future will need an enormous effort (and funding, good luck) to complete meta-analyses of a lot of research and correct the apparent "garbage in, garbage out" problems -- especially as so much that is harmful to health care consumers is taught in medical school and regurgitated in practice for yet another generation. We need 'inductive' reasoners to put it all together when it's more in an information digestion process...

I understand the many perspectives of members here and their hungry preferences for quick, simple, elegant answers. Drilling down deep into layers of understanding is frustrating when we've all been unconsciously media-programmed to get health (and other) fixes in 30-second 'Plop, Plop, Fizz, Fizz' take-a-pill TV advertising. I'm showing my age but it's still going on at fantastic levels of digital, carrier-wave audio-visual-induced 'persuasion.' It was so much easier to notice the population effects back when the media advertising field was less sophisticated. For example, are parents wondering if exposing baby to Sesame Street might be interfering with brain development for later acquisition of reading & writing skills? Is there, perhaps, one explanation (among many) for the obvious demise in critical thinking skills in the over-stimulated color & sound media aimed at little ones who someday need to have more abilities going on in their heads (for their survival) than being acutely aware of how they 'feel' (gut affect 'intelligence' is soaring here, Millennials and all that!) . This selfie-status ordering of The Individual is so disconnected from reasoned and reasonable interaction with reality that we've become Half-Individual? Oops. Oh, they're perfectly trainable for just about anything in six weeks with an average IQ. But, if something goes counter to or different from such 'vocational training,' as higher education is today, the dismal lack of "education" won't help the outcome. I want my health care provider to be capable of thoughtfulness. Only reading & writing complete school-training into the educated individual. That's largely one half of the brain's potential fallen by the wayside without the brain-benefits of literacy. Yes, about 2/3rds of you will cringe at my sweeping generalizations here, but they are based on above average consumption and interpretation. I'm not wrong, just might not be all that right. The need to question persists, and, tenets that work now might need to be replaced later. Open-minded, doing our best, and forge ahead.

To me, it is easily recognizable that vast ancient to modern information, where the curiosity for Why is located to be retrieved, has to be distilled to practical information -- to assist with the How of taking on, say, the synthesis of circadian biology with medical bio-chemistry. Keeping in mind that the fields of bio-chemistry and medicinal chemistry, I'll suggest, have some wicked lines of socio-political corrupted, or impure, elements throughout their production and distribution. Deductive departmentalization/compartmentalization is the bane of inductive interpretation... Each of us has the human potential to 'whole brain' synthesis of capable human knowingness -- it's just that lately it's like swimming upstream and the current the other way is stronger all the time.

So far, the very newbie that I am, has one 'worry.' I find members' lists of activities toward their individual plan of action enormously useful to start and re-orient me in the practical 'stuff.' Sometimes, I question if a point here and there is 'the best' way of doing something -- mostly because I'm wrangling with the order of solar-cycling and CT-timing. It's then I might ask online 'why-this-way?' I'll still try it myself, pass or fail, not to waste your time. I only seem to be missing self-confidence at this stage (you all going before me and to come are hugely confidence-giving!). I do hope no one will be too shy to point out anyone's possible error if they 'see' it, or everyone might be ready if someone nit-picks some detail for an explanation of 'why?' This is NOT ever to be taken as negative criticism, please. I can't expect Dr. Kruse and perhaps a few assistants to catch any and all errors in my/our thinking -- the kind of 'thinking' we're expressing online using slower reading & writing than a faster podcast, phone call, etc., means of communication!

Update: Once again, I find that... Jack Kruse already said what I'm thinking... "_Once you eliminate epistemology and ignore obvious controls.…..you can 'scientifically prove' just about anything to keep the grant money flowing in. That is modern science in a nutshell and it is why nutrition and food studies are worthless to a mitochondriac._" (I would add, why much of med school chronic disease training and medicinal chemistry research are not much use to Mitochondriacs!)

### mmarston — 2017-08-11T06:15:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-needed-for-new-updates-and-needs.9455/post-221402>

Dr. Kruse, If you haven't done so already, please consider collaborating with architects, planners, designers and engineers to re-design educational and healthcare facilities, corporate buildings, military bases, hotels, etc. Monitoring changes will no doubt reveal the positive impact of re-aligning our 'modern' built environments with our mitochondria. Today I was sitting in the break room of a windowless office reading your blog, when I overheard two women talk about losing weight via weight watchers. I politely interruped and suggested that maybe they would have even more success if they got outside the office and into the sun, which I planned to do myself. We have so many building codes for fire safety, egress, and handicap, etc. yet where are the building codes to protect our most precious guides, our eyes and mitochondria? Just a suggestion, but Google is very focused on creating safe environments for their employees, maybe they would be open to your suggestions. Check out these articles: [https://www.google.com/amp/s/techcrunch.com/2011/08/17/google-pushes-for-greener-buildings-leads-by-](https://www.google.com/amp/s/techcrunch.com/2011/08/17/google-pushes-for-greener-buildings-leads-by-) example/amp/ and [http://dailycommercialnews.com/en-U...ffice-obtains-LEED-v4-certification-1023476W/](http://dailycommercialnews.com/en-US/Projects/News/2017/5/Google-office-obtains-LEED-v4-certification-1023476W/)

# Thread: Doing Leptin Rx, wheres my energy gone? :(

### margor_j — 2014-01-25T01:56:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doing-leptin-rx-wheres-my-energy-gone.9458/post-106946>

So, Iv changed locations in the past two weeks. i for sure, feel sure this is a way better situation - no neighbours' wifi around, near the ocean etc etc etc.

Im sleeping like iv never known! OMG, if i could id be thanking JK in person with tears streaming down my face!! good lord! iv waited 4 years to be able to sleep!! i literally start yawning around 8pm tho, and as a rule, am asleep by ten. wake anywhere from 5.30-6.30am - sounds all good, but i get up and start my day, usually with a walk on the beach and go to come home and it hits me, just dead exhausted. its only been in the past week so what the hell is it?

i do wonder though, its this my body resetting after all these years of chronic insomnia? is it actually something deeper happening on a reset level? anyone else had this?

i do wonder about the whey protein shakes in the morning,, hmm im off dairy otherwise but whey proteins one of those things you think youre going to be ok, so you buy it to try it.. and where im at now, its a lot easier for me to get up and make a smoothie, it is what it is. id like to think its not that though because thats not a hugely drastic change, its not like iv never had it before or wasnt having it before leaving that city i was in - and im at my parents place, nothing has changed here and i usually feel fine...

thanks

im trying to back off eggs 'just incase' im reactive to them? previous tests have shown i am, but i got told that it wasnt a good test (confusion!) or the factor that was tested for reaction, wasnt so reliable..

hmmm so you think its the whey protein caroline?

### caroline — 2014-01-25T02:33:45+0200

### margor_j — 2014-01-25T02:45:43+0200

yeah consult with TJ im still coming to terms with! an hour flies by so fast and i have lots of questions, but i could talk about health stuff all day! at least now, i know more bout digestive issues iv had and how to fix them specifically, and it all helps in building the over all picture im creating.. he went off the extensive testing i had done via a GP last year and also my CDSA.. respect to digestion!

Any help would be appreciated.My estradial number 78.7 (ref. range - 50-350). Progesterone is 15.7 (ref. range -4.4-28)Total testosterone -24 (14-76) and Free Testosterone is .2 (ref. range is 1-5).

Robin

# Thread: Cold acclimatisation and ketogenic diets
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-acclimatisation-and-ketogenic-diets.9461/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-01-25T12:51:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-acclimatisation-and-ketogenic-diets.9461/post-107006>

I've experimented with cold showers and baths a very little on and off over the years. (It's always kind of been there on the fringe: often part of the regimen in sports books from the 30s through to about the 50s. James Bond used to take them!) But I've never done much. I experimented a little more after reading one of Jack's blogs a while ago, but nothing for quite a while.

But here's the thing. I've been face-dunking for about 3 days now and being sure not to wear more clothing than I need (though I tend not to, anyway). And yet, I'm starting to disregard the cold more. I noticed that again walking down the road this morning. I had a waterproof (unlined) coat, but open down the front, and only a T-shirt under. I suddenly realised I could see my breath: not really cold but cold enough for that. But I felt quite comfortable. It can't be 3 days of face-dunking: it must be the ketogenic diet: that's about a month old.

### Lahelada — 2014-01-25T14:05:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-acclimatisation-and-ketogenic-diets.9461/post-107009>

I had the same experience. I was living ketogenically before findingthis board and noticed that I was extremely cold resistant as others were sailing in polar fleeces in winter what passes for winter here  and I in a T shirt. My history is similar. I dabbled in cold showers, Kneipp therapy but nothing consistent over the years.
Well, I tried to find out more, googled, came across the term thermogenesis, landed here and here I am.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-01-25T16:38:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-acclimatisation-and-ketogenic-diets.9461/post-107019>

cold ketosis low 24 hr light levels and hydration must all exist in unison for it to work optimally.

### Dali Dula — 2014-01-27T14:19:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-acclimatisation-and-ketogenic-diets.9461/post-107030>

After almost 2 years of CT and fat adaptation my cold tolerance is incredible. I repair and maintain my own vehicles and have not heated my shop at all this winter. We have had the coldest winter in ten years here in the northeast and I work for many hours in temps close to zero in comfort. I x-country ski in t-shirt or shirtless. This has opened a new winter world for me. Last Bun/creat ratio was 8. I mind my light and sleep like a rock. Thanks, Jack

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-27T15:28:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-acclimatisation-and-ketogenic-diets.9461/post-107031>

I went for a hike for about three hours on Saturday and just wore an unlined waterproof unzipped over a T-shirt. It was fairly cold, and it got uncomfortable right up on the hills (on of the highest points in our county) in the wind. My hands were bright red. I knew I was OK, because I didn't start to shiver, but it was uncomfortable.

I tried a cold shower on Sunday, but I couldn't stay under long. That's a different ball game.

I have seen it said that it's thought that American Indians didn't wear shirts pre-contact. Some people seem to think that the later ones that are seen were essentially copies of European shirts -- or perhaps rather coats or smocks even though worn next the skin by the Indians.

However that may be, you certainly see old paintings and engravings that don't show the native peoples wearing much at all, even in areas of North America that could get very cold. Often no more than a breechclout -- and perhaps leggings to shield the legs from brushwood. These are Iroquians:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" } [](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/0-16702177_303-00-jpg.205/)

[http://www.dw.de/image/0,,16702177\_303,00.jpg](http://www.dw.de/image/0,,16702177_303,00.jpg)

These seem to be wearing some kind of apron (of hide? or traded cloth?) and that's about it.

Incidentally, their physiques are interesting, too. Not an ounce of spare flesh on these fellows. But neither are they exactly "bulked up" as some Paleo people fantasize hunter-gatherers were. I guess they'd be strong, but they haven't exactly been down the gym doing reps with a barbell or on a Nautilus machine!

This has opened a new winter world for me. Last bun/creat ratio was 8. I mind my light and sleep like a rock. Thanks, jack

boom!!!!!!!!!!!

### Jude — 2014-01-28T01:12:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-acclimatisation-and-ketogenic-diets.9461/post-107085>

This has opened a new winter world for me. Last Bun/creat ratio was 8. I mind my light and sleep like a rock. Thanks, Jack

That is really fantastic Dali Dula.....

### caroline — 2014-01-28T09:48:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-acclimatisation-and-ketogenic-diets.9461/post-107128>

Great success story Bob.......

### Lahelada — 2014-01-28T10:49:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-acclimatisation-and-ketogenic-diets.9461/post-107131>

Gagnrad, Inoticed something today and that is that a cold shower is easier when I have the shower on full so that the water really pummels my skin. Have a play and see if you have more success with that.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-28T12:28:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-acclimatisation-and-ketogenic-diets.9461/post-107137>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=107131)
>
> Gagnrad, Inoticed something today and that is that a cold shower is easier when I have the shower on full so that the water really pummels my skin. Have a play and see if you have more success with that.

Sounds good. I haven't got very good water pressure here, unfortunately. It's a while since I've taken cold showers or baths, and it's probably a bad time of year to start as the water is coming out of the ground pretty cold at the moment.

I like cold showers after/in-between saunas, though it's a long time since I've had one of those. After a knee injury once I went to a gym that was part of my physio's business. That had a cold plunge-pool, which I really liked.

If I had a big enough garden I think I'd like to build a sweat-lodge, sit in there with hot stones and then go indoors for a cold shower. That would be authentic hunter-gatherer, wouldn't it? Except they'd probably jump in a river or roll in the snow after.

### nonchalant — 2014-01-28T16:57:06+0200

# Thread: Prehistoric Shell Mounds Threatened by Rising Sea Level
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prehistoric-shell-mounds-threatened-by-rising-sea-level.9465/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-01-27T10:30:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prehistoric-shell-mounds-threatened-by-rising-sea-level.9465/post-107029>

This is in Florida. There are huge middens there:

> They’re digging on a huge pile of debris called midden. Lawson says it’s like a prehistoric garbage dump shaped into architectural mounds. The best modern-day analogy for it, he says, is a collection of beer bottles after a college party.
>
> “Having a giant pile of shells outside your house shows just how big of a party you had and how much you gave away and raises your status,” he explains.

They also "believe they’ve found a unique stone Indian burial mound".

[http://news.wfsu.org/post/rising-sea-adds-urgency-south-florida-archeological-digs](http://news.wfsu.org/post/rising-sea-adds-urgency-south-florida-archeological-digs)

See also:

[http://coaps.fsu.edu/nps](http://coaps.fsu.edu/nps)

# Thread: DNA of Mesolithic Hunter
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-of-mesolithic-hunter.9467/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-01-27T16:58:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-of-mesolithic-hunter.9467/post-107040>

He would have been darker-skinned than modern Europeans but had blue eyes. They say he would have had difficulty digesting starchy foods. (No suprise there.)

> He was probably lactose intolerant and **had more difficulty digesting starchy foods** than the farmers who transformed diets and lifestyles when they took up tools in the first agricultural revolution. ...
>
> Carbon dating put the remains at around 7,000 years old ...
>
> After several failed attempts, they managed to reconstruct the man's entire genome from DNA found in the root of a third molar. It is the first time researchers have obtained the complete genome of a modern European who lived before the Neolithic revolution. ...
>
> They found that the ancient DNA most closely matched the genetic makeup of people living in northern Europe, in particular Sweden and Finland.

[http://www.theguardian.com/science/2014/jan/26/swarthy-blue-eyed-caveman-dna-tooth](http://www.theguardian.com/science/2014/jan/26/swarthy-blue-eyed-caveman-dna-tooth)

Here's the paper:

[http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature12960.html](http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/vaop/ncurrent/full/nature12960.html)

### nonchalant — 2014-01-27T18:56:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-of-mesolithic-hunter.9467/post-107047>

> But scientists may have over-estimated the impact farming had in shaping the human immune system, because tests on the hunter-gatherer's DNA found that he already carried mutations that boost the immune system to tackle various nasty bugs. Some live on in modern Europeans today.

Yeah, Dr. K could have told them that... Immune system enhancements came first, then the enlarged brain.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-28T10:44:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-of-mesolithic-hunter.9467/post-107130>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=107040)
>
> They say he would have had difficulty digesting starchy foods. (No suprise there.)

I haven't got a Nature subscription, so can't see the full article. But thinking about this one, I wonder if this is more a matter of speculation than a finding. AFAIK, what you can digest is more down to your gut microbiome than your DNA. I think there can, though, be some interesting interactions here. Perhaps they mean he's got that human leukocyte antigen allele that's relatively common in Finland and doesn't go well with a starchy diet ... at any rate, if you've got an overgrowth of the wrong bacteria in your gut.

But maybe it was just a generalised remark about Hunter-gatherers -- we would not _expect_ them to eat this or that. In which case it's not a finding; it's speculation.

### nonchalant — 2014-01-28T16:54:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-of-mesolithic-hunter.9467/post-107163>

They always picture the ancients as having scraggly hair. But I have seen pictures of some aborigines with braided or otherwise cared-for hair.
Hmmm, this guy could look like one of the Bee Gees with a little TLC...

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-29T12:05:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-of-mesolithic-hunter.9467/post-107284>

Another report (from the University of Queensland) on this makes sense of that throwaway comment about starch in the _Grauniad_:

> “This Mesolithic hunter-gatherer carried the gene for lactose-intolerance consistent with an inability to digest dairy products, and also **saliva amylase genes indicative of a low-starch diet**,” Dr Sturm said.

[http://www.uq.edu.au/news/article/2...eals-origins-of-blue-eyes-lactose-intolerance](http://www.uq.edu.au/news/article/2014/01/mesolithic-man-reveals-origins-of-blue-eyes-lactose-intolerance)

### Pamela — 2014-04-11T08:19:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2014-q-a.9469/post-120941>

A question that I have not been able to find the answer to: what is the purpose of eating resistant starch? How would it help somebody? And why is it called resistant starch?

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-11T08:41:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2014-q-a.9469/post-120942>

> [Pamela said:](/goto/post?id=120941)
>
> A question that I have not been able to find the answer to: what is the purpose of eating resistant starch? How would it help somebody? And why is it called resistant starch?

In order:--

_It feeds certain gut bacteria (as will soluble fibre in food)._

_It seems only likely to help someone who isn't eating much soluble fibre._

_It's resistant to being metabolised for energy.
_

All explained in greater detail here:

[http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.co.uk/2014/02/resistant-starch-panacea-but-why.html](http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.co.uk/2014/02/resistant-starch-panacea-but-why.html)

### Pamela — 2014-04-11T21:29:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2014-q-a.9469/post-121022>

So does Resistant Starch simply mean "carbs"?

### sjoshua — 2014-04-11T22:25:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2014-q-a.9469/post-121032>

> [Pamela said:](/goto/post?id=121022)
>
> So does Resistant Starch simply mean "carbs"?

Not quite, RS is the allocation of "carbs" that are not digestible by us, the host organism. They are, however, utilized by the bacteria that call our bodies home.

Example: If you look at the nutritional data for a [potato](http://nutritiondata.self.com/facts/vegetables-and-vegetable-products/2770/2), under carbs it also delineates "dietary fiber" and "sugars". If you at all familiar with something like atkins carb counting, they typically say to back out the "dietary fiber" portion of carbs when calculating carb count on an item. The reason for this is, this is the portion humans cannot digest, i.e. the RS.

### Pamela — 2014-04-12T01:04:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2014-q-a.9469/post-121057>

Ah, I see. Thank you. So really sweet potatoes are not RS much.

### sjoshua — 2014-04-12T01:48:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2014-q-a.9469/post-121061>

I should provide one clarification to my statement above - not all of "dietary fiber" is RS, but a percentage of the dietary fiber is RS. Some "dietary fiber" is not utilized by us or our microbiome.

There has actually been quite a few posts over at MDA lately that may help clarify further, and also provide some tips for maximizing the RS content in foods, since RS can actually form AFTER cooking if specific foods are allowed to cool to maximize quantities.

Start here:
[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/the-definitive-guide-to-resistant-starch/](http://www.marksdailyapple.com/the-definitive-guide-to-resistant-starch/)
Next:
[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/resistant-starch-your-questions-answered/](http://www.marksdailyapple.com/resistant-starch-your-questions-answered/)
When you're ready to play with it:
[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/how-to-conduct-a-self-experiment-resistant-starch/](http://www.marksdailyapple.com/how-to-conduct-a-self-experiment-resistant-starch/)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-01-28T04:54:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/redox-potential.9471/post-107103>

A quote from a paper linked in the next few blogs:

"This paper provides a brief personal recount of how an electromagnetic theory of psychology was born out of working in the lab of pioneers in biophysics. The story represents an example of how a useful macroscopically applicable cognitive theory may be derived from immersion in the study of microscopic molecular quantum principles. The example of the discovery and elucidation of quantum mechanical features of biological electron transfer in energy conversion by the author’s mentors are discussed. It is suggested that the electrochemically charged reactions of oxidation‐reduction (**redox**) in enzymes may be conceptually and physically analogous to processes in the brain. However, there are also important lessons learned and caveats about attempting to over‐apply quantum effects that serve other purposes and are not likely to evolve into all components of a biological system. For example, a simple model of electron transfer rates that closely approximates a general biochemical law has proven to be a more useful and realistic metric than those that seek to find special quantum pathways in every part of the structure of the protein. Similarly, the author believes that an electromagnetic theory of consciousness based on sums of cellular electrochemical effects located primarily within the brain and body will ultimately prevail. Much work remains to show precisely how this is the case, but an initial explanation **using redox principles** is suggested.———Alan Haas

BOOM

### Hope — 2014-01-28T14:55:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/redox-potential.9471/post-107145>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=107115)
>
> Since I have added so much more fat and taken away some protein - I have sooooo much more energ. Coincidence? Or just a natural progression? And where do I go from here to keep moving in the right direction? Dr. K. Talked about being able to eat more seasonally etc. but he has also suggested that those of us in the Southern Hemisphere probably should be low carb all year...
> Is anyone adding back grains? Nuts? If so ... How are you doing?
>
> So - is fat and R/O water the game changer?
>
> I have always thought that the simpler I eat - the better off I am .... anyone else feel the same?

I have always loved nuts......especially the soaked and then dried kind, and then made into nut butter...with some raw cocoa.....amazing. it is my one dessert...I don't eat or need sugary things at all. The carbs in the nuts along with the fat and protein are perfect for me. I can't do grains, though when I am in Germany, they do have amazing sourdough rye from the mountains that I will occasionally enjoy without getting hurt, while there. With heaps of butter. Just as a treat. Otherwise, not really...my sleep is way better without.

As to water....I have not been drinking tap water of any kind for 14 years. I could always taste some kind of garbage, and in 2005 I transferred to mostly glass bottled water....with the exception of Fiji when I travel. It's not RO so much, really....but still pretty good, and far better than the tap crap. And definitely no tap in restaurants either. Hasn't been cheap.....some day I MIGHT get a whole house filter....but not sure I would like the pipes unless we build ourselves. But I also spend NO money on juices, soda, coffee or anything. Kombucha is the only thing I will have in the warmer months, outside water. So I don't mind the investment. I just can't drink other stuff. I never understood scientifically how and why water is so important...but my gut knew. Fascinating stuff....

### Josh — 2014-01-28T19:38:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/redox-potential.9471/post-107189>

A word to the wise for which I may get my head handed to me, organisms cannot use amino acids such as Cysteine and other sulfur based amino acids without enough molybdenum. I could not handle pork, fermented vegetables and eggs without extra molybdenum. Manganese came in after that as well and then selenium and zinc. Methylation will not go very well period without molybdenum. THe Spectracell analysis may tell one enough about one's status, and I feel that evidence suggests that some people may need higher doses ata supplemental level to be happy methylators and users of sulfur.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/14972348](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/14972348)

[http://www.arthritistrust.org/wp-co.../Molybdenum-for-Candida-albicans-Patients.pdf](http://www.arthritistrust.org/wp-content/uploads/2013/03/Molybdenum-for-Candida-albicans-Patients.pdf)

### Inger — 2014-01-28T20:46:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/redox-potential.9471/post-107200>

My research about cysteine and glutathione has made me even more certain there is something very beneficial with raw... or very gently cooked. Especially RAW ANIMAL FOODS.

That is exactly what my own Nr=1 is too. And not only me. I know many who have healed in an amazing way starting to eat raw animal foods only. When I read about cysteine.. and amino acids etc. I do not wonder why anymore at all.

Why this is so much better than starting to play with supplements like NAC or other. I am not meaning to say they could not be useful, but I just do not get why so few are willing to try the raw animal food path... just for a while.. to see how they feel. I bet they will feel pretty amazing? Especially when eating much raw fat and raw seafood in addition.

I really start to wonder if eating a mostly all raw animal food diet increases your redox potential quite a bit! I know Peter is doing this... and he is doing great! Are there any other here that does this too? Audrey loves raw too and feels better on it...

I also do not think we must eat pork or veggies to get enough cysteine as it is plenty in beef and other animal food too.... and yes.. it is heat sensitive....

I slowly start to get an idea why I feel so freaking awesome on an almost 100% raw animal diet

I like the souce vide idea too... I get why it is getting popular! Must be pretty much healthier than regular cooked food.

I bet warmed up leftovers or otherwise 2 times heated foods must be the worst.. they are what makes me feel the worst too.. There are many cultures where they never do this. They always cook fresh.

### prAna303 — 2014-01-28T20:58:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/redox-potential.9471/post-107201>

> I really start to wonder if eating a mostly all raw animal food diet increases your redox potential quite a bit! I know Peter is doing this... and he is doing great! Are there any other here that does this too? Audrey loves raw too and feels better on it...
>
> I also do not think we must eat pork or veggies to get enough cysteine as it is plenty in beef and other animal food too.... and yes.. it is heat sensitive....
>
> I slowly start to get an idea why I feel so freaking awesome on an almost 100% raw animal diet

As you said, there is something magic, pure energy in eating raw seafoods. We have so many ideas spread around us that raw food, i mean raw animal food is bad and i think it is a way to do the complete other way around from the widespread thouths.

In winter i eat slow cooked pork, i enjoy the fats that everything is swimming around in, i feel i need it.

When i eat raw salmon i can easily do a nice little run in -10C in shorts only, i feel very warm but if i eat cooked i can feel some cold hitting my bones. Same goes when i bulk up on raw seafoods and go to sleep, slowly i get a warm feeling, like melting chocolate, creeping from my toes and hitting my head, boom i pass out into dreamland.

Been a really raw and fatty couple of months now and boy i must day that i feel more alive when sleeping that being awake, dreams are crazy good and detailed.

Not mostly raw, but I eat raw several times a week. Slowly building up.

### SeaHorse — 2014-01-28T21:29:05+0200

A little wave from me too...I try to eat something raw everyday, but not the bulk of my food. Broth with raw meat and fat is a particular fav. at the moment.

### Josh — 2014-01-28T21:30:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/redox-potential.9471/post-107205>

I have eaten raw vegan for a few years which felt great in some ways but has all of those downsides in our mad mad mad modern world. I am working up to the raw meat idea. Right now I just enjoy it cooked, but I either boil, steam or use a little IR oven from Black and Decker called the Infrawave which seems to do less damage. Raw and fermented make a great deal of sense as long as one can palate it and digest it. I have not tried the smoothie and clothespin trick that Inger uses yet.

I forgot that yes about half of my diet is raw soaked almonds, raw chocolate and raw fermented brassicas. I find blending the ferments with a little vinegar such as Fynbos from Rozendal ( my favorite vinegar in the world and I know the father of the woman with the farm and people who have been there and say that it is farming heaven...

oh my does not sound good Seahorse..... Too cold for outdoor CT?
You mean you cannot get the hole in the pond open/keep it open? I use an ax and a sledgehammer I hack it open every day. I love that work it is funny and gives me some nice outdoor time

If you do not want to do it... you could just roll in the snow? You really should do some outdoor CT it is amazing! I bet it will help your issues! No need to do long, just a minute or even less! My mom just do a few seconds.. and she still get huge benefits. Imagine that! But she does every morning. She dips in the ice hole... sometimes she cannot get it open in the morning as it has frozen too thick an ice on it in the night even if we keep it covered (I then hack it open during the day I just do a short hot shower followed by an ice cold finish in the mornings) and then she feel sorry she could not dip! lol

I know the ice dips, even if super short, helps against mold issues, inflammation... almost everything! So DO NOT GIVE IT UP  just do super short

### Inger — 2014-01-29T10:13:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/redox-potential.9471/post-107270>

Josh That vinegar you use sounds awesome! I often use unfiltered raw organic apple cider vinegar on my raw fish. Love it! I think raw vegan is not a great idea... hehe.. at least not for people living in the north! You really should try the opposite thing, raw high quality animal food + whatever wild carbs and greens you find in season where you live. I would be so curious to know what a difference you would feel.

I really really wonder... I wonder about so many things! :-o

Darn! I cannot get people to try it out because it is so freaking WEIRD (? I do not think so but I know most people do) and not looked at as "acceptable" in our culture as far as I am concerned. Which is pretty sad........

it is just.. we need to switch our minds around and think it is gooood! I know it works, we can learn to like many things we did not before

I do not get why people are willing to try a bunch of unnatural supps before trying the natural way. Maybe it is something I do not see. I do feel puzzled about this issue at times!

That said. Raw is not always the better choice maybe. I have tested to eat raw farmed salmon... and then precooked, prefrozen mussels from Denmark. Cold, not heated. The mussels made me feel better! So looks like wild foods are better even if gently cooked. At least the times I have tried it out the difference was pretty clear.
But canned fish even if wild was not so great. But it does was in tomato sauce/olive oil so it might have been that...... I like to test how foods affect me. It is very fun. MHS makes me feel very good! Also raw, fresh elk heart...

@ Sally, I do think raw meat is good for the gut too! At least my guts are very healthy... they work just perfect!  But I also always do the real squat!

@ Peter, I have dreams like you too... I dream so much every night lol life is good

I actually did my own bio hack today - accidentally really. I was treated to lunch by some very dear Canadian friends who
are passing thru Sydney. We were at a very famous Fish house on a bay overlooking the ocean with sailboats etc. gorgeous blues sky .... I guess I was seduced by it all and the beer.
I wanted a tasting plate and saw oysters and jumbo shrimp and smoke trout and salt and pepper squid ... and so I ordered it. There was also some jumbo stuffed shrimp battered and fried and some french fries.
I thought - lets go for it and see what happens...

within 15 minutes my whole body slowed right down - I could barely function ...like going from 100 - 0 in seconds. It was mesmerising - I really couldn't understand what was the matter with me. We were having such a great time so I wasn't really thinking redox potential or anything.... even tho we were talking about EMF etc. Brian is a Doctor who has retired and now assists in the operating room - so we were talking about health and the increase in diseases and healthcare etc.

I have had amazing energy for the longest time now ... really in super overdrive - but all of a sudden I was in slow motion and my brain was fuzzy and absolutely zero energy. I was even eating in slow motion.

I turned into a zombie .......

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-13T16:22:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/redox-potential.9471/post-109978>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=109886)
>
> aaahhhh - I had forgotten that I knew that....
>
> so transferring water from plastic to glass and then passing it thru a vector into glass is a reasonable solution?

I don't know about the latter part, but I'd avoid buying water in plastic bottles -- unless you're actually dehydrated and that's all there is or something.

These will have BPA in as a plastic-hardener. It's especially a problem if you, say, leave a bottle in a car or a window and it heats up. Then the hardener leaches faster. This is why this water tastes off sometimes.

I do sometimes buy bottled mineral water (not "spring water", which in UK jurisdiction is a term that's not controlled at law and so basically means nothing), but I only buy brands that come in glass -- Perrier, San Pellegrino, whatever ...

It's much harder to find, since supermarkets prefer to deal in plastic, it being lighter and pretty much shatterproof.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-07-11T06:49:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/redox-potential.9471/post-218446>

Salt (without fluoride) is worth it's weight in gold in patient with uncoupled haplotypes. Why you ask? People with uncoupled haplotypes with circadian mismatches have more poor ability to burn fat via beta oxidation because their mitochondria are not oscillating at 100Hz and are not coupled to the SCN oscillations properly to maintain the 100Hz alignment and the geometry of the IMJ's in mitochondria. Also remind yourself that mitochondria make H2O with CO2 as they burn fat fuels. So if you cannot burn fuels well your mitochondria will not make the water that eventually becomes your EZ water on the collagen back bone in the cytosol and this limit the size of the coherent domain (CD). This limits energy flux in a HUGE way in disease with mitochondrial dysfunction. These haplotypes also suffer from this effect in all cases and in some cases, these folks also have low Vitamin D3 levels because of an inability to make Vitamin D3 from LDL cholesterol because of poor isomerization which is a water dependent process. With nnEMF exposure it increases the intracellular dehydration effect and this would push the Vitamin D3 level lower and LDL cholesterol higher while also lowering hormone production from LDL cholesterol as well. This process requires T3 and Vitamin A. Vitamin A levels are destroyed by blue light toxicty via the retina. When photoreceptors are stimulated, the light-sensitive pigment in the retina, called 11-cis retinal which is Vitamin A compound, oxidizes to a light-insensitive form of Vitamin A called all-trans. This changes the photo signaling in the central retinal pathways to the pituitary and leptin receptor. Nobel Prize-winning work conducted 50 years ago demonstrated that all mammals use an enzyme pathway called the visual cycle to convert all-trans back to light-sensitive 11-cis retinal (Vit A). When scientists learned that multi-step enzymatic reactions like this often work slowly, it presented a mystery to them. How could the classic pathway replenish the 11-cis retinal fast enough in bright light (UV and blue in day light), which oxidizes the light-sensitive pigment much more quickly than dim light conditions?
UCLA researchers found that when stimulated by blue light, a phospholipid residing in rod and cone membranes, called N-ret-PE, undergoes conversion to 11-cis retinal without enzymes. It is activated by blue light. It is quantized to sunlight's amount of blue light during daylight and not man made light during dusk and night time. This is why Vitamin A deficits are linked to circadian disruption of blue light at night in diurnal mammals with photopic retinas. When you are blue light toxic you become very dehydrated as well. The RPE thickens as a result and BP and HR goes up. This stimulates the hypothalamus thirst center and the salt osmolarity center. Salt helps the brain off set the ICF and ECF dehydration from poor mitochondrial function. This means in a blue lit microwaved world you need a higher salt intake then you would think. As a result, you become less thirsty when you eat salt because your body is retaining water and as a result begins to be able to burn fat via beta oxidation because the water forms the MINOS layer around the respiratory proteins to bring them closer together. This change in geometry improves the inner mitochondrial oscillation closer to the key 100Hz frequency. In this way salt maybe assisting the calcium MCU pores in the mitochondria fine tuning the oscillations. Salt intake can leads to catabolic muscle mass loss, increased AMP and ADP levels in skeletal muscle which is a stimulus to AMPk pathways, and this can decreases cardiovascular energy expenditure and reduce cardiac mitochondrial reserve. It makes you more tightly coupled and helps affect your redox. [https://www.jci.org/articles/view/88532](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.jci.org%2Farticles%2Fview%2F88532&h=ATND_5GrmbQy8Y7ejSdfgek1pCGXqzMEXw9-USKk9fzjsrKSruQz1a9wc5ve7yYq_YzioEI6422nDzl-jrHdRcyUJ2yNRoKDJr0rVX5_GoP59fBcs7sWmgicdQ-g6kLNnJwH84gBBXAGAcNUAiAj_o5LxqmGODE)

### Cpt.Tired — 2017-11-10T19:44:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/redox-potential.9471/post-227750>

A dielectric is something that can separate positive and negative.

So if you have a dielectric blocker (like fluoride) in the water that you drink the water will lose some of its ability to charge separate. So your entire body becomes a weaker battery.

> In response to your question: Why were we putting salt in our water? I thought it was for treating dehydration. Like the way they use rehydration salts for people who have cholera(ie. people who lose buckets of water due to diarrhoea). The salts enter our cells and water follows by osmosis.
>
> I am also really confused as to why Dr. Kruse says drinking cold water is good when EZ water/structured water is structured from Infra-red light(aka heat)? Am I getting the context wrong(did I miss something?) Do you recall how he was talking about bone broths...and the heat was an external source of energy that could help structure the water.....

Ez water is formed right before the water freezes. If i remember correctly from Gerald Pollacks book. I think a certain wavelength of red light can help it grow also.

# Thread: Bone Broth

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

### healthman — 2014-01-28T11:39:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107132>

I was wondering how many times a week is recommended for a healthly person to have bone broth or people who are not so healthy how many times should they have broths?

### caroline — 2014-01-28T12:19:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107135>

I think the more the better .... a cup a day at least would be a good warm drink .... way better for us than coffee.

### Lahelada — 2014-01-28T12:26:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107136>

Looking at my coffee right now. Sorry no contest. I will have to do both  But as far as good for you you are absolutely right. I second what Caroline said a cup a day should be the minimum.

### Andrea — 2014-01-28T13:14:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107141>

Does the timing of bone broth consumption matter? I.e. would it be more beneficial to drink it in the evening than in the morning or vice versa?

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-28T13:31:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107143>

> [Rerun said:](/goto/post?id=107141)
>
> Does the timing of bone broth consumption matter? I.e. would it be more beneficial to drink it in the evening than in the morning or vice versa?

I should drink it when you eat meat, since it helps protein digestion.

Weston Price used to supervise special dinners for orphans and part of the meal would be good quality meat that he'd have the cooks grill (broil) rare. The meat would then be cut up and served in broth to try to make sure its food value was assimilated. Though he's dated in some things, it's amazing how much thought that man put into diet all those years ago. Nowadays the kids would have food laden with carbs and industrial seed oils thrown at them, and the contract would go to the cheapest suppliers and caterers.

### nicld — 2014-01-28T16:20:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107152>

I use it with thinly shaved raw beef or venison with some raw leeks and mushrooms. The hot broth slightly cooks the meat and is oh so yummy. I still have to make a great big pot this weekend and freeze a bunch.

### healthman — 2014-01-28T19:39:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107190>

Thanks for the answers everyone
The main reason I asked was because I thought I read and I may be wrong on this that one of Jacks blogs mentioned only using bone broths liberally!
So wanted to double check.

Thanks

### sooperb — 2014-01-28T19:45:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107191>

In place of coffee? With the butter and coconut oil

On a completely different subject, I made some coconut manna in my Vitamix today just to see if I could. It's sitting there looking delicious and it is quite sweet. Looking up coconut meat online it suggests that there is about 6 gs of sugar in it. Now I don't know whether to eat it or dump it. Thoughts please.

### cinnamon — 2014-01-28T21:27:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107203>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=107191)
>
> ...I made some coconut manna in my Vitamix today just to see if I could. It's sitting there looking delicious and it is quite sweet. Looking up coconut meat online it suggests that there is about 6 gs of sugar in it. Now I don't know whether to eat it or dump it. Thoughts please.

Is it just pure coconut? I buy something called "creamed coconut" that sounds like it would be the same thing. The nutrition label says it has 2g of carbs per tablespoon.

It is good melted with 100% chocolate to which you add flaked coconut, chopped nuts and cocoa nibs. Yummy!

_To make about six of these decadent treats:_
I put about a tsp of coconut oil in the pot to start the melting process. Use very low heat - the idea is to melt it, not to cook it. Add about 40g of chocolate and 3 tbsp of the creamed coconut. You can sweeten a little if you find the 100% chocolate too bitter. I put about a quarter teaspoon of xylitol and a drop of pure vanilla extract, but I suppose you could use a drop or two of stevia. After it melts, take it off the heat and allow to cool slightly before you throw in a handful of each of chopped raw nuts and cocoa nibs and about a half a cup of unsweetened flaked coconut. Drop by spoonful onto parchment paper, sprinkle with a little more flaked coconut and let set in the fridge.

WARNING - these can be addictive. It isn't necessarily a good idea to make a larger batch! LOL

### BJK77 — 2014-01-28T21:39:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107208>

How long do you cook your bone broth for? Is just a few hours enough or do we have to do 24 hours like the Weston Price foundation recommends?

### caroline — 2014-01-28T22:06:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107216>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=107136)
>
> Looking at my coffee right now. Sorry no contest. I will have to do both  But as far as good for you you are absolutely right. I second what Caroline said a cup a day should be the minimum.

yep - I do both ... can't seem to do without my BPC at the moment. I love what Nicole does with her bone broth!

### sooperb — 2014-01-29T14:46:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107301>

I have already thought of half a dozen ways to use it along those lines but obviously I don't want to spoil my eating plan

Bone broth cooked for 24 hours? There'd surely be nothing left, it would all evaporate unless you started off with a vat full? I give mine several hours but not even all day.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-29T15:17:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107302>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=107301)
>
> Bone broth cooked for 24 hours? There'd surely be nothing left, it would all evaporate unless you started off with a vat full? I give mine several hours but not even all day.

Some people talk of "24-hour bone broth". I don't know that the WAPF necessarily recommends that. Chefs seem to say around 2 hours (or even less) for fish stock and a few hours longer for meat bones.

This from Sally Fallon at the WAPF has recipes that talk of 6-8 hours for chicken and 12 or more for beef:

[http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/broth-is-beautiful](http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/broth-is-beautiful)

I believe you can get unpleasant flavours if you over-cook bones -- either with too long a time or too high a temperature or both. I have read something about the chemistry of what happens but don't recall. AFAIK, as with stews, it should really be low enough to _only just_ simmer.

Anyone who's interested, I know Sean Croxton's got a video of a chef demonstrating -- I think this must be it:

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JCLDGsw7BOw&feature=youtu.be](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JCLDGsw7BOw&feature=youtu.be)

### Andrea — 2014-01-29T15:49:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107304>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=107301)
>
> Bone broth cooked for 24 hours? There'd surely be nothing left, it would all evaporate unless you started off with a vat full? I give mine several hours but not even all day.

I make my bone broth in a pressure cooker...
I keep the safety valve open though, so there is only atmospheric pressure inside. The stove is on the lowest heat and the water in the pot is just about simmering. The lid is tight enough to prevent evaporation, other than the small amounts that leave through the valve. So I can have a batch on the stove for 24 hours - or more - with minimal water loss.

(And no, I don't kill food in my pressure cooker anymore, this is the only thing I use it for nowadays.)

### nonchalant — 2014-01-29T16:58:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107311>

I cook my bone broth gently over a series of days in a crock pot, cooling it at night. But I'll take samples from it daily. 2-3 days is average. The bones really come clean after a couple days.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-29T20:37:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107333>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=107302)
>
> Some people talk of "24-hour bone broth". I don't know that the WAPF necessarily recommends that. Chefs seem to say around 2 hours (or even less) for fish stock and a few hours longer for meat bones.
>
> This from Sally Fallon at the WAPF has recipes that talk of 6-8 hours for chicken and 12 or more for beef:
>
> [http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/broth-is-beautiful](http://www.westonaprice.org/food-features/broth-is-beautiful)
>
> I believe you can get unpleasant flavours if you over-cook bones -- either with too long a time or too high a temperature or both. I have read something about the chemistry of what happens but don't recall. AFAIK, as with stews, it should really be low enough to _only just_ simmer.

I just got home and looked up in my copy of _Understanding Cooking_ by Cesarani, Lundberg, and Kotschevar which I got off a relative who was a Chem. Eng. academic and who, as an extra, used to teach the chemistry of food to catering students.

Cesarani, Lundberg, and Kotschevar is very hot on technique and very science-informed. They say:

> To make 5 litres of good stock, use two kilogrammes of bones and meat, 6 litres of water and about Â½ kilogramme of chopped vegetables … mirepoix … \[and a\] bouquet garni.
>
> … the mirepoix is better added after the bones and meat have been cooked for about half the cooking period; otherwise the vegetables may give a slightly bitter flavour to the stock because of overcooking.
>
> Meat may be removed when tender and the bones left in to continue to cook. The meat is used for other dishes …
>
> Depending on the size of the bones and the way they're cut, a stock from meat and bones is simmered from 4 to 10 hours; from poultry 2 to 6 hours; and from fish 20 minutes. …
>
> If stock has extracted a proper amount of gelatine, it will gel when cold. …
>
> The best bones to use are those from young animals. These contain more gelatine and the marrow contains more flavouring ingredients. Shin and knuckle bones are best, with neck bones next. The bones are best cut into 4 inch lengths and split.

They add that you can reduce the stock after you've made it: this is a glaze.

So, yeah, while Sally is not saying 24 hours, the WAPF cooking times are over-long and out of line both with the practice of chefs and with what the food science people say to do. In particular, it seems fish bones shouldn't be simmered for very long at all. IIRC, the stock will taste pretty bad if you do.

Chefs nowadays also make a point of saying to cool the stock rapidly after you've made it. I don't think anyone bothered much in the old days. But, then, in the old days no-one in the catering trade had much of a clue about health -- chefs used to die very young from the fumes from solid-fuel burning stoves building up in unventilated basement kitchens, helped along by vast quantities of alcohol drunk to slake their thirst. It took Escoffier to change all that:

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/food/0/20123168r](http://www.bbc.co.uk/food/0/20123168r)

Obviously a warm gelatine solution is a pretty good breeding ground for goodness'-knows-what.

IIRC, Chef Lance in the Underground Wellness video links earlier shows how to cool a stock-pot rapidly.

### cinnamon — 2014-01-29T22:51:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-107346>

For beef broth, I will get it started on the stove and then move to the oven. I leave it in the same pot - a big stock pot with a lid. I leave the lid on and set the oven temperature for 200 degrees F or just slightly under. This should keep the stock simmering and not boiling. Last time I left it for about 30 hours. It was excellent. The only part of leaving it so long that I didn't like, was that it smells good all night long when I want to be sleeping and not thinking about food.

It was my understanding that it is best to just have a few bubbles coming to the surface and that the stock should NOT be boiling if you want good collagen levels. I'm not sure if that's just a conventional wisdom belief or if there might actually be some science behind it.

### Lahelada — 2014-02-07T18:19:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-108902>

Just making my bone broth. I am roasting the bones before using them but that is probably ,more of an epicurean instead of epigenetic thing  .A chef told me that the the broth tastes better. I add all the good herbs ,parsley for boron,onions for quercetin, turmeric for detox . I have not yet decided for how long I will cook it, but very probably 6 hours with a a bit of vinegar. Half an hour before the end of cooking time I add vegetables that have been roasted in the pan where the bones were roasting in.

### Gracelind — 2014-02-07T22:21:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.9473/post-108943>

Remember, as the water evaporates, you can always add more while simmering. Or you can refrigerate or freeze the concentrated broth and add water at serving. Or use the concentrated broth in another dish.

Some people keep the pot on the stove or the crockpot brewing, adding more water all week long to the same bones. The advantage to a long simmer would be leaching more of the minerals out of the bones. But it could be detrimental to the gelatin and raise glutamine (natural MSG) levels in the broth--good or bad depending on your personal context.
