# Thread: How not to do research
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-not-to-do-research.9474/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sooperb — 2014-01-28T16:28:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-not-to-do-research.9474/post-107154>

Not sure where to post this link but this article, promoting a TV programme epitomises how misguided some "research" can be:
[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/a...life.html?ito=email-healthnewsletter-20140128](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2546975/One-twin-gave-sugar-gave-fat-Their-experiment-change-YOUR-life.html?ito=email-healthnewsletter-20140128)

### nonchalant — 2014-01-28T16:39:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-not-to-do-research.9474/post-107156>

I think that the object of the typical "research" is to be able to write up a conclusion that would sound reasonable to someone with a smattering of science in their educational background. I'm not really sure why they don't just make everything up, it would be easier and fewer rats would have to die.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-28T21:52:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-not-to-do-research.9474/post-107212>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=107154)
>
> Not sure where to post this link but this article, promoting a TV programme epitomises how misguided some "research" can be:
> [http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/a...life.html?ito=email-healthnewsletter-20140128](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2546975/One-twin-gave-sugar-gave-fat-Their-experiment-change-YOUR-life.html?ito=email-healthnewsletter-20140128)

What I found disappointing was that he was repeating misconceptions that a modicum of research online in places like pubmed could have cleared up. Or if you're supposed to be looking at low carb diets, how about a phoning round people like Steve Phinney till you get pointed in the right direction? They're making a TV programme for goodness' sake: it's not as if people who know about these things won't talk to them.

He was also extrapolating in a hurry from his own (brief and probably badly set-up) experience of a ketogenic diet. Does he suppose whole civilisations have been troubled by poor mental performance, lackadaisical physical performance, and constipation?

This is interesting:

> One of the words you hear a lot when people talk about very low-carb diets is ketosis. This is where your body makes chemicals called ketones, which can act as fuel for the brain, which can't use fat. But they're not great brain food. While I wasn't distracted by hunger for the month, I felt thick-headed, and this was most evident in a stock trading competition with Chris.

He knows enough to have heard about ketones. He doesn't know enough to know you have to adapt to them.

I think he needs to know a lot more than he does know. He's also not aware of information from many different sources. He needs to become aware of that and to synthesise the information. So he should ask himself:

1\. If ketosis is not a normal healthful metabolic state, why do babies spend so much time in it?

2\. How have cultures like the Eskimo and the Plains Indians managed on diets with very little, at times no, carbohydrate in?

3\. How did the Fur Trade -- the equivalent of a billion dollar multinational business in the 18th and 19th centuries -- manage to run with the _voyageurs_ living off pemmican while carrying out incredibly arduous physical tasks?

4\. Why have sports teams -- for example the Australian Cricket Team -- switched to a low-carb diet, if it's so bad for physical performance:

[http://www.thelivinlowcarbshow.com/...kner-2013-low-carb-down-under-seminar-series/](http://www.thelivinlowcarbshow.com/shownotes/9196/759-dr-peter-brukner-2013-low-carb-down-under-seminar-series/)

(There's at least one Australian Rules Football club doing the same; and I'll bet you there are football teams in England, both Rugby Football ("rugger") and Association Football ("soccer"), teams either already doing this or looking into it.)

5\. Why is there a tradition going back thousands of years of the practice of fasting in various religions, since that gets you into ketosis, if not because it helps with calmness and mental acuity:

> Howbeit this kind goeth not out but by prayer and fasting.

[http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+17:21&version=KJV](http://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+17%3A21&version=KJV)

(Going a bit off the point, but while I'm a religious sceptic, I'm also too sceptical to dismiss that kind of thing. I actually know two different people, an Anglican priest (before he was ordained) and a nurse, who both described something similar. In each case they were dealing with an very disturbed and angry person, and each said they "felt" something come out of the person and "pass through" them. In the first case the man sort of slumped afterwards; in the second, the nurse said the patient then became tractable. Neither had attempted to _do_ anything; these incidents just happened. Maybe these were demons -- or call it a kind energy or whatever you want -- but _something_ unusual was obviously going on.

And maybe someone functioning really well at a mental level and with the biological basis for a calm, steady energy from fasting could be more aware of dimensions of existence that pass most of us by.)

6\. Where would people in northern Europe, where he's living, be getting carbohydrate from all year round before the advent of farming -- before, indeed, modern container shipping? No mesolithic hunter could eat gigantic strawberries and over-bred super-sweet peaches at any time of the year let alone during February in England.

To sum up: there's a lot of questions he's not asking himself, and a lot of information he's not become aware of and not tried to synthesise. What he's saying just doesn't make sense in the wider picture. Therefore, he should realise that he can't be right.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Smart meterâ€¦..  or not?â€¦...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/smart-meter%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6-or-not-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6.9477/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tanya — 2014-01-28T18:38:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/smart-meter%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6-or-not-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6.9477/post-107177>

We had a new digital electric meter installed without our knowledge. When I discovered that I called our utility company and asked if it was a smart meter. Their response was that it was not. They described it to me as AMR (automatically read meters). They are equipped to be read remotely, by radio signal, but currently our state laws prevents them from doing so. Therefore, they utilize their vehicles from which their employee activates our meter and obtains the numbers this way. Looks like it is the same situation with all of my nearby neighbors. Am I wrong to think that it is a smart meter, then. My husband thinks that there canâ€™t be that much exposure from them doing it once a month. What do you guys think? Any opinions and suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

### Hope — 2014-01-28T18:48:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/smart-meter%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6-or-not-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6.9477/post-107179>

I would venture to guess that unless the wireless part is actually turned off....there could be radiation. It's just like a cellphone, I would guess...unless you put it in airplane mode, it's sending signals. So perhaps if it's turned off....

I wouldn't give up getting to the bottom of this

### nonchalant — 2014-01-28T19:25:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/smart-meter%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6-or-not-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6.9477/post-107187>

Interesting way of getting around the state law.

### Tanya — 2014-01-29T22:18:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/smart-meter%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6-or-not-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6.9477/post-107342>

I am somewhat relieved to learn that our AMR (automatic meter reader) meter is not in a smart meter category. It is an older technology that only collects our electrical energy consumption and has only one-way communication with the utility to provide them with the numbers. That communication is activated by the company’s driver once a month only and it takes less then 10 sec. for the entire process. Obviously, I would have preferred to keep our old meter instead of this one, but at least it is not as bad as I feared.... Besides, what good would it do me to start this fight with the utility while all of my neighbors are perfectly satisfied with their new meters? If I am missing something here, please enlighten me.....

### cinnamon — 2014-01-29T23:19:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/smart-meter%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6-or-not-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6.9477/post-107352>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=107342)
>
> \[snip\]but at least it is not as bad as I feared.... Besides, what good would it do me to start this fight with the utility while all of my neighbors are perfectly satisfied with their new meters? If I am missing something here, please enlighten me.....

Hi Tanya,

Were you able to get a definitive reply to what Hope referred to below?

> [Hope said:](/goto/post?id=107179)
>
> I would venture to guess that unless the wireless part is actually turned off....there could be radiation. It's just like a cellphone, I would guess...unless you put it in airplane mode, it's sending signals. So perhaps if it's turned off....

It may well be true that the only time the meter transmits **data** is during the 10 seconds each month that the driver requests it. It is possible that the meter is actually transmitting pulses (but not your usage data) all the time if the wi-fi capability is actually on. It's like if you have a wi-fi router but no one is on the internet - it still pulses all the time it's on. It might be in a default setting to transmit - so that it's ready whenever the truck might drive by? What does the driver have to do to "read" the meter?

Still, this might be better than the traditional smart meter where all the meters are pulsing to each other in a "mesh net" all the time, blanketing the whole area with EMF smog. \*shudder\*

### Jack Kruse — 2014-01-29T23:23:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/smart-meter%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6-or-not-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6.9477/post-107353>

I'd break it off.......or I would call the State Atty office and make them aware they are breaking the state law and I would call the local news station and make their consumer law reporters aware. Utilities are tied to public votes......never forget that.

### Tanya — 2014-01-31T17:59:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/smart-meter%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6-or-not-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%C2%A6.9477/post-107592>

Many thanks to all of you for your help! In the spirit of a Chinese New Year today, my fortune cookie tells me to “seek and you find the answer”. Seems like I need to find and dust off my fighting gloves first… So, to be continued....

# Thread: Seaweed
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tanya — 2014-01-28T18:52:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107180>

I try to eat as much seaweed as possible, but I seldom use it in its rehydrated form..with the exception of putting it in my bone broth. Most of the time I add it to my already prepared dishes or just eat it straight out of the bag. I wonder now if I am still getting all the benefits from doing so...

### sooperb — 2014-01-28T19:18:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107184>

Good question Tanya, I have a packet of Wakame and some dried sea vegetables and had wondered exactly the same. I liked them straight from the packet and once or twice added them to a salad with prawns and really enjoyed them. I keep forgetting I have them, usually remember after I've finished.

### Tanya — 2014-01-29T18:14:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107322>

sooperb, as much as I like the crunchy wakame, from now on I am going to try to use much more of it being pre-soaked. I tried to do it briefly this morning, just for like 15 min. and I was astonished just how much water it had absorbed. So, I can only imagine what a dehydrating effect it was doing to me in that dry form. I did not like the taste of the water from it, though and so, I just added it to my broth.....

### nicld — 2014-01-29T22:18:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107341>

Good question. I add dried seaweed to my fish broth when I make my seafood bisque. It does make it take like the ocean.

### Tanya — 2014-01-29T22:22:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107343>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=107341)
>
> Good question. I add dried seaweed to my fish broth when I make my seafood bisque. It does make it take like the ocean.

At least it gets somewhat dehydrated in your fish broth. I was just eating boatloads of it completely dry. I know better now….

### caroline — 2014-01-30T03:04:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107394>

So - is that the case? don't eat it dry??? what about sushi then?

Should we be adding it to our drinking water? isn't that what Inger does?

I am totally confused about water .....

### Inger — 2014-01-30T10:32:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107425>

Jack does have said fresh seaweed is best... then comes dehydrated.

So I figure when you soak the raw dehydrated seaweed it will be a bit closer? I mostly make seaweed water and drink it and then puree the seaweed after a day or two with some more water and drink it too, it is nice, like a green smoothie.

I do use Dulce in my raw fish meals, I cut it with a scissor in small slices and mix it with the raw fish, I love that. But Dulce is not good for making soaked seaweed me thinks. The best for that are Wakame, Kombu, Sea Spaghetti. The Dulce do not really get soft. So I use different seaweeds a little different way.

I never cook my seaweed, only if I add it to broth. Otherwise I see no idea in cooking them, really. I think it might make them less healthy. That is why I also buy raw dried seaweeds, not roasted or dried with high heat. I really can taste a difference with different quality of seaweeds. The french, raw dried seaweeds are by far the best I have had so far.

That seaweed water made from high quality, raw dried seaweeds is addictive, crazy yummy!

### Tanya — 2014-01-31T18:10:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107593>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=107394)
>
> So - is that the case? don't eat it dry??? what about sushi then?
>
> Should we be adding it to our drinking water? isn't that what Inger does?
>
> I am totally confused about water .....

caroline, I don’t think you need to worry about your sushi consumption., unless of course you eat a ton of it... I myself was definitely overdoing with wakame, so much so that I noticed additional signs of dryness in myself that were not there before. It made me wonder why - since I was doing everything else correctly. So, the last few days after my small experiment has proven to me where was my mistake.

### sooperb — 2014-01-31T18:16:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107594>

Interesting  I mean to add it, I just forget. I need to eat more seafood I think, I manage atlantic prawns, they're not raw but much tastier than the larger prawns I used to buy. I've even eaten raw oysters once or twice, I couldn't say I like them but I can swallow them which is more than I thought I'd be able to.

### Josh — 2014-01-31T20:25:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107609>

I figured out that the only way I would reliably consume it was if I blended it. When I steam vegetables or meats, I put it in the water or around the meat or vegetables and then blend it with the water at the end and drink as broth. I also put it in my fermented vegetables such as kimchi and sauerkraut and then blend them with some water as a drink as well. It all does a lot smoother and brings out interesting tastes. I guess you could soak and blend any type raw as well. Probably will try that too.

### Josh — 2014-01-31T20:31:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-107612>

For Caroline the confused, have you watched Gerald Pollack's video and looked at his powerpoint?

[http://www.waterconf.org/participan...ald H. Pollack Bulgaria water and health.pptx](http://www.waterconf.org/participants-materials/presentations/Gerald%20H.%20Pollack%20Bulgaria%20water%20and%20health.pptx)

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=79t-RNEim0s](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=79t-RNEim0s)

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=h4D\_7Cw8aT8](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=h4D_7Cw8aT8)

They are fun and EZ. I tested them on my partner and she got a lot out of it with no explanation.

### Tanya — 2014-02-12T17:48:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-109795>

I need to slow down, really slow down with my sea weed consumption…...[http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2013/08/21/superfood-seaweed-health-benefits\_n\_3786076.html](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2013/08/21/superfood-seaweed-health-benefits_n_3786076.html)

### Josh — 2014-02-12T18:34:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-109798>

There is enough iodine in some varieties to give my wife and I iodine excess symptoms. Typically we need more selenium to handle the higher iodine content seaweeds. As the article notes, it varies a bit from variety to variety and I have seen this in my own usage. Dr. Kharrazian emphasizes how people with Hashimoto's are better off never having a larger than trace dose of iodine as it may trigger AI issues. It has been discussed elsewhere on the forum. Many people are very careful with "supplements" and ignore similar issues with "food".

### Tanya — 2014-02-12T18:59:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-109803>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=109798)
>
> There is enough iodine in some varieties to give my wife and I iodine excess symptoms. Typically we need more selenium to handle the higher iodine content seaweeds. As the article notes, it varies a bit from variety to variety and I have seen this in my own usage. Dr. Kharrazian emphasizes how people with Hashimoto's are better off never having a larger than trace dose of iodine as it may trigger AI issues. It has been discussed elsewhere on the forum. Many people are very careful with "supplements" and ignore similar issues with "food".

I completely agree with what you are saying, Josh. I assumed that I was not lacking in selenium since I was already supplementing it and eating brazil nuts in addition to that. But I went so overboard on seaweed, and that was on top of 2 seafood meals a day, that I was having. I just kept ignoring the fact that my temperature kept getting lower and lower. That was not very smart of me... I checked a wakame package this morning and surprise, surprise - iodine was not even listed in there.

### Ken23 — 2014-05-30T17:55:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-128934>

Tanya, I'm noticing a similar reaction when I up my seaweed. Hands and feet become much colder. Not sure if this means I have Hashi. Did you cut back on the seaweed or up your selenium?

### Tanya — 2014-05-31T03:21:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-128986>

> [Ken23 said:](/goto/post?id=128934)
>
> Tanya, I'm noticing a similar reaction when I up my seaweed. Hands and feet become much colder. Not sure if this means I have Hashi. Did you cut back on the seaweed or up your selenium?

I am still experimenting with my thyroid situation, Ken, but I most definitely cut back on the amount of seaweed I was having before and I also went off my selenium supplement altogether as I felt that I was getting enough of it in my food.

Ken, I don’t know how much of seaweed you are having, but I believe that it is so easy to overdose on it and you can harm your thyroid that way.

### Ken23 — 2014-05-31T19:17:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed.9478/post-129052>

Hmm, prob will try seaweed every other day and see how that goes. Iv been having it with my non fish meals

# Thread: 23andme testing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-testing.9479/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nicld — 2014-01-28T18:55:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-testing.9479/post-107181>

Ok, I got my 23andme results back and ran them through the MTHFRSupport app and if anyone has any insight into what they mean I would greatly appreciate it. I will be trying for a consult with Dr Tim to decipher them a little more maybe next month as will as doing more research on the results. These are all the ones below are ones that showed up red.

**DETOX**
CYP1B1 L432V
NAT1 A803G
SOD2
SOD2 A16V
CTH S4031I
RARA

**Allergy Mold**
No red but both HLA yellow

**IgE**
No reds

**IgG**
FCGR2A
IRF5

**CLOTTING FACTORS**
CEPT
ITGB3 T196C

**METHYLATION**
BHMT-08
COMT H62H
COMT V158M
MAO A R297R
MTHFD1 C105T
MTHFR C677T
NOS2 (BOTH)

**MITOCHONDRIAL FUNCTION**
ONLY 3 YELLOWS, REST GREEN

**OTHER IMMUNE FACTORS**
ATG16L1 (I think this one is for Chrohns)

**SULFONOTRANSFERASE**
SULT1A1

I will work on getting a summary of the stuff that 23andme provided but I do remember that I am 99.7% of European ancestry (duh).

It also said that I did not have a genetic risk of celiac but I do have it. Which makes one wonder how much more power environment has over genetics (huge I think).

Thanks

Nicole

### nonchalant — 2014-01-28T19:46:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23andme-testing.9479/post-107192>

The MTHFR support app gives a spreadsheet. You can click on one column in that spreadsheet to open a browser window to some relevant research on SNPedia.

Yes, my problem with 23andme is that they don't seem to realize that genes can be activated or deactivated based on your environment.

Just discovered this amazing source for water storage....wow. here is what what they do accg to Dr L Williams on her blog post about water....

These violet bottles _**filter out all degrading light frequencies, allowing only healthy UV(a) and far infrared waves inside that help restructure the deformed microclusters in water that have been damaged from turbulent plumbing systems, light, and chemical**_s. Used in ancient Egypt for preserving food, this amazing biophotonic glass makes even your filtered water more vital and â€œwetter,â€￾ providing **proper intercellular hydration** and therefore translating into less of a need to consume excessive water. In fact, the 500 ml Violiv water bottle is the equivalent of a little more than 2 8-ounce glasses of water â€“ just enough to quench your thirst while youâ€™re out and about.

~ that's like exactly what Dr K just talked about in the webinar and here. Biophotonic glass. Wow.

### Josh — 2014-01-28T19:27:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-glass-bottles-that-help-restructure-water-wow.9480/post-107188>

### Josh — 2014-01-28T20:10:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/amazing-glass-bottles-that-help-restructure-water-wow.9480/post-107195>

Functionality

The sun emits visible and invisible light parts. The visible spectrum becomes discernable to our eyes when white light is channeled through a glass prism. At the same time though, light also contains a part which is invisible to our eyes and lies in the radius of electromagnetic wavelengths of infra red and UV-A.

Sunlight enables all plants to grow. If they continue to be exposed to the sun after reaching maturity, the effect of the light changes and accelerates the molecular decaying process. Miron violet glass works like a natural filter that only lets the sunlight that protects and improves the quality of premium and sensitive substances.

There is a large spectrum of packaging materials available nowadays, of which many are permeable to visible light. The following graphics show to which degree light can penetrate the different materials: (go to link below)

Miron Violet glass blocks the complete spectrum of visible light with the exception of the violet part.
At the same time it allows a certain part to be permeable for radiation in the spectral range of UV-A, and infra red light. This unique combination offers optimal protection against the ageing processes that are released by visible light, thus lengthening durability and potency of products.

In order to substantiate these advantages, Miron Violet Glass AG has conducted a series of tests themselves and in cooperation with scientific institutions.
Further confirmation of functionality comes from the science of biophotonics

[http://www.miron-glas.com/en/Functionality](http://www.miron-glas.com/en/Functionality)

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### healthman — 2014-01-28T21:47:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose-question.9482/post-107211>

Can anyone advise on the best form of d ribose to get?
I see life extension and jarrow formulas offer powder, veggie caps and chewable tablets?
Any help appreciated
Thanks

### Shijin13 — 2014-01-28T21:52:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose-question.9482/post-107213>

I use life extensions. I've had the best success with it... but other have had success with other stuff too

### Josh — 2014-01-28T22:01:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose-question.9482/post-107214>

If one reads the label, most supplement companies are using Bioenergy s Ribose including Jarrow, NOW and LEF. I suspect that Nutrabulk which sells it for less is also, but they are not controlling the moisture as well so it comes clumpy. It is very hygroscopic. It gets a lot cheaper as you buy larger quantities. Bioenergy starts at 10kg boxes for wholesale accounts at 47/kg delivered.

[http://www.bioenergyribose.com/?utm...ret\_Billy&utm\_campaign=NI.com\_Website\_Jan2014](http://www.bioenergyribose.com/?utm_source=NutraIngredients.com&utm_medium=Leaderboard&utm_content=Secret_Billy&utm_campaign=NI.com_Website_Jan2014)

It is a sweetner like sugar that dissolves in water and just about anything including your mouth really well. No need to pay people to encapsulate it.

### Tanya — 2014-01-28T22:06:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose-question.9482/post-107215>

I also used to buy Life extensions brand which I liked very much, but later I switched to Healthy Origins and I don't see any difference, except for the price. healthman, I think with the powder, you don't get any additives, it is much better absorbable, and you can have more flexibility as to where and how you want to use it. For traveling purposes, I would use chewable tablets. Just my opinion.

### persistence — 2014-03-12T05:39:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose-question.9482/post-114953>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=107214)
>
> If one reads the label, most supplement companies are using Bioenergy s Ribose including Jarrow, NOW and LEF. I suspect that Nutrabulk which sells it for less is also, but they are not controlling the moisture as well so it comes clumpy. It is very hygroscopic. It gets a lot cheaper as you buy larger quantities. Bioenergy starts at 10kg boxes for wholesale accounts at 47/kg delivered.
>
> [http://www.bioenergyribose.com/?utm...ret\_Billy&utm\_campaign=NI.com\_Website\_Jan2014](http://www.bioenergyribose.com/?utm_source=NutraIngredients.com&utm_medium=Leaderboard&utm_content=Secret_Billy&utm_campaign=NI.com_Website_Jan2014)

### fitness@home — 2014-01-28T23:06:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-bad-idea-technology-gone-wrong.9484/post-107227>

[http://news.discovery.com/tech/apps/futuristic-bra-only-opens-for-true-love-140127.htm](http://news.discovery.com/tech/apps/futuristic-bra-only-opens-for-true-love-140127.htm)

Well, the Japanese have done it again. Apparently this tech-laden bra made by the lingerie company Ravijour will only unhook for â€œtrue love.â€
[**Stealth Clothing Averts Government Snoopers: Photos**](http://news.discovery.com/tech/gear-and-gadgets/stealth-clothing-averts-government-snoopers-pictures-130118.htm)
The â€œTrue Love Testerâ€ bra canâ€™t be masterfully unhooked by some skeevy player who hit on the wearer at a club. No, this bra only comes undone when sensors embedded inside it that are connected wirelessly to a smartphone app detect [**a particular heart rate**](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B8Wd831gUt4) (video, safe for work).
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

According to the Victoriaâ€™s Secret-like company that made the bra, [**Ravijour**](http://www.ravijour.com/), a particular heart rate over time indicates â€œlove.â€ And what do you know, they even have a graph comparing the effects of jogging, shopping, eating spicy food and watching a horror movie with â€œflirtingâ€ and â€œsurprise giftâ€ on a ladyâ€™s heart. What better way to acknowledge being â€œin loveâ€ than having your glittery bra fly open?
BuzzFeedâ€™s Rachel Zarrell put the whole thing into [**a series of awesome GIFs**](http://www.buzzfeed.com/rachelzarrell/this-bra-that-only-unhooks-for-true-love-is-basically-a-chas) to illustrate the ridiculousness of it all. Ravijourâ€™s slogan is â€œWe do anything for women.â€ Apparently they do anything to free the girls, too.
[**Bra Dryer Gets Delicates Ready Fast**](http://news.discovery.com/human/bra-dryer-gets-delicates-ready-fast-130520.htm)
Might not want to wear this thing around in public, though. The dude-designed bra cups pop open pretty dramatically.

# Thread: Opening oyster shells the Mesolithic way
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/opening-oyster-shells-the-mesolithic-way.9489/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-01-29T10:45:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/opening-oyster-shells-the-mesolithic-way.9489/post-107275>

This is very short but to the point. It also has two really nice photos -- a massive blow-up of an oyster shell at the top of the page and oyster shells on a fire lower down.

> The experiments were carried out by archaeologists Esben Kannegaard from Jutland Museum and SÃ¸ren H. Andersen, professor emeritus from the University of Aarhus. â€œYou do not need to use force or any special technique to open an oyster, a brief warming is enough,â€￾ said the archaeologists in the latest issue of the journal _Skalk_.
>
> The background to the experiment was to explain the huge quantities of oyster, mussel and cockle shells found in famous midden sites which are known from ErtebÃ¸lleruten, where there are thick layers dated to c. 6000 years ago. These kitchen middens contain no evidence of tools required to open oyster shells, which would need to be be thin, strong and flexible. In addition, the shells show no damage or evidence of tool use but occasionally exhibit slight traces of exterior scorching. ...
>
> The amount of shells left behind also shows that oysters were not an insignificant supplement to general sustenance but a common food source for our ancestors.

[http://www.pasthorizonspr.com/index.php/archives/01/2014/opening-oysters-shells-the-mesolithic-way](http://www.pasthorizonspr.com/index.php/archives/01/2014/opening-oysters-shells-the-mesolithic-way)

### HoneyChild — 2014-01-29T13:42:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/opening-oyster-shells-the-mesolithic-way.9489/post-107296>

I remember watching a wildlife documentary where some monkeys had worked out how to open shellfish. They basically just hit the shells against a tree and eventually the mollusc got tired and the shell opened.

Although not all of the monkeys had the patience to do this.

I saw this quite a few years ago and can't remember what kind of monkeys they were or even what country they were in.

# Thread: Does ketosis make you high?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ketosis-make-you-high.9495/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### madamechickendance — 2014-01-30T01:39:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ketosis-make-you-high.9495/post-107370>

I have some novel euphoria that I can't pin on anything other than metabolism. It's accompanied by a bit of a headache in the forehead, temples, behind the eyes.

Has anyone else experienced this in ketosis?

### madamechickendance — 2014-01-30T01:41:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ketosis-make-you-high.9495/post-107371>

It's almost like I'm over-stimulated. That's the feeling. What happens to the adrenal system in ketosis?

### nicld — 2014-01-30T03:53:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ketosis-make-you-high.9495/post-107403>

Very interesting and good questions. I can't seem to get into ketosis since pretty sure my redox sucks.

### nonchalant — 2014-01-30T04:14:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ketosis-make-you-high.9495/post-107405>

I experienced a "high" that lasted about a month when getting off the SAD diet and eating animal fats and meats.
As for the headache, I recommend drinking more water.

### caroline — 2014-01-30T05:29:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ketosis-make-you-high.9495/post-107413>

One day I had what I would consider a ketone high ..... but when I really went over my day - I had had a new coffee and I think it had a ton of caffeine. I couldn't sleep that night either. It hasn't happened since. I won't use that coffee again.

### Inger — 2014-01-30T10:20:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ketosis-make-you-high.9495/post-107424>

I would say. oh yes!

I do feel euphoria almost daily... IDK if I am in ketosis, I eat pretty much protein but a load of fat too. I do not have headaches tho and I think they are because of dehydration.
Maybe when you give your body the right fuel you just get those euphoric feelings because it gets so happy ?

Yesterday I was out in the beautiful, sunny -8 degree C weather. I just stood in the backyard looking into the sky and the sun and those huge old firs surrounding me. I was so close they touched my face. I could smell them, and the sun. The sky was so blue and the firs were so green, the sun was shining on them and the sun was so intense, I love the winter sun.
As I stood there and enjoyed it all.. it become so intense and euphoric I felt almost like I melted into the universe and the firs! Huh it was a very intense feeling... very very joyful. I swear I had had no drugs whatsoever, zero - not even coffee! All I had that day was raw meat and raw coconut oil and green tea.

### Marie Negrila — 2014-04-20T15:11:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ketosis-make-you-high.9495/post-122672>

> [madamechickendance said:](/goto/post?id=107370)
>
> I have some novel euphoria that I can't pin on anything other than metabolism. It's accompanied by a bit of a headache in the forehead, temples, behind the eyes.
>
> Has anyone else experienced this in ketosis?

I experience them as well. I take it as a sign that I am indeed reconnecting with my primal instinct. It is also a result of detox. Try to see if you can observe yourself during those moments and experience the true feelings of these moments.

As for the headache, it is often the bodies initial response to the switch...a more ketonic state of the body. For that I have seen Dr. K suggest 400 mg of magnesium and adjust the dose until the headaches are gone.

Dehydration might be an issue also. As far as that is concerned, I have heard Dr. K suggest to take anywhere between 1-1.5 gallons of water daily, in some cases I have even heard to take up to 2 gallons daily.

Hope this helps eve though you have posted a while ago. If not you at least someone else who might experience what you have.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-20T17:49:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-ketosis-make-you-high.9495/post-122690>

# Thread: Should I be doing the Leptin Rx ... or for how long?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-be-doing-the-leptin-rx-or-for-how-long.9496/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sandra — 2014-01-30T02:06:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-be-doing-the-leptin-rx-or-for-how-long.9496/post-107376>

I am trying to get all of this straight in my head, but the more I read, more more questions I have.

I have been eating a Paleo diet for around 12 months. I have no cravings for carbs or sugar and generally eat protein (mainly grass fed beef) with vegetables (2-3 meals a day), rarely fruit, and some nuts and seeds. Lots of RO water, about 4-5L a day (about 1-1.3Gallons). I had been snacking a little - not every day and not all day - until my mate Nick told me how bad that was (even if it was on a vegetable stick or coconut bark, so I've cut that out in the last month or so too. Height 165 cm (5ft 5in) and 65 kg (143 pounds). Rarely drink alcohol - if it is, it's a glass of red wine with dinner, and that might be once a fortnight. Have been sleeping on a magnetico mattress for about 2 months. I'm in my garden regularly, barefoot. And I wear blue-blocker glasses at night (everyday for the last 5 days and prior to that a little less regularly over that last 3 months).

I read "My Leptin Prescription" JUNE 29, 2011 [http://www.jackkruse.com/my-leptin-prescription/](http://www.jackkruse.com/my-leptin-prescription/) and it said **"1. First make sure you really are Leptin resistant (LR) to begin with"**. That's where I first come unstuck as I don't know for sure ... I don't look overweight and my doctor won't order the reverse T3 test unless there is an issue with my general thyroid test (should get the results of that back today). I also asked for a cortisol test, which they also didn't do, so I think I'm going to get this done via a naturopath as I feel like I'm flying a bit blind.

Then in "**5\. Signs that you are becoming Leptin Sensitive (LS) again**", these are listed ...

Men will notice quick weight loss. _**IRRELEVANT TO ME**_

Women will notice mood changes first (calmer/sleepy) and their sleep will improve (huge clue). Their clothes will fit differently but weight may not change drastically initially because of effects on the pituitary. This will change too if they continue moving forward. _**MY CLOTHES ALREADY FIT BETTER SINCE STARTING PALEO AND I SLEEP FINE**_

You will notice a change in your sweating pattern. _**NOT SURE WHAT THIS MEANS - DON'T SWEAT THAT MUCH ANYWAY**_

You will notice you have better recovery from exercise and your energy levels seem to have risen. _**DON'T DO STRENUOUS EXERCISE (SOME SWIMMING AND SOMETIMES WALKING), ENERGY LEVELS ARE WHAT I AM HAVING ISSUES WITH**_

Your hunger is gone and so are your cravings. _**HAS BEEN FINE SINCE STARTING PALEO**_

When you awaken you will feel very refreshed like you slept well. _**I FEEL I SLEEP WELL BUT I HARDLY EVER FEEL REFRESHED**_

Generally when the signs are all present, I then really push HIIT exercise with heavy weights. _**HAVEN'T EVER LIFTED HEAVY WEIGHTS**_

So, if I'm not actually Leptin Resistant, will it matter if I do the Leptin Rx??? Or because I have tiredness and energy issues, does it mean that I am still Leptin Resistant anyway ... or perhaps there other issues at play?

I am going to read up on CT next and I'm thinking of starting face dunking to see if that makes any difference to my tiredness. Is that a good idea? Or am I maybe jumping in too quickly in the absence of any lab results?

Thanks for your advice

### caroline — 2014-01-30T02:35:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-be-doing-the-leptin-rx-or-for-how-long.9496/post-107381>

C/T is hugely important ... so yes ...start with face dunks!

Don't worry so much! .....keep doing the leptin reset for awhile...

don't forget about the C/T webinar tomorrow!

### Sandra — 2014-01-30T02:39:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-be-doing-the-leptin-rx-or-for-how-long.9496/post-107383>

... off to read about face dunking ...

It's just the more I read the more I wonder if I really do have a problem that just hasn't surfaced yet, so I want to sort it out right now ... patience required I guess

### caroline — 2014-01-30T02:45:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-be-doing-the-leptin-rx-or-for-how-long.9496/post-107384>

You need to know where your hormones and cortisol and DHEA are at .... ASI....

and melatonin ..... They should be done at the same time apparently

### Rick — 2014-04-11T15:12:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-be-doing-the-leptin-rx-or-for-how-long.9496/post-120967>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=107381)
>
> C/T is hugely important ... so yes ...start with face dunks!
>
> Don't worry so much! .....keep doing the leptin reset for awhile...
>
> don't forget about the C/T webinar tomorrow!

Hi Caroline, well u said to ask questions and I am so right there with Sandra, the OP above - Why does Jack harp on determining if one is LR first because you seem to be saying, 'just do it and don't worry about it', am I right? What is the harm in doing the LR if one is LS, has Jack ever elaborated on that?

As a holistic nutrition student I am hell bent on learning to apply Jack's leptin info because I firmly believe he is spot on about the importance and pervasiveness of this hormone in relation to human health and my master's program is sorely lacking in this area; so, I need to get this science right on my own!

I apologize in advance for harping on this ancient subject myself, since Jack and everyone else have moved onto redox potential, QB and EMF topics. I am way behind the group, but I need help deciphering the blog and forum format of learning as for me it is all very confusing, contradictory and hard to follow - like Sandra mentioned, the more I read, the more confused I get and the threads just seem to lead me around in circles, so I need help and guidance to navigate these subjects.

Anyway here goes:

Naturally I set out to prove these ideas on myself with labs and he had written that 90% of the time the LR patient will present with his big three markers: hs-CRP>= 1 mg/L, elevated cortisol and a FT3/rT3 less than 20. Well I did not, hs-CRP = 0.71, free and reverse T3 ratio was 34:1 and diurnal cortisol was very low.

When these labs were drawn I was 187 lbs at 5'10" so by the BMI I would be LR but here's the thing, Jack wrote in a very early leptin blog that IL-6, TNF-alpha, and NF-kappa-beta all start to rise at a BMI of 20-24 which CW says is within the "normal" weight range. At my height I would have to weigh less than 140 to get below a BMI of 20, yes? Not sure what he means by this statement at all...

Of course there is Jack's mirror test but I am unclear on proving how someone's weight translates to > 25-30 lbs of just visceral fat as opposed to total body fat. Compared to what measurement? In pounds, waist circumference, again not sure...

Are those three labs all required to be in their altered ranges simultaneously to signify LR or does one indicate central LR (at the HC neurons), and the other two point to peripheral LR, say at the skeletal muscles and the liver? After talking to and reading some of Dr. Tim's hormone blogs I have learned that not only elevated cortisol but low cortisol interferes with thyroid labs and negates TSH and T3 ratios and so forth. So if a very high, a very low or just an altered diurnal graph of cortisol is enough to flag LR why does Jack talk so much about hs-CRP and reverse T3 in relation to LR. Is a bad ASI or poor cortisol to DHEA ratio alone the smoking gun for LR??

I understand it is leptin's structural analog, IL-6 that floods the receptor in the hypothalamus which precipitates LR but the question is how to infer this from more ordinary labwork without ordering an expensive cytokine panel to prove this is happening...Or just ignore the labs and do it from the BMI, waist circumference, weight or other symptoms?

I know Jack will be able to explain all these subtleties but this is not his thread, Ask Jack, and I am not privy to that area, but if anyone out there is willing and able to educate me on the fine points of interpreting leptin resistance vs leptin sensitivity I would be very grateful; or point me to a whitepaper, textbook or other resource I can research for myself if such a thing exists, other than Jack's writings of course.

Thanks for listening,

Rick

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-11T15:21:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-be-doing-the-leptin-rx-or-for-how-long.9496/post-120969>

you said, :"**1\. First make sure you really are Leptin resistant (LR) to begin with"**. That's where I first come unstuck as I don't know for sure ... I don't look overweight and my doctor won't order the reverse T3 test unless there is an issue with my general thyroid test (should get the results of that back today). I also asked for a cortisol test, which they also didn't do, so I think I'm going to get this done via a naturopath as I feel like I'm flying a bit blind. "

go order your own test at lef.org. Why would you wait to get well?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-11T15:24:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-i-be-doing-the-leptin-rx-or-for-how-long.9496/post-120971>

anylabstestnow.com or lef.org or Zrtlabs.com you can get your blood and saliva assayed your self. You do not need a doctor to order your labs any longer. You can do it yourself when they wont help you understand where you are now.

# Thread: Estrogen dominance, DIM, Detox and Methylation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/estrogen-dominance-dim-detox-and-methylation.9500/>
**Subforum:** Heal Your Hormones
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaulG — 2014-01-30T04:03:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/estrogen-dominance-dim-detox-and-methylation.9500/post-107404>

Recently had my serum oestradiol tested over four week period. 1st week it was 256 pmol/L, fourth week it was 90 pmol/L. Reference range for males is 26-156 pmol/L

So I am kind of shocked that I am in normal range whereas I have been estrogen dominant for at least last four years and wondering if it could be a lab error. Added bonus was that testoterone was in upper range whereas it has always been mid-range and am cortisol was normal whereas it was very high before.

Due to mthfr homozygous on C677T I have been supplementing with methyl folate + methyl b12 plus b6, magnesium, selenium, zinc, liver tablets, curcumin, kelp for last 12 months and happy to report that homocystein has dropped from 11 to 7.

I am also homozygous for p450 cyp1b1 detox snp that is involved in Estrogen detox and After reading this excellent article on estrogen metabolism

[http://www.fusionhealthstudio.com/estrogen-the-good-the-bad-and-the-ugly/](http://www.fusionhealthstudio.com/estrogen-the-good-the-bad-and-the-ugly/)

I decided to take DIM (diindolylmethane) first thing in the am and was doing this two weeks leading up to the last test.

So, is DIM responsible for the normal result?

Should I take it every day? Are there risks?

Do men go through any cycles of low and high Estrogen and it was coincidence it was high the previous 5 times I have been tested (1 saliva, 3 bloods, 1 urine)?

### Tff — 2015-03-28T09:20:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/estrogen-dominance-dim-detox-and-methylation.9500/post-159599>

PaulG,

Thanks for the post..that article has some good info.

I'm in somewhat similar situation...male with very high Estrogen levels.

I'm male, 40, <10% bodyfat, eat paleo, <100g carbs per day, and eat lots of supposedly anti estrogenic foods and supplements..green tea, curcumin, pomegranate juice sometimes, veg, fibre, filter my water etc.
Ive been taking 30-50mg of zinc for years on and off. Serum zinc looks average.I also take methylfolate and methyl b12 as im homozygous for MTHFR A1298C. Plus a bunch of other supps.
I have been eating grass fed butter lately, which i feel good on but does contain estrogens.
I do eat non organic non grass fed meat due to super high prices(i live in asia).
I do occasionally have 4-5 alcoholic drinks but always wait 3-4 days after before having any hormone tests.

A year ago i had a hormone tests, after taking DIM and 1-2g of Calcium D glucarate and my followup estrogen results got worse!

Last month I had a hormone re-test and all my estrogens were SUPER HIGH!.
Doc suggested milk thistle to assist the liver and digestive enzymes(I also get acne).

Saliva tests....
Mine: Reference range:
Testosterone 481 100-720
DHT 4.2 2-10
E1 18 0-6
E2 40 9.6-20
E3 47 0-25

Blood tests..
Oddly here the E2 isnt too bad...and my total testosterone is super high..
Mine: Reference range..
Testosterone 34.27 nmol/L (5-32..ie v high!?)
Free T 42.7 pmol/L 22-104
E2 61 pmol/L (20-161 )
DHEAso4 6.9 umol/L 3.8-13
IGF1 137 ng/ml 109-284
cortisol 325 nmol/L 101-535

I'd like to avoid Arimidex if possible, but on the other hand i sure prepared to give it a trial.

Thanks all!!!

### PaulG — 2015-03-28T11:57:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/estrogen-dominance-dim-detox-and-methylation.9500/post-159609>

I think there are a lot of factors that influence point in time hormone testing such as [sleep quality](http://m.ajpendo.physiology.org/content/276/1/E43.long), hormonal cycles (seasonal, circadian (~24 h), ultradian (<24h) and infradian (>24h))

see [http://biology.stackexchange.com/questions/2732/do-men-have-significant-hormonal-cycles](http://biology.stackexchange.com/questions/2732/do-men-have-significant-hormonal-cycles)

**[Infradian rhythmic variations of salivary estradiol and progesterone in healt](http://biomed.wz.cz/pdf/publ/duodecim.pdf)hy men**

> _Background_. Circadian and circannual rhythms of sex steroids in men have been well described, but infradian dynamics of estradiol and progesterone in men are unknown. These hormones play a role in the physiology as well as in the pathophysiology of various clinical entities, and their chronobiology might be of importance.
>
> _Aim_. Infradian dynamics of salivary estradiol, testosterone, and progesterone were analysed for the presence of cyclic patterns.
>
> _Subjects and methods_. Five young healthy men collected saliva samples for 30 consecutive days. Salivary estradiol, testosterone, and progesterone were measured using radioimmunoassay. Analysis of Rhythmic Variance (ANORVA) was used and potential period lengths of 3–15 days were evaluated.
>
> **_Results_. No infradian rhythms were found in testosterone and progesterone, but a significant (_p_ < 0.03) rhythm at a period length of 12 days was found in salivary estradiol levels.**
>
> _Discussion_. We believe that this is the first study to describe a 12-day (duodecimal) rhythm of salivary estradiol in men. This finding might be of importance for physiological and pathophysiological research, though the pattern needs to be investigated in larger studies.

### Sandra — 2014-01-30T04:51:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/some-labs-from-2-yrs-ago-and-some-clues-perhaps.9501/post-107412>

I dug out some old labs from 2 years ago, pre-Paleo, still eating sugar and grains.

I can't work out how to format this any better. The figures in brackets are the ranges they said the results should be in. BOLD numbers are where they were not in the presented range.

Everything is in range except for HDL, and Iron Levels (TIBC and Ferritin). A follow-up iron test confirmed no loss via stool, so they wanted to give me supplements, and I said I wanted to try with diet. I didn't go back to test until last week - so still waiting on those results, but still feeling all the same symptoms of the tiredness, cold hands and feet, and just a bit under the weather.

11-Nov-11
aged 36
not preg

BLOOD COUNT

Haemoglobin............ 133 g/L (115-165)
Haematocrit............ 0.39 (0.35-0.47)
RCC ........................4.2 10\*12/L (3.9-5.6)
MCV ........................94 fL (80-100)

WCC ........................4.7 10\*9/L (3.5-12)
Neutrophils ................2.22 10\*9/L (1.5-8)
Lymphocytes............ 2 10\*9/L (1.0-4.0)
Monocytes ................0.35 10\*9/L (0-0.9)
Eosinophils ................0.06 10\*9/L (0-0.6)
Basophils ................0.06 10\*9/L (0-0.15)

Platelets ................206 10\*9/L (150-400)

CHOLESTEROL

Chol 5.3 mmol/L (3.9-5.5)
Trigs 1.3 mmol/L (0.6-2.0)
HDL **2.1** mmol/L (1.1-1.9)
LDL 2.6 mmol/L (0.0-4.0)
Tot Chol/HDL 2.5 (0-4.5)

THYROID FUNTION

TSH 1.6 mU/L (0.3-3.5)

ANAEMIA

Iron 19 umol/L (5-30)
TIBC **84** umol/L (45-72)
Trans Sat 23 % (20-55)
Ferritin **20** (30-150)

OCCULT BLOOD in reference to above iron deficiency
Occult Blood 1 negative
Occult Blood 2 negative
Occult Blood 3 negative

ROUTINE CHEMISTRY 11 Nov 2011, aged 36, not pregnant

Sodium 138 mmol/L 135-145
Potassium 4.4 mmol/L 3.5-5.5
Chloride 102 mmol/L 95-110
Bicarbonate 24 mmol/L 20-32
Anion Gap 12 mmol/L 5-15
Ca (Corr) 2.32 mmol/L 2.1-2.55
Phosphate 1.2 mmol/L 0.8-1.5
Urea 4.7 mmol/L 2.5-7
Urate 0.3 mmol/L 0.15-0.4
Creatinine 66 mmol/L 45-85
eGFR 88 mmol/L >59
Fast Glucose 4.8 mmol/L 3.6-6.0
Total Protein 69 g/L 64-81
Albumin 42 g/L 37-48
Globulin 27 g/L 20-39
T Bilirubin 8 umol/L 3-15
ALP 42 U/L 20-105
AST 20 U/L 10-35
ALT 20 U/L 5-30
GGT 7 U/L 5-35
LDH 151 U/L 120-250
Cholesterol 5.3 mmol/L 3.9-5.5
Triglyceride 1.3 mmol/L 0.6-2.0
Iron 19 umol/L 5-30
Haemolysis Index 2 0-40

They said the HDL was nothing to worry about, but the iron was a concern. So, if I am getting a lot of iron in my diet, where is it going, and why is it not being absorbed - gut issues??? I am loathe to just take supplements - I'd like to fix it.

# Thread: Hadza gut microbiome
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hadza-gut-microbiome.9503/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-01-30T09:07:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hadza-gut-microbiome.9503/post-107423>

> Anthropologist Jeff Leach hopes to test \[theories of disease\] by studying the microbiota of the Hadza, a community of hunter-gatherers in northern Tanzania whose lifestyle resembles that of humanity before the advent of agriculture. Leach, who's spending 2 years among the Hadza, also plans to adopt their lifestyle for a while to find out how it will change the microbes living in and on his own body. The first results of the study are expected later this year.

[http://news.sciencemag.org/biology/2014/01/slideshow-secrets-inside-guts-africas-hunter-gatherers](http://news.sciencemag.org/biology/2014/01/slideshow-secrets-inside-guts-africas-hunter-gatherers)

Here's his blog (which has a lot more):

[http://humanfoodproject.com/going-f...cquire-healthiest-gut-microbiome-world-heard/](http://humanfoodproject.com/going-feral-one-year-journey-acquire-healthiest-gut-microbiome-world-heard/)

### HoneyChild — 2014-01-30T14:58:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hadza-gut-microbiome.9503/post-107438>

But how much will the change in microbes be related to the diet vs lifestyle? This aspect usually gets overlooked, esp in the Paleo diet community.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-30T15:50:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hadza-gut-microbiome.9503/post-107447>

Leach says in the blog post linked above:

> Excitingly, our work with the Hadzabe hunter-gatherers in Tanzania will allow us to compare our western selves to people who still hunt and gather the majority of their food, have limited access to western medications, are all born naturally and breastfed for 2+ years, live outside more or less 24/7, are covered in microbial-laden soil (natures blanket), and that have an intimate connection to a vast (natural) microbial world that we in the so-called developed world have moved away from.

I guess you could say that, in this phase of the experiment, he isn't trying to isolate any one factor -- if for no other reason than because it's not feasible to even try.

I suppose a lot of people in the Paleosphere think health is pretty much all about diet not just as regards the gut but in general. But you will find mentions of the downside of antibiotics and over-cleanliness (and stuff on movement, light, and a lot of other things) from people like Mark Sisson and Frank Forencich.

I think I heard Jack say recently on someone's podcast that he estimates diet as worth only 10% and other factors -- sleep, light and circadian rhythms; temperature; and EMF radiation exposure -- as worth 90%.

### Josh — 2014-02-01T22:48:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hadza-gut-microbiome.9503/post-107774>

If they do not include the larger helminths and their role in the microbiome, they are missing a lot. This is from personal experience. They have multiple effects at multiple trophic levels in the microbiota and interact in many ways with our own system doing the work of what might require 5-10 medications. There are costs and benefits and a key seems to be the number of larger helminths one carries. I doubt any human was helminth free before the 1930's. Consider the soil microbiome and the roles played by nematodes and worms. What is not seen with worms is how their secretions and signals influence other organisms and the immune system. Current probiotic research is like taking the soil out of your garden and throwing away anything you can see with the naked eye as unimportant.

The collective must know a lot more about water which seems to be one of the most important things but it has not been collated .

Shall we start ? Basically list what you know regarding water in the simplest terms possible.
Include things like "I have fluoridated water " which is why I do X to mitigate or I drink rainwater because I ... .Include where you live,what your external factors are whether you live on top of a hill miles from town or on the top of a skyscraper . In other words make your answer as information dense as possible but use words of 10 letters or less . ### Grizz — 2014-01-30T17:37:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-create-the-ultimate-water-thread.9504/post-107462>

I will be happy to comment. We have well water in West Central TN. It is polluted with chemicals from farming.

We use this distiller to distill all of the water we use for cooking, coffee, tea, & drinking.
[http://www.amazon.com/Water-Distiller-Countertop-Enamel-Collection/dp/B00026F9F8](http://www.amazon.com/Water-Distiller-Countertop-Enamel-Collection/dp/B00026F9F8)

24 Doctors report that distilled water is very healthy:
[http://curezone.com/forums/fm.asp?i=2000592](http://curezone.com/forums/fm.asp?i=2000592)

You should see the smelly, slimy yuck left behind in the distiller. Our water is pure, cleaner than rain water which picks up pollution from the air. It also tastes GREAT. The wife & I use a little more than 1 gallon per day. Our distiller produces 1 gallon in about 6 hours. Amazon sells larger distillers for larger families. IMO, this distiller was a great investment toward our health. For those who are concerned about missing minerals ( we are not ) you can order mineral additives for distilled water at Amazon.

This is far more efficient than using a reverse osmosis (RO) filter that wastes more water down the drain than you get.

Grizz

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-30T19:32:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-create-the-ultimate-water-thread.9504/post-107470>

I was interested to read once that anthropologists had theorised that Australian Aborigines would be biologically adapted to need less water and would urinate and sweat less than people living in more humid conditions. This theory was totally wrong:

> … compared with the white men in Lungly Gully the aboriginal men and women drank more water and drank it ten times as fast, sweated twice as much, had 63 percent greater body turnover of water, and urinated up to twelve times as much. I remembered David Carnegie's observation of an aborigine he had sent for water after discovering Empress springs:
>
>
> > He drank a great deal more than any of us and yet had been a comparatively short time without water, whilst we had been walking and working on starvation rations for a good number of days.

Here's a recent paper on "Water, Hydration and Health" that I just found:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2908954/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2908954/)

> oh sorry the pic came out sideways.

OK......long discussion alert. The cause is protons. Ice only forms when when protons get free and go between every other oxygen atom and keep the hexagonal sheets of water ordered and in registered. When water is liquid it is normally out of register. In ice the angles are close to 60 degrees. The crazy part.........protons are needed to freeze water and can only come from charge separation. Charge separation requires energy for this to happen. What does this imply? In order for water to freeze it must have energy added too it to free the protons.........do interdigitate in the sheets. Cold temps are not REQUIRED for ice formation contrary to your belief. This is also why you got a point out of your ice.......it is evidence of a supercooled water that had a proton tube that formed and went north......this is the exact same thing that happens in a tree or plant and why water can rise up xylem tubes to deliver water to the leaves from the roots.......it is the proton gradient that drives it.......all causes by the action of electromagnetic spectrum on water.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-01T17:58:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-create-the-ultimate-water-thread.9504/post-107726>

Because it depends upon the flow of protons which directly depends upon the water's ability to maximally absorb infra red heat......all water does this to a degree but not all water has the same purity.........and there in lies water's magic and why all people's results with health biohacks, hormones, and experiments always varies.......it is due to the quantum weirdness of water chemistry. This is why QED is quirky and why all of what you see is not reality when it comes to things we talk about here.

### Tanya — 2014-03-03T17:05:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-create-the-ultimate-water-thread.9504/post-113169>

This excerpt is from a book Serve to Win by Novac Djocovic - men’s tennis champion:

“ I once saw an amazing test that has to do with Eastern medicine. A researcher filled two glasses with water - the same kind of water and the same amounts. With one glass, he shared positive energy: love, joy, happiness, all the goodness of life. He nurtured it.

He gave the other glass all his negative energy: anger, fear, hostility. He swore at the glass.

Then he let both glasses of water sit unattended for several days.

The difference in the waters after a few days was immense. The water that had negative thoughts and influences directed at it was tinted slightly green, as though algae were growing inside. The other glass was still bright and crystal clear.”

Looks to me like what kind of energy you give is what you get back.....

Dr. M. Emoto Had demonstrated this in his Healing with Water. This link includes photos of frozen water crystals. Positive energy seemed to result in clear, snowflake like crystals, while the water exposed to negative energy had darker and ill-formed crystals.

### Josh — 2014-03-03T18:40:29+0200

# Thread: The REdox video blog is live:  EE 12
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-redox-video-blog-is-live-ee-12.9506/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx

# Thread: Ketogenic Mediterranean Diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-mediterranean-diet.9507/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-01-30T16:33:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-mediterranean-diet.9507/post-107456>

I stumbled on this looking for something else. I haven't had much of a look yet.

The site seems to be run by a doctor. But I'm not sure about some of this. For example:

> Very-low-carb ketogenic diets may have the potential to cause osteoporosis (thin, brittle bones), kidney stones, low blood pressure, constipation, gout, high uric acid in the blood, excessive loss of sodium and potassium in the urine, worsening of kidney disease, deficiency of calcium and vitamins A, B, C, and D, among other adverse effects.

I think "may" is acting as a weasel-word here. I mean that in the original sense of "weasel word" -- i.e., a word that sucks meaning out of another word -- so-called because of the line from the Shakspeare play:

> I can suck melancholy out of a song as a weasel sucks eggs.

So in that sentence "may" is sucking the meaning out of "potential", so that the sentence appears to be saying more than it really is. But have they this "potential" or not? Have one or more of the effects ever been observed? Where are the references?

And what does "among other adverse effects" mean, for goodness' sake? Say what they are, rather than hinting darkly! And is he saying these _unnamed_ adverse effects will occur or are these also something that _may_ be potential. IOW, things someone's said will happen with, perhaps, no evidence at all.

It's shoddy writing.

However, I think the idea of ketogenic, and low-carb, Mediterranean diets is an interesting one. After all "Mediterranean" usually indicates much use of seafood, fresh produce, olive oil, and some red wine. Take the excess carbs out and that could be a good diet.

So here's the link:

[http://advancedmediterranean.com/category/ketogenic-mediterranean-diet/](http://advancedmediterranean.com/category/ketogenic-mediterranean-diet/)

### caroline — 2014-01-30T22:42:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-mediterranean-diet.9507/post-107488>

Isn't this really dated thinking?

I am doing so well on VLCD and now with adding in much more fat and less protein - everything is falling into place ... my Doc couldn't be happier with my numbers.....

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-30T23:26:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-mediterranean-diet.9507/post-107496>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=107488)
>
> Isn't this really dated thinking?

What, Audrey? The bit I was complaining about? Yeah, I guess so … I'm not even sure how much "thinking" ever went into it.

What was in my mind was that a low-carb version of Mediterranean would really be pretty good. Mediterranean is, after all, all about high seafood and fresh produce. Take the grains and legumes out and it would pretty much be Epi-paleo. That's why I linked it. He might at any rate have some good recipes somewhere on that site.

> I am doing so well on VLCD and now with adding in much more fat and less protein - everything is falling into place ... my Doc couldn't be happier with my numbers.....

That's good.

I don't usually crunch numbers on these things, but I did record my day's food today. According to FitDay, I ate 2051 Kcal (74% fat, 18% protein, 8% carbs) today. I guess that's VLC. I'm surprised it didn't come to much in the way of calories: that was a large and satisfying breakfast, and a big salad with some salmon at lunch. For dinner I had some smoked duck, a load of boiled curly kale with plenty of ghee melted over it, a cup of broth, a big lump of pork rilletes, and a handful of macadamias. Not exactly starving oneself is it, yet it doesn't add up to much in energetic terms? I don't think a couple of thousand calories is very much for a 6-foot man, but I'm not at all hungry. I never ever am. I can't really remember what it's like to feel so.

Am I in ketosis? I suppose so. I tend to come pretty far to the right on the ketostix, but the meters don't seem to be around this side of the Atlantic, and I know the ketostix are not accurate. I'd guess I'm only _mildly_ in ketosis. But I'd prefer to eat in a more Paleo style than in a hardcore ketogenic style. The stuff Peter at Hyperlipid eats, for example, doesn't look like ordinary meals, and even he says he "drifts in and out" of ketosis. I don't want to push my protein down to 60 grams a day and start skipping a meal, or a day's meals, in addition -- which is what I guess anyone would have to do to get really deeply into ketosis.

### caroline — 2014-01-30T23:43:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-mediterranean-diet.9507/post-107502>

I used to not get into ketosis very easily - haven't measured in a long time..... can't find my ketostix! I am not eating very much in the way of carbs - I seem to have a thing for carrots tho!

I am not keeping track at all any more really ... my body seems to be functioning so much better with all the fat .... who would have thought? Dr. K. of course!

I am guessing that I am in Ketosis pretty good - I am definitely on a high. I love it! I seem to be attracting positive people like a magnet. It is so much fun every day .... what an adventure!

It sounds like you have your diet nailed ...... It feels great doesn't it?

I see what you mean about the med diet ..... are you eating seafood every day?

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-31T08:20:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-mediterranean-diet.9507/post-107557>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=107502)
>
> ..... are you eating seafood every day?

Pretty much, if it's only canned sardines. Often twice a day. Remembering Dr. K's nutrient demonstrations, I include shellfish, too … when I can get them. I had oysters a couple of times a while ago, but I haven't seen any in the fishmonger's for some time.

I suppose you'd get a really good choice of seafood in Australia.

### caroline — 2014-01-31T09:49:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-mediterranean-diet.9507/post-107563>

Yes we do but very expensive ........

### caroline — 2014-01-31T09:50:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-mediterranean-diet.9507/post-107564>

Did you see Dr. K's demo for baked oysters? that looked really good.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-31T12:19:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketogenic-mediterranean-diet.9507/post-107572>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=107564)
>
> Did you see Dr. K's demo for baked oysters? that looked really good.

I didn't. It sounds good.

Actually, Dr. K puts out so much stuff, I don't suppose I've seen the half of it. Plus he's scared me so much over sleep  (even more than the authors of _Lights Out_ had done) that I don't tend to watch video material in the evening, which is my only really free time.

I still haven't seen that kitchen video that was linked recently. I bet that's good.

I'm listening to a podcast about cooking while working at the moment. It's pretty good. It's an interview with a chef called Peter Servold, who's just written a book called _Paleo by Season_:

[www.blogtalkradio.com/drloradio](http://www.blogtalkradio.com/drloradio)

I may have to buy his book.

### caroline — 2014-01-30T22:11:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ee-12.9511/post-107483>

I am still working my way thru this - but I wanted to say that I love the video. I find it enormously helpful. Thanks Dr.K.

I am, however, distracted by Jack's kitchen! I love kitchens!!! I Love to see what people have in their kitchen - occupational hazzard I guess!

How is everyone doing with this blog? any lightbulb moments so far?

### nicld — 2014-01-30T22:56:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ee-12.9511/post-107491>

### Lahelada — 2014-01-30T23:40:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ee-12.9511/post-107500>

Eureka moment in the video too. I sort of get it but could not explain it to anyone else yet . A bit like when you are learning a new language,spend time with native speakers and with your passive vocabulary you understand but you have no active vocabulary to take part in the conversation.

### caroline — 2014-01-31T00:00:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ee-12.9511/post-107505>

### caroline — 2014-01-31T00:07:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ee-12.9511/post-107507>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=107500)
>
> Eureka moment in the video too. I sort of get it but could not explain it to anyone else yet . A bit like when you are learning a new language,spend time with native speakers and with your passive vocabulary you understand but you have no active vocabulary to take part in the conversation.

I sort of think that we expect too much of ourselves some time ... us non geeks that is .......When I read in the comments section how a Doctor can not quite grasp something ...

But - don't get me wrong....I think we need to expect extra ordinary things from ourself ... at least strive for it!

I can't possibly know what a neurosurgeon knows ...... especially, and in particular, this one .....

### caroline — 2014-01-31T00:09:25+0200

### sjoshua — 2014-01-31T18:23:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ee-12.9511/post-107596>

My understanding of the PPP in laymens terms:

Our cells are very effective at determining their own health, and in turn their own potential. The PPP is a very specific pathway that, when utilized by our body to derive ATP from fat, requires optimized cellular function. The reason for this is the cell membrane must be able to hold up to the redox (voltage) potential of this conversion - which is very high. If your cells "know" that they will likely self destruct by trying, then they aren't going to pursue this pathway for energy.

Why is this pathway so logical? Where would it function the best? In cold conditions where our semiconduction functions more effectively... The same locations where (prior to the last ~150 years) there would have been zero access to carbohydrates during the winter months.
