# Thread: Iodine,  Epi-paleo Rx, thyroid, and carb linkage
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-epi-paleo-rx-thyroid-and-carb-linkage.9535/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 62
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-01T05:47:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-epi-paleo-rx-thyroid-and-carb-linkage.9535/post-107681>

You all need to read this paper. [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15142639](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15142639) â€¦

The paper has some issues but nothing major which eliminates it from consideration. Its data suggests that modern levels of T3 are 1.5 times higher than they were before the agricultural revolution of carbohydrates for modern humans. This appears to give the idea that iodine deficiency diseases are more likely the result of excess dietary carbohydrate than lack of iodine in the diet. Or more aptly put, if you eat more carbs you need a ton more seafood. T3 is designed to drop when dietary glucose is low. This set of circumstance needs to be examined a bit. This implies that the human body seeks tight control on glucose. It opens my curiosity why glucose has to be kept in a restricted range within tissues and plasma. It also implies humans have a very active mechanism to use glucose first as we saw in EMF 4. Mind you this is not out of preference for glucose as a metabolic fuel source. It appears to people that since glucose is used first it is preferred when in reality it is something harmful to mitochondria chronically so the body looks to clear it fast. To me, it appears the body is handling it when present in excess amounts, as a toxin to be removed until proper levels are reestablished. Now if you have hypothyroidism........you got to ask yourself are you low in T3 and eating too little fat and too many carbs. This is where I see most of the people in hypothyroid land.

The implications for those with hypothyroidism should be very clear: The smaller the amount of T3 required to process blood sugar, the greater the amount available to perform more important metabolic functions, like converting LDL cholesterol to pregnenolone. This is why one must focus on the personal context and not the labs alone. It leads to myopic thinking. I see this a lot from Guyenet and Jaminet when they talk about T3 and carb loads.

CITES:
Kopp W.: Nutrition, evolution and thyroid hormone levels â€“ a link to iodine deficiency disorders?; Med Hypotheses. 2004;62(6):871-5.

Abstract: An increased iodine requirement as a result of significant changes in human nutrition rather than a decreased environmental iodine supply is suggested to represent the main cause of the iodine deficiency disorders (IDD). The pathomechanism proposed is based on the fact that serum concentrations of thyroid hormones, especially of trijodothyronine (T3), are dependent on the amount of dietary carbohydrate. High-carbohydrate diets are associated with significantly higher serum T3 concentrations, compared with very low-carbohydrate diets. While our Paleolithic ancestors subsisted on a very low carbohydrate/high protein diet, the agricultural revolution about 10,000 years ago brought about a significant increase in dietary carbohydrate. These nutritional changes have increased T3 levels significantly. Higher T3 levels are associated with an enhanced T3 production and an increased iodine requirement. The higher iodine requirement exceeds the availability of iodine from environmental sources in many regions of the world, resulting in the development of IDD.

### caroline — 2014-02-01T07:52:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-epi-paleo-rx-thyroid-and-carb-linkage.9535/post-107688>

and Flouride affects T3?

### yewwei.tan — 2014-02-01T09:11:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-epi-paleo-rx-thyroid-and-carb-linkage.9535/post-107692>

Got it. Essentially the lines of causality drawn by most practitioners are in the the wrong direction.

Carbs drive the need for more T3, and the body tries to increase T3 as a compensatory mechanism.

The reason that people "feel better" on higher carb diets whilst having hypothyroidism, is then because carbs provide FAST energy.

\---

The following ramblings are me thinking aloud after listening to Dominic D'Agostino in his recent interview with John Kiefer: [https://soundcloud.com/body-io-fm/the-ketogenic-diet-and-cancer](https://soundcloud.com/body-io-fm/the-ketogenic-diet-and-cancer)

Oncogenes were mentioned in that podcast, and the speculation was made that these genes may actually be the "original" genetic material of a cell back when it took a more primitive form.

That is, before the cell managed to engulf and take control of mitochondria, it depended largely on glycolytic pathways, and it's sole role was to replicate itself further. (sounds like cancer ...)

Engulfing a mitochondria basically introduced a whole new set of cellular regulatory mechanisms as a tradeoff of being able to produce a ton load more energy for the cell through the mitochondrial ETC.

I need to wait for Jack to return to writing more about mitochondria

\---

Another thing that Dominic has always stressed is that exogenous ketones (esters or salts) work best with an already ketogenic diet.

That led me thinking about what would happen if you put a high-carb diet with ketone salts (as most modern humans would).

Obviously the body would need to "get rid" of the carbs, but how would ETC function with competing substrates? (this is related to the Hyperlipid reference below)

And of course, different tissues use carbs and fat differently; key quote from Dominic: "you almost NEVER see cancer in muscle cells".

\---

I'm currently reading Pollack's 'Fourth Phase of Water' book, and in Chapter 6, he talks about how the de-structuring of structured (EZ) water can produce a lot of ROS if there isn't adequate levels of hydronium ions to re-form H2O. He states that this could happen if there are other strongly negatively charged molecules present to pull said hydronium ions away from the EZ boundary, while simultaneously having other protons (salts!!) that penetrate and disrupt the EZ planes to destroy an EZ region.

I'm still thinking about situations where this would occur. Obviously, not having a biology or physics degree means that I'm not familiar enough with the concepts.

When is the blog going to start covering protons!

\---

Speaking of protons, this also reminds me of Peter's (of Hyperlipid) proton 34 blog post ([http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2014/01/protons-34-rotenone.html](http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2014/01/protons-34-rotenone.html)), which I made some notes of here: [http://tanyewwei.com/notes/hyperlipid-protons-34](http://tanyewwei.com/notes/hyperlipid-protons-34)

A paraphrased quote from him:

> Complex I dysfunction leads to intracellular lipid accumulation.

which is true given HIGH insulin sensitivity and lots of glucose.

Now, I'm sure complex I dysfunction isn't a black-or-white definition, and eating carbs have a huge impact on that.

\---

Anyway, enough incoherent rambling for now.

### ATL_Paleo — 2014-02-02T17:30:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-epi-paleo-rx-thyroid-and-carb-linkage.9535/post-107907>

Dr. Mercola calls bromine "the bully of the halide group". [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/dr-mercola/thyroid-health\_b\_472953.html](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/dr-mercola/thyroid-health_b_472953.html)

This article includes a discussion of a common source of bromines .... bread and baked goods. Another good reason to avoid grains.
\--------------

When you ingest or absorb bromine, it displaces iodine, and this iodine deficiency leads to an increased risk for cancer of the breast, thyroid gland, ovary and prostate -- cancers that we see at alarmingly high rates today. This phenomenon is significant enough to have been given its own name -- the Bromide Dominance Theory.

Aside from its effects on your endocrine glands, bromine is toxic in and of itself. Bromide builds up in your central nervous system and results in many problems. It is a central nervous system depressant and can trigger a number of psychological symptoms such as acute paranoia and other psychotic symptoms.

In fact, in an audio interview, physician Jorge Flechas reported that, between 1920 and 1960, at least 20 percent of all hospital admissions for "acute paranoid schizophrenia" were a result of ingesting bromine-containing products.

In addition to psychiatric problems, bromine toxicity can manifest as the following:

•Skin rashes and severe acne

•Loss of appetite and abdominal pain

•Fatigue

•Metallic taste

•Cardiac arrhythmias

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-03T08:31:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-epi-paleo-rx-thyroid-and-carb-linkage.9535/post-108063>

> [ATL\_Paleo said:](/goto/post?id=107907)
>
> Dr. Mercola calls bromine "the bully of the halide group". [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/dr-mercola/thyroid-health\_b\_472953.html](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/dr-mercola/thyroid-health_b_472953.html)

Thanks.

> This article includes a discussion of a common source of bromines .... bread and baked goods. Another good reason to avoid grains.

Oh, lord! In _bread_ in the U.S. -- and Britain and Canada until the 90s. Actually, I find it interesting that the supermarkets having mostly put the butchers, fishmongers, greengrocers, and bakers out of business over the past twenty or thirty years, the bakeries are now coming back. I wouldn't eat bread, but it's telling that even bread-eaters can't stand the product they're expected to eat under the name "bread". Our local town now has two craft bakeries opened over the past year -- unbleached flours, long fermentation of the dough, etc. The supermarkets are now trying to hit back with "in-store bakeries" -- they'd sooner just sell people something in a plastic bag, of course -- but their stuff is still fairly low-grade, so there's plenty of business for craft-bakeries.

The other boom area for craft products in food and drink is microbreweries. There are now something like thirty in our county -- all over the past ten years and most far more recent. Beer is also, of course, a gluten-containing product. Still, I think both phenomena are interesting and helpful: there are enough people -- a minority, sure, but enough -- who won't take whatever's thrown at them by supermarkets and "food-manufacturers". We need revolts like this.

> When you ingest or absorb bromine, it displaces iodine, and this iodine deficiency leads to an increased risk for cancer of the breast, thyroid gland, ovary and prostate -- cancers that we see at alarmingly high rates today. This phenomenon is significant enough to have been given its own name -- the Bromide Dominance Theory.

And doubtless another reason for the increase in hypothyroidism.

… Yes, that's what he's saying:

> Hypothyroidism is far more prevalent than once thought in the U.S. The latest estimates are that 13 million Americans have hypothyroidism, but the actual numbers are probably higher. Some experts claim that 10 to 40 percent of Americans have suboptimal thyroid function.

The wholemeal is Â£2.70 a loaf, a sliced wholemeal loaf from a national baker is around Â£1 cheaper and in some stores like Aldi/Lidl can be nearly Â£1.70 cheaper. Where money is an issue, most people will buy the cheaper bread because they have to. We stopped eating bread nearly 2 years ago, I seldom miss it. ### Gagnrad — 2014-02-03T19:45:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-epi-paleo-rx-thyroid-and-carb-linkage.9535/post-108133>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=108121)
>
> Einkorn wheat is the last wheat grass from biblical times. Bill Davis talks about that in his book

It's a marvellous book, isn't it?

IIRC, he momentarily dabbles in the idea that maybe we could go back to old varieties of wheat like this even while admitting that it's still a sub-par strategy. By the time you've finished his book, let alone read David Perlmutter's, it's clear we can't.

Besides, that's just not going to fly as a farming strategy. Farmers are not going _en masse_ to go back to growing something with lower yields. And what are they going to do with all the long straw? That went for thatch in the old days (now, for the few remaining thatched buildings, they have to use reed). You see a few bags of spelt and emmer in posh health food stores now and then. You'd pay an arm and a leg for them, and they probably go somewhat rancid before someone who might want a bag happens to walk in.

As a farmer you'd only basically be serving the sandal-wearing, juice-drinking market (or part of it) -- an even smaller market than the market for craft-bakery bread. Paleo folk don't want bread at all; nor does the low-carb crowd; and those who can't afford premium breads (or who choose to spend their money otherwise) wouldn't, as sooperb points out, pay for anything other than the cheapest product however poor in quality.

From the paleo/low-carb point of view, why bother? Dr Art Ayers had a blog recently detailing how to make sourdough bread based on suggestions by Michael Pollan. Well â€¦ let Pollan eat it. It's simply not worth all the hassle for something that is eventually destined to turn into glucose in your bloodstream anyway and whose gluten is _still_ likely to be troublesome for many if not most people.

It's historically interesting to look at the (many) reasons why bread may not have been as troublesome in the past for people as it is now. But trying to reverse-engineer those reasons in order to "hold onto" bread is an exercise in futility. According to _Nourishing traditions_, Sally Fallon even has a small electrically-operated grain mill imported from Germany, since she buys her grain unground and only grinds it as she needs it, as people would have done in the distant past, so that the oils don't go rancid. All that effort for what? â€¦ And I've heard her say in a podcast interview with Jimmy Moore that she sometimes gets stomach problems after eating even her own very carefully prepared bread, and she has wondered if she might be gluten-sensitive. How about just not eating the stuff?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-04T04:11:29+0200

This is the pay off for sticking on the road to optimal. The more redundancy you learn the more you see the elegant design. Nanotechnology is critical for future wellness in my view. Why? We need to add the quantum physics to modes of action: Visual molecular dynamic simulations allows us to see why the specific arrangement of atoms are critically important to drugs working and those not. It is also the same way anyone who says bio identical hormones and synthetic hormones are idenitical has zero clue about how quantum biology acts. Let me give you an example. Molecular motors are efficient nanoscale machines destined to make any human designed engine look clumsy. The F1-ATPase is such a nano machine, or what I call a Maxwell Demon.
It is so powerful that a spoonful of it could produce as much torque as your car's engine. the power comes in its quantum efficiency at a subatomic level. It amazes to me we understand the power of a nuclear bomb's energy, but we do not realize that that exact energy is harnessed in quantum design.

As part of the enzyme ATP synthase, the protein can work as an engine but also operate in reverse as a generator. In the latter mode it is responsible for the synthesis of the energy-rich molecule ATP that serves as fuel driving proteins to fully open their protein back bones to bind water to fuel many processes in biological cells. This nano machine can also convert the energy stored in ATP into mechanical rotation as we see in the F1 ATPase. A quantum chemical description of the reaction of ATP combined with a simulation of the protein has revealed to computational biophysicists that an amino acid side group of the protein, called the "arginine finger", controls the progression of the catalytic event, much like a spark plug controls the combustion process in a car engine. That catalytic event also happens to require water to hydrate that arginine finger to work at speeds at 10 to the 35th power. It is an enormous energy source waiting for us to figure it out.

I believe the future of medicine to build prevention programs will be based upon visual molecular dynamic simulations to understand quantum action of nanomolecular machines.

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=108133)
>
> It's a marvellous book, isn't it?
>
> IIRC, he momentarily dabbles in the idea that maybe we could go back to old varieties of wheat like this even while admitting that it's still a sub-par strategy. By the time you've finished his book, let alone read David Perlmutter's, it's clear we can't.
>
> Besides, that's just not going to fly as a farming strategy. Farmers are not going _en masse_ to go back to growing something with lower yields. And what are they going to do with all the long straw? That went for thatch in the old days (now, for the few remaining thatched buildings, they have to use reed). You see a few bags of spelt and emmer in posh health food stores now and then. You'd pay an arm and a leg for them, and they probably go somewhat rancid before someone who might want a bag happens to walk in.
>
> As a farmer you'd only basically be serving the sandal-wearing, juice-drinking market (or part of it) -- an even smaller market than the market for craft-bakery bread. Paleo folk don't want bread at all; nor does the low-carb crowd; and those who can't afford premium breads (or who choose to spend their money otherwise) wouldn't, as sooperb points out, pay for anything other than the cheapest product however poor in quality.
>
> From the paleo/low-carb point of view, why bother? Dr Art Ayers had a blog recently detailing how to make sourdough bread based on suggestions by Michael Pollan. Well â€¦ let Pollan eat it. It's simply not worth all the hassle for something that is eventually destined to turn into glucose in your bloodstream anyway and whose gluten is _still_ likely to be troublesome for many if not most people.
>
> It's historically interesting to look at the (many) reasons why bread may not have been as troublesome in the past for people as it is now. But trying to reverse-engineer those reasons in order to "hold onto" bread is an exercise in futility. According to _Nourishing traditions_, Sally Fallon even has a small electrically-operated grain mill imported from Germany, since she buys her grain unground and only grinds it as she needs it, as people would have done in the distant past, so that the oils don't go rancid. All that effort for what? â€¦ And I've heard her say in a podcast interview with Jimmy Moore that she sometimes gets stomach problems after eating even her own very carefully prepared bread, and she has wondered if she might be gluten-sensitive. How about just not eating the stuff?

Your points are quite valid. But as a marketing guy, I think I know how I would position and advertise an Einkorn based product.

The key insight is that some people are addicted to bread. Intellectually they understand why they should not eat it. But they cannot overcome their preference and habit. My mom is such a person, and no amount of Paleo education will ever stop her from eating morning or lunch bread. I have tried Almond bread and tried various recipes, and she just rejects them all. So I would position the product as a safe bread for the person who wants to avoid the health impacts of bad bread proteins but cannot resist bread. Call the product something like "Bread Cheaters".

And the bottom line is that nothing makes a high quality bread like wheat because of the gluten. If this Einkorn is actually a "safe starch" (in the Perfect Health Diet sense of safe meaning lack of toxins not in the sense of avoiding carbs) then it surely has a role for even a person who allows intake of moderate carbs.

Acronyms are the bane of my existence. I need a reference book just to read half of Jack's posts.  Yes, I thought SOD = Sodium, sorry.

Ketogenic diets are known in the research literature to cause electrolyte imbalances. Volek and Phinney's book summarize research where ketogenic diets cause imbalances that were corrected by five grams of sodium per day. Phinney wrote me an email saying that the sodium losses in a ketogenic diet dwarf the losses from sweat in exercise. For me, five grams was too much sodium. But the point is that if you are ketogenic and you believe electrolytes are not a problem, a red warning flash should be going off. You need to go do some reading. I'm particularly concerned that you would have that feeling and then have a serious cardiac rhythm issue. Go read "The Art and Science of Low Carbohydrate Living" and turn to page 150.

My frustration with all of this is that - for \*SOME\* people - ketogenic and low carb dieting can be technically very difficult and tricky to implement. For me, I feel it requires medical supervision, and the problem is there is NO medical resource. Very few doctors understand these diets. I cannot locate any professional able to supervise and help implement the diet in a way that monitors for side effects and corrects along the way. I feel frustrated for your situation as well. Because that is \*potentially\* a classic example of how ketogenic diet could cause some electrolyte issue, and none of your doctors have a clue about that. And they treat with a calcium channel blocker...more treating the symptom and not understanding the cause.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-09-21T20:50:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-epi-paleo-rx-thyroid-and-carb-linkage.9535/post-141537>

Light polarizes water to lead to health. How this effect is generalized to your epigenetic switches and how mitochondria handle electrons is among the most complex dances in biology. It is not easy to understand but it begins with the marine sources of electrons, to capture sunlight's electrons and photons by using DHA amazing abilities. Without DHA the sun is just a star. With it.........life eats its frequencies to organize some amazing abilities.

Without iodine from seafood, DHA won’t work optimally in human tissue to change light into an electrical signal properly. In the brain, iodine is found in the choroid plexus, the area on the ventricles of the brain where cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is produced. Children with autism have been found to have a poor ability to concentrate iodine in their CSF. The interaction of iodine and DHA to move electrons well is fundamentally how circadian signaling begins in the suprachiasmatic nucleus of the human brain. This is where circadian signaling begins. It is a critical to understand how the brain deciphers environmental signals.

The general picture that emerges within the brain that T4 and T3 hormones enter the brain through
specific CNS transporters as the brain forms. T4 is converted to the active hormone, T3, in glial cells, astrocytes, and tanycytes, although the main target cells of these hormones are neurons and maturing oligodendrocytes within the suprachiasmatic nucleus. This is ultimately how the direction of time is initially set within this newly forming brain. T3, acting through the nuclear receptors, controls the expression of genes involved in myelination, cell differentiation, migration, and signaling. I went over this in energy and epigenetics 1. Few people understand why iodine and DHA are critical in human SCN and myelination patterns. In addition to transducing the proper T3 signal, the nuclear receptors also have activity in the un-liganded state. When circadian signaling is off in the maternal germ line before fertilization, this favors the un-liganded state and this strongly favors the development of hypothyroid phenotype centrally within the brain. This is where leptin resistance begins. This is why early onset hypothyroidism in the maternal tree is always a key clue to the astute clinician that a circadian mismatch is the key feature in many diseases. MS, Hashimoto’s, and melasma being common one’s in modern women. This un-liganded action occurs via aporreceptors that repress normal thyroid transcription. This is how epigenetic switches are set in the germ line. This effect is how transgenerational hypothyroid phenotypes occur in subsequent generations of children. These children do not even need to face the same environment their mothers did to get the same disease. They will manifest the disease much earlier in life if they face a lack of iodine or DHA in their first 6 years of life. They begin with the breakdown of the quantum chemistry between iodine, DHA, and water in the unfertilized egg. These effects then become generalized within the developing embryo after fertilization to effect brain function and mitochondrial development in all tissues. This is how the redox shift occurs transgenerationally in humans. The interaction between iodine DHA and water is critical in setting the proper signaling in how mitochondria can handle electrons at a very fundamental level. This is why understanding SCALE matters. ### Jack Kruse — 2014-09-21T21:18:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-epi-paleo-rx-thyroid-and-carb-linkage.9535/post-141542>

There are two equinoxes every year – in September and March – when the sun shines directly on the equator and the length of day and night is nearly equal. It begins tomorrow. Light changes.......will your choices? Your mitochondria will be paying attention even if you're not.

The Earth during the September equinox.
Seasons are opposite on either side of the equator, so the equinox in September is also known as the "autumnal (fall) equinox" in the northern hemisphere. However, in the southern hemisphere, it's known as the "spring (vernal) equinox".

What happens during an equinox?
The September equinox occurs the moment the Sun crosses the celestial equator – the imaginary line in the sky above the Earth’s equator – from north to south. This happens either on September 22, 23, or 24 every year. The Earth's axis is always tilted at an angle of about 23.5° in relation to the ecliptic, the imaginary plane created by the Earth's path around the Sun. However, the tilt's orientation changes throughout the year. On any other day of the year, the Earth's axis tilts a little away from or towards the Sun. But on the two equinoxes, the Earth's axis tilts neither away from nor towards the Sun and is perpendicular to the Sun's rays, like the illustration shows.

Why is it called equinox?
On the equinox, night and day are nearly exactly the same length – 12 hours – all over the world. This is the reason it's called an "equinox", derived from Latin, meaning "equal night". However, even if this is widely accepted, it isn't entirely true. In reality equinoxes don't have exactly 12 hours of daylight.

To me, it appears the body is handling it when present in excess amounts, as a toxin to be removed until proper levels are reestablished. Now if you have hypothyroidism........you got to ask yourself are you low in T3 and eating too little fat and too many carbs. This is where I see most of the people in hypothyroid land.
>
> The implications for those with hypothyroidism should be very clear: The smaller the amount of T3 required to process blood sugar, the greater the amount available to perform more important metabolic functions, like converting LDL cholesterol to pregnenolone.

I read the above and took notes and thought I had this all figured out.. I thought this was suggesting that T3 is required to process blood sugar/glucose, which come from carbs and are bad, and was further suggesting that only once this has happened does the body move on to using whatever T3 that's left to convert LDL to pregnenalone, and then all the other sex hormones from there. So I was thinking "aha! avoiding carbs will mean more T3 left to eventually create sex hormones!"

But then I read the below quote:

> High-carbohydrate diets are associated with significantly higher serum T3 concentrations, compared with very low-carbohydrate diets.

... and became confused because THIS one seems to be saying that if your T3 levels are low, you WANT to eat more carbs, because then you will make more T3 which is a GOOD thing because you can then more effectively convert that LDL to pregnenalone and onward.

Perhaps having no science background whatsoever beyond public school's ninth grade curriculum is what is holding me back.

### Josh — 2014-02-02T19:20:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-107942>

Consider, Grandfather found Tom Brown when he was 83 and Tom was 8. Tom and Grandfathers real grandson learned with him for 8 years. Grandfather left and cast off his body, his real grandson died in a horseback riding accident, Tom tested his teaching for ten years, Tom was so frustrated with the world that he was going to take his family to live in the woods, but he had written an article for Reader's Digest and made the comment that he would open a school. ON the day he was going to leave, the mailman showed up with a sack of letters. Starting in 1978 without the internet, he now has 20,000 students worldwide...Grandfather was told by the great spirit that it would take 60 years to find the right student, so be patient and do your own work. Every alive group looks like a cult or worse from the outside, I know from experience.

### MamaGrok — 2014-02-02T19:36:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-107948>

Someone pointed me here. Howdy! Sean, here's my update. (Since this thread is supposedly about me, I'll talk unabashedly about me!)

The only aspect of the leptin reset that seemed to do anything for me was the BAB - and I needed it BIG. 9oz of protein in the morning finally killed my cravings dead. I could make my food decisions solely based on what I felt would nourish my body, not based on irrational cravings. After 6 months of that, cravings started again, very mildly. They increased, very gradually. On my 365th day binge-free, I binged (Oct '12). And the cravings continued to grow, and grow, for another year. In Jan '13, I started iodine and DHEA at the same time. Dr. K & another poster here recommended I try 50mg daily of DHEA, since standard doses had failed to achieve the hormonal effects I had been wishing for. I gained energy and I gained 20lb in 2 months and I saw no impact on cravings. When I stopped the DHEA, the weight gain stopped immediately (but wouldn't go away). In retrospect, I believe the iodine was the reason for the energy gain, and the DHEA converted to estrogen in me instead of any testosterone at all (confirmed by blood tests), and that is the reason for the weight gain (in very estrogen-y places).

(Also, I found that eating just meat & fat no longer kept me bloat-free. It didn't seem to matter what I ate; it was a matter of bloating a little or bloating a lot. Also, since the 9oz of BAB was no longer stopping cravings and since I also finally realized that all that morning protein was the cause of the cramps & runs that had grown stronger and stronger every since I started the leptin reset, I dialed back to 4-5oz each morning. No change in cravings for better or worse with that.)

I continued to get early AM light & temp exposure daily, keep my internet off when not using it, vacation in places with virtually zero EMF, etc. EMF does not seem to play a role in my issues. Even going to completely isolated areas of the US for three weeks in a row (no cell phone, no nighttime light, sleeping in a tent in desolate national parks in the wide expanses of the US west, certainly no wifi) changed nothing.

From Dec-Aug '13, I also experienced disturbing mood swings. I'd have random headaches, inability to cope with simple stresses, want to curl up and hide in a corner from simple interactions with my children, etc. That got better in many ways with the iodine, but then continued to decline in different ways, more slowly. I became unable to deal with stressful interactions with others, exploding in ways completely unlike me.

Then I finally tried the amino acids I'd been reading about for years. Actually, I started them in April '13, and while most people apparently see improvements "in 24 hours," it took 2-3 weeks of doses above the levels called for in The Diet Cure to see a difference, and I had to add the amino complex (recommended for people with anorexia or "severe endorphin depletion) to see a difference, but it worked. It gradually and strongly decreased my cravings. I lost about 7 of the lb I had gained and could choose to eat well, although not quite at the zero-cravings level I had seen before. When I went on said 3-week trip, I accidentally miscalculated the amount of AA's I needed to bring, and ran out. Within a week, I was bingeing hard again, nearly daily, and getting back on all the AA's didn't solve the problem anymore. I binged frequently and out of control, thinking and praying about what might be behind all this. I realized that the only thing that had ever helped my cravings was an increase in AA intake, whether through protein or through supplementation. But it seemed that it always worked, but gradually the effect wore down over time till I upped the ante again. It seemed to me that my problem was very likely a severe, severe endorphin depletion. This fit with all my history, including patterns of dissociation from all negative emotions, serious decrease in enjoyment of hobbies I used to love, and the fact that when I first read the main sentence in Julia Ross' paragraph on endorphins, I burst into sobs without knowing why.

So I started to think about looking for an inpatient treatment program for eating disorders that used amino acids, one that was about 10 days long (not the traditional 20 or 30 days). I called Ross' clinic to see about a visit to them, and looked for other clinics. Then POOF in my email inbox appeared an invitation to a local treatment program for drug & alcohol addicts that used IV amino acid therapy. It was a 10 day treatment. Talk about an answer to prayers. I signed up, and at the end of 10 days, had a complete elimination of sugar & carb cravings, but still strongly desired concentrated tart fruit. After a few more days (still continuing my full oral AA regimen), I had a complete cessation of all cravings. It has been like that in the two weeks since, although a slight increase for carb and fruit last week while on vacation and eating non-ideally with exposure to trace sugars. I go back for a booster next week and once a month for several more months. In addition to cravings being gone, I've had significant improvement in many other things, many, things that AA's had improved slightly, but are now majorly improved, including the emotional swings and other things I had never imagined would be changed.

The doctor running the clinic is also determined to help me get to the root of my issue, which he strongly believes (and blood tests showing terrible absorption of AA's from food) is poor digestion. Hence the bloating. He believes, and my experience seems to confirm, that I can't eat dairy without the right enzymes supplemented, and that I should never eat corn at all. We're working and praying together.

### MamaGrok — 2014-02-02T21:15:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-107966>

THe price is obscene, but most alcohol & drug treatment is. Typically, you pay $30k for 30 days (counseling, 12-step, maybe some medical treatment) and they have a 10-20% success rate. With places like this, you pay $15k for 10 days of IV AAs (counseling & 12-step may or may not be included) & they have a 90% success rate. I would say that \*for me\*, it was 100% worth it, but I can't say for sure until this time next year. I've had 6 months of success before (see my LR journey), so it ain't over till it's over.

They did not provide any meals. I ate my regular meals, and just stopped my oral AA regimen during the treatment, and picked it up again immediately after. They are also compounding a specialized AA blend based on my blood test results.

For my cravings, mood swings, and several other issues, it has WORKED. (so far) For bloating, well now I can get back to work on that, b/c it wasn't possible to work on it so long as I couldn't control my food intake.

I would recommend it for ANYone who has tried all the standard means of curing addiction and hasn't succeeded. I strongly believe it is much more likely to succeed than standard addiction treatment, or standard eating disorder treatment. But I can't say whether any given person struggling with cravings or mood stuff should. I would point such a person to The Diet Cure and/or The Mood Cure first, encourage doing everything in one of those books that appears even remotely relevant (read the whole book! don't skip parts like I did for so long!), then consider the crazy $$$ treatment if nothing else works.

I will point out again that I did go completely EMF-free for three weeks, and even while in the middle of nowhere, surrounded by natural light, water, space, & air, when I ran out of AA's, I started bingeing again, in the middle of the wilderness.

Thinking about amino acids and stuff.. and why someone seems to not be able to digest them properly - I really wonder how healing high meat and raw animal protein would be in such cases. ### freesia — 2014-02-03T23:02:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-108174>

> [Hope said:](/goto/post?id=108148)
>
> As an aside.....you've never had issues with mold have you? Just wondering. I'm on a big mold path bc I found out that is my issue....and now I think a lot of people with long standing issues would do well to at least check it out. Just because it added so much to my story.

Just adding my own mold path....

The first thing that happened last summer when I couldn't CT for a couple of weeks was a flare up of old sinus problems (sinus congestion, swelling and severe headache on the right side). I'd had eight months completely _symptom_ free to that point, after 15 years of problems. That flare up made it clear to me, despite general immune strengthening with regular use of CT, there's something in my sinuses, on the right side at least, that really needs dealing with.
Dr Joe Brewer has found mycotoxins (from three common types of household mold - after water damage) in the urine of most ME/CFS patients he's tested. I know a patient with a similar illness presentation to me has tested positive for all three. He thinks mold colonizes in the sinuses and thrives there protected by biofilm.

I have been using a colloidal silver nasal spray. Dr Brewers patients use a Nasa Touch, some use Colloidal Silver and others use a chelator then an antifungal to break sinus biofilm. The treatment seems very effective..and could be highly relevant, IMO, in at least some cases of mold sensitivity.
Silver also breaks the biofilm MRSA makes. When I first used it I unleashed a BEAST!  Anyone thinking of trying it...start slow.

I'll add some links to recent studies after I pull them up again. Just mentioning the above in case it's useful for other mold sensitive peeps. I believe mycotoxins and biotoxins can really hinder progress for some people if you don't get to the source.

### TheKid — 2014-07-07T16:18:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133394>

So I came over to this thread because it popped up in a search for vaccines somehow. Obviously not the topic. However, @[MamaGrok](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1281/) 's update regarding amino acid supplementation hit home and I thought I would share as well.

My doctor has been tracknig me every 3 months for the past year and half. We've built an impressive list of labs indeed; stuff I didn't know you could even test, such as neurotransmitters. So we've been tracking dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine and epinephrine in an effort to assess why I, like MamaGrok have been so resistant to improvement. In fact, after getting almost no bump in energy, mood and well-being from a cycle of testosterone, we turned to the neurotransmitters. As a result we've been playing with 5HTP, Acetyl L-Carinitine and L-Tyrosine. The first two failed to have much impact, and I suspect by initially addressing serotonin deficiency in the absence of addressing dopamine at the same time, we may have depleted dopamine.

What I suspect may be giving me a bump now is Mucuna Pruriens, which is basically L-DOPA, a catecholamine precursor ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mucuna\_pruriens).](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mucuna_pruriens) So at least for me, it may be shaping up that addressing the neurotransmitters is going to be key moving forward, especially since I have SNPs indicating a problem with neurotransmitter availability and lab findings to support that. I also continue to address mitochondrial performance, and my doc is essential in lock step with @[Jack](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5/) in that regard, despite not coming to his conclusion via the impact of EMF. He is however very open to the EMF stuff.

So, yeah ... neurotransmitters. If they suck, you're gonna suck. Take it from me, I've been doing this for years.

He is however very open to the EMF stuff.
>

This sounds like you are on a track I have walked and lately I am finding that the brain really is an energy hog; and when I work harder and longer using it, it needs more concentrated electrons from fat and some protein. I realized that because I was less physically active, I would make the mistaken assumption that the body needs less food. I do not have the time to spend all day outside and I feel that my redox limits my ability to gather photons directly so more high fat and cysteine foods are the key to keep the brain working. More stress also requires more hormones and when one is adrenal fatigued, I do not feel that there are ever enough from endogenous production. DHEA and pregnenolone titrated to stress levels seem essential. Never forget the inflammatory drivers such as parasites and other infections which take the system down quickly destroy reliable neurotransmitter cycling. It is a dance until when and if one can restore a robust and resilient redox to buffer the stresses of "modern" life. With the clarity I am gaining here, so far I am doing better than ever under more stress than I have experienced at anytime since I cared for my mother when she had cancer. As I understand it, the neurotransmitter cycling is way up at the top of the tree and is lost early in the loss of redox/energy. It is therefore one of the hardest to recover.

the challenge is how do you set yourself up when you're over stressed due to modern life and you can't unplug as much as you want/need to.... get as much grounding, photo electric effect and massive quantizes of DHA as possible....

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T04:17:41+0300

Stay engaged cantweight. We have a lot more to discover with one another before I am done on the net. Time. Most people cling to the idea the future is very much connected to the past and present. The notions of time, eternity, the end of time -- these are some of the most profound questions that we deal with as human beings.
But you have to listen very carefully to what nature's trying to tell you to discover fundamental truth. I think this is why Gretchen and John were first shocked but no longer scared. Sometime we have to look at what we are facing and think differently about the problem to see a new solution. For example, most physicists have failed to fully understand the nature of time because of the "manner" they insist on doing experiments -- smashing particles together in giant accelerators. This is the MO at CERN.
Maybe, instead of smashing particles to bits at CERN, we just need to give them a little push and watch the collisions in slow speed to discern things best? Yeah..........this thought is a bread crumb............

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T04:22:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133425>

What if more physicists took up the gentle sport of curling? Embrace your Canadian...........you can see what their athletes are doing, they set the stone going and they sweep a little bit to try to direct the stone going somewhere. Seems kinda nutty to do something to the ice to affect the collision of stones huh? Or is it really brilliant?
In a sense, this sweeping of the ice is kind of like a very gentle physical interaction. Think the electromagnetic force interaction on electrons and protons. You're not actually touching the stone, are you? You're kind of making the ice a little bit smoother by increasing the amount of protons on its surface by melting the ice a tad bit so it would tend to go in one direction versus another direction. I think many of my blogs are like sweeping the ice for those who read them..............

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T04:26:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133426>

Let us think about this deeper. With curling as our model of particle interaction, you can understand everything about the way _**time**_ really works in the universe by watching curling. Why you ask? Curling has a beginning and end..........when we watch this horribly boring sport, you can see the start at the beginning when the stone is thrown. This is very similar to the the idea of time Isaac Newton had 500 years ago. So, the stone starts from some definite place in the past, it goes to some definite place in the present, and it goes to a definite place in the future. So, from that perspective of classical physics, the universe looks like it's a big machine, like a big, very perfectly tuned clock. this idea has permeated science since Newton and all the way until today..............not even Einstein's brilliance has stopped anyone to think that maybe time is a real problem for experimental design in biology and medicine.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T04:33:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133429>

But then, about a century ago, along came Quantum Mechanics because of Planck, Heisenberg, Bohr, and Einstein. It was pushed along by Dirac and DeBroglie.
It took away all that **_certainty_** from the universe by unmasking the subatomic world. Certainty is something science and medicine believe in. It is something most of believe in............You may start getting the sense that I do not believe in it.
When we act with complete certainty. Do not look back. This certainty is only our own delusion. This is fundamentally why science and medicine are stuck.............you may not see it...........but it is a huge problem.

Let go of certainty. We should believe in our inspirations and trust out intuitions...We often think and feel that we are correct yet, we never know that that intuition is true. Become addicted to curiosity. You'll become open and understand paradox when no one else does. The greatest challenge in life is to come as we are, but never stop trying to go and learn and grow from where our roots lie.

Those roots are not in cause and effect...........time dictates one beleives in those things..........so if you reject one you must consider rejecting both.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T04:38:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133434>

The quantum world is very counterintuitive and different. It makes different predictions from the classical view of things that Newton and most of science still believes.
In quantum mechanics, you could start these curling stones the same and you notice that, incredibly, one stone goes to the left and the other stone goes to the right.
In the microscopic world of atoms, nothing is known for sure. Atoms are not solid, defined objects as the stones are, are they?
They are waves of probabilities that tell you where, when you look for a particle, you are most likely to find it.

Things are based upon the uncertainty principle in a fundamental way.

This is very counterintuitive but it has been proven by experiment time and time again.........so why do experiments look for cause and effect when nature is based upon probabilities?

### cantweight — 2014-07-08T04:46:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133438>

Reminds me of this....

You want a physicist to speak at your funeral. You want the physicist to talk to your grieving family about the conservation of energy, so they will understand that your energy has not died. You want the physicist to remind your sobbing mother about the first law of thermodynamics; that no energy is created in the universe and none is destroyed. You want your mother to know that all your energy, ever vibration, every BTU of heat, every wave of every particle that was her beloved child remains with her in this world. You want the physicist to tell your weeping father that amid the energies of the cosmos, you gave as good as you got.

And at one point, you'd hope that the physicist would step down from the pulpit and walk to your brokenhearted spouse there in the pew and tell him that all the photons that ever bounced off your face, all the particles whose paths were interrupted by your smile, by the touch of your hair, hundreds of trillions of particles, have raced off you like children, their ways forever changed by you. And as your widow rocks in the arms of a loving family, may the physicist let her know that all the photons that bounced from you were gathered in the particle detectors that are her eyes, that those photons created within her constellations of electromagnetically charged neurons whose energy will go on forever.

And the physicist will remind the congregation of how much of all our energy is given off as heat. There may be a few fanning themselves with their programs as he says it. And he will tell them that the warmth that flowed through you in life is still here, still part of all that we are, even as we who mourn continue in the heat of our own lives.

And you'll want the physicist to explain to those who loved you that they need not have faith; indeed, they should not have faith. Let them know that they can measure, that scientists have measured precisely the conservation of energy and found it accurate, verifiable and consistent across space and time. You can hope your family will examine the evidence and satisfy themselves that the science is sound and that they'll be comforted to know your energy is still around. According to the law of the conservation of energy, not a bit of you is gone. You're just less orderly. Amen.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T04:48:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133439>

still think its not right? Consider this: A subatomic particle will move or disappear if it's observed directly.
It's as if they have to put a particle in a box, not look at it, and allow it to carry on existing as they spread out a wave of probability. When they do that, they can begin to see the effect of the future on the present. So, we have the red boxes that are going forward in time. And now you have to think about the backward evolving state. So we're gonna represent that by blue boxes. Same particle, right? We have one particle. But coming from the future, we're saying the present is created out of a combination of the forward evolving and the backward evolving.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T04:54:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133440>

Why have not not talked deeply about magnetism yet? It is the most quantum weird of the 3 legged stool. Physicists have now tested this idea in the lab experiment.
They give a series of very gentle magnetic nudges to subatomic particles. They measure them at 2:00 and then at 2:30. They do this over and over again.
Some but not all of the particles are also measured again at 3:00. And what they found is that taking their measurement at 3:00 seemed to influence the apparently random readings they got at 2:30. The future seemed to affect the present, even though it hadn't happened yet. If you're trying to understand the present moment, the past is relevant, as we knew before, but the future is just as relevant to the present as the past. So far, these experiments have only been carried out on the microscopic level, and the effects of the future on the present are very subtle. Subtle in the quantum world, means that a small change can lead to massive alterations in energy by mass equivalence. When you consider that magnetism and technology uses this feature of nature you begin to realize that our future is clearly affecting our present..........this is fundamentally why Gretch and John could be both disturbed and at peace..........both of their brain's pre supposed what I wrote in EMF 7 because of blogs written before it. Do I use this idea to teach all of you? Yep............It is how your brain works by nature's laws and I decided to use it.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T04:56:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133441>

So why I am not as worried as many of you? This quantum weirdness has taught me a bigger lesson. It suggests that buried somewhere in the apparently random motion of all the particles in the Universe there is such a thing as cosmic destiny. There's an ocean flowing here. There's a constant current flowing from past to future and from future to past. This means the present has clues for us..............and it is our job to decipher that cipher. ### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T04:57:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133442>

We understand how planets, stars, and galaxies work. But to know the fate of the entire universe, just imagine how much more there is to know. So perhaps it's time to ask ourselves an important question. Are there some things we just aren't meant to understand?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T05:02:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133443>

Maybe the best way to understand eternity is to calculate how much we can ever know. And what we can know is what we can measure.
So, you can see the Pacific Ocean on TV, and the Pacific Ocean is huge by any account. We couldn't possibly measure it with rulers, so we measure it by using trigonometry, all kinds of math. The Pacific Ocean may be massive, but we've traversed its length and breadth and mapped out all of its 64 million square miles.
However, it isn't even a speck compared with the entire universe. Consider that scale for a moment. It is much too big for us to physically measure.
Our Universe -- we can't even get out there to most of it. And we measure it by receiving light from it, sending light out to it, and getting all kinds of signals.
And we figure out where things are, how far away they are. But the Universe does not just stretch out over space as most believe. They forget it also extends over time. So the Universe does the same, from its beginning in the Big Bang to the far future. What would it really take to know everything about such a vast place?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T05:09:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133444>

Consider a schematic theory or drawing of this conundrum: the theory of causal diamonds. This is my past. This is my future. And this diamond represents everything I could've done **_experiments_** on during that whole history from the beginning to the end. That region in space-time, it forms a diamond shape because light goes out in sort of a cone like this, and then if I look back from the latest time, it goes backwards in a cone. You put those two cones together, and they're sort of a diamond shape. A causal diamond marks the limit of how much of the Universe a measuring device could ever hope to reach. When that device sends out a light beam, it heads out into the Universe, bounces off some distant galaxies, and finally returns to the device billions of years later. Physicists been able to calculate that the amount of information existing inside that diamond is related to the area of a sphere that just fits around it at its widest point, a sphere he calls "the holographic screen.

This screen.........is found in the cosmos and I believe it is built into your brain. It links consciousness to memory bidirectionally.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T05:14:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133445>

So now we can ask the question, suppose there was some machine that lived forever from the beginning of the Universe to the end? How big does the holographic screen of the causal diamond of that infinitely long-lived detector ever get? And it's very important, because that determines how much information there could've possibly been in this region of space and time. Knowing absolutely everything there is to know about every atom and every subatomic particle in existence would mean collecting a truly mind-blowing amount of data. This number is 10 to the 10 to the 123. It's a 1 with 10 to the 123 zeros after it. That number is so huge that it's hard to imagine it. If I started trying to write that number down and I wrote a zero every second, I would run out of time long before the whole history of the Universe, and I would never get to the end of it.

Now think about the vase analogy I gave you in the blogs...........

We can collect some particles.........well.......we can never see the vase in total (uncertainity) but the more electrons and protons we collect we can make it out.........and our frontal lobes are designed to see and decipher patterns.........and predict things.

See any congruence yet?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T05:19:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133446>

Now back to the Pacific ocean thought experiment. Could an advanced civilization actually collect this much data and know everything about the Universe and thus learn its fate? The answer, is contained in this tiny cup of water. So, in this little bit of water I just got out of the Pacific, there are sextillion atoms. That's trillions of trillions. If we wanted to measure all those atoms, we'd have to have a really big machine. We'd need a device that was larger than the United States. But collecting data on the entire Universe is not just a monumental engineering challenge. The laws of physics actually prevent us from doing it. This may be more shocking to you. If we tried to measure every atom in existence, we would end up using so much equipment that we'd fill space with more stuff than it could handle, and the entire experiment would collapse into a black hole, destroying all that information with it.

So stop for a moment and think about how we study nature........we use experiments that do not fathom much less understand these problems when we design experiments. It means what we believe today is a house of cards..........because the future tells us something radically different. But today we are unaware of what we do not know.

In western cultures, we can't help but impose time geometry on our lives at every step. A person's life is like a line that stretches from birth to death, and so we "imagine" the Universe, too, must have a timeline -- from its birth in the Big Bang, to some far future date when it will die.
There was no time before the beginning, and time will eventually disappear when the Universe meets its apocalyptic end. The Amondawa tribe in Brazil does not live by a calendar, and they don't use clocks.
In fact, there isn't even a word for "time" in their language. They live as if there is no time..........I think they have it correct. Just sayin' and just thinking.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T05:27:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133450>

Physicists have calculated that we can measure no more than 10 to the 10 to the 90 bits of information before we cause the entire Universe to collapse into a black hole.
This may seem like a gigantic number, but it is actually just a tiny fraction of 10 to the 10 to the 123, which is all that there is to know.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T05:30:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133451>

That number is so incredibly smaller than this number, that there's no hope that any civilization, no matter how sophisticated, could possibly measure all of the information that there is in the Universe throughout its entire history. All we can ever learn about the Universe is an impossibly tiny morsel of what's out there. This is why your brain is organized around a hologram...........the whole picture of something can be recreated from a small piece of the part of the whole. It does not need all the particles in the universe to be understood............we just need the parts with the most energy and information to make educated guesses.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T05:38:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133452>

When you consider this small thought experiment you begin to realize trying to predict the future based on such scant knowledge is utterly futile based upon quantum mechanics. So why did EMF 7 not bother me as much as it bothered all of you? Why did Gretchen get it intuitively quickly? Because we intuitively know we can not know everything in totality. We can make guesses on the probabilities based upon the sub atomic particles we do understand. Electrons and protons. They are controlled by the electromagnetic force. Our society uses that force to our benefit and detriment. So, perhaps we should quit worrying about the end of time and learn to live for the now.
It's natural for us to want to know everything. And we like to make up stories about everything. And those stories are often wrong. So people are people. We're finite. I am finite and my ideas are finite based upon the patterns I can discern. None of us are Gods. We're small specks in a bigger whole. We don't own the Universe, we participate in its cycles. We're a very tiny portion of the Universe. And we've now discovered that we're a much tinier portion than we might've thought before. Fundamentally this is why religion and science really butt heads in my opinion. We don't have the right, in some sense, to expect to know everything that there is to know, so we make shit up about things we do not understand. Will the Universe last forever? Is eternity already out there, projecting the present back to us from the far future? Or will a cosmic apocalypse destroy everything in the blink of an eye? We don't know because we can not physically know, therefore we probably never will, because some questions require more knowledge than we can ever get. QED shows us this..........hard to fathom? Yes, but it is part of our reality. And maybe that's not so bad. After all, what fun would life be if we already knew how it was going to end?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-08T05:47:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133453>

So if you want to continue to think small.........do so. It is not about food, it is about something far bigger than most of us can fathom.

How do I bring this very abstract thought experiment back to E=mc2?

Why is change hard for us all? Why will science and medicine find what I just wrote hard to believe? Yep, because it breaks their franchise model. You find change hard because you forgot the power of momentum in physics. Only you can create momentum in your life. The most important thing you can do to achieve your goals is to make sure that as soon as you set them, you immediately begin to create momentum. Successful change requires first expending ten units of effort to produce one unit of results. You must get moving first to attain your goals to overcome the inertia or procrastination within you. Once you are moving, you need to stay moving. A boulder is hard to get moving but when it does it is power.**
Momentum represents movement towards the change. Monument represents movement in the past.** You must become momentum and not a monument.
Momentum is the hardest thing to start but its even harder to stop.
Monuments are for the ones that used to move forward but now stopped to celebrate and give up.
Momentum is fueled by challenging ourself with greater Vision. If you have limited Vision then your momentum will stop after you are successful.
Monuments are build by people who had limited vision and could see no further.
Momentum uses the power of gravity to its advantage. It tries to gain more momentum by leveraging gravity .
Monuments are built by people who are conquered by gravity. They no longer move forward since gravity has taken over.
Momentum builds up when you invest in people and build leaders. When you raise leaders you multiply, when you win followers you have addition.
Monuments are build to glorify your achievements and it tell people great you used to be.
Don't stop if you are moving forward. Don't have limited vision that involves success only for yourself. Don't settle down in this life by being satisfied with just a Monument .
Momentum shows that your have a greater vision, you are investing in building people, you are not easily satisfied, you keep challenging yourself to push higher.
Physics defines momentum as a product of mass and velocity. This means everything in the Universe is, at its core a thermodynamic problem.
In life, momentum is a product of inner vision and daily actions; what sets difference in momentum or our inner drive between different people is how they envision their goals, envision themselves and do something about it.

Science says momentum is a conserved quantity unless **_affected by external forces_**. (think epigenetics)

In life we can enhance or preserve our momentum by deciding what force we will expose our mind too. Will we be exposing our mind to positive forces , similar or higher spirited individuals to increase our momentum or to negative people or negative news , unfruitful activity to decrease the momentum.
We increase our "inner mass and velocity" is by increasing the energy we add energy to our life: This brings us to the realm of E= MC2 once again. How do you employ E=mc2 to your benefit?
\# setting big goals and following it till the end.

\# by noting that we all have something to contribute to the world.

\# taking inspiration from something positive of our past.

\# learning from the life of great individuals (either by meeting them ) or reading /learning.

\# Joining seminars / life skill learning events to get into the environment.

\# By remembering what we used to dream about positively impacting the world as a child.

\# Feeling valuable ourselves by embracing our curiosity.

\# Knowing the world is still a great place, full of wonderful people and things.

\# listening to self rather than some people who says it can't be done or it is impossible.

\# appreciating and feeling gratitude for what we already have and what is on it's way

“Reality isn’t the most pleasant of atmospheres, Lieutenant. But we like to think we’re engineered for it. It’s a pretty fine piece of engineering, the kind an engineer can respect. The human race will come and go.....the energy will remain. We are truly just its servants.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-09T01:09:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133570>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=133446)
>
> Now back to the Pacific ocean thought experiment. Could an advanced civilization actually collect this much data and know everything about the Universe and thus learn its fate? The answer, is contained in this tiny cup of water. So, in this little bit of water I just got out of the Pacific, there are sextillion atoms. That's trillions of trillions. If we wanted to measure all those atoms, we'd have to have a really big machine. We'd need a device that was larger than the United States. But collecting data on the entire Universe is not just a monumental engineering challenge. The laws of physics actually prevent us from doing it. This may be more shocking to you. If we tried to measure every atom in existence, we would end up using so much equipment that we'd fill space with more stuff than it could handle, and the entire experiment would collapse into a black hole, destroying all that information with it.
>
> So stop for a moment and think about how we study nature........we use experiments that do not fathom much less understand these problems when we design experiments. It means what we believe today is a house of cards..........because the future tells us something radically different. But today we are unaware of what we do not know.
>
> In western cultures, we can't help but impose time geometry on our lives at every step. A person's life is like a line that stretches from birth to death, and so we "imagine" the Universe, too, must have a timeline -- from its birth in the Big Bang, to some far future date when it will die.
> There was no time before the beginning, and time will eventually disappear when the Universe meets its apocalyptic end. The Amondawa tribe in Brazil does not live by a calendar, and they don't use clocks.
> In fact, there isn't even a word for "time" in their language. They live as if there is no time..........I think they have it correct. Just sayin' and just thinking.

even the headlines show how wrong we have it with our views : [http://www.bbc.com/news/science-environment-13452711](http://www.bbc.com/news/science-environment-13452711)

"lacks" abstract idea of time
in reality, the western reductionist world "lacks" life as it should appear, due to the constraints of the abstract idea of time!

### yewwei.tan — 2014-07-09T01:09:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133571>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=133544)
>
> Here is a random musing: **Humans are the universe in miniature, almost a caricature of nature’s full complexity.**

These random thoughts came up when I read this line.

First I though of this quote from Alan Kay:

Alan Kay: quoted in "Dealers of Lightning" by Michael Hiltzik ([http://www.amazon.com/dp/0887309895](http://www.amazon.com/dp/0887309895))

[It's almost impossible for most people to see technology as the tool rather than the end. People get trapped in thinking that anything in the environment is to be taken as a given. It's part of the way our nervous system works. But it's dangerous to take it as a given because then it controls you, rather than the other way around. That's McLuhan's insight, one of the bigger ones in the twentieth century. Zen in the twentieth century is about taking things that have been rendered invisible by this process and trying to make them visible again.](http://www.amazon.com/dp/0887309895)
​

Then I thought about the concept of 'the Technium' as put forth by Kevin Kelly in his book 'What Technology Wants' ([http://www.amazon.com/What-Technology-Wants-Kevin-Kelly/dp/B004Y6MT6O](http://www.amazon.com/What-Technology-Wants-Kevin-Kelly/dp/B004Y6MT6O)), and I remembered a random thought I had while reading Kelly's book: **Humans are one of the tools that the Universe uses to further its pursuit of complexity. Don't mistake the master for the servant.**

Then while searching for that Alan Kay quote, I saw this famous quote from Max Planck:

[A new scientific truth does not triumph by convincing its opponents and making them see the light, but rather because its opponents eventually die, and a new generation grows up that is familiar with it.](http://web.missouri.edu/~hanuscind/8710/Barber1961.pdf)
​

As well as this quote by David Hestenes:

David Hestenes: Notes for a Modeling Theory of Science, Cognition, and Instruction
[While science is a search for structure, mathematics is the science of structure.](http://geocalc.clas.asu.edu/pdf/NotesonModelingTheory.pdf)
​

And I said to myself, I already understand the math of special relativity. Now I need to understand quantum mechanics so that I can replace the dying breed

\===

Sidenote: I found that Alan Kay quote on Bret Victor's quotes page. It's worth a browse -- [http://worrydream.com/#!/quotes](http://worrydream.com/#!/quotes)

### SeaHorse — 2014-07-09T01:27:46+0300

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=133425)
>
> What if more physicists took up the gentle sport of curling? Embrace your Canadian...........you can see what their athletes are doing, they set the stone going and they sweep a little bit to try to direct the stone going somewhere. Seems kinda nutty to do something to the ice to affect the collision of stones huh? Or is it really brilliant?
> In a sense, this sweeping of the ice is kind of like a very gentle physical interaction. Think the electromagnetic force interaction on electrons and protons. You're not actually touching the stone, are you? You're kind of making the ice a little bit smoother by increasing the amount of protons on its surface by melting the ice a tad bit so it would tend to go in one direction versus another direction. I think many of my blogs are like sweeping the ice for those who read them..............

I love this curling vision….but have you ever been to a game?? There's a lot of screaming and crazy beating/sweeping and then nothing…no sounds…then …a tiny little sweep and then a huge cry "HURRY HARD" and they all go mad again….I find it incredibly amusing. My husband finally thinks you might be on to something if you can link curling, the cosmos and our journey to health all in one post…you scored a snowman with this one!

gretchen - what you just said made me realise something.
We had a restaurant for 35 years and made everything from scratch ...the one question everyone asked me over and over and over - "How do you know how much food to prepare? for how many people?"
My answer was always the same " I just know" and I did! How would I know to factor in a bus load of tourists?
How could I possibly know how many people would show up for lunch or dinner - How many people from out of town came back for a visit? How many people from the university would bring out of town guests? and it goes on and on.... quantum energy....

### nonchalant — 2014-07-09T05:13:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-mamagrok-still-visit-these-forums.9537/post-133635>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=133572)
>
> I love this curling vision….but have you ever been to a game?? There's a lot of screaming and crazy beating/sweeping and then nothing…no sounds…then …a tiny little sweep and then a huge cry "HURRY HARD" and they all go mad again….I find it incredibly amusing. My husband finally thinks you might be on to something if you can link curling, the cosmos and our journey to health all in one post…you scored a snowman with this one!

When Jack brought up the curling analogy, I thought of Strong and Weak forces. When the Newtonian forces become weaker, other forces come into play. Or at least we start noticing them more. I'm not sure what to make of it yet, though. That, and Jack said to listen with the intent of understanding, not responding. Well, it's been a few hours, perhaps I can respond just a bit?

My dear father, when I was very young, used to make me wait. I would ask some question, and he wouldn't reply for what seemed an eternity to my young mind. When he finally spoke, it was to start telling a story. I would listen, in a bit of confusion, to his new story. Finally, he tied it in with my question, which I had all but forgotten in the mean while... I think he was trying to teach me to think. Not sure if the lesson stuck, though. Hey, I was only about 8 years old!
