### K123 — 2014-02-02T21:11:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-query.9562/post-107965>

Hi! I try to buy wild caught fish whenever I can find it. But when it comes to other seafood like squids, scallops, oysters, mussels etc I really do like them all but I can't figure out if they have to be wild or not. Does anyone have any pointers for me? I have never seen these being sold as wild. With prawns I try to buy cold water Atlantic prawns which are tiny but at least they are not farmed!

### caroline — 2014-02-02T22:40:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-query.9562/post-107977>

Food is only 10% ...you have bigger things to worry about ...like your non native EMF exposure and water and sunshine.

Tell us more ... please start a journal. Dr. K. likes journals so he can get some context.

### nicld — 2014-02-03T16:05:55+0200

### sooperb — 2014-02-03T18:52:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-query.9562/post-108129>

I think I'd go for tinned salmon that's wild caught than fresh that's farmed. I also think that the size and location of the fishery is important. In Scotland they have fish farms which lose a percentage of fish to disease, wild fish sometimes get in and infect the farmed which have little natural resistence and worse still, some of the farmed get out and infect their sea faring brothers. I have switched to atlantic farmed shrimp as I'm sure I read there's actually nothing wrong with them. They have a lovely sweet taste, better than the meatier tiger prawns. Line caught tuna is available tinned too. I like that better than fresh, especially canned in olive oil.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-03T04:07:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-query.9562/post-240384>

The topic of radiation in seafood is overrated and misunderstood (few know about hormesis and transcription factors like PARP1 and Ku70). Sharing this for people who say, "I'm not sure about eating seafood due to Fukushima." [https://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/seafood-fukushima-radiation\_us\_56d4f6e6e4b0871f60ec971f](https://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/seafood-fukushima-radiation_us_56d4f6e6e4b0871f60ec971f)

Radiation has been condemned by the entire scientific community as being a “dangerous bar”. This is surprising, however, since there is strong scientific evidence for the molecular mechanisms of how transient “radiation drinks” could promote health. I tell my patient's seafood radiation has many surprising benefits. Today we know that low doses of radiation stimulate DNA repair through the activation of four transcription factors, PARP-1, PARP2, ATM, and Ku70. In response to low dose, sublethal radiation (or xenohormetic compounds). PARP-1 and PARP-2 activate two DNA repair pathways called “Single strand break repair” (SSBR) and one called “Base Excision Repair” (BER). Both of these repair single-stranded DNA breaks. In response to low dose, sublethal radiation (or xenohormetic compounds), Ku-70 activates the DNA repair pathway called “Non-homologous end joining” (NHEJ). [https://scholar.google.com/scholar?...+parp1+parp2&btnG=&hl=en&as\_sdt=0,22&as\_vis=1](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=radiation+DNA+repair+parp1+parp2&btnG=&hl=en&as_sdt=0%2C22&as_vis=1)
Science evolves even if your thinking remains stagnant. We must expand our grip of science…………or risk falling from the cliff. I tell this to my patients and all the alternative functional medicine doctors who keep telling their patients seafood is bad. It is really bad advice based on outdated thinking.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-03T04:09:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-query.9562/post-240385>

Eat your damn seafood. DHA helps fix the inflammatory reflex because it improves the efferent and afferent reflex arcs of this reflex from the brainstem. How much do you know about the inflammatory reflex that controls all gut and brain inflammation? It is huge for autism and autoimmunity. The vagus nerve has an important role in the regulation of metabolic homeostasis of the microbiome, and efferent vagus nerve-mediated cholinergic signaling controls immune function and proinflammatory responses via the inflammatory reflex. Dysregulation of metabolism and immune function in obesity are associated with chronic inflammation in the gut that cannot be turned off. The critical step allows a loss of control of the circadian release of insulin with the pathogenesis of insulin resistance and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Cholinergic mechanisms within the efferent pathway of inflammatory reflex have, in the past 2 years, been implicated in attenuating autistic and obesity-related inflammation and metabolic complications. This pathway synapses in the celiac ganglion which send fibers to the gut and spleen where immune cells can be altered from their normal programming. The efferent part of the reflex is supposed to release norepinephrine (NE) that binds to T regulatory cells. These T cells are activated by NE to trigger production of acetylcholine that binds to macrophages receptors which produce TNF in the spleen and gut where massive arrays of T regulatory cells are located. When acetylcholine binds to the alpha-7 nAChR is causes the complete shut down of TNF production in the gut and spleen inhibiting two key molecular pathways of inflammation. I talked about the TNF and NF-kappa beta way back in the leptin series.
One pathway controls NF-kappa beta that instructs genes in the nucleus of the macrophage to initiate making TNF. The other pathway governs the release of IL-1 and other inflammatory molecules. Research has now been worked out that shows that the immune response in humans can be controlled by the nervous system. Stimulation of the vagus nerve and quieting the output of the PVN is the key options that must be met for helping these people out. LUPUS is an autoimmune disease. Researchers have now found that when lupus was triggered by crystalline silica, a toxic mineral, and DHA blocked the activation of the disease. "96% of the lung lesions were stopped with DHA," noted one of the study authors. Silica has some unusual effects on the polarization of tissues. DHA works in non-linear fashion help the situation. [https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2016/09/160929133613.htm](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2016/09/160929133613.htm)

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-03T15:01:09+0300

### Maggie P — 2018-05-03T19:01:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-query.9562/post-240421>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=240384)
>
> The topic of radiation in seafood is overrated and misunderstood (few know about hormesis and transcription factors like PARP1 and Ku70). Sharing this for people who say, "I'm not sure about eating seafood due to Fukushima." [https://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/seafood-fukushima-radiation\_us\_56d4f6e6e4b0871f60ec971f](https://www.huffingtonpost.com/entry/seafood-fukushima-radiation_us_56d4f6e6e4b0871f60ec971f)
>
> Radiation has been condemned by the entire scientific community as being a “dangerous bar”. This is surprising, however, since there is strong scientific evidence for the molecular mechanisms of how transient “radiation drinks” could promote health. I tell my patient's seafood radiation has many surprising benefits. Today we know that low doses of radiation stimulate DNA repair through the activation of four transcription factors, PARP-1, PARP2, ATM, and Ku70. In response to low dose, sublethal radiation (or xenohormetic compounds). PARP-1 and PARP-2 activate two DNA repair pathways called “Single strand break repair” (SSBR) and one called “Base Excision Repair” (BER). Both of these repair single-stranded DNA breaks. In response to low dose, sublethal radiation (or xenohormetic compounds), Ku-70 activates the DNA repair pathway called “Non-homologous end joining” (NHEJ). [https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=radiation+DNA+repair+parp1+parp2&btnG=&hl=en&as\_sdt=0,22&as\_vis=1](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=radiation+DNA+repair+parp1+parp2&btnG=&hl=en&as_sdt=0%2C22&as_vis=1)
> Science evolves even if your thinking remains stagnant. We must expand our grip of science…………or risk falling from the cliff. I tell this to my patients and all the alternative functional medicine doctors who keep telling their patients seafood is bad. It is really bad advice based on outdated thinking.

Thanks for pointing out that science is always evolving, and we shouldn't get stuck. I left conventional medicine care in 1990, and it's been a long journey to really understand what health is, but you are right that holistic doctors get stuck in their paradigm just like conventional ones. I fell prey to that, and I don't want to, yet I did. We all have a tendency to love dogma, maybe because it provides certainty in an uncertain world. Your book and this forum have really opened my eyes, and I look forward to the many benefits I can reap from applying what you share here. I'm going to step back and rethink everything I've come to believe. Don't hesitate to poke me if I backslide.  I'm very grateful you invest so much time in spreading this information.

just find young tuna fillets. Young tuna under the age of 9 months have very little to no mercury. The garbage you buy in the stores like Bumble Bee brand come from very old tuna weighing over 350 lbs. With age comes mercury. Most people aren't aware if you eat the meat near the tail of a fish it has less mercury.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-05T05:28:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-query.9562/post-240536>

**Belonging means being yourself**
Human beings are social. We survive and thrive when we come together. We naturally seek belonging. We want to be part of something that represents what we value. Whether it be the neighborhood in which we choose to live, the friends with whom we associate, or the social environments in which we choose to spend our time, we are drawn to the places and to the people that allow us to feel the greatest sense of belonging. When we feel like we don’t belong, it prevents us from being our natural best. We either withdraw into solitude or depression or we struggle to find the connection we crave.

**Fitting in feels fake**
Fitting in is very different. Fitting in means changing parts of who we are so that we are perceived as acceptable, lovable, or popular within a group or tribe. Mitochondriacs abhor groupthink. As you can imagine, this is easy to detect in a school setting. In my experience, it was high school. We had very distinct groups of kids, as most schools do. The jocks, the stoners, the rich kids, the tree-huggers … the list goes on.

Instead of expending energy to fit into the group, it’s better to expend energy to find the group in which we fit.

Fitting in lacks authenticity. Belonging oozes authenticity because you have skin in the game. Fitting in feels fake because you are living a life by someone else's narrative. Belonging feels natural in nature. Fitting in takes so much energy. Belonging gives us energy. Fitting in creates fickle relationships. Belonging breeds trust and love.

Mitochondriacs feel well only when they are unleashed in nature doing what she wants us to do. Humans must be biased toward her ways and not their own

# Thread: Smartphone Use at Night May Lower Productivity
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/smartphone-use-at-night-may-lower-productivity.9566/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ATL_Paleo — 2014-02-03T00:40:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/smartphone-use-at-night-may-lower-productivity.9566/post-108018>

[http://psychcentral.com/news/2014/01/23/smartphone-use-at-night-may-lower-productivity/64892.html](http://psychcentral.com/news/2014/01/23/smartphone-use-at-night-may-lower-productivity/64892.html)

The ubiquitous smartphone may ironically be counterproductive to work efficiency as a new study suggests use of the device for late night work lowers next day productivity.

In a pair of studies over a broad spectrum of U.S. workers, Michigan State Universityâ€™s Russell Johnson, Ph.D., and colleagues found that people who monitored their smart phones for business purposes after 9 p.m. were more tired and were less engaged the following day on the job.

â€œSmartphones are almost perfectly designed to disrupt sleep,â€￾ said Johnson â€” who acknowledges keeping his own smartphone at his bedside at night.

â€œBecause they keep us mentally engaged late into the evening, they make it hard to detach from work so we can relax and fall asleep.â€￾

# Thread: Scrambled eggs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scrambled-eggs.9571/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 40
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-02-03T10:35:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scrambled-eggs.9571/post-108071>

I wonder how people like to do these.

They seem quite a good option -- cleaning the pan aside -- since you can get a good bit of fat into them. Same, to some extent with omelettes, but not with boiled or even fried eggs.

I understand the classic method is as follows. Take a small thick-based saucepan and, off the heat, put in a tablespoon of butter for each person; break in two eggs per person. (There's no need to beat the eggs first, because they get plenty of stirring from before the pan begins to warm and because, classically, you don't add milk.) So then, on a very low heat, stir pretty much continuously, removing from the heat temporarily, if the mixture begins to thicken too quickly and unevenly. When it begins to thicken nicely but has not gone to far and begun to set, season with salt and pepper and serve. You can also "finish" it with a tablespoon of double cream, letting it heat briefly afterwards, and finally stir in a handful of chopped fresh herbs, if you've got them. the secret seems to be very slow and even heating: I believe in the old days chefs would even use a _bain marie_ (a water-bath) to ensure that.

Here's what I tried this morning. I thought I had some ham, but hadn't, but I had some ducjk eggs and some peppers and spring onions. I put 2 teaspoons of duck fat and 1 of ghee in a saucepan (I'm avoiding milk solids at the moment). In that I gently cooked the chopped vegetables. Took it off the heat and cracked in the duck eggs while stirring, so they didn't start to cook before they were mixed. I then continued cooking on the heat, finishing with salt and pepper and some turmeric.

It was too bland and too sweet. I think _something_ like this should work for a quick Paleo breakfast, but this was not quite right. Butter seems just right, and other fats just aren't the same. Maybe it needed more salt (since butter is usually salted already)? Perhaps spring onions are really only for salad?

### Lahelada — 2014-02-03T13:10:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scrambled-eggs.9571/post-108079>

I think scrambled eggs all depends on the quality of the egg otherwise they are a bland food. I like your breakfast but what i would do is this . I would cook teh ham vegetables separately so you can fry them hard which will give them more flavour. I do scrambled eggs as you do,perhaps with the exception that both the butter and the eggs are put into a cold pot and then put onto the fire. Maybe I misunderstood and you do the same.

### Andrea — 2014-02-03T13:19:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scrambled-eggs.9571/post-108080>

I like my scrambled eggs moist and creamy, which is achieved by low heat and patience.
And I like them fat...

<Geek warning>

I melt a big tablespoon of coconut oil per egg in a pot and when it's warm enough to make a water drop dance I add the eggs. In the eggs I have some salt and vinegar (lemon juice etc. would work fine as well) because:
\- acids decrease the pH, which decreases the proteins' negative charge
\- salt dissolves into positive and negative charged ions that cluster around the charged proteins and neutralize them
So, the proteins won't be as repelled from each other and will bond together sooner, at a lower temperature, while they still can't connect with each other as tightly = moist and creamy.

Anyway, I stir constantly and remove from heat while they are still somewhat undercooked, because they will keep thickening due to the residual heat.

<Side note>
If you add milk/cream to the eggs, the proteins will be surrounded by more water and the proteins will have to be warmer to find a bond, i.e. increased cooking temperature.
Also, if you add some form of sugar, sucrose molecules will surround the proteins, which also will result in an increased cooking temperature.
<End of side note>

<End of geek warning>

Having said that, I prefer my scrambled eggs creamy and simple, with a little black pepper and salt on top.
Maybe with a grass fed beef chorizo on the side.

Thanks, Lahelada. Yes, I usually do the same as you.

This time I was trying to have vegetables in the eggs, but it didn't really work. Not a good choice of vegetables perhaps -- just what I happened to have. And maybe, as you say, it was the eggs that were too bland. I like duck eggs, but I think hen's eggs might be better in a dish like this.

I had what one does with one of those Italian open-faced omelettes (frittata?) partly in mind. I've seen those suggested as a paleo breakfast and used to make them with all kinds of seasonal veg and sometimes chopped bacon or ham or grated gruyere as well. They do take longer, though, and need putting under the grill, so it's more fuss on a weekday.

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Coconut oil then, Rerun. Jimmy Moore uses that -- though I think he says he uses a frying pan and cooks the eggs fast so that the whites and yolks cook somewhat separately.

I might try coconut oil. I don't really care for it, though. I sometimes eat a spoonful from the jar for health's sake, but the taste doesn't suit me somehow. I do like coconut flesh and coconut water, just not the oil.

### AnnieW — 2014-02-04T04:07:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scrambled-eggs.9571/post-108239>

I make mine very different and people like them. They are not creamy or runny but fluffy and drier. I crack them into a bowl with a good splash of milk (I use maybe a tbs per egg) and then whip them by hand with a whisk. I then take a huge knob of butter and heat it over medium until it gets hot and then add the eggs. I cook mine until they are totally done and fluffy. I have to have mine with cheddar cheese melted in with salt on top. My husband eats his with mozarella cheese and BBQ sauce and Texas Pete.

I know culinarily speaking it isn't the correct way to make them but I just can't do warm and "loose" eggs. I'm not scared of getting sick, it's just a textural issue for me. I don't have them much anymore because with PCOS I'm trying to stay off the dairy. Now I just fry them in coconut oil.

### AnnieW — 2014-02-04T04:13:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scrambled-eggs.9571/post-108242>

I have both refined and unrefined coconut oil. I use unrefined when I want the coconut flavor.

As for the veggie/egg thing, I never got into it. I make omelets for my husband but the veggies must be cooked separately because they tend to expel a lot of water when they cook.

Guys, have you seen the little scrambled egg muffins? You can also line the pans with bacon.

[http://www.tasteofhome.com/recipes/scrambled-egg-muffins](http://www.tasteofhome.com/recipes/scrambled-egg-muffins)

### Andrea — 2014-02-05T10:37:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scrambled-eggs.9571/post-108438>

How is it with the biotin vs avidin story?
I once learnt that raw egg whites should be avoided, because they contain avidin, which binds biotin. I remember some source saying that the avidin from one egg white can bind more biotin than there is in one yolk and thus cause biotin deficiency.
So I mostly fry my eggs - I fry them so that the whites coagulate more or less completely (which of course denatures the other proteins as well) and put raw yolks on top.

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=108471)
>
> Gangrad I feed the ducks myself so I know what they get.. they do make a lot of mess LOL I do take care there have a pretty nice winter home tho.

I think that's probably the key.

Pork you've got potential problems with stuff like trichinosis; chicken and eggs, with salmonella. I suspect it all goes back to questionable animal husbandry practices, though.

I've heard Sally Fallon say that Louis Pasteur's solution for milk was actually a solution for filthy urban dairies in 19th-century Paris where the cows were fed on spent brewery grain and not much else. Yikes! I suspect she's right. Heat-treating the foodstuff seems a poor solution beside addressing the problem at a system-wide level.

Do you know Joel Salatin's stuff? E.g.

[http://www.amazon.com/Sheer-Ecstasy-Being-Lunatic-Farmer/dp/0963810960/](http://www.amazon.com/Sheer-Ecstasy-Being-Lunatic-Farmer/dp/0963810960/)

Joel Salatin says he's only once had a bad case of sickness in animals, and he now realises it was the result of his doing something stupid.

He talks of moving animals around to keep the farm healthy, pointing out that if you put cows on concrete and stuck in a small corner of your farm, you've got all their dung and urine concentrated in a small area, which (a) causes pollution problems and (b) will also mean you have poor meadows because none is falling there. He has it all worked out -- like a kind of symbiosis -- so that his chickens scratch around where his cattle were, and so on. So clever.

I've seen old accounts that speak of Plains Indian encampments as being filthy. But, being nomadic, Plains Indians never stayed anywhere long, so that wouldn't have mattered.

I think you get problems where you have permanent set-ups that are dirty, overly cramped, and not properly thought out at a systems level.

I should suppose that Paleolithic people ate the eggs of waterfowl raw. You can roast eggs in the ashes of a fire, and maybe they did that, too, but I bet they usually didn't bother. If we really can't do the same, the question has to be: "Why?"

I bet no-one's going to stop the French making mayonnaise.

### Tanya — 2014-02-05T21:26:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scrambled-eggs.9571/post-108525>

In addition to my previously mentioned reason for avoiding egg whites in general, I would be especially concerned about raw egg whites. They contain avidin - glycoprotein that binds to biotin and prevents its absorption. Cooking egg, destroys only one third of avidin. Raw egg whites also contain inhibitors of trypsin enzyme and therefore significantly reduce protein digestion

### badger — 2014-02-05T21:29:09+0200

minus the rustic country bread...

# Thread: Does human energy production have any limits?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-human-energy-production-have-any-limits.9573/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tony D — 2014-02-03T14:59:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-human-energy-production-have-any-limits.9573/post-108088>

When demanding more energy from your body than it is used to providing, mitochondria will grow bigger and divide (mitochondrial biogenesis) to keep up with demand. This has been shown to occur after just an hour of exercise. There is a bicycle race called the Race Across America, where cyclists travel 3000 miles in under 10 days. This would seem to smash any notions of a finite boundary on physical effort. Do we really have any limits on energy production?

(I would like to see the labs on these competitors before and after the event!)

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=alEHvr-zKm0](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=alEHvr-zKm0)

### ATL_Paleo — 2014-02-03T17:01:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-human-energy-production-have-any-limits.9573/post-108108>

Ben Greenfield wrote an article on his website awhile back about his labs before and after a major competition. The after labs had "Trashed" as the word.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-03T17:47:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-human-energy-production-have-any-limits.9573/post-108120>

Yes it does......it is tied to mitochondrial efficiency and capacity.......do I think we can improve it? Yes......CT is the best way to do both.

### Tony D — 2014-02-03T17:50:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-human-energy-production-have-any-limits.9573/post-108122>

I would imagine the fact that many of the racers experience hallucinations along the way, has something to do with overheating?

### Tony D — 2014-02-03T22:07:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-human-energy-production-have-any-limits.9573/post-108162>

> [ATL\_Paleo said:](/goto/post?id=108108)
>
> Ben Greenfield wrote an article on his website awhile back about his labs before and after a major competition. The after labs had "Trashed" as the word.

Thanks ATL\_Paleo, think I found it. I have never seen such a disciplined prep and recovery regimen for an athlete, but he was in bad shape for sure after back-to-back triathlons, with a sharp elevation in inflammation (CRP). Unfortunately, his cortisol levels were abnormally high BEFORE the races, which he determined to be a symptom of his stressful lifestyle. I think that one factor may have had a big influence on his post-lab results. No matter what, this is an amazing amount of energy expenditure in a short period of time. Can't say its healthy, but I am impressed with his dedication to performance!
[http://www.bengreenfieldfitness.com...-you-do-a-hard-workout-triathlon-or-marathon/](http://www.bengreenfieldfitness.com/2013/05/what-happens-to-your-body-after-you-do-a-hard-workout-triathlon-or-marathon/)

### Josh — 2014-02-07T02:46:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-human-energy-production-have-any-limits.9573/post-108802>

Consider:

[http://www.sheknows.com/living/articles/1007019/cancer-survivor-helene-neville-runs-across-america](http://www.sheknows.com/living/articles/1007019/cancer-survivor-helene-neville-runs-across-america)

training?

[http://evoultrafit.com/stories/helene-neville/](http://evoultrafit.com/stories/helene-neville/)

I am not endorsing this. I will say that it challenges one's redox potential and she must have a good one.

No labs though and the cancer does seem to reappear...

### Josh — 2014-02-07T03:00:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-human-energy-production-have-any-limits.9573/post-108803>

The most intense thing I ever did was NOLS Alaska Mountaineering when I was 19. Skied with about 100lbs on my back up glaciers, camped at 9,000 feet and climbed a few mountains between 10-13,000 and skied down some more glaciers. Moved and or climbed everyday for 30 days. Some people had not even trained for it. All were under 35. Living on water, drinking melted snow, eating a lot of fat and a lot of carbs-about 5000 cal/day. Before I got sick at 38, I could still do things like go from 3,000 to 11,000 feet in a day, camp and get up in the middle of the night to climb a 14,000 peak in snow and ice with 2 liters of water and 1,000 cal assorted carbs with some fat. I got caught in a blizzard on top and had to come down in whiteout. Needed to keep moving or die. Starting hallucinating at 13 hours, stopped at 14 and came into camp to find my guide and father pretty much resigned to my death at 15 hours. No rescue teams and helicopters in that area. Had some tea and dinner, went to sleep and got up to walk down the mountain. Note in Weston Price the description of the driver whose truck breaks down in the Yukon. He gets out, walks 60 miles, gets another truck and comes back without sleep, etc.. Tom Brown Jr. describes how his mentor could walk/run over 100 miles a day in his 80's in the cold. We are surprising creatures who rarely get anywhere near our limits....

### Tony D — 2014-02-07T18:36:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-human-energy-production-have-any-limits.9573/post-108906>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=108803)
>
> The most intense thing I ever did was NOLS Alaska Mountaineering when I was 19. Skied with about 100lbs on my back up glaciers, camped at 9,000 feet and climbed a few mountains between 10-13,000 and skied down some more glaciers. Moved and or climbed everyday for 30 days. Some people had not even trained for it. All were under 35. Living on water, drinking melted snow, eating a lot of fat and a lot of carbs-about 5000 cal/day. Before I got sick at 38, I could still do things like go from 3,000 to 11,000 feet in a day, camp and get up in the middle of the night to climb a 14,000 peak in snow and ice with 2 liters of water and 1,000 cal assorted carbs with some fat. I got caught in a blizzard on top and had to come down in whiteout. Needed to keep moving or die. Starting hallucinating at 13 hours, stopped at 14 and came into camp to find my guide and father pretty much resigned to my death at 15 hours. No rescue teams and helicopters in that area. Had some tea and dinner, went to sleep and got up to walk down the mountain. Note in Weston Price the description of the driver whose truck breaks down in the Yukon. He gets out, walks 60 miles, gets another truck and comes back without sleep, etc.. Tom Brown Jr. describes how his mentor could walk/run over 100 miles a day in his 80's in the cold. We are surprising creatures who rarely get anywhere near our limits....

Wow, Josh! You still like hiking in the snow? These stories are amazing, and yes, I don't think we have good grasp at what our bodies are capable of. I imagine MOTIVATION plays a significant role. World records are not broken during practice, they are broken during competition. These stories of seemingly heroic effort, including your own, illustrate our ability to accomplish a task, whether it be to win, to inspire, or to survive. Looks like we can set our goals quite a bit higher than we are used to, but sometimes we need a little push!

### Josh — 2014-02-07T18:37:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-human-energy-production-have-any-limits.9573/post-108907>

Then there is this kind of thing......

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-devon-26086133](http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-devon-26086133)

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-wales-25526541](http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-wales-25526541)

### Josh — 2014-02-07T18:37:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-human-energy-production-have-any-limits.9573/post-108908>

NOLS courses are good for getting pushed, next best thing to the military I guess....

Go look for the story of Rheinhold Messner looking for his brother on top of an 8000 meter peak in the Himalaya above 8000 meters fro 7 days without food and little water in technical conditions alone...He never found his brother, but he lived.

### sjoshua — 2014-02-07T22:58:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-human-energy-production-have-any-limits.9573/post-108949>

Wow, great stories. Reminds me of a quote from The Matrix: "The body cannot live without the mind."

Whatever you believe is possible in your mind can be achieved. As soon as your mind doubts success, success will doubt you.

### nicld — 2014-02-03T22:41:49+0200

But just do it, it will help, trust me. Do you use a digital thermometer and how long do you leave it in? I have found that if I check the reading just after it beeps then leave it is for another minute I get two different temps.

I think I remember Jack saying that a lower body temp is not too bad but I cannot recall if he said how low.

### freesia — 2014-02-03T23:11:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/300-hypothyroid-symptoms.9575/post-108177>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=108161)
>
> I can relate to that. My temp. rarely goes above 95 F. Kind of hard to do CT with that, but knowing how important it is, I just have to do it...

I started with a really low basal temp and frequent blue fingernails and cold extremities in response to even a minor cold temperature change. The key is not how hard and fast you go but consistency.....always just a little colder or longer...even if you start with a 70 F bath.

And if you have a difficult warm up after one session then the progress you should be looking for the next day is NOT a further reduction in temp or duration - you'll put yourself off that way by making it too hard - but a slightly easier warm up. Pace yourself based on _your_ own responses - not someone else's - so you fall forward gently.

### Tanya — 2014-02-04T16:54:09+0200

I switched to the digital thermometer a while back for convenience and it is close enough with all the previous readings that I was getting with the old one. Thank you for your suggestion - I will leave it on a little longer after it beeps tomorrow and see if it would make any difference.

### Tanya — 2014-02-04T16:57:58+0200

# Thread: Any techies out there?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-techies-out-there.9576/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### K123 — 2014-02-03T17:44:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-techies-out-there.9576/post-108117>

Hi guys
I want to specifically talk to people who's work involves working on computers all day long. I would like to learn how you balance your occupation with your health. I have bought blue blockers now so I use them in the evenings. My husband refuses to switch the wi-fi off because first he is not convinced about the impact of EMF on our health and secondly we live in flats and we get wi-fi from all the neighbouring flats and he doesn't think switching off ours it going to help in any way. Would love to learn from your experience.

### sooperb — 2014-02-03T18:54:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-techies-out-there.9576/post-108130>

I spend a lot of time at my computer. I bought an earthing mat that I use with my bare feet.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-02-03T19:12:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-techies-out-there.9576/post-108131>

I am a software engineer, blogger, smartphone addict and almost all-things IT related, and I spend almost all day long in front of the PC, bad bad. So far, I've not implemented many changes in that regard, just installed f.lux although I don't use it very often because honestly, it's a little bit unstable and once it crashed I forget to run it again, I don't believe it will be so effective as true blue light blockers, which I always swear I'll buy but in the end I forget...  I also have some supps for mitochondrial support as I'm also an endurance runner... so lots of stress to my body, and drink a lot of non fluoridated water in the hope of getting rid of the terror

I'm pretty sure I'll implement some more of the EMF Rx tips any time sooner or later, by the mean time will be interested to hear what more techies have to say

### nicld — 2014-02-03T20:12:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-techies-out-there.9576/post-108141>

> [K123 said:](/goto/post?id=108117)
>
> Hi guys
> I want to specifically talk to people who's work involves working on computers all day long. I would like to learn how you balance your occupation with your health. I have bought blue blockers now so I use them in the evenings. My husband refuses to switch the wi-fi off because first he is not convinced about the impact of EMF on our health and secondly we live in flats and we get wi-fi from all the neighbouring flats and he doesn't think switching off ours it going to help in any way. Would love to learn from your experience.

I have grounded both my work laptop and home laptop, turned off Wi-Fi at home (can't at work except on my computer) and I have 250# pull magnets under my desk and where I sit at home. We also live in the country with all buried powerlines so that helps.

The other thing I do is lots of cold cold CT. I try for 45-50 minutes 2X during the week and 4-5 hours on the weekends. Like Jack, I really like my job so I need to do what I can to offset the non-native EMF's that I get at work.

Next step might be to get a magnetico sleep pad. I got one for the youngest daughter but none for us yet since I need a king ($$$$$$). Blueblockers will help as well as trying to turn the lights down/off at night (hubby won't turn everything off).

Unplug your at night and keep the phones out of the bedroom. Every little bit should help. Just ask him to explain why people are getting sicker and fatter then 100 years ago and see if he can connect the dots. Technology has developed so rapidly that a human has not been able to adapt genetically.

I am reading a book called The Story of the Human Body by Daniel Liberman [http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00C8S9VCK/ref=kinw\_myk\_ro\_title](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00C8S9VCK/ref=kinw_myk_ro_title) and it talks about how the human body has been able to adapt over the centuries to environmental changes but with all this new technology (including new foods) the body cannot adapt quick enough thus why we are getting so sick. Maybe get that book and see if he will read it but I cannot get my husband to read it yet so good luck.

### JoeBranca — 2014-02-03T21:30:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-techies-out-there.9576/post-108160>

I work IT/sysadmin at a university in Irvine, CA. I also have to work remotely on a regular basis. All of this meaning I'm on the computer way too often and work and commute through major EMF soup. Here are details on the items and habits I've implemented so far, based on what I think will help my situation, though not clear yet on which changes are the most or least effective:

At home:

\- I have everyone sleeping on magnetico pads now. My wife and I are on a 10gauss pad (I'll get another 10gauss when the opportunity is right), and our 3 year old sleeps on a 5g pad. Even the dogs sleep on a 5g often. Our water supply also "sleeps" on 5gauss ; )

\- No devices plugged in any of the bedrooms, in fact the circuits feeding most of the bedroom outlets are permanently off.

\- If I wake early enough I try to make time to take the dogs out for a nature hike along a local crest of hills, barefoot and in the morning sun. The preferred spot is where a lot of pine trees are along the path.

\- For post sunset lighting conditions, most light bulbs have been changed out for amber-ish "bug lights" from Walmart, low blue light LEDs, and a couple of himalayan salt lamps. Additionally I wear red blue blocking "safety" glasses made by Dewalt, which does a tremendous job of blocking out most blue as well as green light, though some slips through the sides now and then since they're not completely wrap around.

\- In the past year I eliminated Wi fi from the home and gone back to cat6 cable, also opting out of our smart meter for the extra fee, so there are no longer any RF sources inside the home.

\- 95% of the day I keep my cheap cell phone in airplane mode, and when I do have it on it is outside of the house but in earshot if it rings. All calls are taken outside on speaker phone, and kept short as necessary. No smart phones in the family

\- we've almost completely stopped watching blu rays or Netflix at night time. Our night time activities are more relaxing now and For reading I use a non-backlit Kindle 3 and a red light headlamp for light.

\- When I have to work from home, I try to "batch" my computer tasks around morning early daylight hours or near sunset, but many times I have to work later at night as well. So in addition to the safety glasses and f.lux, I also place a rectangular cutout red film over the laptop screen, the kind used for filtering theatrical lighting. I use USB connected keyboard and mouse to the laptop to keep some distance from it, though it is also a low voltage aluminum cased HP Spectre ultrabook it still picks up significant magnetic field readings near the motherboard. I also ground the laptop with the USB/ground wire concoction mentioned from the EMF webinar.

\- I turn off all circuit breakers at night except for the fridge. In the daytime I also keep the bedroom breakers off since our toddler takes afternoon naps and plays in her bedroom, etc.

At work:

(My office is shared, with fluorescent light, on the second floor of a building with antennas above me and Wi Fi router/repeater right outside in the hallway. There are also classrooms and science labs in the building.)

\- I have one salt lamp and one Citrine & Amethyst crystal "tree" on my desk near the computer, they are also between me and the wi fi router on the other side of the wall:
[http://www.ebay.com/itm/Citrine-Amethyst-Geode-Crystal-Cluster-Wire-Life-Tree-TWIN-SPIRIT-TREE-COOL-/300985264062](http://www.ebay.com/itm/Citrine-Amethyst-Geode-Crystal-Cluster-Wire-Life-Tree-TWIN-SPIRIT-TREE-COOL-/300985264062)
[http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B001892AX2/ref=oh\_details\_o05\_s00\_i00](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B001892AX2/ref=oh_details_o05_s00_i00)

\- I wear a black Tourmaline necklace, and carry an extra chunk of Amethyst crystal in the pocket

\- when I commute in my truck I have the salt lamp, a couple of magnets under the driver's seat, and a 44lb cast iron kettlebell in the back seat.

\- I drink up to 2 ice cold Gerolsteiners during the work day, as well as additional RO water I bring from home. Typically they've been sitting on top of magneticos overnight or longer

\- I do have servers and systems I manage in campus data centers, but like most in our organization I am able to limit my physical presence in those data centers with remote access tools. If I do have to spend a lot of time in the data center for some reason, I try to mitigate by taking extra CoQ10, Mg, glutamine, water, etc. And some relaxation breathing exercises with additional grounding outside when possible.

\- for lunch hours I don't eat lunch because of high fat/protein breakfasts, so I often just get outside and get moving, in the sun, barefoot and shirtless when possible. Either go to a large park off campus with lots of movnat potential, or to one of the local beaches which are only a 10 - 15 minute drive. I'll often try to swim in the cold Pacific Ocean for at least 20 minutes, do some mobility work on the sand or climb beachside cliffs, etc.

The biggest concerns still to address:

\- The smart meter mesh we live in our neighborhood. Though our smart meter is gone, our closest neighbor's meter is only an additional 20 - 30 feet away, and one of the neighborhood collector meters is at the end of the street a little over 100 yards away, likely most of the signals coming from the direction of the other side of our house. I don't have adequate measurement tools or knowledge of the right kind of materials to block /absorb some of this RF we are caught in without doing it wrong and deflecting EMF back at us. I'm considering ponying up the cash for professional consulting. I may also speak with my neighbor soon about the idea of opting out of the smart meter.

\- Our toddler's new bedroom also happens to have the breaker box mounted on the outside corner of the house where that room is. I arranged it so the bed is farthest from that corner, and a large dresser directly in front of where the magnetic field measures strongest. Once the magnetic field reader is moved away from that part of the wall and beyond the dressers, the milligauss readings drop off almost completely. Nevertheless, I am not sure if that's reassuring enough to not worry about trying to relocate the breaker box.

\- Need to test/get consult for dirty electricity in the home, and the integrity of the ground

\- The closest above ground high voltage power lines are 1 mile away from the home, the closest RF mobile tower is 1/2 mile away. I have not done enough measuring or research to know if these are big enough factors to consider moving, let alone the smart meter situation.

\- Eventually I want to work towards a schedule of working 1 day a week from home, but this is assuming the EMF situation is better at home than it is at work, without thorough testing for accurate comparison. I get the sense this is the case based on what I think I know about the EMF sources present in each environment.

### AnnieW — 2014-02-04T04:18:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-techies-out-there.9576/post-108245>

My husband is also uncooperative with my suggestions. He's a PC tech and refuses to turn off the router at night because the one we have is crappy and apparently it needs to be reconfigured any time he plugs it back in. He has a whole Cisco lab across from our bedroom. He also said since we live in a townhouse there is nothing we can do to completely rid ourselves of EMF, and that cell phones are more dangerous. I've basically stopped talking to him about this stuff. I'm thinking about wearing a foil helmet to bed, lol.

### scubasteve — 2014-02-05T04:30:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-techies-out-there.9576/post-108395>

I work nightshift at an IT company and am around massive electrical fields from thousands of computers in a very close proximity. I know..... its not a great situation. I do LOADS of CT, I have an ice vest strapped to me most of the time at home, have been using sunlight for water charge separation post CT when possible, don't wear any warming clothes, just a T shirt and pants in the coldest weather. Drink RO water, eat strictly keto and eat fish eggs daily and eat a pack of sardines plus some oysters a few times a week.
I own a 10g magnetico and schumann resonator,additionally I have purchased a biomat to use post CT, I haven't tested this much yet so I don't vouch for it.

Was grounded at home through a wire running down a window but after renting emf sensors I found that the highway behind my house was outputting a lot of signals. I removed the grounding mat since I was worried signal would travel up the line.

Supplements
Ubiquinol, Acetyl L carnitine, Bitter Melon, Capsaicin, D Ribose, Magnesium Malate, Magnesium Threonate.

So how does all of this stack up... doing okay. Whenever I am off and on a day schedule every few months I feel amazing.

I used to live in a flat covered in multiple wifi and moved recently. One thing I noticed is that I am not nearly as thirsty and I do not feel a need to stress eat.

Have you all ever gone and stayed in a cabin out in the wilderness? A night out in the boonies with zero tech and you will probably feel like a million bucks in the morning. I used to think this was due to a much needed vacation but this blog has taught me otherwise.

If he needs to be convinced there are tons of educational blogs on this site, once you get the idea across that water is the primary energy medium in the body and that emf causes calcium efflux to dehydrate your cells you simply have to connect the dots. Jack has also stated that emf also breaks quantum tunneling in the mitochondria but this is a little bit tougher to educate new people on. Maybe you could just leave a copy of the body electric on the counter table and encourage him to read it. I wish you luck on convincing your husband to your cause and I can tell you that with a supportive significant other this journey gets way better.

### Shijin13 — 2014-02-05T19:17:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-techies-out-there.9576/post-108498>

Here's my set up. I've docked my macbook pro - its sitting on a wooden file cabinet, along with the wired modem and wired router. I have a himalayan salt lamp sitting next to all of these things. My Monitor is on my standing desk. My work PC is next to that, along with my work Monitor (those are only on when working) I ground my mbp and pc using the grounding USB - but due to wiring issues I've got to run an extension cord to ground them properly.... waiting for the electrician to come give us an estimate to properly ground the plugs in my office.

at night we turn off the breakers w/the exception to the fridge and deep freezer. Lights are turned off at sunset, and we use candles and red head lamps for light.

everyone has a 20gauss magnetico.
we're still trying to figure out the best solution for addressing the electrical transmission line that runs along the top of the house to the meter along the kids' bedroom. So far we haven't had success.... so we're trying to decide if we should just move the kids downstairs or if we should put up some mu metal. We know we'll be moving sometime in the future.... just plotting our escape route.

CT /ground/sun as much as possible. temp in the house is kept at 62 deg on the main level, but due to the single pane windows the rest of the house runs below 60 most of the time.

I also ground the laptop with the USB/ground wire concoction mentioned from the EMF webinar.
>
> \- I turn off all circuit breakers at night except for the fridge. In the daytime I also keep the bedroom breakers off since our toddler takes afternoon naps and plays in her bedroom, etc.
>
>
> At work:
>
> (My office is shared, with fluorescent light, on the second floor of a building with antennas above me and Wi Fi router/repeater right outside in the hallway. There are also classrooms and science labs in the building.)
>
> \- I have one salt lamp and one Citrine & Amethyst crystal "tree" on my desk near the computer, they are also between me and the wi fi router on the other side of the wall:
> [http://www.ebay.com/itm/Citrine-Ame...Life-Tree-TWIN-SPIRIT-TREE-COOL-/300985264062](http://www.ebay.com/itm/Citrine-Amethyst-Geode-Crystal-Cluster-Wire-Life-Tree-TWIN-SPIRIT-TREE-COOL-/300985264062)
> [http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B001892AX2/ref=oh\_details\_o05\_s00\_i00](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B001892AX2/ref=oh_details_o05_s00_i00)
>
> \- I wear a black Tourmaline necklace, and carry an extra chunk of Amethyst crystal in the pocket
>
> \- when I commute in my truck I have the salt lamp, a couple of magnets under the driver's seat, and a 44lb cast iron kettlebell in the back seat.
>
> \- I drink up to 2 ice cold Gerolsteiners during the work day, as well as additional RO water I bring from home. Typically they've been sitting on top of magneticos overnight or longer
>
> \- I do have servers and systems I manage in campus data centers, but like most in our organization I am able to limit my physical presence in those data centers with remote access tools. If I do have to spend a lot of time in the data center for some reason, I try to mitigate by taking extra CoQ10, Mg, glutamine, water, etc. And some relaxation breathing exercises with additional grounding outside when possible.
>
> \- for lunch hours I don't eat lunch because of high fat/protein breakfasts, so I often just get outside and get moving, in the sun, barefoot and shirtless when possible. Either go to a large park off campus with lots of movnat potential, or to one of the local beaches which are only a 10 - 15 minute drive. I'll often try to swim in the cold Pacific Ocean for at least 20 minutes, do some mobility work on the sand or climb beachside cliffs, etc.
>
>
> The biggest concerns still to address:
>
> \- The smart meter mesh we live in our neighborhood. Though our smart meter is gone, our closest neighbor's meter is only an additional 20 - 30 feet away, and one of the neighborhood collector meters is at the end of the street a little over 100 yards away, likely most of the signals coming from the direction of the other side of our house. I don't have adequate measurement tools or knowledge of the right kind of materials to block /absorb some of this RF we are caught in without doing it wrong and deflecting EMF back at us. I'm considering ponying up the cash for professional consulting. I may also speak with my neighbor soon about the idea of opting out of the smart meter.
>
> \- Our toddler's new bedroom also happens to have the breaker box mounted on the outside corner of the house where that room is. I arranged it so the bed is farthest from that corner, and a large dresser directly in front of where the magnetic field measures strongest. Once the magnetic field reader is moved away from that part of the wall and beyond the dressers, the milligauss readings drop off almost completely. Nevertheless, I am not sure if that's reassuring enough to not worry about trying to relocate the breaker box.
>
> \- Need to test/get consult for dirty electricity in the home, and the integrity of the ground
>
> \- The closest above ground high voltage power lines are 1 mile away from the home, the closest RF mobile tower is 1/2 mile away. I have not done enough measuring or research to know if these are big enough factors to consider moving, let alone the smart meter situation.
>
> \- Eventually I want to work towards a schedule of working 1 day a week from home, but this is assuming the EMF situation is better at home than it is at work, without thorough testing for accurate comparison. I get the sense this is the case based on what I think I know about the EMF sources present in each environment.

### Michele Wilson — 2014-09-30T03:37:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-techies-out-there.9576/post-142211>

can anyone here tell me if looking at computers during the day (with their high blue light emittance) have a negative effect? I use blue blockers during the evenings, but at work I am surrounded by bright computers and sometimes get headaches. I specifically want to know if they affect circadian rhythm or melatonin production if you see the screens during daylight hours. I understand the amount of blue emitted is a higher proportion than natural sunlight, unless I am wrong?

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-01-05T08:19:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-techies-out-there.9576/post-151663>

> [JoeBranca said:](/goto/post?id=108160)
>
> I work IT/sysadmin at a university in Irvine, CA. I also have to work remotely on a regular basis. All of this meaning I'm on the computer way too often and work and commute through major EMF soup. Here are details on the items and habits I've implemented so far, based on what I think will help my situation, though not clear yet on which changes are the most or least effective:
>
> At home:
>
> \- I have everyone sleeping on magnetico pads now. My wife and I are on a 10gauss pad (I'll get another 10gauss when the opportunity is right), and our 3 year old sleeps on a 5g pad. Even the dogs sleep on a 5g often. Our water supply also "sleeps" on 5gauss ; )
>
> \- No devices plugged in any of the bedrooms, in fact the circuits feeding most of the bedroom outlets are permanently off.
>
> \- If I wake early enough I try to make time to take the dogs out for a nature hike along a local crest of hills, barefoot and in the morning sun. The preferred spot is where a lot of pine trees are along the path.
>
> \- For post sunset lighting conditions, most light bulbs have been changed out for amber-ish "bug lights" from Walmart, low blue light LEDs, and a couple of himalayan salt lamps. Additionally I wear red blue blocking "safety" glasses made by Dewalt, which does a tremendous job of blocking out most blue as well as green light, though some slips through the sides now and then since they're not completely wrap around.
>
> \- In the past year I eliminated Wi fi from the home and gone back to cat6 cable, also opting out of our smart meter for the extra fee, so there are no longer any RF sources inside the home.
>
> \- 95% of the day I keep my cheap cell phone in airplane mode, and when I do have it on it is outside of the house but in earshot if it rings. All calls are taken outside on speaker phone, and kept short as necessary. No smart phones in the family
>
> \- we've almost completely stopped watching blu rays or Netflix at night time. Our night time activities are more relaxing now and For reading I use a non-backlit Kindle 3 and a red light headlamp for light.
>
> \- When I have to work from home, I try to "batch" my computer tasks around morning early daylight hours or near sunset, but many times I have to work later at night as well. So in addition to the safety glasses and f.lux, I also place a rectangular cutout red film over the laptop screen, the kind used for filtering theatrical lighting. I use USB connected keyboard and mouse to the laptop to keep some distance from it, though it is also a low voltage aluminum cased HP Spectre ultrabook it still picks up significant magnetic field readings near the motherboard. # Thread: Interesting NASA development this weekend regarding COLD
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-nasa-development-this-weekend-regarding-cold.9577/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-03T17:44:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-nasa-development-this-weekend-regarding-cold.9577/post-108118>

Looks like they are looking for the BEC I spoke about in EE 6.

"NASA has revealed its plans to create the coldest spot in the known universe on board the International Space Station in 2016. The researchers are preparing to study matter at temperatures near absolute zero, revealing the world of quantum mechanics.
Weâ€™re going to study matter at temperatures far colder than are found naturally,â€￾ said the projectâ€™s head scientist Rob Thompson of Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL)."We aim to push effective temperatures down to 100 pico-Kelvin."

"One hundred pico-Kelvin is remarkable in that it is a mere ten billionth of a degree above absolute zero (0K or −273.15Â°C) â€“ a point on an imaginary thermometer where all thermal activity of atoms theoretically halts. When temperatures are so low, our traditional ideas of atomic behavior cease to apply. The matter is no longer solid, liquid or gas â€“ its atoms tend to create quantum forms of matter."

[http://science.nasa.gov/science-news/science-at-nasa/2014/30jan\_coldspot/](http://science.nasa.gov/science-news/science-at-nasa/2014/30jan_coldspot/)

# Thread: Plant eyes and your eyes: the quantum tie.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plant-eyes-and-your-eyes-the-quantum-tie.9582/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-03T20:09:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/plant-eyes-and-your-eyes-the-quantum-tie.9582/post-108139>

Many important biological processes taking place in cells are driven and controlled by events that involve electronic degrees of freedom and, therefore, require a quantum mechanical description. An important example are enzymatically catalyzed, cellular biochemical reactions. Here, bond breaking and bond formation events are intimately tied to changes in the electronic degrees of freedom. Key events during photosynthesis in plants and energy metabolism in eukaryotes also warrant a quantum mechanical description - from the absorption of light in the form of photons by the photosynthetic apparatus to electron transfer processes sustaining the electrochemical membrane potential. Because of the importance of sensing light to both plants (for regulating vital functions) and animals (for vision), the interaction between light and biological photoreceptors is widespread in nature, and also requires a quantum mechanical description.

A prime example is the protein rhodopsin which is present in the retina of the human eye and plays a key role in vision. New computational tools are now being developed to combine quantum mechanical/molecular (QM/MM) computer simulations, that allow to combine an electronic level description of the active region with a classical model of the environment provided by the remainder of the biomolecular system and solvent. This allows researchers to study the electronic level processes underlying these systems in their natural cellular environment.

How plants see and and how this system translates in a quantize framework:

The ability to sense light is crucial for both plants and animals; animals use their vision to navigate and interact with their surroundings, whereas plants grow toward light to optimize photosynthesis. One of the most important photosensors in plants relies on tiny molecular switches (Maxwell Demons) known as LOV domains.

When light strikes a LOV domain, it causes the formation of a **single chemical bond**; the unique structure of the LOV domain converts this **subtle change** into a protein unfolding event that triggers neural signaling. The mechanism through which LOV domains **amplify bond formation** into large-scale molecular motion is of great interest both for designing light-activated proteins for synthetic biology applications, and as a model for understanding the harder-to-study molecular switches that govern most of the functions of living cells.

As reported recently researchers used a series of long-timescale molecular dynamics simulations to show the locations of molecular levers (Maxwell Demons) that allow light-induced bond formation to rearrange the entire structure of the LOV domain. Here we see where light can change the structure of matter. This is clearly a quantized effect. The simulations highlighted two main paths of information flow from the heart of the photoreceptor to the surface of the protein, giving unprecedented insight into the function of this light-activated molecular switch. (A Maxwell Demon)

CITES:
When light falls in LOV: A quantum mechanical/molecular mechanical study of photoexcitation in Phot-LOV1 of Chlamydomonas reinhardtii. Markus Dittrich, Peter L. Freddolino, and Klaus Schulten. Journal of Physical Chemistry B, 109:13006-13013, 2005.
Dynamic switching mechanisms in LOV1 and LOV2 domains of plant phototropins. Peter L. Freddolino, Markus Dittrich, and Klaus Schulten. Biophysical Journal, 91:3630-3639, 2006.
Modulating LOV domain photodynamics with a residue alteration outside the chromophore binding site. Sang-Hun Song, Peter Freddolino, Abigail Nash, Elizabeth Carroll, Klaus Schulten, Kevin Gardner, and Delmar S. Larsen. Biochemistry, 50:2411-2423, 2011.
Signaling mechanisms of LOV domains: new insights from molecular dynamics studies. Peter L. Freddolino, Kevin H. Gardner, and Klaus Schulten. Photochemical & Photobiological Sciences, 12:1158-1170, 2013.
